<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are calibrated to **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns: Part A carries 3 marks each, Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice (typically a/b choice between two questions of 7 marks each, or two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define flame retardants. List any two families of flame retardants with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
Flame retardants are chemical additives compounded into polymers to suppress ignition, slow flame propagation, and reduce smoke emission during combustion.

1. **Halogenated family** — Example: Decabromodiphenyl ether (DecaBDE); acts via gas-phase radical quenching.
2. **Phosphorus family** — Example: Ammonium polyphosphate (APP); acts via condensed-phase char promotion.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for families and examples.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI)? State the LOI value above which a polymer is considered self-extinguishing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
LOI is the **minimum concentration of oxygen (in %)** in an oxygen–nitrogen mixture that will just support combustion of a polymer under specified test conditions (ASTM D2863).

$$\text{LOI (\%)} = \frac{V_{O_2}}{V_{O_2} + V_{N_2}} \times 100$$

A polymer is considered **self-extinguishing** when $\text{LOI} \geq 27\%$, since atmospheric oxygen is only ~21%.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the four major mechanisms by which flame retardants act in polymers. Give suitable examples for each. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**1. Gas-Phase (Vapour-Phase) Radical Quenching Mechanism** *(2 marks)*:
- Halogenated FRs decompose in the flame to release HX (X = Cl, Br).
- HX reacts with the highly reactive H• and OH• radicals in the flame:
$$\text{HX} + \text{H}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{X}^{\bullet}$$
$$\text{HX} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{X}^{\bullet}$$
- The halogen radicals (Cl•, Br•) are far less reactive, breaking the chain reaction in the flame.
- **Example**: TBBPA + Sb₂O₃ synergist in HIPS.

**2. Condensed-Phase Char-Formation Mechanism** *(2 marks)*:
- Phosphorus FRs (e.g., DOPO, APP) decompose to form phosphoric acid, which dehydrates the polymer to yield a carbonaceous char.
$$\text{Polymer–OH} + \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Char} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- The char coats the surface, blocking heat and oxygen transfer.
- **Example**: DOPO in epoxy resins.

**3. Endothermic Cooling Mechanism** *(2 marks)*:
- Metal hydroxides (ATH, MDH) absorb heat on decomposition, releasing water vapor that dilutes combustible gases.
- **Example**: ATH in polyolefin wire and cable compounds.

**4. Intumescent Mechanism** *(1 mark)*:
- Three-component system (APP + PER + MEL) forms a foamed, multicellular char that swells and insulates.
- **Example**: APP/PER/MEL in PP intumescent coatings.

---

#### (b) A polymer sample leaves 30% char residue at 800°C in TGA. Calculate its LOI using Van Krevelen's equation. Comment on its fire behaviour. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Given Data** *(1 mark)*:
- Char residue, CR = 30%

**Step 2 — Van Krevelen's Equation** *(1 mark)*:
$$\text{LOI} = 17.5 + 0.4 \times \text{CR}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution and Calculation** *(2 marks)*:
$$\text{LOI} = 17.5 + 0.4 \times 30 = 17.5 + 12.0 = 29.5\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation** *(3 marks)*:
- Since $\text{LOI} = 29.5\% > 27\%$, the polymer is **self-extinguishing** in normal atmospheric conditions (21% O₂).
- It will not sustain combustion in air and is suitable for **fire-critical applications** (e.g., electrical insulation, aircraft interiors).
- The high char yield (30%) indicates excellent char-forming tendency, likely due to aromatic or heterocyclic backbone structure.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### (a) Classify flame retardants based on (i) method of incorporation and (ii) chemical family. Explain intumescent flame retardant systems in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Classification by Method of Incorporation** *(2 marks)*:
1. **Additive FRs**: Physically blended into the polymer (e.g., ATH, APP, DecaBDE).
2. **Reactive FRs**: Chemically incorporated into the polymer backbone (e.g., DOPO-containing epoxies, vinyl phosphonate monomers).

**Classification by Chemical Family** *(2 marks)*:
- Halogenated, Phosphorus, Nitrogen, Metal Hydroxides, Intumescent, Silicon-based, Nanocomposite.

**Intumescent Flame Retardant System** *(3 marks)*:
An intumescent system requires **three components** working in synergy:

| Component | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Acid source | Acts as dehydrating catalyst | Ammonium polyphosphate (APP) |
| Carbon source | Provides char-forming scaffold | Pentaerythritol (PER) |
| Blowing agent | Releases non-flammable gas to swell the char | Melamine (MEL) |

**Mechanism**: On heating, APP decomposes to phosphoric acid, which esterifies PER to form a char. MEL decomposes to release NH₃, which inflates the char into a **foamed, multicellular barrier** (swelling up to 10× original thickness). This insulating shield:
- Reduces heat transfer to underlying polymer
- Blocks oxygen diffusion
- Traps combustible volatiles

**Applications**: PP intumescent coatings, fire-resistant paints, PU foams, and cable jacketing.

---

#### (b) Discuss the role of antimony trioxide (Sb₂O₃) as a synergist in halogen-based flame retardant systems. Why is it used? Write the key reactions involved. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Role of Sb₂O₃ as Synergist** *(3 marks)*:
- Sb₂O₃ alone has poor flame retardant action, but when combined with halogenated FRs, it produces a **synergistic effect** (1+1 > 2).
- It reacts with HX released from halogenated FRs to form volatile antimony trihalides (SbX₃), which are highly effective flame inhibitors.

**Key Reactions** *(3 marks)*:

$$\text{Sb}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\text{HX} \rightarrow 2\text{SbX}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{SbX}_3 \rightarrow \text{SbX}_2^{\bullet} + \text{X}^{\bullet} \quad \text{(radical release in flame)}$$

The antimony halides act in the gas phase by:
- Releasing halogen radicals (X•) that interrupt the flame chain reaction.
- Forming a dense vapor that displaces oxygen near the burning surface.
- Acting as a "carrier" for the halogen into the gas phase.

**Why Synergistic?** *(1 mark)*:
The Sb:X ratio is typically **1:2 to 1:3** by mass, allowing efficient capture and transport of halogen. This significantly reduces the total FR loading required, minimizing mechanical property degradation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** — Common Mistakes Students Make:
> 1. **Confusing additive vs. reactive FRs** — Reactive FRs are chemically bonded; additive FRs are blended. Examiners deduct 1 mark for confusing these.
> 2. **Writing "Sb₂O₃ is a flame retardant"** — It is a **synergist**, not a primary FR. Saying it is a flame retardant on its own loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units in LOI calculation** — Always state LOI in **%** with reference to **ASTM D2863**.
> 4. **Mixing up mechanisms** — Halogen = gas phase; Phosphorus = condensed phase. Examiners are strict about this.
> 5. **Skipping the LOI = 27 cutoff** — Always mention the **self-extinguishing threshold** when comparing polymer fire performance.
> 6. **Omitting Van Krevelen derivation steps** — Just writing the final formula is not enough; show the substitution and interpretation (2–3 marks lost if skipped).
> 7. **Incorrect Mermaid syntax in diagrams** — Use clean alphanumeric node IDs and avoid markdown bold inside labels.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Definition**: Flame retardants are additives or reactive monomers that suppress ignition and flame propagation in polymers.
- **Four Mechanisms**: Gas-phase (halogen + Sb), Condensed-phase (phosphorus), Endothermic cooling (ATH/MDH), Intumescence (APP/PER/MEL).
- **Two Major Classes**: Additive FR vs. Reactive FR.
- **LOI Definition**: Minimum % O₂ to sustain combustion (ASTM D2863).
- **LOI Self-Extinguishing Cut-off**: **27%**.
- **Van Krevelen Equation**: $\text{LOI} = 17.5 + 0.4 \times \text{CR}$ (CR in %).
- **UL-94 Hierarchy**: V-0 (best) → V-1 → V-2 → HB (worst).
- **Char Yield Calculation**: $\text{CR (\%)} = \dfrac{m_{\text{char}}}{m_{\text{initial}}} \times 100$ (at 700–800°C in TGA).
- **ATH Loading**: 40–65 wt% in polyolefins; $\Delta H \approx -1050$ kJ/kg.
- **MDH Loading**: Similar range; $\Delta H \approx -1300$ kJ/kg (more endothermic, higher thermal stability).
- **Sb₂O₃ Synergist Ratio**: Sb:X = 1:2 to 1:3 by mass.
- **Huggett's Constant**: 13.1 MJ per kg O₂ consumed (cone calorimetry).
- **DOPO**: Phosphorus content ~14.4%, used in epoxy reactive systems.
- **Cone Calorimeter Outputs**: HRR (kW/m²), THR (MJ/m²), SPR (m²/s), TTI (s).
- **Trade-off**: Higher FR loading → better fire performance but worse mechanical properties and processability.
- **Modern Trends**: Shift from halogenated (banned/restricted PBDEs) to **phosphorus-nitrogen intumescent** and **nano-FR** systems.
- **Standards to Remember**: ASTM D2863 (LOI), UL-94 (vertical burn), ISO 5660 (cone calorimeter), ASTM E84 (flame spread index), ISO 4589.
- **Common Applications**: Wire & cable (XLPE/ATH), electronics (PC/ABS/DOPO), automotive (PA66/PBT), textiles (back-coating), construction foams (PIR/PUR).
- **Safety Note**: Modern FR research focuses on **low-smoke, low-toxicity (LSZH)** systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Peptiser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Peptiser — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peptiser (Peptizing Agent):** A *chemical processing additive* used during the **mastication of natural rubber (NR)** that accelerates the oxidative and mechanical breakdown of long polyisoprene macromolecular chains, thereby reducing the **Mooney viscosity**, shortening the mastication cycle, and improving the processability of the raw elastomer without permanently impairing its ultimate physical properties.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, peptisers belong to the broader class of **rubber processing aids** and are functionally classified as *mastication accelerators*. They function by interrupting the natural tendency of mechanically scissioned free-radical chain ends of polyisoprene to recombine (re-crosslink) under the action of atmospheric oxygen and shear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCPOT502 – Module 1):**
> Peptisers are covered under *"Compounding ingredients of polymers – processing aids"*. They are evaluated in board questions linked to **CO1**: *"Understand the fundamental principles of polymer processing and the role of various additives."*

---

## 2. Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to **chew a very tough, rubbery caramel** (long, entangled polymer chains). As you chew (mechanical shear), the strands stretch, snap at weak points, but the broken ends keep sticking back together like magnets — your jaw gets tired and the caramel remains tough.

Now sprinkle a **little lemon juice** on the caramel. The acid (peptiser) "caps" the freshly broken ends, preventing them from re-sticking. Now every snap is **permanent**, so with far less chewing you get a soft, easy-to-swallow lump.

That "lemon juice" in rubber technology is the **peptiser**.

- **Caramel strands** → long polyisoprene chains ($\text{MW} \approx 10^6 \text{ g/mol}$)
- **Chewing force** → mechanical shear in a Banbury / two-roll mill
- **Sticky re-attachment** → free-radical recombination
- **Lemon juice** → peptiser (e.g., pentachlorothiophenol)
- **Soft lump** → low-viscosity, processable rubber

---

## 3. Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following numerical anchors are used throughout this note:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Peptiser dosage in NR | **0.1 – 0.5 phr** | parts per hundred rubber |
| Mastication temperature | **80 – 130 °C** | optimum range |
| Natural rubber raw Mooney | **60 – 90 MU** | before peptisation |
| Target peptised Mooney | **40 – 60 MU** | after peptisation |
| Free-radical bond energy (C–C) | **~ 348 kJ/mol** | scission energy |

> **phr** stands for *parts per hundred rubber* — the standard dosing unit in the elastomer industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Why is Peptisation Needed? — The Molecular Story

When natural rubber is masticated in air (oxygen present), two competing reactions occur simultaneously at the chain scission sites:

1. **Mechanical Chain Scission** — Shearing forces rupture the C–C bonds of polyisoprene, generating two macro-radicals:
   $$\text{R–R} \xrightarrow{\text{shear}} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}\text{R}$$
2. **Radical Recombination (Re-crosslinking)** — In the absence of oxygen or a radical acceptor, the two macro-radicals immediately rejoin:
   $$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}\text{R} \longrightarrow \text{R–R}$$
   This *reverses* the mastication effort and is why mechanical breakdown alone is highly inefficient.

When oxygen is present, the macro-radicals react with O₂ to form peroxy radicals, which leads to chain scission — this is the basis of *oxidative mastication*. Peptisers dramatically accelerate this pathway.

---

## 2. Mechanism of Peptiser Action (Free-Radical Pathway)

The classical free-radical mechanism for a thiophenol-type peptiser (e.g., pentachlorothiophenol, **PCTP**) proceeds in three logical steps:

**Step 1 — Initiation by mechanical shear:**
$$\text{–(CH}_2\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)=CH–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–} \xrightarrow{\text{shear, }\Delta} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}\text{R}$$

**Step 2 — Hydrogen donation by peptiser (radical capping):**
$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{Ar–S–H} \longrightarrow \text{R–H} + \text{Ar–S}^{\bullet}$$

The peptiser donates a hydrogen atom to the macro-radical, *capping* it permanently as a stable hydrocarbon. The peptiser itself becomes a stable thiyl radical (Ar–S•).

**Step 3 — Thiyl radical quenching by oxygen:**
$$2\ \text{Ar–S}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Ar–S–S–Ar} + \text{oxygenated products}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The disulfide product (Ar–S–S–Ar) is *inert* and remains dispersed in the rubber — it neither plasticises nor cures, so it does not interfere with downstream vulcanisation.

**Net Effect:** Each peptiser molecule permanently *caps* one macro-radical, ensuring the scission is irreversible. The molecular weight drops monotonically, and the Mooney viscosity falls rapidly.

---

## 3. Classification of Peptisers

| Class | Example | Mechanism | Typical Loading (phr) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Aryl mercaptans** | Pentachlorothiophenol (PCTP) | H-atom donor | 0.1 – 0.3 |
| **Disulfides** | Dibenzamidodiphenyl disulfide | Radical disproportionation | 0.2 – 0.5 |
| **Metal salts of dithiocarbamates** | Zinc dimethyldithiocarbamate | Ionic / redox | 0.2 – 0.5 |
| **Salts of thiophenols** | Zinc pentachlorothiophenate | Combined H-donor + ionic | 0.2 – 0.4 |
| **Sulfonic acid derivatives** | β-Naphthalenesulfonic acid | Acid-catalysed hydrolysis | 0.5 – 1.0 |

---

## 4. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the *only mathematical tools* required for any KTU board question on peptisers. Memorise the *form*, not just the *symbols*.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{phr} = \dfrac{m_{\text{additive}}}{m_{\text{rubber}}} \times 100$ | Dosing convention | dimensionless |
| 2 | $[\eta] = K\, M_v^{\,a}$ | Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (viscosity–MW link) | dL/g |
| 3 | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average MW after scission | g/mol |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{M_n(t)} - \dfrac{1}{M_n(0)} = k_{\text{pep}}\,t$ | Kinetics of peptisation (pseudo-first order) | mol/g·s |
| 5 | $\Delta E_{\text{scission}} \approx 348\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | C–C bond dissociation energy | kJ/mol |
| 6 | $\text{MU}_{\text{final}} = \text{MU}_{\text{initial}} - \kappa\,[\text{Pep}]$ | Linear Mooney drop vs. peptiser load | MU |

Where:
- $M_v$ = viscosity-average molecular weight
- $K, a$ = Mark–Houwink constants (for NR: $K \approx 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$, $a \approx 0.67$)
- $k_{\text{pep}}$ = peptisation rate constant (depends on T and [O₂])
- $\kappa$ = empirical sensitivity factor, typically $\approx 30$–$60\ \text{MU/phr}$ for PCTP/NR at 100 °C

---

## 5. Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tyre manufacturing**: Peptised NR is the base stock for *tread compounds*, where low Mooney ensures uniform carbon-black dispersion.
- **Energy savings**: Reduces Banbury mixing cycle time by 30 – 50 %, directly cutting factory electricity bills.
- **Improved product consistency**: Tight Mooney distribution → predictable extrusion and calendering behaviour.
- **Latex dipping**: Peptised latex gives thinner, more uniform films for glove / balloon production.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation: Kinetics of Peptisation

We will derive Equation (4) of the formula sheet — the **kinetic law for peptiser-induced molecular-weight reduction** — because it is the single most-likely 7-mark derivation question on this topic.

### 1.1 Starting Hypothesis

Assume that *each successful peptiser-mediated radical-capping event produces exactly one permanent chain scission*. Let the number of rubber molecules per gram be $N(t)$. Then:

$$N(t) = \frac{N_A}{M_n(t)}$$

where $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$ (Avogadro's number).

### 1.2 Rate of Chain Scission

If the peptiser-mediated scission follows first-order kinetics with respect to the number of chain bonds present, then:

$$\frac{dN(t)}{dt} = k_{\text{pep}}\,[\text{Pep}]\,[\text{Bonds}] \approx k'\, N(t)$$

Since $[\text{Bonds}] \propto N(t)$ for long chains, we can collapse the constants:

$$\frac{dN(t)}{dt} = k'\, N(t)$$

### 1.3 Integration

Separate variables and integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = t$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = \int_{0}^{t} k'\, dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right) = k'\, t$$

$$\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = e^{\,k' t}$$

### 1.4 Convert to Molecular Weight

Substitute $N(t) = N_A / M_n(t)$ and $N_0 = N_A / M_n(0)$:

$$\frac{N_A / M_n(t)}{N_A / M_n(0)} = e^{\,k' t}$$

$$\frac{M_n(0)}{M_n(t)} = e^{\,k' t}$$

### 1.5 Rearrange into Linear Form

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{M_n(t)}{M_n(0)}\right) = -k' t$$

Exponentiating back and solving for $1/M_n(t)$:

$$\frac{1}{M_n(t)} = \frac{1}{M_n(0)} + k' t$$

Defining $k_{\text{pep}} = k'$ for the *peptisation-specific* rate constant:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{M_n(t)} - \frac{1}{M_n(0)} = k_{\text{pep}}\, t\;}$$

This is **Equation (4)** from the formula sheet. ✔

---

## 2. Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
> Natural rubber with an initial number-average molecular weight $M_n(0) = 8.0 \times 10^5\ \text{g/mol}$ is masticated at 100 °C with **0.2 phr pentachlorothiophenol**. The peptisation rate constant at this temperature is $k_{\text{pep}} = 4.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{mol/(g·s)}$.
> Calculate the molecular weight after **10 minutes** of mastication.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Convert time to seconds.
$$t = 10\ \text{min} \times 60\ \text{s/min} = 600\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the kinetic equation.
$$\frac{1}{M_n(t)} = \frac{1}{M_n(0)} + k_{\text{pep}}\, t$$

$$\frac{1}{M_n(0)} = \frac{1}{8.0 \times 10^5} = 1.25 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol/g}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the increment term.
$$k_{\text{pep}}\, t = (4.5 \times 10^{-9}) \times 600 = 2.7 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol/g}$$

**Step 4:** Sum.
$$\frac{1}{M_n(t)} = 1.25 \times 10^{-6} + 2.7 \times 10^{-6} = 3.95 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol/g}$$

**Step 5:** Invert.
$$M_n(t) = \frac{1}{3.95 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.53 \times 10^5\ \text{g/mol}$$

### Interpretation

$$\text{Molecular-weight reduction} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^5 - 2.53 \times 10^5}{8.0 \times 10^5} \times 100\% \approx 68.4\%$$

The peptiser reduced the average chain length to **less than one-third** of its original value in just 10 minutes — illustrating the dramatic effectiveness of peptisation in industrial rubber processing.

---

## 3. Analytical Comparison Matrix — With vs. Without Peptiser

| Parameter | Mechanical Mastication Only | With Peptiser (0.2 phr PCTP) | Change |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mastication time to reach MU 50 | 25 – 30 min | 8 – 12 min | ↓ 60 % |
| Energy consumed (kWh/batch) | 18 – 22 | 7 – 10 | ↓ 55 % |
| Final $M_n$ ($\times 10^5$) | 4.5 – 5.0 | 2.0 – 2.5 | ↓ 50 % |
| Batch temperature peak | 150 – 170 °C | 95 – 115 °C | ↓ 35 °C |
| Risk of scorch (premature cure) | Moderate–High | Low | ↓ |
| Final vulcanisate tensile strength | Slightly reduced | Equivalent | ≈ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that **peptiser does NOT significantly reduce final vulcanisate properties** because the peptiser (and its disulfide by-product) is *chemically inert* under curing conditions — it merely accelerates processing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1. Molecular Mechanism Flow — How a Peptiser Works

The following Mermaid block maps the *entire free-radical peptisation pathway* in a single block-level architecture diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Long polyisoprene chain MW approx 10 to power 6 g per mol"] --> B["Mechanical shear in Banbury or two roll mill"]
    B --> C["Chain scission generates two macro radicals R dot"]
    C --> D{"Peptiser present?"}
    D -- "NO" --> E["Radicals recombine R-R reversible scission"]
    D -- "YES" --> F["Peptiser donates H atom to R dot"]
    F --> G["Stable R-H molecule permanent scission"]
    F --> H["Thiyl radical Ar-S dot"]
    H --> I["Two thiyl radicals react with O2"]
    I --> J["Inert disulfide Ar-S-S-Ar dispersed in matrix"]
    G --> K["Lower MW and Mooney viscosity achieved"]
    E --> L["Poor mastication efficiency high energy cost"]
    J --> K
```

### Reading Guide for Students

- **Node A** → the entangled raw NR chains entering the mill
- **Node D** is the **decision branch** — the entire value of the peptiser lies in *forcing the reaction down the YES arm*
- **Nodes E vs. G** are the *two competing outcomes*; peptiser ensures we reach G, not E

---

## 2. Functional Block Diagram — Role of Peptiser in a Rubber Compounding Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph upstream["Upstream"]
        P1["Natural rubber bale"] --> P2["Weighing and cutting"]
        P2 --> P3["Pre-heating 70 to 80 C"]
    end
    subgraph mastication["Mastication Stage"]
        P3 --> M1["Banbury internal mixer"]
        M1 --> M2{"Peptiser addition point"}
        M2 -- "0.2 phr PCTP" --> M3["Shear and oxidation for 8 to 12 min"]
        M3 --> M4["Target Mooney 40 to 60 MU"]
    end
    subgraph downstream["Downstream"]
        M4 --> D1["Masterbatch with fillers and oils"]
        D1 --> D2["Final mixing with curatives"]
        D2 --> D3["Extrusion or calendering"]
        D3 --> D4["Vulcanisation"]
    end
```

---

## 3. Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Peptiser

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Need peptiser?"] --> Q2{"Target Mooney less than 55?"}
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3["Skip peptiser use mastication only"]
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q4{"Operating temperature?"}
    Q4 -- "Less than 100 C" --> Q5["Use PCTP aryl mercaptan"]
    Q4 -- "100 to 140 C" --> Q6["Use zinc salt of pentachlorothiophenate"]
    Q4 -- "Greater than 140 C" --> Q7["Use disulfides high temp stable"]
    Q5 --> Q8["Typical dose 0.1 to 0.3 phr"]
    Q6 --> Q8
    Q7 --> Q8
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Define a peptiser. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A peptiser is a chemical processing aid that accelerates the mechanical breakdown (mastication) of natural rubber by capping the macro-radicals generated during shear, thereby preventing their recombination and producing a permanent reduction in molecular weight and Mooney viscosity.

Examples:
1. **Pentachlorothiophenol (PCTP)** — an aryl mercaptan, used at 0.1 – 0.3 phr.
2. **Dibenzamidodiphenyl disulfide** — a disulfide-type peptiser, used at 0.2 – 0.5 phr.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Why is a peptiser not generally used in synthetic rubbers like SBR?**

**Model Answer:**
Synthetic rubbers such as SBR and BR are polymerised at controlled molecular weights directly during emulsion or solution polymerisation, and their macro-radicals do not recombine as readily as those of NR. Additionally, the residual polymerisation chain-transfer agents already present act as built-in radical capping agents. Hence, peptisers are primarily a **natural-rubber-specific processing aid**.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Peptiser Questions:**
> 1. Many students write *platiciser* instead of *peptiser* — these are **completely different additives**. Platicisers reduce Tg and soften the polymer; peptisers reduce MW during processing. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark per occurrence**.
> 2. When drawing the mechanism, students frequently **omit the role of oxygen** in quenching the thiyl radical. This is a **3-mark loss** in any 14-mark mechanism question.
> 3. Always quote **phr units** and state the **typical dosage range** (0.1 – 0.5 phr) — a numerical anchor signals board-level preparation and earns easy partial credit.

---

### Question B1 (Option A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Choice A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat free-radical mechanism, explain how pentachlorothiophenol acts as a peptiser during the mastication of natural rubber. State the role of oxygen in the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural-rubber batch has an initial Mooney viscosity of 78 MU. After adding 0.25 phr of a peptiser and masticating at 110 °C for 12 minutes, the Mooney drops to 48 MU. Calculate:
  - (i) The percentage reduction in Mooney viscosity. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) The empirical sensitivity factor $\kappa$ (in MU/phr). **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) The peptiser loading that would be required to reach a target Mooney of 40 MU, assuming linear behaviour. **(2 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B1(a)

**Step 1 — State the problem:**
During mastication, mechanical shear ruptures the C–C backbone of the cis-1,4-polyisoprene chain, generating two macro-radicals.

$$\text{R–R} \xrightarrow{\text{shear}} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}\text{R}$$

**Step 2 — Role of the peptiser:**
The S–H bond of pentachlorothiophenol is relatively weak (~ 290 kJ/mol), allowing it to readily donate its hydrogen atom to a macro-radical:

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{C}_6\text{Cl}_5\text{–S–H} \longrightarrow \text{R–H} + \text{C}_6\text{Cl}_5\text{–S}^{\bullet}$$

This *capping* step is irreversible under processing conditions, ensuring permanent chain scission.

**Step 3 — Role of oxygen:**
The thiyl radical $\text{C}_6\text{Cl}_5\text{–S}^{\bullet}$ is itself reactive and would otherwise re-initiate radical reactions. Atmospheric oxygen rapidly quenches it to form a stable, inert disulfide:

$$2\ \text{C}_6\text{Cl}_5\text{–S}^{\bullet} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{Cl}_5\text{–S–S–C}_6\text{Cl}_5$$

This is why peptisation is performed in air (and is actually less effective under nitrogen).

**Step 4 — Net outcome:**
Each peptiser molecule causes one permanent chain scission. The molecular weight drops, Mooney viscosity falls, and processing energy is saved.

---

### Model Solution to Question B1(b)

**(i) Percentage reduction:**
$$\%\ \text{Reduction} = \frac{78 - 48}{78} \times 100\% = \frac{30}{78} \times 100\% \approx 38.46\%$$

**(ii) Empirical sensitivity factor $\kappa$:**

Using $\Delta \text{MU} = \kappa \cdot [\text{Pep}]$:

$$\kappa = \frac{\Delta \text{MU}}{[\text{Pep}]} = \frac{78 - 48}{0.25} = \frac{30}{0.25} = 120\ \text{MU/phr}$$

**(iii) Required loading for MU = 40:**

Total Mooney drop required:
$$\Delta \text{MU}_{\text{target}} = 78 - 40 = 38\ \text{MU}$$

Required peptiser loading:
$$[\text{Pep}]_{\text{required}} = \frac{\Delta \text{MU}_{\text{target}}}{\kappa} = \frac{38}{120} \approx 0.317\ \text{phr} \approx 0.32\ \text{phr}$$

---

### Question B2 (Option B — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Choice B]**

**(a)** Classify peptisers with suitable examples. Explain why peptisers are used only in natural rubber and not in most synthetic rubbers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the kinetic equation governing molecular-weight reduction during peptisation:
  $$\frac{1}{M_n(t)} - \frac{1}{M_n(0)} = k_{\text{pep}}\, t$$
  and use it to compute the time required to reduce $M_n$ from $9.0 \times 10^5\ \text{g/mol}$ to $3.0 \times 10^5\ \text{g/mol}$ at 100 °C, given $k_{\text{pep}} = 3.0 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{mol/(g·s)}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B2(a)

**Classification Table:**

| Class | Example |
| :--- | :--- |
| Aryl mercaptans | Pentachlorothiophenol |
| Disulfides | Dibenzamidodiphenyl disulfide |
| Metal dithiocarbamates | Zinc dimethyldithiocarbamate |
| Metal salts of thiophenols | Zinc pentachlorothiophenate |
| Sulfonic acids | β-Naphthalenesulfonic acid |

**Why only for NR?**

1. Natural rubber (cis-1,4-polyisoprene) is a high-MW polymer with a strong tendency toward oxidative chain scission followed by radical recombination during mastication.
2. Synthetic rubbers like SBR, BR, and IIR are synthesised at pre-controlled, lower MWs and contain residual chain-transfer agents that already cap radicals.
3. Hence, the marginal benefit of peptisers in synthetic rubbers is negligible — they are a **natural-rubber-specific processing aid**.

---

### Model Solution to Question B2(b)

**Derivation Outline (already shown in detail in Section 3):**

- Step 1: Number of molecules $N(t) = N_A / M_n(t)$.
- Step 2: First-order rate: $dN/dt = k' N$.
- Step 3: Integrate: $N(t) = N_0 e^{k't}$.
- Step 4: Convert to $M_n$: $M_n(t) = M_n(0) e^{-k't}$.
- Step 5: Rearrange: $\dfrac{1}{M_n(t)} - \dfrac{1}{M_n(0)} = k_{\text{pep}}\, t$.

**Numerical Computation:**

Rearrange for $t$:

$$t = \frac{1}{k_{\text{pep}}}\left(\frac{1}{M_n(t)} - \frac{1}{M_n(0)}\right)$$

Substitute values:

$$\frac{1}{M_n(t)} = \frac{1}{3.0 \times 10^5} = 3.333 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol/g}$$

$$\frac{1}{M_n(0)} = \frac{1}{9.0 \times 10^5} = 1.111 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol/g}$$

$$\Delta\!\left(\frac{1}{M_n}\right) = (3.333 - 1.111) \times 10^{-6} = 2.222 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol/g}$$

$$t = \frac{2.222 \times 10^{-6}}{3.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 740.7\ \text{s}$$

Converting to minutes:

$$t = \frac{740.7}{60} \approx 12.35\ \text{minutes}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **12.4 minutes** are required to achieve the desired molecular-weight reduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Peptisers** are *mastication accelerators* — they shorten mastication time, save energy, and lower Mooney viscosity.
- ✅ They work via a **free-radical capping mechanism** — hydrogen donation from S–H group to macro-radicals.
- ✅ **Pentachlorothiophenol (PCTP)** is the *most commonly cited example* in KTU questions.
- ✅ Typical dosage: **0.1 – 0.5 phr** of NR.
- ✅ Peptisers are **effective only in the presence of oxygen** (oxidative mastication).
- ✅ By-product is an *inert disulfide* (Ar–S–S–Ar) that does not interfere with vulcanisation.
- ✅ The peptisation kinetic equation is:
  $$\dfrac{1}{M_n(t)} - \dfrac{1}{M_n(0)} = k_{\text{pep}}\, t$$
- ✅ Peptisers are **specific to NR** — rarely used with SBR, BR, EPDM, or other synthetic elastomers.
- ✅ Operating temperature window: **80 – 130 °C** for optimum peptiser action.
- ✅ Do **NOT confuse** peptiser with **plasticiser** — they are functionally and chemically distinct additives.
- ✅ KTU board questions on peptisers typically test: **(i)** definition + examples (3 marks), **(ii)** mechanism with role of O₂ (7 marks), and **(iii)** kinetics derivation + numerical (7 marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retarder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Retarder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — RETARDERS IN POLYMER PROCESSING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **DEFINITION — RETARDER (CURING INHIBITOR)**
> A **retarder** (also called a *curing inhibitor*, *stabiliser*, or *latency-providing agent*) is a low-concentration additive deliberately incorporated into a thermosetting resin system to **decelerate or temporarily suppress the crosslinking (curing) reaction** at processing temperatures, thereby extending the **pot life**, **shelf life**, and **storage stability** of the resin without permanently arresting the cure.

In the strict context of **PCPOT502 — Polymer Processing**, retarders belong to the broader family of *cure modifiers* used predominantly in **thermoset moulding compounds** such as **Phenolic (PF), Urea-Formaldehyde (UF), Melamine-Formaldehyde (MF), Unsaturated Polyester (UPE), Vinyl Ester, and Epoxy systems** processed by **compression moulding, transfer moulding, injection moulding of BMC/SMC, and pultrusion**.

The typical dosage is **extremely low — in the range of 100 ppm to 0.5 phr** (parts per hundred parts of resin), and yet it dramatically alters the processing window.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Marathon Sprinter"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INTUITION — THE SPRINTER WITH TRAINING WHEELS**
> Imagine a marathon runner (the resin) who is *bursting* with energy and wants to sprint the moment the starting pistol fires (the heat + catalyst in the mould). A **retarder** is like attaching *training wheels with a friction brake* to the sprinter. The runner can still warm up, stretch, jog slowly through the mixing and moulding stages (low-temperature stability), but only when the *actual race heat* (mould temperature ~150–180 °C) is applied for a sustained time does the brake release and the sprinter runs at full pace (rapid cure). This *delayed sprint* is exactly what polymer processors need for safe handling.

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Resin System | Common Retarder | Typical Loading |
|---|---|---|
| Unsaturated Polyester | Hydroquinone, *tert*-Butyl Catechol (TBC), Toluhydroquinone (THQ) | 100 – 500 ppm |
| Vinyl Ester | Hydroquinone, MEHQ (Mono-methyl ether of hydroquinone) | 50 – 300 ppm |
| Phenolic Resin (Resol) | Mineral acids (HCl, H₂SO₄), phthalic anhydride, lactic acid | 0.1 – 2 phr |
| Epoxy (DGEBA + amine) | Boric acid, salicylic acid (latent systems) | 1 – 5 phr |
| Unsaturated Polyester → SMC/BMC | Polymerisation inhibitor (e.g., benzoquinone) | 200 – 1000 ppm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** Effective retarder concentration is typically reported in **ppm (parts per million)** for liquid resins and **phr (parts per hundred resin)** for bulk moulding compounds. **1 phr = 10,000 ppm** for a single-resin matrix.

### 1.4 Why Retarders Are Essential — Processing Viewpoint

1. **Latency** in one-pack systems (no mixing required at mould).
2. **Shelf Life Extension** of pre-pregs, SMC/BMC sheets, powder coatings.
3. **Flow Time Extension** during mould filling, especially in large/thick mouldings.
4. **Exotherm Control** — prevents runaway curing in bulk castings.
5. **Viscosity Stability** — avoids premature gelling during storage or pumping.
6. **Demould Time Optimisation** — gives a flat, controllable cure onset temperature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Retardation (Free-Radical Capture Theory)

Most retarders used in vinyl-addition thermosets (UPE, VE) operate via a **radical-capture (chain-breaking) mechanism**. The cure proceeds via free-radical initiation:

$$\text{Initiator} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{R}^{\bullet}$$

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{M} \longrightarrow \text{RM}^{\bullet}$$

Without retarder, propagation continues unimpeded. **With retarder (InH)**, a competing chain-transfer step occurs:

$$\text{RM}^{\bullet} + \text{InH} \longrightarrow \text{RMH} + \text{In}^{\bullet}$$

The resulting **inhibitor radical (In•)** is **resonance-stabilised** (e.g., the semi-quinone radical from hydroquinone) and is **incapable of reinitiating polymerisation** efficiently. The active radical is effectively *quenched*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CORE PRINCIPLE** — The retarder radical (In•) is much less reactive than the propagating radical (RM•). It does *not* abstract hydrogen from the monomer, so the chain is *temporarily* suspended. Once the retarder is *consumed*, normal propagation resumes. This is why retarders are sometimes called *delayed-action inhibitors* — they are *consumed stoichiometrically* during cure.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Retarder Action

- **Step 1 — Mixing Stage (Room Temperature):** Retarder + catalyst + resin co-exist; no significant radical flux.
- **Step 2 — Induction Period (t < tᵢₙd):** Initiator radicals are preferentially trapped by retarder; no polymerisation.
- **Step 3 — Induction Time End (t = tᵢₙd):** Retarder concentration drops to a critical threshold; polymerisation begins.
- **Step 4 — Acceleration:** Normal propagation dominates; cure proceeds rapidly.
- **Step 5 — Termination:** Retarder by-products remain trapped in the network (low concentration — negligible property impact).

## 2.3 Acid Retarders in Phenolic Resols (Condensation Systems)

Phenolic resols are alkaline-catalysed condensation resins of phenol + formaldehyde. Here, retarders act by **lowering the pH or buffering the catalyst activity**:

$$\text{OH}^- + \text{H}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

By adding a weak acid (e.g., lactic, phthalic), the **available hydroxide catalyst concentration drops**, which:

- Reduces the rate of methylene-bridge formation ($\text{–CH}_2\text{–}$ and $\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–CH}_2\text{–}$).
- Lengthens the *B-stage* working life of resol-impregnated substrates.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU EXAM FORMULA SHEET — RETARDERS**

| Equation / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Induction time with retarder | $t_{\text{ind}} \approx \dfrac{[\text{InH}]_0}{R_i \cdot f}$ | $t_{\text{ind}}$ = induction period (s); $[InH]_0$ = initial retarder conc. (mol/L); $R_i$ = rate of initiation (mol/(L·s)); $f$ = efficiency factor | Valid for radical-retardation (chain-transfer model). |
| Rate of polymerisation (classical) | $R_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{R_i}{k_t}} \cdot k_p \cdot [M]$ | $k_p$ = propagation rate constant; $k_t$ = termination constant; $[M]$ = monomer conc. | Reduced effectively during induction. |
| Effect of retarder on $R_p$ | $R_p \downarrow \text{ as } [\text{InH}] \uparrow$ | Inverse relation | Hallmark of retardation. |
| Shelf-life approximation (Arrhenius) | $\ln t_{\text{shelf}} = \dfrac{E_a}{R T} + \text{const}$ | $E_a$ = activation energy; $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K); $T$ = storage temp (K) | Doubling retarder conc. roughly *doubles* $t_{\text{shelf}}$. |
| Phr ↔ ppm conversion | $\text{ppm} = \text{phr} \times 10^4$ | (for single-resin basis) | Memorise. |
| Pot life (gel time) | $t_{\text{gel}} = \dfrac{[\text{InH}]_0}{2 f k_d [I]_0}$ | $[I]_0$ = initiator conc.; $k_d$ = dissociation constant | Used for practical compounding. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive SMC body panels** — retarder-tuned shelf life of 30–90 days at 25 °C.
- **Pultrusion of GRP rebars** — retarder allows resin bath immersion without premature gel.
- **Phenolic brake-pad manufacture** — retarder controls flow during long compression cycles.
- **Epoxy powder coatings** — latent retarders give *one-pack* storage with rapid oven cure.
- **Filament winding (rocket motor casings)** — controlled gel extends the winding window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction frequently tested in KTU:** A *retarder* **delays** the cure and is **consumed** during it. A *plasticiser* **softens** the polymer but does **not participate chemically**. A *stabiliser* (e.g., UV) protects the *finished* article, not the cure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Induction Time as a Function of Retarder Concentration

We begin with the **chain-transfer-to-retarder model** for a free-radical cure (typical of UPE + MEKP cobalt-accelerated system).

**Step 1 — Rate of initiation:**
The initiator (e.g., MEKP) decomposes homolytically under heat.

$$R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$$

**Where:**
- $f$ = radical efficiency (0 ≤ f ≤ 1)
- $k_d$ = rate constant of initiator dissociation (s⁻¹)
- $[I]$ = initiator concentration (mol/L)

**Step 2 — Reaction of initiator radical with retarder (InH):**

$$R^{\bullet} + \text{InH} \xrightarrow{k_x} RH + \text{In}^{\bullet}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state on retarder radicals:**
Because the retarder radical is resonance-stabilised, it neither reinitiates nor propagates appreciably. Apply steady state on $[R^{\bullet}]$:

$$\frac{d[R^{\bullet}]}{dt} = R_i - k_x [R^{\bullet}][\text{InH}] \approx 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $[R^{\bullet}]$:**

$$[R^{\bullet}] = \frac{R_i}{k_x [\text{InH}]}$$

**Step 5 — Rate of consumption of retarder:**

$$-\frac{d[\text{InH}]}{dt} = k_x [R^{\bullet}][\text{InH}]$$

**Substitute** Step 4 into Step 5:

$$-\frac{d[\text{InH}]}{dt} = k_x \cdot \frac{R_i}{k_x [\text{InH}]} \cdot [\text{InH}] = R_i$$

**Step 6 — Recognise that retarder is consumed at the rate of initiation:**

$$-\frac{d[\text{InH}]}{dt} = R_i = \text{constant}$$

**Step 7 — Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = t_{\text{ind}}$:**

$$\int_{[\text{InH}]_0}^{[\text{InH}]_{\text{crit}}} d[\text{InH}] = -R_i \int_{0}^{t_{\text{ind}}} dt$$

$$[\text{InH}]_{\text{crit}} - [\text{InH}]_0 = -R_i \cdot t_{\text{ind}}$$

**Step 8 — At the end of induction, retarder concentration has dropped to a critical (near-zero) value:**

$$t_{\text{ind}} = \frac{[\text{InH}]_0}{R_i}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{ind}} = \frac{[\text{InH}]_0}{2 f k_d [I]_0}\,}$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- Induction time is **directly proportional** to the *initial retarder concentration*.
- Induction time is **inversely proportional** to the *initiator concentration* and *decomposition rate constant*.
- Doubling the retarder roughly **doubles the processing window** — an important design lever for moulders.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A UPE resin contains 300 ppm (by mass) of hydroquinone (MW = 110 g/mol) and 1 phr of MEKP initiator (MW ≈ 230 g/mol). Density of resin ≈ 1.1 g/mL. Calculate the theoretical induction time at 80 °C.

$$k_d(\text{MEKP at 80 °C}) \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{s}^{-1},\quad f \approx 0.6$$

**Convert concentrations (assume resin basis = 1 kg = 909 mL):**

$$[\text{InH}]_0 = \frac{0.3\,\text{g}}{110\,\text{g/mol} \times 0.909\,\text{L}} \approx 3.00 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/L}$$

$$[I]_0 = \frac{10\,\text{g}}{230\,\text{g/mol} \times 0.909\,\text{L}} \approx 4.78 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{mol/L}$$

**Apply the induction-time formula:**

$$t_{\text{ind}} = \frac{3.00 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.78 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$t_{\text{ind}} = \frac{3.00 \times 10^{-3}}{8.60 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t_{\text{ind}} \approx 3\,487\,\text{s} \approx 58\,\text{min}$$

**Conclusion:** The moulder has approximately **58 minutes of safe working time** at 80 °C before the gel kicks in — sufficient for mould filling of medium-sized SMC parts.

## 3.3 Comparative Matrix — Retarder vs. Accelerator vs. Stabiliser

| Property | Retarder | Accelerator | Stabiliser |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effect on cure rate | Decreases | Increases | Negligible |
| Participates in reaction | Yes (consumed) | Yes (catalyst) | No |
| Typical loading | 100 – 1000 ppm | 0.1 – 2 phr | 0.1 – 3 phr |
| Common example | Hydroquinone, TBC | Cobalt naphthenate, DMA | UV-327, HALS |
| Effect on shelf life | Increases dramatically | Decreases | Increases |
| Used in | UPE, VE | UPE + MEKP | Finished articles |

## 3.4 Effect of Temperature on Retarder Action

Retarders exhibit **negative temperature coefficient of effectiveness** — they are *more effective at low temperatures* and *less dominant at high cure temperatures*. The selectivity ratio:

$$S = \frac{k_{\text{prop}}}{k_x}$$

decreases as $T$ rises (because $k_{\text{prop}}$ has higher activation energy than $k_x$), so at mould temperature the retarder is *overwhelmed* and cure proceeds normally.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Retarder's Role in a Compression-Moulding Cure Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resin + Filler + Catalyst] --> B[Add Retarder InH]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper PCPOT502]`

> Define a **retarder** in the context of thermoset polymer processing. Give two examples and state one typical application.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A retarder is a low-concentration additive (typically 100–1000 ppm) used in thermosetting resin systems to **delay or temporarily suppress the crosslinking (curing) reaction**, thereby extending the *pot life* and *shelf life* without permanently arresting the cure.
>
> *Examples:* (i) Hydroquinone or *tert*-butyl catechol in unsaturated polyester resin, and (ii) Mineral acid or phthalic anhydride in phenolic resol resin. (1 mark each)
>
> *Application:* Used in **SMC/BMC compounding** for compression moulding of automotive panels, where it provides a 30–90 day shelf life at room temperature while ensuring rapid cure at mould temperature (~150 °C). (1 mark)

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional II PCPOT502]`

> Differentiate between a **retarder** and an **accelerator** in thermoset cure with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Retarder | Accelerator |
|---|---|---|
| Effect | Decreases cure rate / delays gel | Increases cure rate / reduces gel time |
| Example | Hydroquinone in UPE | Cobalt naphthenate in UPE + MEKP |
| Typical loading | 100 – 500 ppm | 0.1 – 1 phr |

(1 mark for each correct pair, 1 mark for example distinction — full marks for tabular clarity.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a **mechanism and chemical equations** how a hydroquinone-based retarder controls the cure of an unsaturated polyester (UPE) resin. State the induction-time formula.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **role of retarders in SMC/BMC compounding** for compression moulding. List three retarders commonly used in vinyl ester resins and explain how retarder concentration affects (i) shelf life and (ii) mould-flow time.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism [7 marks]:**

UPE cures via free-radical addition across C=C double bonds, initiated typically by **methyl ethyl ketone peroxide (MEKP)** and accelerated by **cobalt naphthenate**.

*Initiation:*

$$\text{MEKP} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ \text{Co}^{2+}} 2\,\text{R}^{\bullet}$$

*Propagation (without retarder):*

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2=\text{CH} \longrightarrow \text{R-CH}_2\text{-CH}^{\bullet}$$

*With hydroquinone (HO–C₆H₄–OH) as retarder:*

$$\text{R-CH}_2\text{-CH}^{\bullet} + \text{HO-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-OH} \longrightarrow \text{R-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2 + {}^{\bullet}\text{O-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-OH}$$

The resulting **semi-quinone radical** is resonance-stabilised by the aromatic ring and the adjacent –OH oxygen, making it **incapable of efficiently reinitiating** chain growth.

*Induction-time formula (derive or recall):*

$$t_{\text{ind}} = \frac{[\text{InH}]_0}{2 f k_d [I]_0}$$

This shows **direct proportionality** with retarder concentration. (1 mark for mechanism, 2 marks for equations, 2 marks for resonance argument, 1 mark for induction formula, 1 mark for interpretation.)

**(b) SMC/BMC Application [7 marks]:**

In **Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC)** and **Bulk Moulding Compound (BMC)**, the resin is pre-mixed with fillers (CaCO₃, glass fibres), thickeners (MgO), catalyst, and **retarder** at room temperature. The compounded sheet must:

- Remain mouldable for weeks.
- Flow under 50–100 bar mould pressure.
- Cure in <2 minutes at 150–160 °C.

*Common retarders in vinyl ester resins (3 marks for the list with role):*

1. **Hydroquinone (HQ)** — classical retarder, very effective, low cost.
2. ***tert*-Butyl catechol (TBC)** — gives lower colour, used in light-coloured SMC.
3. **Mono-methyl ether of hydroquinone (MEHQ)** — soluble in styrene, preferred for VE resins.

*Effect of concentration (2 + 2 marks):*

(i) **Shelf life** — directly proportional to retarder concentration. Each doubling of HQ roughly doubles the shelf life at 25 °C.

(ii) **Mould-flow time** — inversely proportional to retarder concentration. *Excess retarder* may result in *under-cure* (residual unsaturation, low $T_g$, low mechanical strength) because cure onset is delayed too long within the mould heating window. Practical optimum: **200–500 ppm** for typical SMC.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Classify the various **types of retarders** used in thermosetting resins. Give suitable examples and write the **chemical equation** for retarder action in a phenolic resol resin.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **induction-time expression** for a free-radical cure retarded by an inhibitor, showing each step. Comment on how the induction time influences the choice of retarder dosage in industrial moulding.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Retarders [7 marks]:**

*Type 1 — Free-radical retarders (for vinyl-addition thermosets):*

- Quinones: Hydroquinone, benzoquinone, toluhydroquinone (THQ).
- Phenolic inhibitors: *tert*-butyl catechol (TBC), MEHQ.

*Type 2 — Acid retarders (for condensation thermosets such as phenolic resols):*

- Mineral acids: HCl, H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄.
- Organic acids: Phthalic anhydride, lactic acid, benzoic acid.

*Type 3 — Latent / blocked retarders (for one-pack epoxies):*

- Boric acid, salicylic acid, dicyandiamide (DICY).

*Equation for phenolic resol retardation [2 marks]:*

In phenolic resols, the alkaline catalyst ($\text{OH}^-$ from NaOH) drives condensation. Phthalic anhydride reacts with alkali and reduces effective catalyst concentration:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{CO})_2\text{O} + 2\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{COONa})_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This **neutralises NaOH**, lowers pH, and slows the formation of methylene ($\text{–CH}_2\text{–}$) bridges between phenol rings.

**(b) Derivation of induction time [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 — Rate of initiation:*

$$R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$$

*Step 2 — Radical-retarder reaction:*

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{InH} \xrightarrow{k_x} \text{RH} + \text{In}^{\bullet}$$

*Step 3 — Steady state on active radicals:*

$$\frac{d[\text{R}^{\bullet}]}{dt} = R_i - k_x [\text{R}^{\bullet}][\text{InH}] = 0$$

*Step 4 — Solve:*

$$[\text{R}^{\bullet}] = \frac{R_i}{k_x [\text{InH}]}$$

*Step 5 — Retarder consumption:*

$$-\frac{d[\text{InH}]}{dt} = k_x [\text{R}^{\bullet}][\text{InH}] = R_i$$

*Step 6 — Integrate:*

$$[\text{InH}]_0 - [\text{InH}]_{\text{crit}} = R_i \cdot t_{\text{ind}}$$

*Step 7 — Final result (assuming $[\text{InH}]_{\text{crit}} \approx 0$):*

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{ind}} = \frac{[\text{InH}]_0}{2 f k_d [I]_0}\,}$$

*Comment on dosage choice (1 mark):*

In industrial compression moulding of SMC, the moulder needs $t_{\text{ind}}$ long enough for **mould filling (typically 10–30 s at 150 °C)** but short enough that **cure is complete within 60–120 s**. Therefore, retarder dosage is **empirically tuned** to balance (i) adequate *shelf life* and (ii) acceptable *demould time*. Typical industrial range = **200–500 ppm HQ or TBC**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — RETARDER QUESTIONS**
>
> 1. **Confusing retarder with plasticiser** — students often write "softens the resin" → lose 1 mark. Retarder is a *kinetic modifier of cure*, not a softening agent.
> 2. **Omitting the resonance argument** — hydroquinone works because its radical is *resonance-stabilised*, not merely because it "absorbs radicals". Examiners allocate **2 marks specifically** for this.
> 3. **Forgetting the induction-time derivation** — for 7-mark parts, you must *derive* or at least write the final equation with all variables. Just stating "retarder increases pot life" is incomplete.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion** — confusing ppm and phr. Remember: $\text{ppm} = \text{phr} \times 10^4$ for single-resin basis.
> 5. **Not giving examples** — for definition-type questions, at least **two named retarders** are expected.
> 6. **Mixing retarder with anti-oxidant** — they serve different functions (cure vs. ageing); examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — RETARDER**
>
> - **Definition:** Additive that *delays* (does not stop) thermoset cure; consumed during the reaction.
> - **Typical loading:** 100 – 1000 ppm (liquid resins); 0.1 – 2 phr (bulk compounds).
> - **Most common retarders:** Hydroquinone (HQ), *tert*-butyl catechol (TBC), MEHQ, THQ, phthalic anhydride, mineral acids.
> - **Mechanism (UPE/VE):** Free-radical capture → resonance-stabilised semi-quinone radical → dormant chain.
> - **Mechanism (Phenolic resol):** Acid-base neutralisation of alkaline catalyst → reduced condensation rate.
> - **Induction time formula:** $t_{\text{ind}} = [\text{InH}]_0 / (2 f k_d [I]_0)$ — direct with retarder, inverse with initiator.
> - **Shelf-life Arrhenius:** $\ln t_{\text{shelf}} = E_a / (R T) + C$ — colder storage → longer shelf life.
> - **Effect of concentration:** ↑ Retarder → ↑ Shelf life, ↑ Pot life, ↑ Flow time, ↓ Risk of under-cure.
> - **Effect of temperature:** Retarder loses effectiveness at high $T$ — selectivity ratio $k_p / k_x$ decreases with $T$.
> - **Difference from accelerator:** Retarder *delays* (chain-transfer radical); accelerator *boosts* (e.g., Co²⁺ + MEKP).
> - **Difference from stabiliser:** Stabiliser protects *finished* article (e.g., UV); retarder protects *processing* stage.
> - **Industrial application hotspots:** SMC/BMC automotive, phenolic brake pads, epoxy powder coatings, GRP pultrusion, filament winding.
> - **Typical acid retarders in phenolics:** Phthalic anhydride (1–2 phr), lactic acid (0.5–1 phr), HCl (trace).
> - **Always quote two examples** in KTU definition questions.
> - **Always include the induction-time formula** in 7-mark derivations.
> - **Always mention resonance stabilisation** when explaining hydroquinone action.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curing Agents, Plasticisers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Curing Agents, Plasticisers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Curing Agents — Formal Definition

A **curing agent** (also referred to as a hardener, crosslinker, or vulcanizing agent) is a reactive chemical substance that, when combined with a polymer matrix—predominantly a **thermosetting resin** or an **elastomer**—initiates and propagates **crosslinking reactions** between adjacent polymer chains. These covalent or ionic bridges convert a low-molecular-weight, often liquid or soft polymeric precursor into a rigid, three-dimensional, infusible, and insoluble network.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Curing agents are explicitly listed under "Compounding ingredients" in Module 1. Examiners expect students to know: (a) the chemical class of the curing agent, (b) the parent polymer it cures, (c) the crosslinking mechanism, and (d) typical cure conditions (temperature, time, stoichiometry).

#### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "Molecular Nails"

Think of uncured polymer chains as a pile of loose, slippery spaghetti lying on a plate. They slide past one another easily — that's why raw rubber is sticky and weak. Now imagine taking **rusty nails (curing agents)** and hammering them through several strands of spaghetti wherever they touch. The nails permanently lock the strands together at those contact points. You can no longer slide the spaghetti apart, but the assembly remains flexible between the nails (just like crosslinked rubber is elastic but not flowable). Each nail is a **crosslink**, and the act of nailing is **curing**.

### 1.2 Plasticisers — Formal Definition

A **plasticiser** is a relatively low-molecular-weight, non-volatile organic liquid (or, less commonly, a solid) that is incorporated into a polymer to **modify its mechanical and rheological properties** — primarily to increase flexibility, extensibility, and processability, while reducing the glass transition temperature ($T_g$) and elastic modulus. Plasticisers function by **inserting themselves between polymer chains**, disrupting secondary bonding (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding) and increasing the free volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A plasticiser is **NOT** a filler. Fillers are cheap, rigid, particulate additives used to reduce cost or modify stiffness. Plasticisers are typically low-viscosity liquids that act at the molecular level through polymer–plasticiser interactions.

#### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — "Molecular Ball Bearings"

If you stack books tightly on a shelf, friction between them makes it hard to slide one out. If you sprinkle fine **ball bearings (plasticiser molecules)** between the books, each book can now glide over its neighbour. The polymer chains behave the same way — the plasticiser molecules lubricate the inter-chain motion, lowering the energy barrier for chain segmental mobility. The result: the polymer becomes softer, more flexible, and easier to process.

### 1.3 The 60-Second Classification Snapshot

| Additive Type | Primary Function | Typical Polymer | Final Network State |
|---------------|------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Curing Agent | Forms covalent crosslinks | Thermosets, Elastomers | 3-D Crosslinked Network |
| Plasticiser | Weakens inter-chain forces | Thermoplastics, sometimes rubbers | Linear/Branched but lubricated |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard industrial metric: **Phthalate-free plasticiser demand grew at ~6.2% CAGR (2018–2023)** owing to REACH and FDA regulations — relevant for the modern processing engineer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Curing Agents — Deep Dive

#### 2.1.1 Classification by Chemistry

- **A. Sulfur & Sulfur Donor Systems** (used for **diene rubbers** — NR, SBR, BR, NBR, EPDM)
  - Elemental sulfur (S₈ rings)
  - Sulfur donors: Tetramethylthiuram monosulfide (TMTM), Tetramethylthiuram disulfide (TMTD), Morpholine disulfide (MOR), Dipentamethylenethiuram tetrasulfide (DPTT)
  - Requires **activators** (ZnO + stearic acid) and **accelerators** (MBT, CBS, TBBS, DPG) to achieve practical cure rates at 140–180 °C.

- **B. Peroxides** (used for **saturated polymers** — EPDM, silicone, PE, HNBR, FKM)
  - Dicumyl peroxide (DCP)
  - Di-tert-butyl peroxide (DTBP)
  - Benzoyl peroxide (BPO)
  - 2,5-Dimethyl-2,5-di(tert-butylperoxy)hexane
  - Cure via **free-radical mechanism** — works on polymers lacking C=C double bonds.

- **C. Amine Hardeners** (for **epoxy resins**)
  - Aliphatic amines: Ethylenediamine (EDA), Diethylenetriamine (DETA), Triethylenetetramine (TETA)
  - Aromatic amines: m-Phenylenediamine (mPDA), 4,4′-Diaminodiphenylmethane (DDM), Diaminodiphenylsulfone (DDS)
  - Polyamidoamines

- **D. Anhydride Hardeners** (for **epoxy resins** — high-Tg, electrical applications)
  - Phthalic anhydride (PA)
  - Maleic anhydride (MA)
  - Hexahydrophthalic anhydride (HHPA)
  - Methyl nadic anhydride (MNA), Nadic methyl anhydride (NMA)
  - Benzophenonetetracarboxylic dianhydride (BTDA)

- **E. Isocyanate Crosslinkers** (for **polyurethanes, hydroxyl-terminated polymers**)
  - TDI (Toluene diisocyanate)
  - MDI (Methylene diphenyl diisocyanate)
  - HDI (Hexamethylene diisocyanate), IPDI

- **F. Metal Oxides** (for **polysulfide, chloroprene, halogenated butyl rubbers**)
  - ZnO, MgO, PbO (legacy), CaO

- **G. Resin Curing Systems** (e.g., **PF resols**, **UF/MF resins**)
  - Acid catalysts (HCl, H₂SO₄, pTSA) for novolacs
  - Heat (with built-in methylol groups) for resols

#### 2.1.2 Mechanism of Sulfur Vulcanization (Why & How)

**The Why:** Unsaturated rubbers (containing C=C allylic hydrogens) react with sulfur at elevated temperatures to form monosulfidic, disulfidic, and polysulfidic bridges ($C–S_x–C$) between chains. ZnO/stearic acid activate sulfur; accelerators (typically benzothiazole sulfenamides) dramatically lower activation energy.

**The How (Simplified Radical Sequence):**

1. **Initiation:** Accelerator (Acc–S–N) decomposes → Acc–S• radical + amine fragment.
2. **Propagation:** Acc–S• abstracts allylic H from rubber chain → rubber alkyl radical (R•) + Acc–SH.
3. **Sulfur Insertion:** R• attacks S₈ ring → R–S₈• (polysulfenyl radical).
4. **Crosslink Formation:** R–S₈• + R′–H → R–S₈–R′ + H• (forms a polysulfidic bridge).
5. **Network Maturation:** Polysulfidic bridges shorten (desulfuration) to di- and monosulfidic bonds over time at cure temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **accelerator-to-sulfur ratio** dictates the **crosslink type distribution**:
> - High S, low accelerator → **conventional (polysulfidic)** cure: good tensile, poor aging.
> - EV (efficient vulcanization) system: high accelerator, low S → **mono/disulfidic** cure: better heat aging, lower flex resistance.

#### 2.1.3 Mechanism of Peroxide Curing

- **Step 1 — Homolysis:** RO–OR → 2 RO•  (peroxide bond dissociation energy ≈ 150 kJ/mol; triggered thermally at 150–180 °C).
- **Step 2 — H-Abstraction:** RO• + P–H → ROH + P•  (P = polymer chain).
- **Step 3 — Radical Coupling:** P• + P• → P–P (covalent C–C crosslink).
- **Optional Co-agent:** Triallyl cyanurate (TAC), triallyl isocyanurate (TAIC), or zinc diacrylate may be added to boost crosslink density.

**Why C–C bonds?** Peroxide curing produces **carbon–carbon crosslinks** (high bond energy ~348 kJ/mol) — hence superior heat resistance compared to sulfur systems (~270 kJ/mol for C–S–C).

#### 2.1.4 Mechanism of Amine Curing of Epoxy

For a **primary amine** and a **diglycidyl ether of bisphenol A (DGEBA)** epoxy:

$$\text{R–NH}_2 + \text{CH}_2\text{–CH(–O–R')–CH}_2 \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–CH}_2\text{–CH(OH)–CH}_2\text{–O–R'}$$

- Each primary amine N–H opens one epoxide ring → secondary amine.
- Secondary amine N–H opens another epoxide → tertiary amine.
- One primary amine H → **two epoxide rings** opened, creating a branch point.
- Theoretically, **one mole of primary amine cures 2 moles of epoxide** (per active H).

### 2.2 Plasticisers — Deep Dive

#### 2.2.1 Classification

- **By Origin:**
  - **Phthalate esters** (the largest commercial class, ~70% of global plasticiser consumption) — DEHP, DINP, DIDP, DBP, BBP, DEP, DMP.
  - **Adipates, Sebacates, Azelates** — for low-temperature flexibility (DOA, DOS).
  - **Trimellitates** — TOTM, TINTM (low volatility, high-temperature wire/cable).
  - **Phosphate esters** — TCP, TPP, CDP (flame-retardant, also plasticise).
  - **Epoxidized oils** — epoxidized soybean oil (ESBO), epoxidized linseed oil (ELO).
  - **Polymeric plasticisers** — polyesters, liquid NBR (low migration, permanence).
  - **Bio-based / non-phthalate** — ATBC (acetyl tributyl citrate), ESBO, DINCH.

- **By Function:**
  - **Primary plasticiser** — highly compatible, can be used alone (e.g., DEHP in PVC at 40–80 phr).
  - **Secondary plasticiser** — limited compatibility, used to extend/reduce cost (e.g., chlorinated paraffins, epoxidized oils).

- **By Mechanism:**
  - **External plasticiser** — physically blended; not chemically bonded.
  - **Internal plasticiser** — chemically incorporated into the polymer backbone (e.g., copolymer of vinyl chloride with 2-ethylhexyl acrylate).

#### 2.2.2 The Plasticisation Mechanism (Why & How)

**The Why:** Rigid polymers like PVC possess strong dipole–dipole interactions between C–Cl groups. These secondary forces restrict chain mobility, raising $T_g$ to ~82 °C. Plasticiser molecules possess polar groups that **interact preferentially with the polymer's polar sites**, **screening the polymer–polymer interactions** and providing an internal lubricating layer.

**The How (Three-Layer Model — sometimes taught as the *Gel Theory*):**

1. Polymer chains are loosely bonded at **nodes** (crystallites or polar contact points).
2. Plasticiser molecules break these nodes.
3. Plasticiser molecules get trapped in the polymer matrix, **expanding the free volume** and **reducing the effective inter-chain interaction energy**.

#### 2.2.3 Key Quantitative Relationships

**Free-Volume Theory (Fox–Flory-derived):**

$$T_g(\phi_2) = T_{g,1}\,\phi_1 + T_{g,2}\,\phi_2 + k\,\phi_1\,\phi_2$$

where subscripts 1 and 2 refer to polymer and plasticiser, $\phi$ is volume fraction, and $k$ is an interaction parameter. For most miscible plasticiser–polymer systems, $k < 0$ (negative deviation), meaning $T_g$ drops non-linearly.

**A simpler and more KTU-favoured form:**

$$\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

where $w_i$ is the **weight fraction**. (This is the inverse-rule-of-mixtures, often called the **Fox equation**.) $T_g$ values must be in **kelvin (K)**.

**Plasticiser Efficiency (the 100-modulus temperature — $T_{100}$):**

$$T_{100} = T_{g,\text{pure}} - \Delta T \cdot \phi_{\text{plasticiser}}$$

where $\Delta T$ is the plasticiser efficiency factor (in K per unit $\phi$). More efficient plasticisers give a larger $T_g$ depression per unit loading.

**Compatibility Parameter (Hildebrand Solubility, $\delta$):**

$$\Delta \delta = \vert \delta_{\text{polymer}} - \delta_{\text{plasticiser}} \vert$$

For good miscibility, $\Delta \delta \le 1.0 \ \text{(cal/cm}^{3}\text{)}^{1/2}$. For PVC ($\delta \approx 9.5$), DOP (DEHP, $\delta \approx 8.9$) is a near-ideal match.

#### 2.2.4 The Antiplasticisation Phenomenon

At **low plasticiser loadings** (typically 5–15 phr in PVC), certain rigid plasticisers (e.g., chlorinated biphenyls) cause a **paradoxical increase in modulus and $T_g$** while reducing elongation. This occurs because the small plasticiser molecules occupy free-volume voids and *stiffen* the matrix at low concentrations. Above a critical concentration, the matrix softens normally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **KTU-favourite conceptual question** — examiners often ask: "Why do some plasticisers increase modulus at low concentrations?" The correct answer invokes **antiplasticisation** and free-volume annihilation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ | Fox equation for $T_g$ of plasticised polymer | $T_g$ in **K**, $w_i$ = weight fraction |
| 2 | $T_g = T_{g,1} \phi_1 + T_{g,2} \phi_2 + k\phi_1\phi_2$ | Modified $T_g$ with Gordon–Taylor $k$ | $\phi$ = volume fraction |
| 3 | $\Delta \delta = \vert \delta_P - \delta_{pl} \vert$ | Hildebrand compatibility | $\Delta\delta \le 1.0$ ideal |
| 4 | $M_c = \dfrac{\rho\, RT}{G}$ | Avg. mol. wt. between crosslinks (rubber elasticity) | $\rho$ = density (g/cm³), $G$ = shear modulus (Pa) |
| 5 | $\nu_e = \dfrac{\rho\, N_A}{M_c}$ | Crosslink density (number of effective chains per unit volume) | $\nu_e$ in mol/m³ |
| 6 | $E = 3\nu_e RT$ | Young's modulus of ideal rubber network | Pa |
| 7 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Elastic modulus relationship | $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 8 | $t_{90}$ | Time to 90% of maximum torque (rheometer cure index) | minutes |
| 9 | $\text{phr} = \dfrac{m_{\text{additive}}}{m_{\text{polymer}}} \times 100$ | Parts per hundred rubber/resin | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Stioichiometry (epoxy)}: \dfrac{\text{eq}_{\text{amine}}}{\text{eq}_{\text{epoxy}}} = 1$ | Equivalent ratio for full cure | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Equation 9, **phr**, is the universal compounding unit in the rubber industry. **60 phr DEHP** in PVC means 60 g of DEHP per 100 g of PVC resin.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Crosslink Density from Swelling (Flory–Rehner)

> **Setup:** A crosslinked rubber sample of mass $m_1$ (dry) is immersed in a good solvent (e.g., toluene) and allowed to swell until equilibrium. The swollen mass is $m_2$. The volume fraction of polymer in the swollen gel, $\phi_2$, relates to the crosslink density via the **Flory–Rehner equation**.

**Step 1 — Experimental Determination of $\phi_2$:**

$$\phi_2 = \dfrac{V_{\text{polymer}}}{V_{\text{swollen}}} = \dfrac{m_1/\rho_{\text{polymer}}}{m_1/\rho_{\text{polymer}} + (m_2 - m_1)/\rho_{\text{solvent}}}$$

**Step 2 — Free Energy Balance:** At swelling equilibrium, the osmotic swelling pressure equals the elastic retractile pressure of the network.

$$\Delta\mu_{\text{mixing}} + \Delta\mu_{\text{elastic}} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Flory–Rehner (full form):**

$$\chi\,\phi_2^2 + \ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 = -\nu_e\, V_1\,\left(\phi_2^{1/3} - \dfrac{2\phi_2}{f}\right)$$

where:
- $\chi$ = Flory–Huggins interaction parameter (polymer–solvent)
- $V_1$ = molar volume of solvent (cm³/mol)
- $\nu_e$ = effective crosslink density (mol/cm³)
- $f$ = functionality of crosslink (f = 4 for sulfur/ peroxide)

**Step 4 — Solved for $\nu_e$:**

$$\nu_e = -\dfrac{\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi\,\phi_2^2}{V_1 \left(\phi_2^{1/3} - \dfrac{2\phi_2}{f}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Average Molecular Weight Between Crosslinks:**

$$M_c = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{polymer}}}{\nu_e}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**

> *A natural rubber sample (density 0.92 g/cm³) is crosslinked and swells in toluene ($V_1 = 106.3$ cm³/mol, $\chi = 0.39$). Dry mass $m_1 = 0.45$ g; swollen mass $m_2 = 2.10$ g; $\rho_{\text{toluene}} = 0.867$ g/cm³. Calculate $M_c$ and $\nu_e$.*

**Step A — Compute $\phi_2$:**

$$V_{\text{polymer}} = \dfrac{0.45}{0.92} = 0.4891 \ \text{cm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{solvent}} = \dfrac{2.10 - 0.45}{0.867} = \dfrac{1.65}{0.867} = 1.9032 \ \text{cm}^3$$

$$\phi_2 = \dfrac{0.4891}{0.4891 + 1.9032} = \dfrac{0.4891}{2.3923} = 0.2045$$

**Step B — Compute $\nu_e$ (using $f = 4$):**

$$\ln(1 - 0.2045) = \ln(0.7955) = -0.2288$$

$$0.2045 + (-0.2288) + 0.39 \times (0.2045)^2 = 0.2045 - 0.2288 + 0.0163 = -0.0080$$

$$\phi_2^{1/3} = (0.2045)^{1/3} = 0.5898$$

$$\dfrac{2\phi_2}{f} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.2045}{4} = 0.1023$$

$$\phi_2^{1/3} - \dfrac{2\phi_2}{f} = 0.5898 - 0.1023 = 0.4875$$

$$\nu_e = -\dfrac{-0.0080}{106.3 \times 0.4875} = \dfrac{0.0080}{51.82} = 1.544 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{mol/cm}^3$$

**Step C — Compute $M_c$:**

$$M_c = \dfrac{0.92}{1.544 \times 10^{-4}} = 5958 \ \text{g/mol} \approx 5.96 \ \text{kg/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A **higher $M_c$** means *fewer* crosslinks per unit volume (looser network, more swelling, softer rubber). A **lower $M_c$** indicates a tighter network. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Does higher crosslink density mean higher or lower $M_c$?"* — Answer: **inversely related**.

### 3.2 Derivation: $T_g$ Depression in Plasticised PVC (Fox Equation)

> **Setup:** 70 g of PVC ($T_{g,1} = 355$ K, ~82 °C) is blended with 30 g of DOP plasticiser ($T_{g,2} = 173$ K, ~−100 °C). Predict the $T_g$ of the blend.

**Step 1 — Compute Weight Fractions:**

$$w_1 = \dfrac{70}{70+30} = 0.70, \quad w_2 = 0.30$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Fox Equation:**

$$\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{0.70}{355} + \dfrac{0.30}{173}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_g} = 0.001972 + 0.001734 = 0.003706 \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_g = \dfrac{1}{0.003706} = 269.84 \ \text{K} = -3.31 \ \text{°C}$$

**Step 3 — Verification by Rule of Mixtures (Linear, approximate):**

$$T_{g,\text{linear}} = 0.70 \times 355 + 0.30 \times 173 = 248.5 + 51.9 = 300.4 \ \text{K} = 27.4 \ \text{°C}$$

The **Fox equation** (harmonic mean) always predicts a **larger $T_g$ depression** than the linear rule of mixtures. This is the mathematically correct form for miscible amorphous blends and is preferred by KTU examiners.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality check:** Plasticised PVC in real products (vinyl flooring, cables) typically operates at 30–60 phr of DOP, giving $T_g$ in the range of −20 to +10 °C — a remarkable ~70–100 K depression. This validates the plasticiser's industrial importance.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Curing Agent vs. Polymer

| Polymer Family | Preferred Curing Agent | Cure T (°C) | Network Type | Key Advantage |
|----------------|------------------------|-------------|--------------|----------------|
| NR (Natural Rubber) | Sulfur + CBS + ZnO/stearic acid | 140–160 | Polysulfidic C–Sₓ–C | High tensile, flex |
| SBR | Sulfur + TBBS | 150–170 | Polysulfidic | Tire treads |
| EPDM | Peroxide (DCP) or sulfur | 160–180 | C–C (peroxide) | Heat/ozone resistance |
| Silicone rubber | Peroxide (DCP, 2,4-DCP) | 170–180 | C–C | High temp (200 °C+) |
| Epoxy (DGEBA) | DETA, TETA (aliphatic) | RT–80 | C–N | Room-temp cure |
| Epoxy (DGEBA) | DDS, mPDA (aromatic) | 150–180 | C–N | High $T_g$, aerospace |
| Epoxy (DGEBA) | HHPA + accelerator | 100–150 | Ester | Electrical, electronics |
| Polyurethane | MDI / TDI + polyol | RT–120 | Urethane / urea | Elastomers, foams |
| PF (Resol) | Heat (built-in) | 150–180 | Methylene | Heat-resistant laminates |
| PF (Novolac) | Hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) | 150–180 | Methylene | Brake linings |
| Silicone (addition cure) | Pt-catalyst + SiH | 100–150 | Si–CH₂–CH₂–Si | Medical, food grade |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-link architecture (KTU high-yield):**
> - **Sulfur** → C–S–C (low bond energy, **~270 kJ/mol**) — gives **dynamic, flexible** network.
> - **Peroxide** → C–C (high bond energy, **~348 kJ/mol**) — gives **rigid, thermally stable** network.
> - **Amine–epoxy** → C–N (bond energy **~305 kJ/mol**) — intermediate properties.

### 3.4 Worked Cure Cycle (Oscillating Disc Rheometer — ODR)

A typical sulfur-cured NR compound at 160 °C (MDR cure data):

- **$M_L$ (Minimum Torque):** 1.8 dN·m — represents viscosity of uncured compound.
- **$M_H$ (Maximum Torque):** 14.2 dN·m — represents fully cured shear modulus.
- **$t_{s2}$ (Scorch Time):** 2.1 min — time to start of cure (processing safety window).
- **$t_{90}$ (Optimum Cure Time):** 7.4 min — time to reach 90% of $M_H$.
- **Cure Rate Index (CRI):**

$$\text{CRI} = \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}} = \dfrac{100}{7.4 - 2.1} = \dfrac{100}{5.3} = 18.87 \ \text{min}^{-1}$$

> Higher CRI ⇒ **faster cure** ⇒ **shorter mould cycle** ⇒ economic benefit.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Define a plasticiser. With one example, explain its role in PVC processing.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A plasticiser is a low-molecular-weight, non-volatile organic compound added to a polymer to **increase flexibility, reduce $T_g$, and improve processability** by lowering the polymer–polymer secondary bonding. It works by inserting itself between polymer chains and increasing free volume.
>
> **Example:** **Di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP/DOP)** is added to PVC at 40–80 phr. Pure PVC has $T_g \approx 82$ °C and is rigid; with 50 phr DEHP, the $T_g$ drops below 0 °C, making the compound flexible and suitable for cable insulation, vinyl flooring, and tubing. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for example with use.)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*List any three classes of curing agents used for epoxy resins and state the key advantage of each.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Aliphatic amines** (e.g., DETA, TETA) — *Advantage:* **room-temperature cure**, fast set, suitable for adhesives and coatings. (1 mark)
> 2. **Aromatic amines** (e.g., DDS, DDM) — *Advantage:* **high $T_g$ and excellent heat resistance**, used in aerospace composites. (1 mark)
> 3. **Anhydrides** (e.g., HHPA, MNA) — *Advantage:* **low exotherm, long pot life, excellent electrical insulation** properties, used in castings and PCB encapsulation. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Sample, Module 1 Choice]

**(a)** Classify the different types of curing agents used in polymer processing. Discuss the mechanism of sulfur vulcanization of natural rubber with the role of accelerators and activators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the term 'plasticiser'. Explain the mechanism of plasticisation in PVC. Discuss the factors affecting plasticiser efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Classification of Curing Agents (3 marks):**

| Class | Example | Used For |
|-------|---------|----------|
| Sulfur & donors | S₈, TMTD, DTDM | Diene rubbers (NR, SBR, BR) |
| Peroxides | DCP, BPO | Saturated polymers, silicone |
| Amines | DETA, DDS | Epoxy |
| Anhydrides | HHPA, MNA | Epoxy |
| Isocyanates | MDI, TDI | Polyurethanes |
| Metal oxides | ZnO, MgO | Polysulfide, CR, IIR |
| Resins | HMTA, resorcinol | PF, NR-RF bonding |

**Mechanism of Sulfur Vulcanization (4 marks):**

1. **Activation (1 mark):** At 140–160 °C, the **accelerator (e.g., CBS — N-cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulfenamide)** decomposes to give a benzothiazolyl radical and an amine. The radical attacks the S₈ ring to give a **polysulfenyl accelerator–sulfur fragment (Acc–Sₓ–S)**. **ZnO + stearic acid** form zinc stearate in situ, which complexes with the accelerator and polarises the S–S bond, making sulfur highly reactive.

2. **Crosslink Formation (2 marks):** The active sulfurating species abstracts an **allylic hydrogen** from the rubber chain (a low-energy C–H bond adjacent to a C=C), generating a rubber macro-radical. This radical attacks another sulfur in the Acc–Sₓ chain, leading to a **C–Sₓ–C** bridge between two rubber chains. Polysulfidic bridges initially dominate, and during **maturation** (over-cure), they shorten to disulfidic and monosulfidic linkages, with release of sulfur.
