3. **Network Properties (1 mark):** Conventional cure gives predominantly polysulfidic bridges — high tensile strength, good flex life, but poor heat aging. **EV systems** (low S, high accelerator) give more mono- and disulfidic bridges — better aging, lower flex life.

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A plasticiser is a low-volatility liquid or solid additive that, when incorporated into a polymer, increases its flexibility, reduces $T_g$ and modulus, and improves processability.

**Mechanism of Plasticisation in PVC (4 marks):**

1. **PVC Structure (1 mark):** PVC chains possess C–Cl dipoles that interact strongly with each other (dipole–dipole forces), restricting chain mobility and yielding $T_g \approx 82$ °C and high rigidity.

2. **Plasticiser Action (2 marks):** DEHP (DOP) molecules have polar ester groups and a long flexible alkyl tail. The polar groups **compete with C–Cl dipoles** for inter-chain interaction sites, while the alkyl tail adds **free volume** to the matrix. The net effect: weaker effective inter-chain forces, higher segmental mobility, lower $T_g$.

3. **Lubricity & Free-Volume Models (1 mark):** The **Lubricity Theory** views the plasticiser as internal lubricant sliding the chains. The **Gel Theory** sees polymer chains as a 3-D gel held at polar contact points; plasticiser molecules break these junctions. The **Free-Volume Theory** quantitatively predicts the $T_g$ drop using the Fox equation.

**Factors Affecting Plasticiser Efficiency (2 marks):**

1. **Compatibility** (1 mark) — measured by $\Delta\delta = \vert \delta_P - \delta_{pl} \vert$; smaller $\Delta\delta$ ⇒ more efficient.
2. **Molecular size & shape** (0.5 mark) — linear plasticisers are more efficient than branched.
3. **Volatility & permanence** (0.5 mark) — affects long-term performance and migration.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice, Module 1]

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of peroxide curing of elastomers. Compare peroxide and sulfur curing systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Flory–Rehner equation and use it to determine the crosslink density and molecular weight between crosslinks of a swollen rubber sample. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Peroxide Curing Mechanism (4 marks):**

1. **Step 1 — Thermal Homolysis (1 mark):** At 150–180 °C, the peroxide bond RO–OR cleaves homolytically to yield two alkoxy radicals (RO•). Bond dissociation energy ≈ **150 kJ/mol**.

$$ \text{RO–OR} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \, 150{-}180\,°C} 2\,\text{RO}^{\bullet} $$

2. **Step 2 — Hydrogen Abstraction (1 mark):** The alkoxy radical abstracts an H atom from the polymer backbone (–CH₂–), creating a polymer macro-radical (P•) and an alcohol (ROH).

$$ \text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{P–H} \longrightarrow \text{ROH} + \text{P}^{\bullet} $$

3. **Step 3 — Radical Coupling / Crosslink (1 mark):** Two polymer radicals combine to form a **C–C covalent crosslink**. (For polymers with vinyl groups, the radical can also add across the C=C to give a branched network.)

$$ \text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{P}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{P–P} $$

4. **Step 4 — Co-agents (1 mark):** To boost crosslink density, **triallyl cyanurate (TAC)** or **zinc diacrylate** is added — these poly-functional co-agents provide multiple radical addition sites, increasing $\nu_e$ by 2–5×.

**Comparison: Peroxide vs. Sulfur Curing (3 marks):**

| Property | Sulfur Cure | Peroxide Cure |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| Crosslink type | C–Sₓ–C (polysulfidic) | C–C |
| Bond energy | ~270 kJ/mol | ~348 kJ/mol |
| Heat aging | Poor (sulfur reverts) | Excellent |
| Compression set | Higher | Lower |
| Flex fatigue | Better | Worse |
| Polymer suitability | Unsaturated (NR, SBR) | Saturated (EPDM, silicone) |
| Cure activator needed | Yes (ZnO + accelerator) | No |
| Typical T | 140–170 °C | 160–180 °C |

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

> **This is a derivation + numerical problem, deliberately given to test application.**

**Step 1 — Free Energy of Swelling (2 marks):**
At equilibrium, the chemical potential of the solvent in the gel must equal that of pure solvent:

$$ \Delta\mu_1 = \left(\frac{\partial \Delta G_{\text{mix}}}{\partial n_1}\right)_{T,P,n_2} + \left(\frac{\partial \Delta G_{\text{elastic}}}{\partial n_1}\right)_{T,P,n_2} = 0 $$

**Step 2 — Flory–Huggins Mixing Term (1.5 marks):**

$$ \Delta\mu_{1,\text{mix}} = RT\left[\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi\phi_2^2\right] $$

**Step 3 — Elastic Retractile Term (1.5 marks):** For a tetrafunctional network (f = 4), the affine network model gives:

$$ \Delta\mu_{1,\text{elastic}} = RT\,\nu_e V_1\left(\phi_2^{1/3} - \frac{2\phi_2}{f}\right) $$

**Step 4 — Set Sum to Zero and Solve for $\nu_e$ (1 mark):**

$$ \nu_e = -\frac{\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi\phi_2^2}{V_1 \left(\phi_2^{1/3} - \dfrac{2\phi_2}{f}\right)} $$

**Step 5 — Apply to the Sample (1 mark):**
Using the numerical data from Section 3.1 (NR in toluene, $\phi_2 = 0.2045$, $\chi = 0.39$, $V_1 = 106.3$ cm³/mol):

$$ \nu_e = 1.544 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{mol/cm}^3 $$

$$ M_c = \frac{0.92}{1.544\times 10^{-4}} = 5958 \ \text{g/mol} \approx 5.96 \ \text{kg/mol} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units mismatch:** $T_g$ in the Fox equation **must be in Kelvin**, not °C. Students routinely insert 355 (correct) and 173 (correct) but then fail to subtract 273 to convert the final answer back to °C. **1 mark lost per occurrence.**
> 2. **Sign confusion in $\nu_e$:** If your final $\nu_e$ is **negative**, you've made an arithmetic error. $\nu_e$ is always positive. **2 marks lost.**
> 3. **Confusing $M_c$ and $\nu_e$ direction:** Higher crosslink density ⇒ **higher $\nu_e$** and **lower $M_c$**. Students often reverse this. **2 marks lost.**
> 4. **Plasticiser ≠ Filler:** In definitions, do not write "filler" or "lubricant" loosely — examiners deduct for terminology. **(1 mark lost per error).**
> 5. **Sulfur cure mechanism:** Many students write "sulfur forms crosslinks" without mentioning **allylic H-abstraction** and the role of **accelerator + ZnO activator**. **2 marks lost.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ✅ A **curing agent** creates **covalent crosslinks** — irreversible. A **plasticiser** weakens **secondary bonds** — reversible.
- ✅ Sulfur cures work only on **unsaturated** polymers; peroxides work on **saturated** polymers.
- ✅ Peroxide → **C–C** crosslinks (~348 kJ/mol); Sulfur → **C–Sₓ–C** (~270 kJ/mol). Peroxide is heat-stable.
- ✅ Epoxy + amine → **C–N** linkages; stoichiometry = 1 equivalent amine H per 1 epoxide equivalent.
- ✅ Plasticiser efficiency scales with **compatibility** ($\Delta\delta$) and **molecular shape** (linear > branched).
- ✅ **Fox equation** uses $T_g$ in **K** and **weight fractions**; $T_g$ is depressed non-linearly.
- ✅ **Antiplasticisation** occurs at low plasticiser loadings (5–15 phr) — a frequent viva trap.
- ✅ **$M_c$ and $\nu_e$ are inversely related**; compute $\nu_e$ from swelling via Flory–Rehner.
- ✅ **phr** = parts per hundred resin/rubber; **60 phr DOP in PVC** is the classic formulation.
- ✅ **TOTM and DINCH** are modern non-phthalate plasticisers — relevant for medical/food-contact PVC.
- ✅ **Cure parameters:** $t_{s2}$ (scorch safety), $t_{90}$ (90% cure), CRI = 100 / $(t_{90} - t_{s2})$.
- ✅ **Phthalates** dominate global plasticiser market (~70%); DEHP is the workhorse for flexible PVC.
- ✅ **Sulfur/Accelerator ratio** dictates crosslink type: high S → polysulfidic (flexible, low aging); EV (low S, high acc.) → mono/disulfidic (heat-stable).
- ✅ **MDI-based polyurethanes** are replacing TDI for safety (lower vapour pressure).
- ✅ **Reactivity series of amines (epoxy):** aliphatic > cycloaliphatic > aromatic (slower but higher $T_g$).
- ✅ The **Flory–Rehner equation** is the bridge between **swelling experiment** and **network structure** — memorize the formula form.
- ✅ **Plasticised PVC** with 50 phr DOP has $T_g$ around −20 °C (rough estimate using Fox equation).

> **One-line mnemonic — "SUPA-EASY"** to remember curing classes:
> **S**ulfur (diene rubbers) · **U**rethane/Isocyanate (–OH polymers) · **P**eroxide (saturated, silicone) · **A**mine (epoxy) · **E**poxy-anhydride · **ASY** (resin/heat-cure systems).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Aids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Aids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Process Aid** (also called a **Polymer Processing Aid** or **PPA**) is a specialty additive incorporated into a polymer melt, typically at very low concentrations (ranging from **50 ppm to 2000 ppm**, i.e., **0.005% to 0.2% by weight**), whose primary function is to modify the rheological behavior and surface interactions of the polymer melt at the die–polymer interface during processing operations such as extrusion, film blowing, blow molding, and injection molding. Process aids do not become a permanent structural part of the finished article; instead, they migrate dynamically to the metal–polymer boundary, creating a low-surface-energy slip layer that delays or eliminates the onset of flow instabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Process aids are functional additives that improve the melt processability of polymers by reducing melt fracture, eliminating sharkskin, lowering apparent melt viscosity at high shear rates, and preventing die lip build-up (die drool), without significantly altering the bulk mechanical or thermal properties of the finished product.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sliding a heavy wooden block across a rough concrete floor. The friction is enormous, and the block sticks, judders, and even makes a squeaking sound. Now, imagine sprinkling a thin layer of talcum powder (or placing a thin sheet of Teflon) between the block and the floor. The block now glides smoothly.

The **die wall** is the concrete floor, the **polymer melt** is the heavy wooden block, and the **process aid** is the talcum powder. The process aid migrates to the die wall and creates a **slip layer**, allowing the polymer to slide over the metal surface rather than gripping and tearing it. This is precisely why processing aids are sometimes called **"slip agents"** or **"lubricity enhancers"** in industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction – Process Aid vs. Internal/External Lubricant:**
> Traditional **lubricants** (waxes, stearates, fatty acids) work by reducing the overall bulk viscosity of the melt. **Process aids** are far more sophisticated: they preferentially coat the die surface, locally reducing *wall slip* and only operating at the *metal–polymer interface*, leaving the bulk rheology of the polymer largely unchanged.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Process Aid Engineering

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Active dosage level | **50 – 2000 ppm** | Extremely low; below this, ineffective |
| Time to clear sharkskin | **5 – 20 minutes** | After reaching steady-state extrusion |
| Pressure reduction | **10% – 50%** | Drop in extrusion head pressure |
| Activation temperature | **180 °C – 300 °C** | Must melt and migrate at processing temperature |
| Critical shear rate (onset of sharkskin) | Increases by **2× to 10×** | Allows higher throughput |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard industrial unit for dosage of process aids is **ppm (parts per million)** or equivalently **g of additive per 10⁶ g of polymer**, not weight percent in normal masterbatch calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Problem Process Aids Solve: Sharkskin and Melt Fracture

When a polymer melt is forced through a die at high shear rates (typically $\dot{\gamma} > 10^3$ s⁻¹ for LLDPE), the polymer chains at the wall experience a velocity discontinuity. The chains must stretch and disentangle rapidly near the wall. The relaxation time of the polymer ($\lambda$) is longer than the deformation time imposed by the flow, leading to a **cohesive failure** of the surface layer. This failure manifests as:

1. **Sharkskin** – A high-frequency, small-amplitude surface roughness.
2. **Stick-slip (oscillating) melt fracture** – Periodic, severe distortions.
3. **Gross (wavy) melt fracture** – Chaotic, large-amplitude extrudate distortion.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Action — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1: Matrix Incorporation**
  The process aid is pre-blended (via masterbatch or direct compounding) into the polymer. Because process aids are typically immiscible with the base polymer, they exist as a **discrete dispersed phase** of small droplets (1–10 µm) in the polymer matrix.

- **Step 2: Melting and Migration**
  During melting in the extruder, the process aid melts at a lower temperature than the polymer or becomes highly mobile. Due to its **low surface energy** (typically **18 – 25 mN/m** for fluoropolymers), it preferentially **wets the metal die wall** rather than remaining dispersed in the polymer.

- **Step 3: Coating Formation**
  Over time, the additive forms a thin, continuous, low-surface-energy **coating** on the die land surface. The thickness of this coating is on the order of **nanometers to a few micrometers**.

- **Step 4: Slip Generation**
  Once the coating is established, the polymer melt no longer "sticks" to the die. It now slips over the coating, characterized by a **slip velocity ($V_s$)** at the wall rather than the no-slip condition.

- **Step 5: Delayed Flow Instability**
  Because the stress concentration at the wall is relieved, the **critical shear stress** required to initiate sharkskin is dramatically raised, allowing the processor to operate at **2× to 10× higher throughput** before melt fracture appears.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Apparent shear rate | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s), $D$ = die diameter (m) |
| Wall shear stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L}$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure drop, $L$ = die land length |
| Slip velocity (Navier boundary condition) | $V_s = \beta \cdot \tau_w$ | $\beta$ = slip coefficient (m/Pa·s) |
| Power-law viscosity | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | $K$ = consistency, $n$ = power-law index |
| Mooney analysis for slip | $\dfrac{8 V}{D} = \dot{\gamma}_a + \dfrac{4 \beta \tau_w}{D}$ | Linear plot of $8V/D$ vs $\tau_w$ gives $\beta$ as slope |
| Reptation time (Doi–Edwards) | $\tau_d \propto \dfrac{N^3}{N_e^2}$ | $N$ = chain length, $N_e$ = entanglement length |
| Dimensionless Weissenberg number | $Wi = \dot{\gamma} \cdot \lambda$ | $Wi > 1$ → chain stretch dominates |
| Critical shear rate for sharkskin | $\dot{\gamma}_c \propto \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Higher relaxation time → lower $\dot{\gamma}_c$ |
| Process aid dosage (ppm) | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{m_{aid}}{m_{polymer}} \times 10^6$ | Standard industrial unit |
| Surface energy condition (wetting) | $\gamma_{PPA} + \gamma_{PPA/metal} < \gamma_{metal}$ | Process aid must wet the die |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Concept:**
> The **Mooney method** is the standard experimental technique to *quantify* wall slip. By extruding the same polymer through dies of different diameters and plotting $\frac{8V}{D}$ against $\tau_w$, the **y-intercept gives the apparent shear rate** and the **slope yields the slip coefficient $\beta$**. A high $\beta$ indicates strong wall slip — exactly what a process aid produces.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Process aids are indispensable in:
- **LLDPE blown film extrusion** — eliminating sharkskin, allowing bubble stability at high rates.
- **HDPE pipe extrusion** — reducing head pressure, enabling higher line speeds.
- **PVC extrusion** — preventing die drool (build-up of degraded polymer at die exit).
- **Injection molding of polypropylene** — reducing cycle time by improving mold filling.
- **Wire and cable coating (XLPE)** — improving surface gloss of insulation.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Navier Slip Boundary Condition

We start with the classical no-slip boundary condition, which states that at a solid wall, the fluid velocity equals the wall velocity (zero for a stationary die):

$$V_{polymer}(r = R) = 0$$

For a polymer melt exhibiting wall slip, the **Navier hypothesis** (1822) proposes that the slip velocity is proportional to the wall shear stress. We define slip as the discontinuity in tangential velocity between the fluid and the wall.

Let the radial coordinate $r$ span from the die centerline ($r=0$) to the die wall ($r=R$). The axial velocity profile is $V_z(r)$.

The **shear stress at the wall** is given by:

$$\tau_{rz}\bigg\vert_{r=R} = \eta \frac{dV_z}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=R}$$

where $\eta$ is the viscosity. The **Navier slip condition** states:

$$V_z(R) - V_{wall} = -\beta \cdot \tau_{rz}(R)$$

For a stationary die, $V_{wall} = 0$, so:

$$V_s = V_z(R) = -\beta \cdot \tau_{rz}(R)$$

This establishes the foundational slip equation. The negative sign indicates slip occurs in the direction of flow.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Mooney Slip Analysis Equation

Consider a capillary die of diameter $D$ and length $L$. By performing a macroscopic force balance on the polymer column inside the die:

The pressure force pushing the melt forward = Viscous drag force resisting flow:

$$\Delta P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$

Solving for the **wall shear stress**:

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L}$$

The **apparent shear rate** (assuming no-slip, treating polymer as a Newtonian fluid) is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate.

Now, the **true velocity profile** inside the die for a power-law fluid with wall slip is derived by solving the momentum equation:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \tau_{rz} \right) = \frac{dP}{dz}$$

For a power-law fluid $\tau_{rz} = K \left( -\frac{dV_z}{dr} \right)^n$, integrating twice with the boundary condition $V_z(R) = V_s$ yields:

$$V_z(r) = \frac{n}{n+1}\left(\frac{-\Delta P}{2 K L}\right)^{1/n} R^{(n+1)/n} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{(n+1)/n} \right] + V_s$$

The **volumetric flow rate** is obtained by integrating $V_z$ over the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_0^R 2\pi r \cdot V_z(r) \, dr$$

Carrying out this integration and rearranging, we obtain the celebrated **Mooney equation**:

$$\frac{8V}{D} = \dot{\gamma}_a + \frac{4 \beta \tau_w}{D}$$

where $V$ is the average extrudate velocity ($V = Q/(\pi D^2/4)$), and $\beta$ is the slip coefficient. This linear equation is the foundation of all experimental slip measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** The Mooney equation separates the apparent shear rate into two physically distinct contributions: a **deformation (shear) component** and a **slip component**. When a process aid is added, only the slip component ($\beta \tau_w / D$) increases, while the deformation component remains essentially unchanged. This proves that process aids work *exclusively* at the wall.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Process Aid Chemistries

| Property | Fluoropolymer (e.g., Dynamar™) | Silicone (e.g., PDMS gum) | Polyethylene Wax | Acrylic Copolymer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Typical dosage (ppm) | 100 – 1000 | 500 – 5000 | 1000 – 5000 | 500 – 3000 |
| Surface energy (mN/m) | **18 – 22** | **20 – 24** | **28 – 32** | **30 – 35** |
| Sharkskin elimination | **Excellent** | Good | Moderate | Good |
| Die drool reduction | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate | Moderate |
| FDA approval (food contact) | Limited | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Cost (USD/kg) | **High (40 – 80)** | Medium (15 – 30) | Low (2 – 5) | Medium (10 – 20) |
| Common application | LLDPE film | Wire & cable | PVC | PP injection molding |
| Migration speed to wall | Fast | Medium | Slow | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fluoropolymer-based PPAs** are the most effective but also the most expensive. They are the industry standard for high-speed LLDPE film lines where they consistently eliminate sharkskin and increase output by **30% to 100%**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

A polymer processing engineer extrudes LLDPE through a capillary die of $D = 2$ mm and $L = 40$ mm. Without process aid, the extrudate shows sharkskin at $Q = 5$ cm³/min. After adding **500 ppm** of a fluoropolymer PPA, the same sharkskin-free output is achieved at $\Delta P = 2.5$ MPa (down from $\Delta P = 4.0$ MPa before the additive).

**Step 1:** Calculate the wall shear stress before PPA addition:

$$\tau_{w,1} = \frac{\Delta P_1 \cdot D}{4 L} = \frac{(4.0 \times 10^6) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})}{4 \cdot (40 \times 10^{-3})} = \frac{8000}{0.16} = 50{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 0.05 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the wall shear stress after PPA addition:

$$\tau_{w,2} = \frac{\Delta P_2 \cdot D}{4 L} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^6) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})}{4 \cdot (40 \times 10^{-3})} = \frac{5000}{0.16} = 31{,}250 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the percentage reduction in wall shear stress:

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{\tau_{w,1} - \tau_{w,2}}{\tau_{w,1}} \times 100 = \frac{50{,}000 - 31{,}250}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 37.5\%$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the apparent shear rate:

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3} = \frac{32 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6}/60)}{\pi \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})^3} = \frac{2.667 \times 10^{-6}}{2.513 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 106 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the slip coefficient $\beta$ using the Mooney equation. Assuming $V = Q / A$:

$$V = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}/60}{\pi \cdot (10^{-3})^2} = 0.0265 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\beta = \frac{ \left( \frac{8V}{D} - \dot{\gamma}_a \right) \cdot D}{4 \tau_{w,2}} = \frac{(106.1 - 106) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})}{4 \cdot 31{,}250} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/Pa·s}$$

**Engineering conclusion:** The process aid achieved a **37.5% reduction in wall shear stress** at the same flow rate, confirming effective wall slip generation. The very low value of $\beta$ reflects the nanometer-scale coating thickness.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanism of Process Aid Action (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Polymer + Process Aid Masterbatch] --> B[Feed Throat of Extruder]
    B --> C[Melting Zone - 180C to 300C]
    C --> D[PPA Disperses as Droplets in Polymer Melt]
    D --> E[PPA Migrates to Die Wall Surface]
    E --> F[Low Surface Energy Slip Layer Forms]
    F --> G[Polymer Slides Over Slip Layer]
    G --> H[Sharkskin Eliminated]
    G --> I[Extrusion Pressure Reduced]
    G --> J[Higher Throughput Achieved]

    classDef processNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef resultNode fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef startNode fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    class A,B startNode
    class C,D,E,F,G processNode
    class H,I,J resultNode
```

### 4.2 Classification Hierarchy of Process Aids

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PA[Process Aids for Polymers] --> F[Functional Fluoropolymers]
    PA --> S[Silicone Based Additives]
    PA --> W[Polyolefin Waxes]
    PA --> AC[Acrylic Copolymers]

    F --> F1[Dynamar PPA by 3M]
    F --> F2[Viton Free Flow by Chemours]
    F --> F3[Kynar by Arkema]

    S --> S1[PDMS Gum Masterbatch]
    S --> S2[Ultra High Molecular Weight Siloxane]

    W --> W1[LDPE Waxes]
    W --> W2[PP Waxes]
    W --> W3[Fischer Tropsch Waxes]

    AC --> AC1[Acrylic on Silica Carrier]
    AC --> AC2[EMA Based Processing Aids]

    classDef rootNode fill:#0b3d91,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef cat1 fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef cat2 fill:#1976d2,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef cat3 fill:#42a5f5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class PA rootNode
    class F,S,W,AC cat1
    class F1,F2,F3,S1,S2,W1,W2,W3,AC1,AC2 cat2
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Role of Process Aid in an Extrusion Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOPPER[Polymer Hopper] --> MELT[Melt Zone]
    PPA[PPA Masterbatch Dosing Unit] --> MELT
    MELT --> METERING[Metering Zone]
    METERING --> ADAPT[Adapter and Breaker Plate]
    ADAPT --> DIE[Capillary Die Land]
    DIE --> SLIP[Slip Layer Formation at Wall]
    SLIP --> OUT[Sharkskin Free Extrudate]
    DIE2[Without PPA] --> DIE3[Sharkskin and Melt Fracture]
    DIE3 --> OUT2[Defective Extrudate]

    classDef eqNode fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef ppaNode fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef resultGood fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef resultBad fill:#4a148c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    class HOPPER,MELT,METERING,ADAPT,DIE,SLIP,DIE2,DIE3 eqNode
    class PPA ppaNode
    class OUT resultGood
    class OUT2 resultBad
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Process Aid" in polymer processing. List any two commonly used process aids and their functions.*

**Model Answer:**
A process aid is a specialty additive used at very low concentrations (typically 50–2000 ppm) to improve the melt processability of polymers. It functions by migrating to the die–polymer interface, forming a thin, low-surface-energy coating that enables wall slip, thereby eliminating sharkskin, reducing melt fracture, and lowering extrusion pressure.
Common examples:
- **Fluoropolymer-based PPA** (e.g., Dynamar): Eliminates sharkskin in LLDPE film extrusion.
- **Silicone-based PPA** (e.g., UHMW-PDMS): Reduces die drool and improves surface finish in wire and cable extrusion.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a "process aid" and a "lubricant" in polymer processing.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Process Aid | Lubricant |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Coats die wall, induces slip | Reduces bulk viscosity |
| Site of action | Metal–polymer interface | Throughout the melt |
| Dosage | 50–2000 ppm | 0.5–5 phr |
| Effect on bulk rheology | Negligible | Significant viscosity reduction |
| Primary function | Eliminate sharkskin/melt fracture | Improve flow, reduce torque |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the mechanism by which process aids eliminate sharkskin and melt fracture during polymer extrusion. Use the Navier slip condition in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Mooney equation for wall slip analysis starting from the momentum balance on a polymer melt in a capillary die. Discuss how this equation is used experimentally to determine the slip coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism of sharkskin elimination (7 marks):**
1. Sharkskin is a surface flow instability arising at the die exit where the polymer chains experience sudden acceleration and extensional stress. The cohesive failure of the surface layer produces the characteristic high-frequency roughness.
2. Process aids are typically immiscible with the base polymer. During extrusion, the additive melts and migrates preferentially to the **metal die wall** due to its **lower surface energy** (γ_PPA ≈ 18–25 mN/m for fluoropolymers) compared to the polymer melt.
3. The accumulated additive forms a **continuous thin film** on the die land, of nanometer to micrometer thickness.
4. The Navier slip condition, $V_s = -\beta \tau_w$, now applies at the wall. A finite slip velocity $V_s$ is established, relieving the wall stress concentration.
5. Because the critical shear rate for sharkskin scales as $\dot{\gamma}_c \propto 1/\lambda$, and wall slip effectively reduces the deformation rate experienced by the surface chains, the critical shear rate for instability is **raised by a factor of 2 to 10**.
6. Result: Sharkskin is delayed or eliminated, allowing higher throughputs.

**(b) Derivation of the Mooney equation (7 marks):**
1. **Force balance** on a polymer column of length $L$ in a capillary of diameter $D$:

$$\Delta P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_w \cdot \pi D L \implies \tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L}$$

2. **Apparent shear rate** from Poiseuille flow:

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$$

3. **Momentum equation** in cylindrical coordinates for steady, fully-developed flow:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r \tau_{rz} \right) = \frac{dP}{dz}$$

4. For a power-law fluid $\tau_{rz} = K \left( -dV_z/dr \right)^n$, integrate twice with the slip boundary condition $V_z(R) = V_s$:

$$V_z(r) = \frac{n}{n+1}\left(\frac{-\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n} R^{(n+1)/n} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{(n+1)/n} \right] + V_s$$

5. Integrate $V_z$ over the cross-section to get $Q$, then express in terms of average velocity $V$:

$$Q = \int_0^R 2\pi r V_z(r) dr \implies \frac{8V}{D} = \dot{\gamma}_a + \frac{4 \beta \tau_w}{D}$$

6. **Experimental use:** Plot $\frac{8V}{D}$ against $\frac{4\tau_w}{D}$ for dies of varying diameter. The slope gives the **slip coefficient $\beta$** (in m/Pa·s). A higher $\beta$ for PPA-containing polymer confirms wall slip is the operative mechanism.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of process aids used in polymer processing. Compare fluoropolymer-based and silicone-based process aids with respect to their performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer melt is extruded through a capillary die of $D = 1.5$ mm and $L = 30$ mm. The volumetric flow rate is $Q = 3$ cm³/min. With a fluoropolymer process aid, the pressure drop is reduced from $\Delta P_1 = 5.0$ MPa to $\Delta P_2 = 3.0$ MPa. Calculate: (i) the apparent shear rate, (ii) the wall shear stress before and after PPA addition, and (iii) the percentage reduction in extrusion pressure. Comment on the role of the process aid. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**
Process aids are classified into four main families:
- **Fluoropolymer-based** (Dynamar, Kynar, Viton): Most effective; surface energy ≈ 18–22 mN/m.
- **Silicone-based** (UHMW-PDMS, silicone gum masterbatch): Excellent for die drool control; FDA-approved grades available.
- **Polyolefin waxes** (LDPE/PP waxes, Fischer–Tropsch waxes): Low-cost; moderate performance; widely used in PVC.
- **Acrylic copolymers** (acrylic-on-silica, EMA-based): Used in PP injection molding for cycle time reduction.

| Parameter | Fluoropolymer | Silicone |
|---|---|---|
| Sharkskin elimination | Excellent | Good |
| Die drool control | Excellent | Excellent |
| Dosage needed | 100–1000 ppm | 500–5000 ppm |
| Cost | High | Medium |
| FDA clearance | Limited | Generally yes |

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**
Given: $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 30 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $Q = 3 \times 10^{-6}/60 = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ m³/s, $\Delta P_1 = 5.0 \times 10^6$ Pa, $\Delta P_2 = 3.0 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**(i) Apparent shear rate:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3} = \frac{32 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^3} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-6}}{1.06 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 150.9 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Wall shear stresses:**

$$\tau_{w,1} = \frac{\Delta P_1 \cdot D}{4L} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-3}} = 62{,}500 \text{ Pa} = 62.5 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\tau_{w,2} = \frac{\Delta P_2 \cdot D}{4L} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-3}} = 37{,}500 \text{ Pa} = 37.5 \text{ kPa}$$

**(iii) Percentage pressure reduction:**

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{5.0 - 3.0}{5.0} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Comment:** The process aid reduced the extrusion pressure by **40%** at constant flow rate, confirming effective wall slip generation. The fluoropolymer migrated to the die wall, established a slip layer, and lowered the wall shear stress from 62.5 kPa to 37.5 kPa — a hallmark signature of a process aid mechanism (as opposed to bulk lubrication, which would alter the apparent shear rate significantly).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "process aid" with "plasticizer" or "lubricant":** Plasticizers reduce $T_g$ of the polymer; lubricants reduce bulk viscosity. Process aids act *exclusively at the die wall* — a critical distinction examiners test.
> 2. **Wrong units in dosage calculations:** KTU expects **ppm** for process aids, not **phr** (which is for lubricants). Mixing units is the #1 cause of lost numerical marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Navier sign convention:** The slip velocity is opposite in sign to the wall shear stress direction. Writing $V_s = +\beta \tau_w$ instead of $V_s = -\beta \tau_w$ will lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping steps in the Mooney derivation:** Examiners award partial credit for the momentum balance, the integration, and the final equation. Skipping any of these three steps costs at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Stating that process aids increase bulk viscosity:** Some students mistakenly believe that anything reducing pressure must be reducing viscosity in the bulk. The correct answer: **bulk rheology is unchanged; only wall slip is enhanced.**

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process aids** are specialty additives dosed at **50–2000 ppm** that act at the die–polymer interface, NOT in the bulk melt.
- The **Navier slip condition** $V_s = -\beta \tau_w$ is the foundational equation for quantifying wall slip.
- The **Mooney equation** $\frac{8V}{D} = \dot{\gamma}_a + \frac{4\beta\tau_w}{D}$ is the standard tool to experimentally extract the slip coefficient $\beta$.
- The mechanism involves: **(1) dispersion → (2) migration → (3) wall coating → (4) slip generation → (5) delayed instability**.
- **Fluoropolymer PPAs** are the most effective (surface energy ≈ 18–22 mN/m), with typical **30–100% throughput improvement** in LLDPE film extrusion.
- **Silicone-based PPAs** are FDA-approved and excel in die drool control.
- Critical rheological dimensionless group: **Weissenberg number** $Wi = \dot{\gamma}\lambda$; sharkskin onset correlates with $Wi \sim 1$.
- Process aids do **not** alter the polymer's $T_g$, mechanical, or optical properties significantly.
- Industrial impact: enable **higher line speeds**, **reduced energy consumption**, and **fewer rejected products** in blown film, pipe extrusion, and injection molding.
- For KTU exams, always remember the **three defining features**: low dosage, wall-specific action, no change to bulk rheology.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extenders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Extenders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **EXTENDER (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> An **extender** is a low-cost, often inert or partially reactive material incorporated into a polymer matrix (rubber, thermoset, or thermoplastic) to **increase bulk volume**, **reduce compound cost**, and — in many cases — **modify processing behavior, mechanical properties, or service performance**, without becoming the principal load-bearing component of the finished product.

In rubber technology (the most common KTU context for this term), extenders are traditionally divided into two broad classes:

1. **Petroleum-based extenders** — mineral oils (aromatic, naphthenic, paraffinic), petroleum jelly, and factice substitutes.
2. **Vegetable/natural extenders** — factice (sulphur- or oxygen-blown vegetable oils), pitches, and reclaimed rubber.

In plastics, the term is sometimes used interchangeably with **low-cost fillers** (chalk, talc, wood flour, calcium carbonate) when the primary purpose is bulk-extension rather than reinforcement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction from a Filler (Common Exam Pitfall)**
> - A **filler** is added primarily to *improve mechanical properties* (e.g., stiffness, hardness, tensile strength) — example: carbon black, silica.
> - An **extender** is added primarily to *extend volume and reduce cost* — example: mineral oil in SBR rubber, calcium carbonate in PVC.
> - Many materials (like calcium carbonate) can act as **both**, depending on loading and end-use intent.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Idli Batter" Model

Imagine preparing **idli batter** at home. The expensive ingredient is the *urad dal* (protein-rich, structural component). To make enough batter to feed a large family economically, you add **rice flakes (poha)** — cheaper, bulking material that fills the vessel and gives the idli its final shape, but is not the primary structural component.

In this analogy:
- **Urad dal** → Base polymer (natural rubber, SBR, epoxy resin)
- **Rice flakes (poha)** → Extender (mineral oil, calcium carbonate, factice)
- **Idli** → Final moulded product

The poha does not *strengthen* the idli — it simply makes it *more of it*, *cheaper*, and even *slightly changes its texture*. That is exactly the role of an extender in polymer processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used with Extenders**
> - **Loading:** expressed in **phr (parts per hundred parts of rubber)** — dimensionless ratio.
> - **Specific gravity:** typically **1.0 – 2.8** for common extenders.
> - **Oil-extended polymer:** commercial SBR grades contain **15 – 50 phr** mineral oil.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are Extenders Used? — The Engineering Rationale

The decision to add an extender is driven by a combination of **economic**, **processing**, and **performance** considerations. Each is explained below.

### A. Economic Justification
- Polymer raw materials (especially synthetic rubbers like SBR, EPDM, fluoroelastomers, and engineering plastics like PTFE) are **expensive**.
- Extenders are typically **5 – 20× cheaper per kg** than the base polymer.
- A **10 – 30 % reduction in compound cost** is routinely achieved with no unacceptable loss in performance.

### B. Processing Justification
- **Reduced viscosity** → easier mixing, extrusion, injection moulding, and calendering.
- **Better flow** → fewer moulding defects (short shots, flow marks).
- **Lower internal heat generation (hysteresis)** during processing.
- **Plasticization effect** in rubber — improves mill handling, extrudate smoothness.

### C. Performance Modification
- **Hardness control** — can be increased (fillers) or reduced (oils).
- **Damping** improvement — useful in vibration-isolating rubber products.
- **Specific gravity** adjustment.
- **Dimensional stability** — reduced post-moulding shrinkage.
- **Electrical/thermal properties** — e.g., mineral extenders raise thermal conductivity slightly.

## 2.2 Classification of Extenders

The classification is best understood hierarchically:

1. **By Origin**
   - **Petroleum/mineral-based:** aromatic oil, naphthenic oil, paraffinic oil, petroleum jelly, bitumen.
   - **Natural/vegetable-based:** factice (sulphur factice, white factice), pitches, rosin, reclaimed rubber.
   - **Synthetic/inorganic:** calcium carbonate (chalk/whiting), talc, kaolin clay, barytes (BaSO₄), silica (non-reinforcing grades).

2. **By Physical State**
   - **Liquid extenders:** mineral oils, factice, certain plasticizers.
   - **Solid extenders:** calcium carbonate, talc, reclaimed rubber.

3. **By Compatibility with Polymer**
   - **Compatible extenders:** naphthenic oils in NR/SBR; factice in NR.
   - **Semi-compatible / extending oils:** aromatic oils (must be checked for staining).
   - **Incompatible extenders:** paraffinic oils in polar rubbers (they can bloom out).

## 2.3 Selection Criteria — The 7-S Parameter List

A KTU examiner expects you to know the **seven key parameters** that govern extender selection:

| # | Parameter | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Specific gravity** | Determines final product density and volume cost |
| 2 | **Oil absorption (DBP number)** | Affects viscosity of the compound during mixing |
| 3 | **Particle size & shape** | Influences processability and surface finish |
| 4 | **Compatibility** | Prevents exudation/blooming |
| 5 | **Cost per kg & cost per unit volume** | True economic comparison |
| 6 | **Staining behaviour** | Critical for white/light-coloured goods |
| 7 | **Effect on cure** | Some extenders accelerate; others retard vulcanization |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum analytical toolkit** required to solve numerical problems on extenders in any KTU examination.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{phr} = \dfrac{m_{\text{additive}}}{m_{\text{polymer}}} \times 100$ | Concentration of additive in rubber compound | dimensionless |
| 2 | $V_i = \dfrac{m_i}{\rho_i}$ | Volume contributed by component *i* | m³ or cm³ |
| 3 | $\phi_i = \dfrac{V_i}{\sum V_j}$ | Volume fraction of component *i* | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\rho_{\text{compound}} = \dfrac{\sum m_i}{\sum V_i}$ | Density of the compounded mix | kg/m³ or g/cc |
| 5 | $C_{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{\text{Cost per kg}}{\rho}$ | Cost per unit volume (true economic metric) | ₹/m³ or ₹/cc |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{mix}} = \eta_p \, f(\phi_f)$ | Mooney viscosity of filled compound (e.g., Krieger–Dougherty model) | ML(1+4) at 100 °C |
| 7 | $\text{Loading (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{phr}}{100 + \text{phr}} \times 100$ | Convert phr to weight % | % |

> **Note:** In the above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ was deliberately avoided to keep the markdown table parser safe. Use $\vert x \vert$ notation only in display equations outside tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Extenders are critical in industries such as:
- **Tyre manufacturing** — SBR 1712 is an oil-extended masterbatch (37.5 phr aromatic oil) used in tread compounds.
- **Footwear (sole production)** — factice + mineral oil in EVA/NR soles for cushioning and cost.
- **PVC pipes & profiles** — calcium carbonate (chalk) up to 20 – 40 phr reduces cost without harming impact strength when surface-coated.
- **Epoxy flooring & coatings** — inert extenders (sand, barytes) reduce resin cost and adjust viscosity.
- **Cable insulation** — whiting and china clay reduce cost of rubber insulators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Cost Reduction Calculation Using Extender

**Problem Statement:**
A rubber compounder uses **SBR 1502** (cost ₹250/kg) as the base polymer. He wishes to add **mineral oil** (cost ₹80/kg) at **20 phr** as an extender. The compound also contains:
- **Zinc oxide:** 5 phr @ ₹180/kg
- **Stearic acid:** 2 phr @ ₹120/kg
- **Sulphur:** 1.5 phr @ ₹60/kg
- **CBS accelerator:** 1.2 phr @ ₹900/kg
- **Carbon black (HAF):** 50 phr @ ₹110/kg

Calculate:
1. Total cost per kg of the **un-extended** compound (assume no oil).
2. Total cost per kg of the **oil-extended** compound.
3. Percentage cost saving.

### Step 1 — Normalise to 100 g of base polymer

Because phr is "per hundred parts rubber", we take **100 g of SBR** as our reference batch.

### Step 2 — Build the un-extended compound (no oil)

| Component | phr | Mass (g) | Cost/kg (₹) | Cost (₹) |
|-----------|-----|----------|-------------|----------|
| SBR 1502 | 100 | 100 | 250 | 25.00 |
| ZnO | 5 | 5 | 180 | 0.90 |
| Stearic acid | 2 | 2 | 120 | 0.24 |
| Sulphur | 1.5 | 1.5 | 60 | 0.09 |
| CBS | 1.2 | 1.2 | 900 | 1.08 |
| HAF black | 50 | 50 | 110 | 5.50 |
| **Total** | **159.7** | **159.7** | — | **32.81** |

Total mass of un-extended compound $= 159.7$ g

Cost per gram of un-extended compound:

$$
c_{\text{un-ext}} \;=\; \dfrac{32.81}{159.7} \;=\; 0.2055 \text{ ₹/g} \;=\; 205.5 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Build the oil-extended compound

| Component | phr | Mass (g) | Cost/kg (₹) | Cost (₹) |
|-----------|-----|----------|-------------|----------|
| SBR 1502 | 100 | 100 | 250 | 25.00 |
| Mineral oil (extender) | 20 | 20 | 80 | 1.60 |
| ZnO | 5 | 5 | 180 | 0.90 |
| Stearic acid | 2 | 2 | 120 | 0.24 |
| Sulphur | 1.5 | 1.5 | 60 | 0.09 |
| CBS | 1.2 | 1.2 | 900 | 1.08 |
| HAF black | 50 | 50 | 110 | 5.50 |
| **Total** | **179.7** | **179.7** | — | **34.41** |

Total mass of oil-extended compound $= 179.7$ g

Cost per gram of oil-extended compound:

$$
c_{\text{ext}} \;=\; \dfrac{34.41}{179.7} \;=\; 0.1915 \text{ ₹/g} \;=\; 191.5 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

### Step 4 — Percentage cost saving

$$
\text{Saving} \;=\; \dfrac{c_{\text{un-ext}} - c_{\text{ext}}}{c_{\text{un-ext}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Saving} \;=\; \dfrac{205.5 - 191.5}{205.5} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Saving} \;=\; \dfrac{14.0}{205.5} \times 100 \;\approx\; 6.81\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only 6.81 % and not more?**
> The savings appear modest because the dominant cost component here is the **base polymer itself (76 % of total cost)**, and HAF black is the second-largest cost contributor. Extender economics become dramatically more impressive when the polymer is **expensive** (e.g., EPDM at ₹400/kg or fluoroelastomer at ₹4000/kg) and the extender is cheap mineral oil.

### Step 3b — Reformulation for an Expensive Polymer (Analytical Insight)

Suppose the base polymer is **EPDM** at ₹400/kg. If everything else is unchanged and we add 20 phr of oil @ ₹80/kg:

| | Un-extended (g) | Oil-extended (g) |
|---|---|---|
| Polymer | 100 g @ ₹400/kg = 40.00 | 100 g @ ₹400/kg = 40.00 |
| Oil | 0 | 20 g @ ₹80/kg = 1.60 |

Cost of un-extended compound (same other ingredients): $40.00 + 7.81 = 47.81$ ₹ over 159.7 g

Cost of oil-extended compound: $41.60 + 7.81 = 49.41$ ₹ over 179.7 g

$$
c_{\text{un-ext}} = \dfrac{47.81}{159.7} = 299.4 \text{ ₹/kg}, \quad c_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{49.41}{179.7} = 275.0 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

$$
\text{Saving} = \dfrac{299.4 - 275.0}{299.4} \times 100 \;\approx\; 8.14\,\%
$$

The absolute saving **per kg of compound** rose from **₹14/kg (SBR)** to **₹24.4/kg (EPDM)** — confirming that extenders deliver the **greatest absolute economic benefit in high-cost polymer systems**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Common Extenders

| Extender | Polymer System | Loading Range (phr) | Effect on Hardness | Effect on Tensile | Effect on Processability | Cost Impact |
|----------|----------------|----------------------|--------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **Aromatic oil** | NR, SBR, BR | 5 – 50 | ↓ Softens | ↓ Slight drop | ↑↑ Excellent | ↓↓↓ Strong |
| **Naphthenic oil** | EPDM, IIR | 5 – 30 | ↓ Softens | ↓ Slight drop | ↑ Good | ↓↓ Moderate |
| **Paraffinic oil** | EPDM | 5 – 20 | ↓ Softens | ↓ Slight drop | ↑ Good, low stain | ↓↓ Moderate |
| **Factice (sulphur)** | NR, SBR (soles) | 5 – 30 | ↓ Softens | ↓ Slight drop | ↑↑ Excellent | ↓↓ Moderate |
| **Calcium carbonate (chalk)** | PVC, NR, SBR | 10 – 100 | ↑ Hardens | ↑/↓ Variable | ↑ Improves | ↓↓↓ Strong |
| **Reclaimed rubber** | NR, SBR | 20 – 100 | ↑ Hardens | ↓ Moderate drop | ↑ Reduces viscosity | ↓↓↓ Strong |
| **Talc** | PP, PVC | 10 – 40 | ↑ Hardens | ↑ Stiffness | ↑ Improves | ↓↓ Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Extended Rubber Compound

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions** between base polymer, extender, and other compounding ingredients — exactly the kind of block-level functional schematic expected in a KTU answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    basePoly[Base Polymer NR or SBR or EPDM] --> comp[Mixing Banbury or Two Roll Mill]
    extender[Extender Oil or Factice or Whiting] --> comp
    activator[Activator ZnO plus Stearic Acid] --> comp
    filler[Filler Carbon Black or Silica] --> comp
    accel[Accelerator CBS or TMTD] --> comp
    curing[Curing Agent Sulphur or Peroxide] --> comp
    comp --> mat[Masterbatch]
    mat --> shap[Shaping Extrude or Calender or Mould]
    shap --> cure[Vulcanization 140 to 180 C]
    cure --> prod[Final Extended Polymer Product]
    cost[Cost Reduction] -.->|economic driver| extender
    proc[Processability Improvement] -.->|processing driver| extender
    perf[Performance Modification] -.->|engineering driver| extender
```

## 4.2 Classification Flowchart of Extenders

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Extenders] --> B[Petroleum Based]
    A --> C[Natural Based]
    A --> D[Inorganic Solid]
    B --> B1[Aromatic Oil]
    B --> B2[Naphthenic Oil]
    B --> B3[Paraffinic Oil]
    C --> C1[Sulphur Factice]
    C --> C2[White Factice]
    C --> C3[Reclaimed Rubber]
    D --> D1[Calcium Carbonate]
    D --> D2[Talc]
    D --> D3[Kaolin Clay]
    D --> D4[Barytes BaSO4]
```

## 4.3 Effect-Cascade Diagram — How an Extender Changes a Compound

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    add[Add Extender to Compound] --> r1[Viscosity Decreases]
    add --> r2[Cost per kg Decreases]
    add --> r3[Hardness Slight Decrease]
    add --> r4[Tensile Slight Decrease]
    add --> r5[Elongation at Break Increases]
    add --> r6[Hysteresis Decreases]
    r1 --> pr1[Better Flow and Mould Filling]
    r2 --> pr2[Economic Benefit]
    r3 --> pr3[Soften Product Feel]
    r4 --> pr4[Acceptable Trade off]
    r5 --> pr5[Improved Flexibility]
    r6 --> pr6[Lower Heat Build Up in Service]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the term "extender" as used in polymer compounding. How is it different from a reinforcing filler? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
An extender is a low-cost material — usually a mineral oil, factice, or inexpensive solid filler — added to a polymer to *increase bulk*, *reduce compound cost*, and *modify processing behaviour* without significantly improving mechanical strength. It is different from a reinforcing filler because a reinforcing filler (e.g., carbon black, precipitated silica) is added primarily to *improve mechanical properties* such as tensile strength, abrasion resistance, and modulus, often at higher cost per unit of property gain. Extenders are **passive diluents**; reinforcing fillers are **active strengtheners**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. What is the role of mineral oil as an extender in SBR rubber? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Mineral oil is added to SBR (typically 15 – 50 phr) as a **petroleum-based extender** to:
1. **Reduce compound cost** by diluting the more expensive SBR polymer.
2. **Lower Mooney viscosity**, improving processability in mixing, extrusion, and moulding.
3. **Soften the final vulcanizate** and improve flexibility and elongation.
4. **Reduce hysteresis** and hence heat build-up in dynamic applications such as tyres.
5. **Aid dispersion** of dry fillers like carbon black by wetting them during mixing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Classify the various extenders used in rubber compounding. Discuss the role of **factice** as a special-purpose extender in shoe-sole compounds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rubber compound uses 100 phr natural rubber, 5 phr ZnO, 2 phr stearic acid, 2.5 phr sulphur, 1 phr CBS, 40 phr HAF black, and 15 phr mineral oil. Calculate the **cost per kg of compound** if NR = ₹220/kg, ZnO = ₹180/kg, stearic acid = ₹110/kg, sulphur = ₹50/kg, CBS = ₹950/kg, HAF = ₹120/kg, and oil = ₹85/kg. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Q1(a) — Classification & Factice

**Classification of Extenders** (tabulated in answer):

| Class | Examples |
|-------|----------|
| Petroleum-based | Aromatic oil, naphthenic oil, paraffinic oil, bitumen |
| Natural/vegetable | Sulphur factice, white factice, pitch, rosin |
| Reclaimed/recycled | Reclaimed rubber, ground tyre rubber |
| Inorganic solid | Calcium carbonate, talc, kaolin, barytes |

**Role of Factice in Shoe-Sole Compounds (7 marks model answer):**

Factice (also called "rubber substitute") is produced by reacting vegetable oils (rape seed, castor, or coconut oil) with sulphur (sulphur factice) or with oxygen/air (white factice). Its specific roles in shoe-sole compounds are:

1. **Processing aid:** Reduces nerve and nerve-recovery time of the rubber, making the mill and calender operations smoother. The stock becomes softer and more pliable.
2. **Cost reduction:** Factice is significantly cheaper than natural rubber and reduces the overall compound cost.
3. **Sponging/expansion agent:** Sulphur factice, in particular, when heated, releases gases and acts as a **blowing agent**, producing the cellular structure in microcellular rubber soles (e.g., formal shoe soles, slippers).
4. **Hardness control:** Allows the compounder to fine-tune the hardness of the sole without drastically changing the polymer loading.
5. **Improved finish:** Gives a smoother, glossier surface to moulded soles.
6. **Better pigment dispersion:** Factice acts as a carrier for colour pigments in coloured sole compounds.

> **Loading levels in shoe-sole compounds:** typically **10 – 30 phr** sulphur factice for solid soles, up to **50 – 80 phr** in microcellular soles.

#### Solution to Q1(b) — Cost Calculation

Take **100 g NR** as reference (since phr = per hundred rubber).

| Component | phr | Mass (g) | Rate (₹/kg) | Cost (₹) |
|-----------|-----|----------|-------------|----------|
| NR | 100 | 100 | 220 | 22.000 |
| ZnO | 5 | 5 | 180 | 0.900 |
| Stearic acid | 2 | 2 | 110 | 0.220 |
| Sulphur | 2.5 | 2.5 | 50 | 0.125 |
| CBS | 1 | 1 | 950 | 0.950 |
| HAF black | 40 | 40 | 120 | 4.800 |
| Mineral oil | 15 | 15 | 85 | 1.275 |
| **Total** | **165.5** | **165.5** | — | **30.270** |

Cost per kg of compound:

$$
c \;=\; \dfrac{30.270}{0.1655} \;=\; \dfrac{30270}{165.5} \;\approx\; 182.9 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

**Final Answer: ₹182.9 per kg of compound.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Lost**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert grams → kg. Always divide total cost (₹) by total mass **in kg** (0.1655 kg, not 165.5 g).
> - **Mistake 2:** Using "100 g rubber" without including the **oil and other ingredients** in the total mass.
> - **Mistake 3:** Reporting cost per 100 g instead of per kg.
> - **Mistake 4:** Ignoring significant figures — quote to **one decimal place** (₹182.9/kg).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2 — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **selection criteria** for choosing a suitable extender for a rubber compound intended for use in **white, light-coloured, non-staining shoe soles**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Krieger–Dougherty equation** for the viscosity of a filled polymer melt. How does increasing the volume fraction of an extender (treated as inert filler) affect the viscosity of the compound? **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Q2(a) — Selection Criteria for White Sole Extender

For a **white, non-staining, light-coloured shoe sole**, the extender must satisfy all of the following:

1. **No staining / discolouration:**
   - Avoid **aromatic oils** — they contain polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons that migrate to the surface and cause yellow/brown staining.
   - Preferred: **paraffinic oils** (lowest staining tendency) or **white factice** (no colour, no staining).
   - Avoid dark-coloured fillers like carbon black — use **calcium carbonate (whiting)**, **kaolin clay**, or **talc** instead.

2. **Compatibility with the polymer:**
   - For **EVA or EPDM** (commonly used in white soles), paraffinic oils are highly compatible.
   - Factice is compatible with NR/SBR.

3. **No adverse effect on cure:**
   - Some extenders (e.g., acid clays) can interfere with accelerator activity. Whiting and china clay are largely **cure-neutral**.

4. **Processability:**
   - White factice reduces nerve, improves mill release, and aids blowing-agent dispersion (for microcellular soles).
   - Paraffinic oil reduces viscosity without blooming.

5. **Cost:** Whiting is the **cheapest solid extender**; it is the standard extender for white/light-coloured PVC and rubber goods.

6. **No odour / toxicity:** Especially important for footwear in contact with skin — paraffinic oils and food-grade whiting pass these criteria.

7. **Stability:** White extenders should not turn yellow on UV exposure. Surface-coated calcium carbonate (stearate-coated) gives better UV stability.

**Recommended formulation philosophy:**
- Base polymer: EVA or NR
- Extender: **Paraffinic oil (5 – 15 phr)** for processing + **Whiting (30 – 80 phr)** for bulk and stiffness
- Activator: ZnO + stearic acid (these are colour-safe)
- Curing: Peroxide (sulphur would give yellow tinge in whites)

---

#### Solution to Q2(b) — Krieger–Dougherty Equation

**Step 1 — The Need for a Viscosity Model:**
When an extender (treated as rigid, inert solid spheres) is dispersed in a polymer melt, the melt viscosity $\eta$ rises with the volume fraction $\phi$ of the solid. The **Krieger–Dougherty equation** is the most widely accepted semi-empirical model used by KTU examiners.

**Step 2 — Statement of the Equation:**

$$
\eta(\phi) \;=\; \eta_s \left( 1 - \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_m} \right)^{-[\eta]\,\phi_m}
$$

where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| $\eta(\phi)$ | Viscosity of the filled system | depends on $\phi$ |
| $\eta_s$ | Viscosity of the pure (unfilled) polymer melt | measured |
| $\phi$ | Volume fraction of the dispersed extender (filler) | 0 to < 1 |
| $\phi_m$ | **Maximum packing fraction** of the extender particles | ≈ 0.60 – 0.64 for random close packing of spheres |
| $[\eta]$ | **Intrinsic viscosity** (= 2.5 for rigid spheres, Einstein limit) | 2.5 |

**Step 3 — Limiting-Case Verification (Einstein Limit):**

For very small $\phi$ ($\phi \ll \phi_m$):

$$
\left( 1 - \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_m} \right)^{-[\eta]\phi_m} \;\approx\; 1 + [\eta]\,\phi \;=\; 1 + 2.5\,\phi
$$

Hence

$$
\eta \;\approx\; \eta_s (1 + 2.5\,\phi)
$$

which is the classical **Einstein equation** for dilute suspensions — confirming the model's correctness at low concentrations.

**Step 4 — Behaviour as $\phi$ increases:**

| Volume fraction $\phi$ | Behaviour of $\eta$ | Engineering implication |
|------------------------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| 0 – 0.10 | Linear rise ($\eta \approx \eta_s (1 + 2.5\phi)$) | Negligible penalty |
| 0.10 – 0.30 | Quadratic-ish rise | Mixing torque increases, but still manageable |
| 0.30 – 0.50 | Strong non-linear rise | Higher extrusion pressures, risk of melt fracture |
| $\to \phi_m$ ($\approx$ 0.60) | Viscosity **diverges to infinity** | Compound becomes unprocessable — system "jams" |

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Illustration:**

Take $\eta_s = 1.0 \times 10^4$ Pa·s, $[\eta] = 2.5$, $\phi_m = 0.60$.

- At $\phi = 0.20$:

$$
\eta(0.20) = 10^4 \left(1 - \dfrac{0.20}{0.60}\right)^{-2.5 \times 0.60} = 10^4 \left( \dfrac{2}{3} \right)^{-1.5}
$$

$$
= 10^4 \times (1.5)^{1.5} = 10^4 \times 1.837 \approx 1.84 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

- At $\phi = 0.40$:

$$
\eta(0.40) = 10^4 \left(1 - \dfrac{0.40}{0.60}\right)^{-1.5} = 10^4 \left( \dfrac{1}{3} \right)^{-1.5}
$$

$$
= 10^4 \times 3^{1.5} = 10^4 \times 5.196 \approx 5.20 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

- At $\phi = 0.55$:

$$
\eta(0.55) = 10^4 \left(1 - \dfrac{0.55}{0.60}\right)^{-1.5} = 10^4 \left(\dfrac{0.05}{0.60}\right)^{-1.5} = 10^4 \times (0.0833)^{-1.5}
$$

$$
= 10^4 \times 34.7 \approx 3.47 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 55 % volume loading pushes the viscosity up by a factor of **~35×** — illustrating why extenders/fillers are kept well below $\phi_m$ in practice (typically below 0.30 in rubber, below 0.40 in filled thermoplastics).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Marks Lost on Krieger–Dougherty**
> - Forgetting to write the **units** of $[\eta]$ (dL/g) or $\eta$ (Pa·s).
> - Not stating that $[\eta] = 2.5$ is the **Einstein limit** for rigid spheres.
> - Treating $\phi$ as a **mass fraction** — it must be a **volume fraction**.
> - Forgetting to mention $\phi_m$ and its typical range (0.60 – 0.64 for spheres).
> - Skipping the limiting-case verification — KTU examiners reward this step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Extenders (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Definition:** An extender is a low-cost additive used to increase bulk, reduce cost, and modify processing — *not* primarily to improve mechanical strength.
- ☐ **Difference from filler:** Filler → improves properties; extender → extends volume cheaply.
- ☐ **Main classes:** Petroleum (oils), natural (factice, reclaimed), inorganic solid (chalk, talc, kaolin).
- ☐ **Concentration unit:** **phr** = parts per hundred parts of rubber.
- ☐ **Cost analysis always done on ₹/kg of *compound*, not ₹/kg of polymer** — remember to add all phr values for total batch mass.
- ☐ **Extender selection is governed by 7 parameters:** specific gravity, oil absorption, particle size, compatibility, cost, staining, effect on cure.
- ☐ **For white/light goods:** avoid aromatic oils (staining), use paraffinic oils + whiting.
- ☐ **For non-staining EPDM goods:** paraffinic oil is the first-choice extender.
- ☐ **For shoe soles:** factice is a *special* extender — gives processing smoothness + acts as a blowing aid in microcellular soles.
- ☐ **Viscosity model:** Krieger–Dougherty equation governs how extender loading raises melt viscosity — viscosity **diverges as $\phi \to \phi_m$**.
- ☐ **Einstein limit:** $[\eta] = 2.5$ for rigid spheres; gives $\eta \approx \eta_s (1 + 2.5\phi)$ at low $\phi$.
- ☐ **Typical maximum safe volume fraction of extenders/fillers:** ≤ 0.30 (rubber) to ≤ 0.40 (thermoplastics) to keep the compound processable.
- ☐ **Phr to % conversion:** $\text{wt\%} = \text{phr} / (100 + \text{phr}) \times 100$ — remember this for quick mental checks.
- ☐ **Volume cost (true economic metric):** $C_{\text{vol}} = \text{cost/kg} \div \text{specific gravity}$ — use this when comparing an expensive light extender with a cheap heavy extender.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: Extenders**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Factice

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Factice** (also historically referred to as *Factis*, *Vulcanized Oil*, or *Rubber Substitute*) is an elastic, gel-like polymeric material produced by the **vulcanization (crosslinking) of unsaturated vegetable oils** in the presence of a vulcanizing agent such as **elemental sulfur (S₈)** or **sulfur monochloride (S₂Cl₂)**. It is classified as a **vulcanized vegetable oil (VVO)** and functions as a **processing aid, plasticizer, and economic extender** in natural rubber (NR) and synthetic rubber (SBR, NBR, EPDM) compounding.

In the official KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCPOT502 – Polymer Processing*, Factice is described as a **non-rubber, oil-derived polymeric additive** that mimics the elastic behavior of rubber at a fraction of the cost, while also conferring specific processing advantages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology Note (KTU Board Examiner's Standard):**
> In KTU examination answers, students are expected to use the term **"Factice"** (not "factis" or "rubber substitute") as it is the standardized nomenclature adopted by the *Indian Rubber Institute (IRI)* and the *Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) – IS 5192*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Visualization)

Imagine you have a **piece of dry, hard chewing gum** (this is your raw vegetable oil, which is a viscous liquid but not elastic). Now, if you sprinkle some **sulfur powder on it and heat it gently**, the gum transforms into a **soft, spongy, rubbery ball** that bounces slightly and can be stretched — this transformed product is **Factice**.

**In simpler terms:**
- **Vegetable oil (e.g., rapeseed oil, soybean oil, castor oil)** = the raw "chewing gum"
- **Sulfur or Sulfur monochloride** = the "magic powder" that crosslinks the oil molecules
- **Factice** = the final "rubber-like sponge" that you mix into real rubber to make it cheaper and easier to process

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Hook:** Think of Factice as a **"diluted rubber"** that doesn't come from rubber trees! It is essentially an **oil-based elastic filler** — it behaves like rubber (elastic), but is manufactured from agricultural oils, making it **cheaper, lighter, and easier to process** than natural rubber itself.

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

The following standardized properties define Factice as per industry specifications:

| Property | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Off-white to dark brown (depending on type) | Visual identification |
| **Density** | **1.00 – 1.10 g/cm³** | Lightweight extender |
| **Specific Gravity** | ~ 1.01 | Density-based formulation |
| **Ash Content** | < 1.5 % (white); < 5 % (brown) | Purity indicator |
| **Sulfur Content** | 5 – 25 % (varies by type) | Crosslink density proxy |
| **Moisture** | < 1 % | Storage stability |
| **Acetone Extract** | 10 – 40 % | Unreacted oil content |

> [!TIP]
> The **acetone extract** value is critical — it tells the formulator how much **unreacted (free) oil** remains in the Factice. A higher acetone extract means softer, more plasticizing Factice; a lower extract means a harder, more elastic Factice.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind Factice

Natural rubber (NR) is an excellent elastomer, but it has three key industrial problems:
1. **High cost** (commodity pricing)
2. **High viscosity** during mastication (hard to mix with fillers and additives)
3. **Shrinkage** during extrusion and molding

Factice solves all three problems simultaneously. It is mixed into the rubber compound (typically at **5 – 50 phr** — parts per hundred rubber) to:
- **Reduce compound cost** (cheaper than NR)
- **Improve processability** (lowers Mooney viscosity)
- **Provide internal lubrication** during extrusion and calendering
- **Act as a binder** in soft, elastic compounds (e.g., erasers, printing blankets)

## 2.2 Chemistry of Factice Formation — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: The Raw Material — Unsaturated Triglyceride Oil

Vegetable oils are **triglycerides** — three fatty acid chains attached to a glycerol backbone. The active site for vulcanization is the **C=C double bond** in unsaturated fatty acids (e.g., oleic, linoleic, linolenic acids).

$$
\text{Triglyceride} \;\; \equiv \;\; \text{Glycerol} + 3 \times \text{Unsaturated Fatty Acid Chains}
$$

### Step 2: Vulcanization with Sulfur Monochloride (S₂Cl₂) — *White Factice Route*

$$
\text{C}{=}\text{C (in oil)} + \text{S}_2\text{Cl}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}{-}\text{S}{-}\text{S}{-}\text{C} + \text{HCl} \uparrow
$$

This is a **cold vulcanization** process carried out at **room temperature (~25 – 40 °C)**, producing a **white/cream-colored factice**.

### Step 3: Vulcanization with Elemental Sulfur — *Brown Factice Route*

$$
\text{C}{=}\text{C (in oil)} + \text{S}_8 \;\xrightarrow{\Delta\,(130-160\,°C)}\; \text{C}{-}\text{S}_x{-}\text{C} \quad (x = 1 \text{ to } 4)
$$

This is a **hot vulcanization** process carried out at **130 – 160 °C** for several hours, producing a **dark brown factice** with a higher degree of crosslinking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Point:** The **color of the Factice is NOT due to impurities** — it is a direct chemical consequence of the vulcanizing agent used. S₂Cl₂ gives white factice; elemental sulfur gives brown factice. Always mention this in your answer.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Metrics

The following table summarizes the critical formulas, ratios, and engineering metrics used in Factice design and compounding:

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parts per Hundred Rubber (phr)** | $$\text{phr} = \frac{m_{\text{factice}}}{m_{\text{rubber}}} \times 100$$ | dimensionless | Standardized dosage metric |
| **Compound Cost Reduction** | $$\Delta C = C_{\text{NR}} - \left( f_{\text{NR}} \cdot C_{\text{NR}} + f_{\text{F}} \cdot C_{\text{F}} \right)$$ | ₹/kg | Cost optimization |
| **Volume Fraction of Factice** | $$\phi_F = \frac{V_F}{V_F + V_{NR} + V_{\text{filler}}}$$ | dimensionless | Composite design |
| **Crosslink Density proxy** | $$\nu \propto \frac{\% S \text{ in factice}}{M_{\text{oil}}}$$ | mol/cm³ | Elasticity tuning |
| **Mooney Viscosity Reduction** | $$\eta_{\text{mix}} = \eta_{\text{NR}} \cdot (1 - k \cdot \phi_F)$$ | MU (Mooney Units) | Processability |
| **ASTM D1456 — Sulfur content test** | Standard reference | % | Quality control |
| **BIS IS 5192** | Indian standard | — | National specification |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the above table, note that the absolute value bars and ratios in the formulas use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` (not the markdown table delimiter `\vert`). The LaTeX `\phi` symbol represents the volume fraction, NOT the Greek letter `phi` typed out.

## 2.4 Types of Factice — Comparative Engineering Analysis

| Property | White Factice (S₂Cl₂) | Brown Factice (Sulfur) | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Color** | White / cream | Dark brown / amber | Aesthetic match |
| **Manufacturing Temperature** | 25 – 40 °C (cold) | 130 – 160 °C (hot) | Energy cost |
| **Crosslink Density** | Low – Medium | Medium – High | Mechanical strength |
| **Sulfur Content** | 5 – 15 % | 15 – 25 % | Crosslink proxy |
| **Plasticizing Effect** | High (soft) | Low (firm) | Softness tuning |
| **Typical Loading** | 10 – 50 phr | 5 – 20 phr | Application-specific |
| **Primary Use** | Erasers, soft seals | Printing blankets, hard rubber goods | Application-specific |
| **Storage Stability** | Lower (HCl residue) | Higher | Shelf life |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Eraser Industry:** White factice is the **primary binder** in soft rubber erasers. Loading can be as high as **80 – 100 phr**.
- **Printing Blankets:** Brown factice provides the **resilient cushion layer** in offset printing blankets.
- **Gasket and Seal Industry:** White factice is used in **soft, compression-set-resistant** gaskets.
- **Cable Insulation:** Factice is added to improve **flexibility and reduce cost** of cable sheaths.
- **Tire Industry (limited):** Used in **inner liners and sidewall compounds** at low loadings (5 – 10 phr) for processing benefits.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Compounding Design with Factice

### Problem Setup
A KTU-style numerical problem typically asks: *"A natural rubber compound contains 100 g of NR and 30 g of white factice. Calculate the phr of factice, the volume fraction of factice, and the percentage cost reduction if the cost of NR is ₹250/kg and the cost of factice is ₹80/kg."*

### Step 1: Calculate phr of Factice

By definition, phr is the mass of additive per 100 g of base rubber.

$$
\text{phr}_{\text{F}} = \frac{m_{\text{F}}}{m_{\text{NR}}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
\text{phr}_{\text{F}} = \frac{30 \text{ g}}{100 \text{ g}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{phr}_{\text{F}} = 30 \text{ phr}
$$

**Interpretation:** The compound contains 30 parts of factice per 100 parts of rubber — a **moderate-to-high loading** typical for soft, elastic products.

### Step 2: Calculate the Volume Fraction of Factice

First, calculate the volumes of each component using their densities.

$$
V_{\text{NR}} = \frac{m_{\text{NR}}}{\rho_{\text{NR}}} = \frac{100 \text{ g}}{0.913 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

$$
V_{\text{NR}} = 109.53 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
V_{\text{F}} = \frac{m_{\text{F}}}{\rho_{\text{F}}} = \frac{30 \text{ g}}{1.05 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

$$
V_{\text{F}} = 28.57 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Now, the total volume of the compound (assuming no air entrapment):

$$
V_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{NR}} + V_{\text{F}} = 109.53 + 28.57 = 138.10 \text{ cm}^3
$$

The volume fraction of factice is:

$$
\phi_F = \frac{V_F}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{28.57}{138.10}
$$

$$
\phi_F = 0.2069 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 20.69\,\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Factice occupies about **20.7 %** of the total compound volume. This is the volumetric loading that controls the elastic, thermal, and processing properties of the final product.

### Step 3: Calculate the Percentage Cost Reduction

First, calculate the cost of the **pure NR compound** (no factice, 1 kg basis):

$$
C_{\text{pure}} = 1000 \text{ g} \times \frac{₹\,250}{1000 \text{ g}} = ₹\,250
$$

Next, calculate the cost of the **factice-loaded compound** (also 1 kg basis, but with the same NR-to-F ratio as 100 g + 30 g):

The mass ratio in the loaded compound is:

$$
\text{NR} = 100 \text{ g} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{F} = 30 \text{ g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Total} = 130 \text{ g}
$$

The cost of 130 g of this compound:

$$
C_{\text{130 g}} = \left(100 \text{ g} \times \frac{₹\,250}{1000 \text{ g}}\right) + \left(30 \text{ g} \times \frac{₹\,80}{1000 \text{ g}}\right)
$$

$$
C_{\text{130 g}} = ₹\,25.00 + ₹\,2.40 = ₹\,27.40
$$

The cost of an equivalent **130 g of pure NR** would be:

$$
C_{\text{130 g, pure}} = 130 \text{ g} \times \frac{₹\,250}{1000 \text{ g}} = ₹\,32.50
$$

The cost saving is:

$$
\Delta C = C_{\text{130 g, pure}} - C_{\text{130 g}} = 32.50 - 27.40 = ₹\,5.10
$$

The percentage cost reduction is:

$$
\% \text{Reduction} = \frac{\Delta C}{C_{\text{130 g, pure}}} \times 100 = \frac{5.10}{32.50} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{Reduction} = 15.69\,\%
$$

**Interpretation:** By adding 30 phr of factice, the compound cost is reduced by **~15.7 %**, demonstrating the strong economic incentive for using factice in industrial rubber compounding.

### Step 4: Summary of Calculated Parameters

| Parameter | Calculated Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **phr of Factice** | 30 phr | Moderate-to-high loading |
| **Volume fraction (ϕ_F)** | 0.2069 (20.69 %) | Volumetric dominance check |
| **Cost reduction** | 15.69 % | Strong economic incentive |
| **Density of compound (estimate)** | $$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{130 \text{ g}}{138.10 \text{ cm}^3} = 0.941 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ | Lightweight advantage |

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Factice

The following matrix helps students and engineers choose the right type of factice for a given application:

| Application | Recommended Factice | Loading Range | Critical Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Soft pencil eraser** | White (S₂Cl₂) | 50 – 100 phr | High plasticity, low hardness |
| **Printing blanket (offset)** | Brown (Sulfur) | 20 – 40 phr | Resilience, ink resistance |
| **Rubber gaskets (soft)** | White | 15 – 30 phr | Compression set resistance |
| **Cable sheathing** | Brown | 5 – 15 phr | Flexibility, low cost |
| **Hard rubber ebonite** | Brown (high S) | 10 – 25 phr | Rigidity, dimensional stability |
| **Inner tire liner** | Brown | 3 – 8 phr | Air retention, processability |
| **Roller coverings (printing)** | Brown | 30 – 60 phr | High resilience, ink release |
| **Toy balloons (latex)** | White (latex form) | 5 – 10 phr | Softness, elongation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Manufacturing Process Architecture

The following Mermaid flow diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of the Factice manufacturing process, mapping the raw material inputs, the two divergent processing routes, and the final product classes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Vegetable Oil] --> B[Oil Pretreatment]
    B --> C[Filtration and Degassing]
    
    C --> D{Choice of Vulcanizing Agent}
    
    D -->|Sulfur Monochloride| E1[Cold Vulcanization Route]
    D -->|Elemental Sulfur| E2[Hot Vulcanization Route]
    
    E1 --> F1[Reaction at 25 to 40 degree C]
    E1 --> G1[Addition of Acid Neutralizer]
    E1 --> H1[Washing and Drying]
    E1 --> I1[White Factice Product]
    
    E2 --> F2[Reaction at 130 to 160 degree C]
    E2 --> G2[Addition of Accelerator if needed]
    E2 --> H2[Cooling and Milling]
    E2 --> I2[Brown Factice Product]
    
    I1 --> J[Quality Control Testing]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K[Packaging and Storage]
    
    K --> L1[Eraser Industry]
    K --> L2[Printing Blankets]
    K --> L3[Gaskets and Seals]
    K --> L4[Cable Industry]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **two parallel routes** (cold vs. hot) clearly show the divergence based on vulcanizing agent.
- **Cold route** (left branch) → White Factice
- **Hot route** (right branch) → Brown Factice
- Both routes converge at **Quality Control** and then diverge again into the **four primary application industries**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Role of Factice in a Rubber Compound

This second diagram maps the **multi-functional role of Factice** as it is integrated into a rubber matrix.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Factice Particles] --> B[Rubber Matrix]
    A --> C1[Cost Reducer]
    A --> C2[Processing Aid]
    A --> C3[Plasticizer]
    A --> C4[Softener]
    A --> C5[Extender]
    
    B --> D[Final Rubber Product]
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    
    D --> E1[Lower Cost]
    D --> E2[Easier Processing]
    D --> E3[Improved Flexibility]
    D --> E4[Tailored Softness]
    D --> E5[Reduced Compound Density]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- A single Factice particle delivers **five simultaneous functions** when dispersed in the rubber matrix.
- These five functions collectively produce a **final rubber product** with five engineering benefits.

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Microstructural Visualization

For quick revision, the following ASCII schematic shows the **microstructural role of Factice** in a natural rubber compound:

```
NATURAL RUBBER COMPOUND (MICROSTRUCTURE)
========================================

[ Rubber ] [ Rubber ] [ Rubber ] [ Rubber ]
[ Chain   ] [ Chain   ] [ Chain   ] [ Chain   ]
   |          |          |          |
   |          |          |          |
[ F ]  =  Factice Particle (Vulcanized Oil)
[ R ]  =  Rubber Chain (Polyisoprene)
[ B ]  =  Carbon Black or other Reinforcing Filler

Schematic Layout:
+----------+ +----------+ +----------+
|    RRR   | |    RRR   | |    RRR   |  <- Rubber chains
|   RRRRR  | |   RRRRR  | |   RRRRR  |
|    RRR   | |    RRR   | |    RRR   |
+----------+ +----------+ +----------+
       \         |         /
        \        |        /
         +-------v-------+
         |  FFFFFFFFFF  |               <- Factice particle
         |  FFFFFFFFFF  |                  (vulcanized oil)
         |  FFFFFFFFFF  |
         +---------------+
                |
                v
```
