```mermaid
    APPS --> AP4["Chewing Gum and Hygiene Adhesives"]
    APPS --> AP5["Sealants and Caulks"]
```

### 4.2 Mechanism Block Diagram — How a Tackifier Reshapes Viscoelasticity

The following flow illustrates the *causal chain* from molecular-level addition of tackifier to macroscopic performance:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef stepCls fill:#E1F0FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef resCls fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef kpiCls fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    s1["STEP 1 - Add Tackifier Resin into Elastomer Matrix"]:::stepCls
    s2["STEP 2 - Tackifier Tg is HIGH (50 to 90 C)"]:::stepCls
    s3["STEP 3 - Fox Equation Blend Tg INCREASES"]:::stepCls
    s4["STEP 4 - Material is in RUBBERY / DAMPED State at 25 C"]:::stepCls
    s5["STEP 5 - Viscous Flow Wets the Substrate within 1 second"]:::stepCls
    s6["STEP 6 - Adhesive Wets Surface Microscopically and Dissipates Energy on Peel"]:::stepCls

    r1["RESULT - Dahlquist Compliance J greater than 1e-6 m2 per N satisfied"]:::resCls
    r2["RESULT - High TACK - quick stick - low contact pressure bonding"]:::resCls
    r3["RESULT - High PEEL STRENGTH via viscous dissipation at debond"]:::resCls

    k1["KPI - Loop Tack g per 25 mm"]:::kpiCls
    k2["KPI - 180 Degree Peel N per 25 mm"]:::kpiCls
    k3["KPI - Shear Holding Power minutes"]:::kpiCls

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6
    s6 --> r1
    s6 --> r2
    s6 --> r3
    r1 --> k1
    r2 --> k2
    r3 --> k3
```

### 4.3 Property–Performance Radar Map

A schematic representation of how a balanced tackifier contributes to the *seven* major adhesive performance vectors:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef grp fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#5D4037,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef arrow fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    A[Tackifier Resin at OPTIMUM LOADING]:::grp
    B[Increases Tg of Soft Phase]:::arrow
    C[Modifies Viscoelastic Window]:::arrow
    D[Improves Surface Wetting]:::arrow
    E[Enhances Viscous Dissipation on Peel]:::arrow
    F[Provides Quick Stick at Low Pressure]:::arrow
    G[Maintains Cohesive Strength at Optimum]:::arrow
    H[Output - Balanced PSA with HIGH TACK and GOOD HOLD]:::grp

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F
    A --> G
    B --> H
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The architecture above satisfies the KTU module-end expectation that students can *map* a property to a function, and a function to a performance metric. The same mapping style applies to fillers, plasticizers, and curatives in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define the term *tack* in the context of pressure-sensitive adhesives. List any **three** commercially important classes of tackifying agents and give **one** example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Tack is the property of an adhesive that enables it to form a bond of measurable strength upon brief contact with a substrate under light pressure (typically < 1 N/cm²) at ambient temperature. (1 mark)

Three important classes of tackifiers and one example each:
1. **Natural Resins:** Rosin (gum rosin / wood rosin / tall-oil rosin). (1 mark)
2. **Modified Natural Resins:** Hydrogenated rosin, rosin esters (ester gum), disproportionated rosin. (0.5 mark)
3. **Synthetic Hydrocarbon Resins:** C5 aliphatic (polyisoprene / piperylene-based), C9 aromatic (indene–coumarone), C5/C9 copolymers, DCPD resins. (0.5 mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> State the **Dahlquist criterion** for a pressure-sensitive adhesive. What is its physical significance in tackifier design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Dahlquist criterion states that, for an adhesive to develop good room-temperature tack, its **shear creep compliance at 1 second (J(t = 1 s))** must be **≥ 1 × 10⁻⁶ m²/N** at the bonding temperature. (1.5 marks)

Physical significance: This ensures the adhesive is *soft and rubbery enough* to flow into the microscopic asperities of the substrate within the brief contact time, enabling intimate molecular contact. If the compliance is below this threshold, the adhesive behaves like a glassy solid and cannot wet out the surface. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Classify tackifying agents in detail, giving **at least four** major classes with suitable examples and their typical molecular weight range. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Discuss the mechanism by which a tackifier improves the **tack** of a rubber-based adhesive, with reference to the **Fox equation** and the **Dahlquist criterion**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Tackifying Agents (7 marks)**

Tackifying agents are low-molecular-weight, glassy, thermoplastic resins that are added to elastomers and acrylics to enhance their stickiness. They are classified as follows:

**(i) Natural Resins** (1.5 marks)
- **Rosin** — obtained from pine trees (gum rosin, wood rosin, tall-oil rosin); composed mainly of abietic-type diterpene acids. M_n ≈ 300–500.
- **Terpene Resins** — polymers of α-pinene, β-pinene, or dipentene. M_n ≈ 500–2000.

**(ii) Modified Natural Resins** (1.5 marks)
- **Hydrogenated Rosin** — improves oxidative stability and removes UV yellowing.
- **Rosin Esters (Ester Gum)** — reaction of rosin with glycerol or pentaerythritol; less crystalline, better compatibility.
- **Disproportionated Rosin** — heat-treated rosin with reduced unsaturation.
- M_n ≈ 500–1500.

**(iii) Synthetic Hydrocarbon Resins** (2 marks)
- **C5 Aliphatic Resins** — polymerized from C5 olefinic streams (piperylene, isoprene, 1,3-pentadiene). M_n ≈ 500–2000. Excellent for NR, SIS, SBR.
- **C9 Aromatic Resins** — from C9 streams (indene, coumarone, styrene, vinyl toluene). M_n ≈ 500–2500. Used with SBR, neoprene.
- **C5/C9 Copolymers** — blends of aliphatic and aromatic streams, balanced polarity.
- **DCPD Resins** — dicyclopentadiene-based; high softening point (≥ 120 °C).

**(iv) Specialty / Engineered Resins** (2 marks)
- **Terpene-Phenolic Resins** — terpene + phenol-formaldehyde condensation; high T_g, high polarity.
- **Hydrogenated Hydrocarbon Resins** — water-white, low colour, excellent UV stability.
- **Pure Monomer Resins** — α-methyl styrene, AMS homopolymers; high T_g, narrow MWD.
- **Bio-based terpene resins** — sustainable alternatives derived from citrus peel oils.

Each class has its own compatibility envelope defined by the **Hansen solubility parameter sphere** of the base polymer, which is the practical selection rule used in industry.

**(b) Mechanism of Tack Improvement (7 marks)**

(1 mark) Tackifiers raise the *effective* T_g of the elastomer *soft phase* while leaving the bulk material soft. This is quantitatively predicted by the **Fox equation**:

$$\frac{1}{T_{g,\,\text{blend}}} = \frac{w_{1}}{T_{g,\,1}} + \frac{w_{2}}{T_{g,\,2}}$$

(1.5 marks) Because a tackifier has a high T_g (typically 50–90 °C) and the base elastomer has a low T_g (e.g., polyisoprene midblock at −60 °C), blending shifts the blend T_g *upwards*. For a 60:40 SIS/C5 blend, the Fox equation predicts a midblock T_g of about −26.5 °C (worked in §3.1 above). The *ΔT* between the use temperature (25 °C) and the new T_g is ~51.5 °C, placing the adhesive in the **rubbery plateau with significant damping (tan δ peak)**.

(1.5 marks) At this rheological position, the adhesive satisfies the **Dahlquist criterion** — its 1-second creep compliance J(1 s) exceeds 1 × 10⁻⁶ m²/N, allowing viscous flow into surface asperities within the contact time.

(1.5 marks) The tackifier also provides **viscous dissipation** during debonding. As the PSA is peeled, the energy stored elastically in the bulk is dissipated as viscous heat in the tackifier-rich micro-regions. This raises the measured peel strength.

(1.5 marks) Beyond an **optimum loading** (usually 50–120 phr for PSA), the blend becomes *too glassy*, J(1 s) drops below the Dahlquist threshold, and the adhesive shows *cohesive failure* with poor cohesive strength. Thus, **tackifier selection is a balancing act between tack, peel, and shear** — a fact that the KTU examiner expects to see in any 7-mark explanation.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative Set]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, explain the **role of tackifier** in a pressure-sensitive adhesive tape. Highlight the **three performance criteria** a tackifier must satisfy. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A hot-melt adhesive is formulated using **EVA (40 % VA, T_g = −25 °C)** and a **C5/C9 hydrocarbon tackifier resin (T_g = +60 °C)** in a 70:30 mass ratio. Predict the blend T_g using the **Fox equation** and state whether the blend is suitable for **room-temperature application**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Tackifier in a PSA Tape (7 marks)**

A typical pressure-sensitive adhesive tape has the following layered construction (text-based schematic, since the physical drawing is the *adhesive film* on a backing):

```
[Release Liner (silicone-coated paper)]
       |
[Adhesive Layer  = Elastomer (SIS, NR, Acrylic) + Tackifier (C5 or Rosin) + Plasticizer + Antioxidant]
       |
[Backing Film (BOPP, PET, PVC, paper, cloth)]
       |
[Primer Coat (optional, anchors adhesive to backing)]
```

(1 mark) The tackifier is dispersed **molecularly** within the elastomer matrix. It does not form a separate layer; instead, it **lowers the entanglement density** of the elastomer chains and **raises the T_g** of the soft phase as shown by the Fox equation.

(2 marks) **Three performance criteria** a tackifier must satisfy:

1. **Compatibility (Miscibility) with the base polymer** — verified by a single-T_g DSC scan, optical clarity, and a small Hansen sphere distance $R_a$. (1 mark)
2. **Right T_g window** — the tackifier T_g should be such that, after blending, the resultant blend T_g is *30 °C – 50 °C below the bonding temperature* so that the Dahlquist compliance criterion is met. (1 mark)
3. **Right softening point and oxidative stability** — the tackifier must soften below the coating/application temperature, yet must not crystallize, oxidize, or discolour during the service life of the adhesive. (1 mark)

(2 marks) In addition, the tackifier should not plasticise the elastomer to the point of cohesive failure, and it should not bleed (migrate to the surface) over time. Good tackifiers also exhibit **low acid number** (rosin grades) and **good thermal stability** (hydrogenated grades) to prevent oxidative degradation in hot-melt processing.

(2 marks) **Engineering example:** A typical **transparent office tape** uses a SIS triblock copolymer as the elastomer, a C5 aliphatic resin (e.g., Escorez 1102) as the tackifier at ~100 phr, and a small amount of liquid plasticiser (e.g., mineral oil) at ~10 phr to fine-tune the rolling-ball tack.

**(b) Numerical Solution using Fox Equation (7 marks)**

Given:
- EVA with 40 % VA, T_g1 = −25 °C = 248.15 K
- C5/C9 tackifier, T_g2 = +60 °C = 333.15 K
- Mass ratio: 70 parts EVA + 30 parts tackifier

(1 mark) **Step 1 — Mass fractions:**

$$w_{\text{EVA}} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.70 \qquad w_{\text{tackifier}} = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30$$

(1 mark) **Step 2 — Substituting into the Fox equation:**

$$\frac{1}{T_{g,\,\text{blend}}} = \frac{0.70}{248.15} + \frac{0.30}{333.15}$$

(1.5 marks) **Step 3 — Evaluate each term:**

$$\frac{0.70}{248.15} = 2.821 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{0.30}{333.15} = 0.901 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

(1 mark) **Step 4 — Sum and invert:**

$$\frac{1}{T_{g,\,\text{blend}}} = 2.821 \times 10^{-3} + 0.901 \times 10^{-3} = 3.722 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_{g,\,\text{blend}} = \frac{1}{3.722 \times 10^{-3}} = 268.7\,\text{K}$$

(1 mark) **Step 5 — Convert to °C:**

$$T_{g,\,\text{blend}} = 268.7 - 273.15 = -4.5\,^\circ\text{C}$$

(1.5 marks) **Step 6 — Suitability check:**

Use temperature = 25 °C.

$$\Delta T = 25 - (-4.5) = 29.5\,^\circ\text{C}$$

Since $\Delta T$ is at the *lower edge* of the recommended 30 °C – 50 °C window, the adhesive is **borderline-acceptable for room-temperature application**. For a *more compliant, higher-tack* PSA, one would *increase* the tackifier loading slightly (to ~40 phr) to push the T_g upward by another 5–10 °C. For a *firmer, higher-shear* tape, one would *decrease* the tackifier loading. This single calculation captures the design intuition behind hot-melt PSA formulation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** in the Fox equation: this is the most common error. Marks are lost *immediately*. (2 marks penalty in a 7-mark sub-question.)
> 2. **Confusing plasticizer with tackifier:** Plasticizers *lower* T_g, tackifiers *raise* it. Many students write the opposite.
> 3. **Ignoring mass-fraction arithmetic** and writing $T_{g,\text{blend}} = w_1 T_{g,1} + w_2 T_{g,2}$ — this is the **linear rule of mixtures** and is *wrong* for T_g; the Fox equation is **inverse-additive**, not linear.
> 4. **Failing to comment on the Dahlquist criterion** after the calculation. The numerical answer without a *physical interpretation* earns at most **4 out of 7 marks**.
> 5. **Not citing a source / saying "according to textbook"** — KTU 2024 scheme requires students to *derive* the formula on the answer sheet, not just invoke it.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Tackifying Agents (KTU Module 1)**
> - **Definition:** Low-MW, glassy, thermoplastic resin added to elastomers to improve **tack** (instant stickiness under light pressure).
> - **Key formula — Fox equation:** $\dfrac{1}{T_{g,b}} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ — always use **Kelvin**.
> - **Dahlquist criterion:** $J(1\,\text{s}) \geq 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{N}$.
> - **Main classes:** Natural (rosin, terpene) → Modified Natural (hydrogenated rosin, ester gum) → Synthetic Hydrocarbon (C5, C9, C5/C9, DCPD) → Specialty (terpene-phenolic, hydrogenated, AMS).
> - **Softening point range:** **60 – 150 °C**; typical **85 – 120 °C**.
> - **Molecular weight range:** **500 – 5000 g/mol**.
> - **Mechanism (3 pillars):** Compliance ↑, T_g shifts upward to damped region, surface energy matches substrate.
> - **Trade-off curve:** More tackifier ≠ more tack. Optimum is **50 – 120 phr** for typical PSA systems; beyond that, **cohesive failure** dominates.
> - **Selection rule:** Use the **Hansen 3D sphere** model — match δ values of tackifier and base polymer.
> - **Applications:** PSA tapes and labels, hot-melt adhesives, rubber compounding (green tack in tire building), chewing gum, hygiene adhesives, sealants.
> - **Contrast to remember:** Tackifier **raises T_g**; plasticizer **lowers T_g**. KTU loves this contrast question.
> - **Test methods to mention in viva:** Rolling ball tack (PSTC-6), 180° peel (PSTC-101), loop tack, shear holding power, DSC single-T_g miscibility check.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blowing Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Blowing Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: BLOWING AGENTS — FUNDAMENTALS & CLASSIFICATION

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **blowing agent (BA)** is a substance (solid, liquid, or gas) that is incorporated into a polymer melt or feedstock and subsequently generates a gas phase through physical volatilization, chemical decomposition, or reactive evolution, thereby creating a **cellular (foamed) morphology** within the solidified polymer matrix.

In the language of cellular polymer engineering, blowing agents are classified into two primary families based on the **mechanism of gas generation**:

| Family | Mechanism | Phase State Added |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Blowing Agents (PBAs)** | Phase change / Pressure drop / Volatilization | Liquid or Compressed Gas |
| **Chemical Blowing Agents (CBAs)** | Thermally-induced decomposition reaction | Solid (typically powder) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The performance of a CBA is universally quantified by its **Gas Yield (GY)**, measured in **cm³ (gas at NTP) per gram of agent**, where NTP = 273.15 K and 1 atm pressure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bread Maker

Imagine you are baking a **bread loaf** (this is your polymer foam):
- The **flour–dough matrix** corresponds to the molten polymer (e.g., polystyrene, PVC, PE).
- The **yeast** is the blowing agent — a hidden, invisible workhorse.
- When the dough is placed in a **hot oven** (the heated extruder/mold), the yeast ferments and releases **CO₂ bubbles**.
- These bubbles get **trapped** in the viscoelastic dough, expanding it into a soft, cellular, sponge-like loaf — the polymer foam.

Similarly, in an **extruder barrel** (180–220 °C), a CBA like **azodicarbonamide** decomposes and liberates gases (N₂, CO, CO₂) that nucleate and grow cells within the molten polymer. The bubble walls then freeze as the foam exits the die.

## 1.3 Engineering Relevance

Polymer foams are everywhere — from **EPS packaging** (thermocol) and **PU shoe soles** to **PVC pipe insulation**, **XLPE cables**, and **EPP automotive bumpers**. Without blowing agents, the entire cellular plastics industry (worth multi-billion USD globally) ceases to exist. For a KTU engineer, mastery of BA selection directly determines **cell density, expansion ratio, insulation value, and mechanical strength** of the final product.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification Hierarchy (The "Why" Behind Each Type)

### A. Physical Blowing Agents (PBAs)
PBAs do not undergo any chemical reaction. They exploit a **physical phase change** from liquid → gas (or a sudden pressure drop).

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. PBA is introduced into the melt under high pressure → it dissolves in the polymer.
2. As the melt exits the die (or mold cavity), the pressure drops sharply → **supersaturation** occurs.
3. The dissolved gas comes out of solution and forms bubbles (nucleation + growth).
4. The polymer solidifies, locking the cellular structure in place.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic Examples of PBAs:**
> - **Aliphatic hydrocarbons**: Pentane (C₅H₁₂), Butane (C₄H₁₀), Hexane (C₆H₁₄) — used in PS/EPS foam.
> - **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)**: CFC-11 (CCl₃F), CFC-12 (CCl₂F₂) — phased out under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** due to ozone depletion potential (ODP).
> - **Hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs)**: HCFC-141b — transitional replacements.
> - **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)**: HFC-134a, HFC-245fa — zero ODP, used in PU foams.
> - **Hydrocarbons + CO₂ blends**: Modern eco-friendly formulations.
> - **Inert gases**: CO₂ (supercritical), N₂ — used in MuCell® microcellular injection molding.

### B. Chemical Blowing Agents (CBAs)
CBAs are thermally labile solids that **irreversibly decompose** at a characteristic temperature to release a large volume of non-corrosive, non-toxic gas.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Solid CBA powder (particle size 1–15 µm) is dry-blended or compounded with polymer granules.
2. Upon heating past its **decomposition temperature (T_d)**, the CBA undergoes a specific chemical bond cleavage.
3. Gases (typically N₂, CO₂, CO, NH₃, H₂O) are evolved.
4. The gas dissolves into the polymer melt, expands, and creates cells.

**Major CBA Chemistries:**

| CBA Compound | Chemical Formula | T_d (°C) | Gas Yield (cm³/g) | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Azodicarbonamide (ADC/ADCA)** | C₂H₄N₄O₂ | 195–215 | **220** | Exothermic |
| **Sodium Bicarbonate** | NaHCO₃ | 100–140 | **125** | Endothermic |
| **4,4′-Oxybis(benzenesulfonyl hydrazide) (OBSH)** | C₁₂H₁₄N₄O₅S₂ | 140–160 | **125** | Exothermic |
| **p-Toluenesulfonyl semicarbazide (TSSC)** | C₈H₁₁N₃O₃S | 213–225 | **145** | Exothermic |
| **5-Phenyltetrazole (5-PT)** | C₇H₆N₄ | 240–250 | **200** | Exothermic |
| **Citric acid + Sodium bicarbonate** (endo blend) | — | 160–210 | Variable | Endothermic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Activation:** Most CBAs require **activators** (ZnO, Zn stearate, urea derivatives, glycerin) to lower T_d and narrow the decomposition window, enabling matched processing with the polymer's melt temperature.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All key quantitative relations tested in KTU ESE are tabulated below. **Memorize the bolded terms and units.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gas Yield (GY)** | $GY = \dfrac{V_{gas} \;(\text{at NTP})}{m_{CBA}}$ | cm³/g | Quality control metric |
| 2 | **Molar Gas Volume at NTP** | $V_m = 22.414$ | L/mol | Universal constant |
| 3 | **Gas Yield (from stoichiometry)** | $GY = \dfrac{n_{gas} \cdot V_m}{M_{CBA} \cdot 10^3}$ | cm³/g | $n_{gas}$ = moles of gas per mole CBA |
| 4 | **Expansion Ratio (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{\rho_{solid}}{\rho_{foam}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 2 to 50 |
| 5 | **Cell Density (N₀)** | $N_0 = \dfrac{6}{\pi d^3} \cdot (ER - 1)$ | cells/cm³ | $d$ = average cell diameter |
| 6 | **Foam Density** | $\rho_{foam} = \dfrac{\rho_{polymer}}{1 + \alpha}$ | g/cm³ | $\alpha$ = void fraction |
| 7 | **Ideal Gas (Cell Pressure)** | $P V = n R T$ | Pa, m³, mol, K | Drives bubble growth |
| 8 | **Nucleation (Classical)** | $\Delta G^* = \dfrac{16 \pi \gamma^3}{3 \Delta P^2}$ | J | Heterogeneous nucleation |
| 9 | **Decomposition Kinetics (1st order)** | $\ln \dfrac{C_0}{C} = k \cdot t$ | s⁻¹ | Arrhenius: $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ |
| 10 | **Blowing Agent Loading (%)** | $\%\;BA = \dfrac{m_{BA}}{m_{BA}+m_{polymer}} \times 100$ | wt% | Typically 0.1 – 5 wt% |

> [!TIP]
> For table readability, absolute values use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert \Delta G^* \vert$) — never use `|` inside table rows.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PBAs dominate in continuous extrusion** (XPS board production using HCFC/HFC or CO₂).
- **CBAs dominate in injection molding and rotational molding** (uniform cell distribution).
- **Endothermic CBAs (NaHCO₃)** are preferred in **PVC foam core pipes** because they absorb heat → better cooling cycles, no yellowing.
- **Exothermic CBAs (ADCA)** give **higher gas yield** but require careful thermal control.
- **Supercritical CO₂ (sc-CO₂)** is the modern green-processing workhorse for **microcellular foams** (cell size < 10 µm).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL WORKED PROBLEMS

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Gas Yield from Stoichiometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Problem 1 (Standard 7-mark KTU problem):**
> Calculate the **theoretical gas yield** of azodicarbonamide (ADC, M = 116.08 g/mol) which decomposes as:
>
> $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{N}_4\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{N}_2 \;+\; 2\,\text{CO} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;+\; \text{HCN}$$
>
> *(Note: Actual gaseous mixture also includes NH₃ depending on activator; use the simplified scheme above.)*

### Solution — Every Step Written Explicitly

**Step 1: Identify moles of gas produced per mole of ADC.**

From the balanced equation, **1 mole** of ADC produces:
- 1 mole N₂
- 2 moles CO
- 1 mole H₂O
- 1 mole HCN

Total moles of gas: $n_{gas} = 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 5$ mol.

**Step 2: Convert moles of gas to volume at NTP.**

Using the molar volume at NTP:

$$V_{gas} = n_{gas} \times V_m = 5 \times 22.414 = 112.07 \; \text{L}$$

**Step 3: Express volume per gram of ADC.**

$$V_{gas}\; \text{per gram} = \dfrac{112.07}{116.08} = 0.9655 \; \text{L/g}$$

**Step 4: Convert to standard cm³/g.**

$$\boxed{GY_{ADC} = 0.9655 \times 1000 = 965.5 \; \text{cm}^3\text{/g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** The textbook *experimental* value is ~220 cm³/g (not 965 cm³/g). The discrepancy arises because **the simplified equation above is not stoichiometrically balanced in carbon/hydrogen** — it is a teaching simplification. Real ADC decomposition gives a complex mixture (~220 cm³/g of mixed gases). **KTU examiners prefer this simplified assumption** in 7-mark derivations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2: Foam Expansion Ratio from Blowing Agent Loading

> **Problem:** A PVC sheet is foamed using 3.0 wt% sodium bicarbonate (GY = 125 cm³/g). The solid PVC density is 1.40 g/cm³. Assume the evolved CO₂ is retained fully within the cells at NTP. Estimate the **expansion ratio** of the foam.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Mass of blowing agent per 100 g polymer.**

Take a basis of 100 g of PVC.
- Mass of BA = 3.0 g (since 3 wt% of 100 g).

**Step 2: Volume of gas evolved at NTP.**

$$V_{gas} = 3.0 \;\text{g} \times 125 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{g} = 375 \;\text{cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Volume of solid PVC at its bulk density.**

$$V_{PVC} = \dfrac{100}{1.40} = 71.43 \;\text{cm}^3$$

**Step 4: Total volume of foam (gas + solid).**

$$V_{foam} = V_{gas} + V_{PVC} = 375 + 71.43 = 446.43 \;\text{cm}^3$$

**Step 5: Foam density.**

$$\rho_{foam} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{V_{foam}} = \dfrac{103}{446.43} = 0.2307 \;\text{g/cm}^3$$

**Step 6: Expansion Ratio.**

$$\boxed{ER = \dfrac{\rho_{solid}}{\rho_{foam}} = \dfrac{1.40}{0.2307} \approx 6.07}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Notes on Nucleation Theory

Cell nucleation follows the **Classical Nucleation Theory (CNT)**. The critical free energy barrier is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{*} &= \frac{16 \pi \gamma_{pl}^{3}}{3 \Delta P^{2}} \quad \text{(Homogeneous)} \\[6pt]
\Delta G^{*}_{het} &= \Delta G^{*} \cdot f(\theta) \quad \text{(Heterogeneous, } f(\theta) \ll 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\gamma_{pl}$ is the polymer-gas interfacial tension and $\Delta P$ is the pressure differential driving nucleation. In **practical foam processing**, heterogeneous nucleation dominates (lower barrier), facilitated by nucleating agents like **citric acid/sodium bicarbonate residues or talc particles**.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — PBA vs CBA

| Parameter | Physical Blowing Agent (PBA) | Chemical Blowing Agent (CBA) |
|---|---|---|
| **State at addition** | Liquid / Gas | Solid powder |
| **Storage & handling** | Requires pressure vessel | Easy (ambient) |
| **Thermal control** | Less critical | Must match polymer T_m |
| **Gas yield** | Variable | High & predictable |
| **Residue left in polymer** | None | Yes (solid residue) |
| **Cost** | Higher (refrigeration needed) | Lower |
| **Environmental concerns** | VOC emissions, GWP | Depends on chemistry |
| **KTU-typical application** | XPS boards, PU spray foam | Injection molded PS, PVC foam |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Foam Production Line

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define blowing agents. Differentiate between physical and chemical blowing agents with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A blowing agent is a substance incorporated into a polymer melt that produces a cellular structure by evolving gas during processing.

- **Physical Blowing Agent (PBA):** Generates gas by a *physical* phase change (volatilization or pressure release), with no chemical reaction. Example: **Pentane** used in expanded polystyrene (EPS).
- **Chemical Blowing Agent (CBA):** Generates gas by *thermally-induced chemical decomposition* of a solid additive. Example: **Azodicarbonamide (ADC)** used in PVC and PE foams.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark each differentiation = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is "gas yield" of a chemical blowing agent? Mention its SI-related unit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Gas Yield (GY)** is the volume of gas (measured at NTP — 0 °C and 1 atm) liberated per unit mass of chemical blowing agent upon complete decomposition. It is a critical quality control parameter for CBAs because it directly determines the **expansion ratio** of the resulting polymer foam.

- **Unit:** **cm³ (NTP) per gram**, often written as **cm³/g** or **mL/g**.
- Higher gas yield → greater potential expansion.
- Example: Azodicarbonamide ≈ 220 cm³/g; Sodium bicarbonate ≈ 125 cm³/g.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Classify the various types of blowing agents used in polymer processing with suitable examples. Explain in detail the mechanism of chemical blowing agents with reference to azodicarbonamide. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PVC compound is to be foamed using **sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃)** as the blowing agent at a loading of **2.5 wt%**. The solid PVC density is **1.38 g/cm³** and the gas yield of NaHCO₃ is **125 cm³/g**. Calculate:
   (i) The volume of CO₂ evolved per 100 g of PVC compound.
   (ii) The expansion ratio (ER) of the resulting foam.
   (iii) The foam density. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Classification and Mechanism (7 marks)

**Classification (write with neat headings — 4 marks):**

**1. Physical Blowing Agents (PBA):**
- Aliphatic hydrocarbons: pentane, butane, hexane (used in EPS).
- Halocarbons: HFC-134a, HFC-245fa (used in PU foams).
- Inert gases: CO₂, N₂ (used in microcellular foaming via MuCell® process).
- Supercritical fluids: sc-CO₂ (green solvent for polymer foaming).

**2. Chemical Blowing Agents (CBA):**
- **Exothermic CBAs:** Azodicarbonamide (ADC, T_d ≈ 200 °C, GY ≈ 220 cm³/g); OBSH (T_d ≈ 150 °C, GY ≈ 125 cm³/g); TSSC; 5-Phenyltetrazole.
- **Endothermic CBAs:** Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃, T_d ≈ 120 °C, GY ≈ 125 cm³/g); citric acid + sodium bicarbonate blends.

**Mechanism of Azodicarbonamide (3 marks):**

The decomposition is a thermally-activated unimolecular reaction:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{N}_4\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta \;(195{-}215\,°C)} \text{N}_2 + \text{CO} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{NH}_3 + \text{HCN}$$

Mechanism steps:
1. **Heat absorption:** As polymer melt temperature crosses 195 °C, ADC molecules absorb thermal energy.
2. **Bond cleavage:** The N=N azo bond and adjacent C–N bonds undergo homolytic cleavage.
3. **Gas evolution:** A mixture of gases (predominantly N₂ and CO) is released into the melt.
4. **Dissolution and supersaturation:** Gases dissolve in the melt under barrel pressure.
5. **Nucleation and growth:** On exiting the die, pressure drop causes gas supersaturation → cell nucleation → bubble growth.
6. **Solidification:** Polymer cools, vitrifies, and locks in the cellular morphology.

Activators (ZnO, Zn stearate, urea) lower T_d to match the polymer's processing window.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- BA loading = 2.5 wt%
- $\rho_{PVC} = 1.38$ g/cm³
- GY = 125 cm³/g
- Basis: 100 g of PVC compound

**Step 1 — Mass of NaHCO₃ in 100 g compound:**
$$m_{NaHCO_3} = \dfrac{2.5}{100} \times 100 = 2.5 \;\text{g}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Volume of CO₂ evolved:**
$$V_{CO_2} = 2.5 \;\text{g} \times 125 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{g} = \boxed{312.5 \;\text{cm}^3}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of PVC in 100 g compound:**
$$m_{PVC} = 100 - 2.5 = 97.5 \;\text{g}$$

**Step 4 — Volume of solid PVC:**
$$V_{PVC} = \dfrac{97.5}{1.38} = 70.65 \;\text{cm}^3$$

**Step 5 — (ii) Expansion Ratio:**
$$ER = \dfrac{V_{foam}}{V_{solid\;PVC}} = \dfrac{V_{CO_2} + V_{PVC}}{V_{PVC}} = \dfrac{312.5 + 70.65}{70.65}$$

$$\boxed{ER \approx 5.42}$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Foam Density:**
$$\rho_{foam} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{V_{foam}} = \dfrac{100}{312.5 + 70.65} = \dfrac{100}{383.15}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{foam} \approx 0.261 \;\text{g/cm}^3}$$

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the criteria for the selection of a blowing agent for polymer foam processing. Explain why **azodicarbonamide** is the most widely used CBA despite the availability of many alternatives. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on:
   (i) Endothermic vs Exothermic blowing agents. **(3.5 marks)**
   (ii) Microcellular foams and supercritical CO₂. **(3.5 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Selection Criteria (7 marks)

**Key Selection Criteria (write any 5 clearly):**

1. **Decomposition Temperature (T_d):** Must match the polymer's processing window. E.g., for LDPE (~110–130 °C melt), low-T_d CBAs like OBSH are preferred. For rigid PVC (~180–200 °C), ADC is ideal.

2. **High Gas Yield (GY):** Greater volume of gas per gram → higher expansion ratio with less additive.

3. **Non-toxic and non-corrosive gases:** Gases evolved (N₂, CO₂) must not degrade the polymer or harm workers.

4. **Good dispersibility:** Fine particle size (1–15 µm) ensures uniform cell distribution.

5. **Storage stability:** Should not decompose prematurely during compounding or storage.

6. **Cost-effectiveness and regulatory compliance:** Must comply with REACH, Montreal Protocol, FDA (for food-contact foams).

**Why Azodicarbonamide dominates (write 3 reasons):**
- **Very high gas yield** (~220 cm³/g) — among the highest of all CBAs.
- **Tunable T_d** (195–215 °C) — broad processing window suitable for PE, PP, PVC, EVA, and rubber.
- **Cost-effective** — produced in bulk (>50,000 tons/year globally), making it 5–10× cheaper than alternatives like 5-PT.
- **Fine, dry powder** — easy to handle, dry-blend, and disperse.
- **Compatible with activators** — ZnO and urea derivatives bring T_d down to ~150 °C.

---

### Part (b) — Short Notes

**(i) Endothermic vs Exothermic Blowing Agents (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | Endothermic CBA | Exothermic CBA |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat flow** | Absorbs heat during decomposition | Releases heat during decomposition |
| **T_d onset** | Often lower (100–160 °C) | Moderate to high (140–250 °C) |
| **Examples** | NaHCO₃, citric acid/bicarbonate | ADC, OBSH, TSSC, 5-PT |
| **Residue** | Carbonates (inert) | Sometimes discoloring (yellow tint) |
| **Cooling cycle** | Shorter (faster cooling) | Longer |
| **Typical use** | Food-grade PVC foam, foam core pipes | Structural foam, injection molding |

Endothermic agents give finer cell structure and are FDA-compliant for food packaging.

**(ii) Microcellular Foams and Supercritical CO₂ (3.5 marks):**

**Microcellular foams** have cell sizes < 10 µm and cell densities > 10⁹ cells/cm³. They offer:
- Superior **strength-to-weight ratio**.
- High **toughness** (small cracks cannot propagate).
- Reduced material usage (15–30% weight savings).

**Supercritical CO₂ (sc-CO₂):** Above its critical point (T_c = 31.1 °C, P_c = 7.38 MPa), CO₂ behaves as a **single-phase supercritical fluid** with both liquid-like solvating power and gas-like diffusivity. Process:
1. Polymer is saturated with sc-CO₂ in a pressure vessel.
2. Rapid pressure drop (or temperature jump) creates **thermodynamic instability**.
3. Cell nucleation occurs uniformly → millions of microcells form.
4. Used commercially by **Trexel (MuCell®)** in injection molding and **Tecnoplismar** in extrusion.

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting NTP conditions:** Always state that gas volume is at **NTP (273.15 K, 1 atm)** when computing gas yield. Marks deducted if STP is used loosely.
> 2. **Mass basis confusion:** When asked for "gas per gram of compound," students often take the BA mass as the *total* mass. Use **mass of BA only** in the denominator.
> 3. **Activation in ADC mechanism:** Many students write "ADC decomposes to give N₂ only." Wrong — it gives a **mixture** of gases.
> 4. **Mixing up T_d and T_m:** T_d of CBA must be **less than or near** the polymer's processing temperature, not its melting point.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Even if final number is correct, **missing cm³/g or g/cm³** units loses 0.5–1 mark per sub-part.
> 6. **Confusing expansion ratio formulas:** Some textbooks use $V_{foam}/V_{solid}$ and others $\rho_{solid}/\rho_{foam}$. Both are equivalent — but **state the formula** explicitly to earn full marks.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — High-Density Summary:**

- ✅ **Blowing agent** = substance that creates cellular structure in polymers via gas evolution.
- ✅ **PBA** = physical phase change; **CBA** = chemical decomposition.
- ✅ **Gas Yield (GY)** = volume of gas at NTP per gram of agent (cm³/g) — **core KTU metric**.
- ✅ **Azodicarbonamide (ADC)**: T_d ≈ 200 °C, GY ≈ 220 cm³/g, exothermic — **most widely used CBA**.
- ✅ **Sodium bicarbonate**: endothermic, T_d ≈ 120 °C, GY ≈ 125 cm³/g — preferred in **PVC foam pipes** (food-grade).
- ✅ **OBSH**: T_d ≈ 150 °C, GY ≈ 125 cm³/g — used where lower processing temperatures are needed.
- ✅ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** phased out CFCs → modern foams use **HCFCs, HFCs, hydrocarbons, sc-CO₂**.
- ✅ **Expansion Ratio (ER)** = $\rho_{solid} / \rho_{foam}$ — typically 2 to 50 in commercial foams.
- ✅ **Nucleation** follows classical theory: $\Delta G^* = \dfrac{16\pi\gamma^3}{3\Delta P^2}$; reduced by **heterogeneous nucleating agents** (talc).
- ✅ **Supercritical CO₂ (sc-CO₂)** is the **green, modern** blowing route — used in **microcellular injection molding (MuCell®)**.
- ✅ **Activators** (ZnO, Zn stearate) lower ADC's T_d to match polymer processing window.
- ✅ **Endothermic CBAs** give better surface finish + shorter cooling cycles.
- ✅ Always specify **NTP conditions** and **units** in numerical answers.
- ✅ Foam production flow: **Polymer + BA → Melt → Decomposition/Volatilization → Pressure Drop → Nucleation → Growth → Solidification → Foam.**
- ✅ Key applications: **EPS cups/packaging, PU shoe soles, PVC pipes, XLPE cable insulation, EPP automotive parts, PE foam sheets.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bonding Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bonding Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bonding Agents (Adhesives)**: In the context of polymer processing, a bonding agent (or *adhesive*) is a non-metallic, polymeric-based substance capable of joining two substrates (adherends) together through surface attachment (adhesion) and internal strength (cohesion). Bonding agents are indispensable in polymer composites, laminates, rubber compounding, textile finishing, and structural assembly of plastics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: A bonding agent is broadly classified as a *substance applied to the surfaces of materials to create a stable, durable, and load-bearing joint* without altering the bulk chemistry of the adherends. In polymer processing, bonding agents include **adhesives**, **coupling agents**, and **primers** used to bridge the chemical incompatibility between dissimilar polymer matrices and reinforcements (e.g., glass fibre, carbon fibre, natural fibre with polymer resins).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to stick two pieces of paper together using ordinary water — it doesn't work, the water evaporates. But if you use **fevicol (PVA-based glue)**, the two pieces become inseparable.

Why?
- Water has **low viscosity** and **zero internal strength** when dry.
- Fevicol contains long **polyvinyl acetate chains** that, after drying, mechanically interlock into the paper fibres and develop strong **cohesive strength** internally.

This is exactly what a **bonding agent does in polymer processing**:
1. **Wets** the substrate (spreads over the surface like water on a clean glass).
2. **Penetrates** surface irregularities (like glue soaking into paper).
3. **Solidifies** to form a load-bearing joint (like glue hardening).

## 1.3 Why Bonding Agents Are Critical in Polymer Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics** (must be remembered for KTU exams):
> - **Lap Shear Strength (LSS)**: typically **1–50 MPa** for structural polymer adhesives.
> - **Peel Strength**: measured in **N/mm** (or **N/25 mm** width).
> - **Service Temperature Range**: $-60^\circ \text{C}$ to $+300^\circ \text{C}$ for high-performance aerospace epoxy-phenolic systems.
> - **Standard Test Methods**: **ASTM D1002** (lap shear), **ASTM D1876** (T-peel), **ASTM D3163** (plastic-plastic adhesion), **ISO 4587**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Five Fundamental Theories of Adhesion

> [!NOTE]
> Adhesion is a **surface phenomenon**; cohesion is a **bulk property**. For a strong bond, **both adhesion AND cohesion must be high**.

### (A) Mechanical Interlocking / Mechanical Theory
- The bonding agent flows into the microscopic pores, crevices, and irregularities of the substrate surface.
- Upon solidification, it forms a **physical anchor** (like roots of a tree into soil).
- **Most effective on** rough, porous substrates (wood, concrete, etched metals, textiles).
- *Limitation*: Fails on perfectly smooth, non-porous polymers like PTFE unless pre-treated.

### (B) Adsorption (Thermodynamic) Theory
- Driven by **intermolecular forces** (van der Waals, dipole–dipole, hydrogen bonding).
- Requires **intimate molecular contact** — hence the substrate must be **wetted** by the adhesive.
- Quantified by **Young's Equation** (derived in Section 3).
- The most widely accepted theory for polymer–polymer and polymer–metal adhesion.

### (C) Chemical Bonding Theory
- Formation of **primary covalent, ionic, or metallo-organic bonds** across the interface.
- Example: **Silane coupling agents** (e.g., γ-aminopropyltriethoxysilane) react with both glass (Si–O–Si) and epoxy resin (–NH–epoxy ring opening).
- Provides the **strongest, most durable** joint — often **10–100× stronger** than van der Waals adhesion alone.

### (D) Diffusion Theory (Voyutskii Theory)
- Proposed for **polymer–polymer adhesion**.
- Polymer chain ends from the adhesive **diffuse across the interface** into the substrate.
- Requires **mutual solubility** and chain mobility above the **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)**.
- Quantified by **reptation time** and interdiffusion depth ($\propto \sqrt{Dt}$, where $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $t$ = contact time).

### (E) Electrostatic Theory (Derjaguin Theory)
- The adhesive and substrate have differing **electron work functions**, generating a **double electrical layer** at the interface.
- Attractive Coulombic forces develop, similar to a parallel-plate capacitor.
- Important in **metal–polymer** contacts.

## 2.2 Classification of Bonding Agents (KTU High-Yield Topic)

| Class | Sub-class | Examples | Typical Applications |
|-------|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| **Natural** | Animal | Casein, hide glue | Woodworking, paper lamination |
| | Vegetable | Starch, dextrin, natural rubber latex | Packaging, labelling |
| **Synthetic – Thermoplastic** | Polyvinyl acetate (PVA) | Fevicol-type white glue | Wood, paper, porous substrates |
| | Polyvinyl alcohol (PVOH) | Water-soluble films | Paper, textile sizing |
| | Polyamides (Nylon grades) | Hot-melt adhesives | Packaging, shoe industry |
| | Ethylene-vinyl acetate (EVA) | Hot-melt, flexible bonds | Book binding, packaging |
| **Synthetic – Thermosetting** | Epoxy | Araldite, structural bonding | Aerospace, composites, automotive |
| | Phenol-formaldehyde (PF) | Bakelite-stage resols | Plywood, brake linings |
| | Urea-formaldehyde (UF) | Wood adhesives | Particleboard, MDF |
| | Polyurethane (PU) | Reactive hot-melt, two-part | Footwear, automotive panels |
| | Cyanoacrylates | Super glue (methyl/ethyl) | Instant bonding of metals, plastics |
| | Acrylics (anaerobic, UV-curable) | Loctite-type | Thread-locking, medical devices |
| **Synthetic – Elastomeric** | Natural rubber solutions | Neoprene contact adhesives | Footwear, laminates |
| | Polychloroprene (CR) | Contact adhesives | Foam-to-fabric bonding |
| | Silicone (RTV) | Room-temp vulcanizing | Sealants, gaskets, electronics |
| **Coupling Agents** | Silanes | γ-MPS, γ-APS | Glass fibre / epoxy composites |
| | Titanates | Neoalkoxy titanates | CaCO₃ filled PP, carbon black |
| | Zirconates | Zirconium aluminates | Specialty composites |

## 2.3 Surface Preparation — The "Make-or-Break" Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 80/20 rule of adhesion**: Approximately **80% of adhesive joint failures** are attributable to **inadequate surface preparation**, not to the adhesive itself.

Standard surface preparation methods for polymer substrates:
1. **Solvent wiping** (IPA, acetone, MEK) – removes oils, mould release agents.
2. **Abrasion** (sandblasting, Scotch-Brite) – increases surface area, mechanical interlocking.
3. **Chemical etching** (e.g., chromic acid for polyolefins, Na–naphthalenide for PTFE).
4. **Corona / Plasma treatment** – raises surface energy ($\gamma_s$) above the adhesive's surface tension.
5. **Priming** – applying a thin coupling-agent layer (e.g., silane on glass).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE**: Vertical bars in mathematical expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the pipe character), to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax.

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Meaning of Terms | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Young's Equation** | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | $\gamma_{sv}$ = solid–vapour surface energy, $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid–liquid interfacial energy, $\gamma_{lv}$ = liquid–vapour surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle | Predicts **wettability**. For good adhesion, $\theta \rightarrow 0$, i.e., $\gamma_{sv} > \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}$ |
| **Young–Dupré Equation** | $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | $W_a$ = work of adhesion (energy per unit area, J/m²) | Quantifies **thermodynamic work** of separating adhesive from substrate |
| **Dupré's Adhesion Energy** | $W_a = \gamma_s + \gamma_l - \gamma_{sl}$ | $\gamma_s, \gamma_l$ = surface energies of solid and liquid | Fundamental definition of work of adhesion |
| **Fowkes Equation** (for polar/dispersive components) | $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_s + \gamma_l - 2\sqrt{\gamma_s^d \gamma_l^d}$ | Superscript $d$ = dispersive (London) component | Used when only **dispersive forces** dominate (e.g., PE–PE) |
| **Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble (OWRK)** | $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_s + \gamma_l - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_s^d \gamma_l^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p \gamma_l^p}\right)$ | Superscript $p$ = polar component | Calculates **total surface energy** with polar/dispersive split |
| **Hansen Solubility Parameters** | $\delta_t^2 = \delta_d^2 + \delta_p^2 + \delta_h^2$ | $\delta_d$ = dispersion, $\delta_p$ = polar, $\delta_h$ = hydrogen bonding | Predicts **polymer–polymer miscibility** and diffusion-based adhesion |
| **Diffusion Depth** | $x(t) = \sqrt{Dt}$ | $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s), $t$ = contact time | Estimates **inter-diffusion depth** for Voyutskii theory |
| **Lap Shear Strength** | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{F}{L \cdot w}$ | $F$ = breaking load, $L$ = overlap length, $w$ = width | Standard strength metric for structural joints |
| **Peel Strength (180°)** | $P = \dfrac{F}{w}$ | Force per unit width (N/mm) | Measures flexible-laminate bond quality |
| **Critical Surface Tension (Zisman)** | $\cos\theta = 1 - b(\gamma_c - \gamma_l)$ | $\gamma_c$ = critical surface tension of solid | Empirical wettability index |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Wettability**: **"Low theta, go ahead-a!"** — Low contact angle = good wetting = good adhesion.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations form the **mathematical backbone of KTU 2024 Polymer Processing examinations**. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation of Young's Equation (Force Balance Method)

**Statement**: At the three-phase contact line of a liquid drop resting on a solid substrate, the vector sum of interfacial tensions must equal zero at equilibrium.

**Setup**: Consider a sessile drop on a flat, smooth, homogeneous, rigid, and non-deformable solid. Three interfaces meet at the contact line:
- **Solid–Vapour** interface with tension $\gamma_{sv}$ (acts along the solid surface, pulling outward from the drop).
- **Solid–Liquid** interface with tension $\gamma_{sl}$ (acts along the solid surface, pulling inward toward the drop).
- **Liquid–Vapour** interface with tension $\gamma_{lv}$ (acts along the liquid surface, pulling tangent to the drop at angle $\theta$ from the solid surface).

**Step 1** — Resolve $\gamma_{lv}$ into components at the contact line.

The liquid–vapour tension $\gamma_{lv}$ acts along the tangent to the drop profile, making angle $\theta$ with the solid surface. Its horizontal (parallel to the solid) component is $\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$, directed **outward** from the drop (away from the bulk solid).

**Step 2** — Apply mechanical equilibrium in the horizontal direction (the vertical components are balanced by the normal stress from the solid).

The two surface tensions along the solid ($\gamma_{sv}$ and $\gamma_{sl}$) act in opposite directions along the same line. The horizontal projection of $\gamma_{lv}$ must balance them. Taking the outward (toward vapour, away from drop) direction as positive:

$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

This is the **Young Equation** (1805, Thomas Young).

**Step 3** — Limiting cases for intuitive checking.

- If $\theta = 0^\circ$ (perfect wetting, drop spreads completely): $\cos\theta = 1$, so $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}$, i.e., $\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}$. This is the **spreading coefficient** condition: liquid spreads spontaneously.
- If $\theta = 180^\circ$ (complete non-wetting, drop is a sphere): $\cos\theta = -1$, so $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}$, requiring $\gamma_{sl} > \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv}$, which is **physically unrealizable** for typical solids. Thus perfect non-wetting is not achievable on flat homogeneous solids.
- If $\theta = 90^\circ$: $\cos\theta = 0$, so $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl}$ — purely horizontal force balance.

## 3.2 Derivation of Young–Dupré Equation

**Starting point** — Dupré's definition of the **thermodynamic work of adhesion** $W_a$ as the reversible free energy required to separate a unit area of the solid–liquid interface into two separate solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces:

$$W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$

> This is purely a thermodynamic definition, valid for any temperature and independent of contact angle.

**Step 1** — Substitute the Young equation $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ into the Dupré equation.

**Step 2** — Replace $\gamma_{sl}$ using Young's rearranged form: $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$.

\begin{aligned}
W_a &= \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl} \\
&= \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta) \\
&= \cancel{\gamma_{sv}} + \gamma_{lv} - \cancel{\gamma_{sv}} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta \\
&= \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)
\end{aligned}

This is the **Young–Dupré Equation** (combining Young 1805 + Dupré 1869).

**Step 3** — Limiting-case validation.

- $\theta = 0^\circ$ (complete wetting): $W_a = 2\gamma_{lv}$. This is the **cohesion work** of the liquid (separating it from itself), confirming the maximum possible work.
- $\theta = 90^\circ$: $W_a = \gamma_{lv}$.
- $\theta = 180^\circ$ (theoretical, never achieved): $W_a = 0$ — no thermodynamic adhesion.

## 3.3 Derivation of the OWRK (Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble) Equation

**Step 1** — Fowkes (1964) proposed that surface energy splits into components, and the **interfacial tension** between two phases (1 and 2) is determined by the **geometric mean of like-component interactions** across the interface.

For two non-polar phases interacting only through London dispersion forces:

$$\gamma_{12} = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - 2\sqrt{\gamma_1^d \gamma_2^d}$$

**Step 2** — Owens, Wendt, Rabel, and Kaelble (1969–1970) extended Fowkes by including a **polar component**, which they likewise approximated as a geometric mean. They assumed that **only same-type components interact geometrically** (i.e., dispersive with dispersive, polar with polar) and that cross-interactions are negligible.

\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sl} &= \gamma_s + \gamma_l - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_s^d \gamma_l^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p \gamma_l^p}\right)
\end{aligned}

**Step 3** — Combining OWRK with Young–Dupré yields the working form used to determine the polar and dispersive components of a solid's surface energy from contact angle measurements with two probe liquids of known $\gamma_l^d$ and $\gamma_l^p$:

$$\gamma_l(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_s^d \gamma_l^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p \gamma_l^p}\right)$$

By measuring $\theta$ for at least two probe liquids (commonly **water** and **diiodomethane**), one solves two linear equations for $\gamma_s^d$ and $\gamma_s^p$, then computes the total $\gamma_s = \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Trick**: A common 7-mark problem gives contact angles of two probe liquids on an unknown polymer and asks you to find $\gamma_s^d$ and $\gamma_s^p$. Memorize the standard values: Water ($\gamma_l = 72.8$ mN/m, $\gamma_l^d = 21.8$, $\gamma_l^p = 51.0$); Diiodomethane ($\gamma_l = 50.8$ mN/m, $\gamma_l^d = 50.8$, $\gamma_l^p = 0$).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem**: A droplet of epoxy adhesive (surface tension $\gamma_l = 45$ mN/m) is placed on an aluminium substrate whose critical surface tension is $\gamma_c = 50$ mN/m. The contact angle is measured as $\theta = 25^\circ$. Calculate:
1. The work of adhesion $W_a$.
2. Whether complete spreading will occur.
3. The solid–liquid interfacial tension $\gamma_{sl}$, given that $\gamma_{sv} \approx \gamma_c = 50$ mN/m.

**Solution**:

**Part 1** — Work of adhesion via Young–Dupré:

$$W_a = \gamma_l(1 + \cos\theta) = 45(1 + \cos 25^\circ) = 45(1 + 0.9063) = 45 \times 1.9063 = 85.78 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

**Part 2** — Spreading criterion. The spreading coefficient is:

$$S = \gamma_{sv} - (\gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}) = W_a - 2\gamma_l = 85.78 - 90 = -4.22 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

Since $S < 0$, **complete spreading will NOT occur**; the drop will form a finite contact angle. (The negative value is small, so wetting is still good, just not perfect.)

**Part 3** — Interfacial tension via Young's equation:

\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sl} &= \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta \\
&= 50 - 45 \times \cos 25^\circ \\
&= 50 - 45 \times 0.9063 \\
&= 50 - 40.78 \\
&= 9.22 \text{ mN/m}
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation**: A small $\gamma_{sl}$ value (9.22 mN/m) confirms **excellent interfacial compatibility** between epoxy and aluminium after proper surface preparation. This is why epoxy–aluminium joints are the workhorse of aerospace and automotive structural bonding.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Adhesion Theory | Dominant Force | Bond Strength Order | Substrate Type | Practical Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical | Physical anchoring | Moderate | Rough/porous | Fails on smooth polymers |
| Adsorption | Van der Waals / dipole / H-bond | Moderate–High | High-$\gamma_s$ polymers | Requires good wetting |
| Chemical | Covalent / ionic | Very high | Functionalised surfaces | Limited reactive sites |
| Diffusion | Chain interdiffusion | High (polymer–polymer) | Polymer–polymer | Needs $T > T_g$ |
| Electrostatic | Coulombic (capacitor) | Moderate | Metal–polymer | Dominant only in specific cases |

## 4.4 Silane Coupling Agent Mechanism (Glass–Epoxy Interface)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GL[Glass SiO2 Surface] -->|Hydrolysis Condensation| SI[Silane Layer Si O Si]
    SI -->|Hydrolysis| SH[Si OH Groups]
    SH -->|Epoxy Ring Opening| EP[Crosslinked Epoxy Matrix]
    note1[Chemical bond across interface] -.-> SH
    note2[Coupling agent bridges two dissimilar materials] -.-> SI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure interpretation**: The silane molecule (e.g., γ-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane, γ-GPS) has **two functional ends**:
> - The **inorganic-reactive end** (Si–OCH₃) hydrolyses and condenses with –OH groups on the glass surface, forming stable **Si–O–Si** siloxane bonds.
> - The **organic-reactive end** (epoxy group) reacts with the epoxy resin during cure, becoming covalently incorporated into the crosslinked network.
>
> The result is a **molecular bridge** between the inorganic fibre and the organic matrix — converting a weak interface into a strong, moisture-resistant chemical junction.
