**(b) Internal Mixer vs. Open Mill (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Two-Roll Mill | Internal Mixer (Banbury) |
|--------|---------------|---------------------------|
| Batch size | Small (lab scale) | Large (industrial) |
| Dust/filler loss | High (open) | Low (enclosed) |
| Operator safety | Lower | Higher (enclosed) |
| Temperature control | Moderate | Excellent (water-cooled jacket) |
| Shear intensity | Moderate | **Very high** (intermeshing rotors) |
| Cycle time | Long (15–30 min) | Short (4–8 min) |
| Capital cost | Low | High |

(4 marks) **Limitations of internal mixer:** high capital cost, difficulty in observing the batch, requires precise recipe programming, less flexibility for small custom batches. (3 marks)

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Mastication Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the 100 °C threshold:** Many students write "mastication happens at high temperature" — *wrong*. The entire efficiency argument hinges on operating **below 100 °C** for cold mastication. Examiners dock 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Confusing "plasticizer" with "peptizer":** Plasticizers are **non-reactive extenders** (e.g., DOP); peptizers are **reactive radical transfer agents**. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Skipping the role of O₂:** Any mastication mechanism answer without explicit mention of **oxygen capturing macroradicals** is incomplete. KTU key papers allocate **1 dedicated mark** for this.
> 4. **Sign error in the $1/M_n$ equation:** It is **$1/M_n - 1/M_{n,0} = k t$**, *not* the reverse. Mixing up the sign yields a *negative* molecular weight and full mark loss.
> 5. **Units of $k$:** Students often forget that $k$ has units of **mol g⁻¹ min⁻¹**. Examiners specifically check unit consistency.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Mastication**

- ☐ Mastication = **mechanical + chemical breakdown** of high-MW rubber to reduce viscosity.
- ☐ Critical threshold temperature = **100 °C** (cold < 100, hot > 100).
- ☐ Cold mastication = **oxidative (O₂-driven)** chain scission; **high efficiency**.
- ☐ Hot mastication = radicals **recombine** → little permanent effect.
- ☐ **Peptizers** (thiophenols, Zn-dithiocarbamates) act as **radical transfer agents**; dosage 0.1–0.5 phr.
- ☐ Key equation: $\dfrac{1}{M_n(t)} = \dfrac{1}{M_{n,0}} + k\,t$ → **linear plot** confirms random scission (first-order kinetics).
- ☐ Shear stress in mill nip: $\tau = \eta \cdot (v_2 - v_1) / h$.
- ☐ Friction ratio $F_R = 1.1$–$1.25$ for breakdown mills.
- ☐ **Equipment:** Open two-roll mill (lab/small batch) vs. **Banbury internal mixer** (industrial, enclosed, high shear).
- ☐ **Plasticity Retention Index (PRI)** quantifies NR's resistance to over-mastication during storage.
- ☐ Avoid using natural rubber **above 100 °C** in the absence of peptizers — you waste energy without reducing molecular weight.
- ☐ Always quote **molecular weight units in g/mol** and **time in minutes** for KTU numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two-Roll Mill Mixing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Two-Roll Mill Mixing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Roll Mill Mixing** (also referred to as **Open Mill Mixing**, **Two-Roll Mixing Mill**, or **Differential Roll Mill**) is a **batch-type, primary compounding unit operation** in polymer processing in which a polymer mass is repeatedly passed, sheared, folded, and re-fed through the **narrow nip region** between two horizontally mounted, counter-rotating rolls maintained at a closely controlled temperature gradient. The differential surface speeds of the rolls generate a **high shear field** within the nip, which performs **dispersive mixing** (breakup of agglomerates and incorporation of fine particulates into the polymer matrix) and **distributive mixing** (spatial homogenization of all formulation ingredients to achieve compositional uniformity).

The two-roll mill is classified as a **low-shear–high-throughput batch mixer** and serves as the oldest, simplest, and most pedagogically important polymer compounding device. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, it is the foundational equipment upon which the concepts of **friction ratio, nip gap, shear rate, bank formation, and mastication** are introduced.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Laundry Wringer Analogy**
> Imagine you are washing a heavy blanket by hand and then feeding it between the **two rotating wooden rollers of an old-fashioned wringer** (the manual mangle used before washing machines). The front roller spins slightly *faster* than the back roller, and the gap between them is just slightly smaller than the blanket's thickness. As the blanket passes through, the rollers **squeeze out the water (analogous to forcing fillers into polymer)**, **stretch and tear at the mass (analogous to mastication and chain scission of polymer)**, and the small reservoir of material that sits on top of the rollers (the *bank*) is continuously scraped, folded, and re-fed by hand to ensure every portion of the blanket gets squeezed evenly.
>
> In a two-roll polymer mill, the **blanket is your polymer compound**, the **rollers are the heated/cooled steel rolls**, the **squeezing action is the shear stress in the nip**, and the **operator's hand-fold action is the manual cutting and re-feeding of the polymer sheet from the operators**. The mill performs *mechanical shearing* and *thermal conditioning* simultaneously — and that combined thermo-mechanical action is what makes it such an effective (though labor-intensive) compounding device.

## 1.3 Principal Applications in Industry

| Application Domain | Typical Use of Two-Roll Mill |
|---|---|
| **Rubber Compounding** | Mastication of natural rubber (NR), addition of curatives, accelerators, fillers (carbon black, silica), antioxidants, plasticizers — *the workhorse of the rubber industry* |
| **PVC Compounding** | Plastisol/dry-blend preparation, plasticizer absorption, stabilization |
| **Masterbatch Manufacture** | Pre-mixing of high-pigment/high-filler concentrates into polymer carrier |
| **Thermoplastic Blends** | Laboratory-scale blending of polymer alloys, reactive compatibilization |
| **Reclaim Rubber** | Devulcanization and reprocessing of waste rubber |
| **Adhesives & Hot-Melt** | Heating and homogenization of tackifier–polymer blends |

## 1.4 Key Operating Terminology — Quick-Reference

- **Nip** — The narrowest gap between the two rolls, typically **0.5 mm to 5 mm** in production mills; the *seat of all the shear*.
- **Bank** — The rolling reserve mass of material that sits between the rolls, *upstream of the nip*, behaving as a temporary reservoir.
- **Friction Ratio (FR)** — Ratio of linear surface speeds of the *fast* (front) roll to the *slow* (back) roll. **1.1 to 1.4** for rubber; **1.0 to 1.1** for plastics.
- **Mastication** — Preliminary breakdown of high-molecular-weight polymer (especially NR) under shear to reduce Mooney viscosity and improve processability.
- **Sheeting** — Final flat sheet product discharged from the mill.
- **Banding** — Condition where the polymer sheet wraps around the front roll, indicating a well-controlled mill with correct viscosity and friction ratio.
- **Tearing / Bagging** — Condition where the sheet tears off the roll — indicates **incorrect temperature balance** or **insufficient friction ratio**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details of a Two-Roll Mill

A standard two-roll mill consists of the following primary components:

1. **Two hollow steel rolls** — Precision-machined, surface-ground, chrome-plated; bored internally to permit circulation of heating/cooling fluid (steam, hot oil, or water). Typical diameters range from **150 mm (lab mill) to 850 mm (production mill)**. Roll length (face width) ranges from **300 mm to 2500 mm**.
2. **Roll bearings and housings** — Heavy-duty anti-friction bearings mounted in eccentric housings so that the **nip gap can be precisely adjusted** by screw or hydraulic mechanism.
3. **Drive system** — Two independent drive trains (or one motor with differential gear) permitting the rolls to rotate at *slightly different speeds* to create the **friction ratio**.
4. **Temperature control system** — Rotary joint + circulating fluid for each roll, enabling *differential roll temperature* (e.g., front roll at 70 °C, back roll at 50 °C).
5. **Safety devices** — Emergency stop bar, roll-separating mechanism, hopper guards.
6. **Operator-side scraper/blade** — Doctor blade for sheet take-off.
7. **Chute / pan** — For catching sheet or for returning trim to the nip.

## 2.2 Operational Principle — The "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental principle of the two-roll mill is that a **polymer mass placed in the nip will be subjected to extremely high shear rates** because the **velocity gradient** between the two rolls is established over a very small distance (the nip gap). This produces a **drum-skin effect**: the outer surface of the polymer adheres to one roll, is dragged through the nip, and the relative motion of the rolls creates a shearing action. The differential surface speed (friction ratio) ensures that *the polymer is stretched and torn internally*, generating **frictional heat** that assists in plasticization. The operator's periodic cutting and re-feeding of the sheet completes the **distributive mixing** loop.

## 2.3 The Two Modes of Mixing on a Two-Roll Mill

| Mode | Mechanism | Function | Occurs Where |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dispersive Mixing** | High local shear stresses break agglomerates (filler clumps, carbon black pellets) into primary particles and wet them into the polymer | Reduces agglomerate size; ensures every filler particle is coated with polymer | **Inside the nip** |
| **Distributive Mixing** | Repeated cutting, folding, and re-orientation distributes all ingredients uniformly throughout the bulk | Achieves compositional homogeneity | **Outside the nip**, on the bank and via operator manipulation |

## 2.4 Critical Operating Parameters

1. **Friction Ratio (FR)**
   $$FR = \frac{V_f}{V_s} = \frac{\pi D_f N_f}{\pi D_s N_s} = \frac{D_f N_f}{D_s N_s}$$
   Since the rolls are usually of the same diameter, $D_f = D_s$, simplifying to:
   $$FR = \frac{N_f}{N_s}$$
   where $V_f, V_s$ are linear surface velocities; $D_f, D_s$ are diameters; $N_f, N_s$ are rotational speeds (rpm) of front (fast) and back (slow) rolls.

2. **Shear Rate in the Nip**
   $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{V_f - V_s}{h_{nip}}$$
   where $h_{nip}$ is the nip gap. Typical values: **100 to 1000 s⁻¹**.

3. **Apparent Shear Stress**
   $$\tau = \eta_a \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

4. **Volume Production Rate (Sheet Output)**
   $$Q_v = v \cdot L \cdot h_{sheet}$$
   where $v$ is linear velocity of the sheet, $L$ is roll face width, and $h_{sheet}$ is final sheet thickness.

5. **Mass Production Rate**
   $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot v \cdot L \cdot h_{sheet}$$

6. **Power Consumption per Roll**
   $$P = 2\pi N T$$
   with $T$ the torque, $N$ the roll speed in rev/s. The **net frictional power** consumed by the polymer is:
   $$P_{net} = (T_f - T_s) \cdot \omega_s \cdot (FR - 1) \cdot \text{(correction factor)}$$

7. **Batch Size (Rule of Thumb)**
   $$V_{batch} \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 0.5 \times V_{roll} = (0.1 \text{ to } 0.5) \cdot \pi D^2 L / 4$$

8. **Heat Generation Rate (Order of Magnitude)**
   The mechanical energy dissipated in shearing appears as heat:
   $$\dot{Q}_{gen} = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} \cdot V_{nip}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Range / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friction ratio | $FR = V_f / V_s = N_f / N_s$ (equal dia.) | 1.0 – 1.4 (rubber); 1.0 – 1.1 (plastics) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Linear surface velocity | $V = \pi D N / 60$ | 0.1 – 1.0 m/s | m/s |
| 3 | Shear rate in nip | $\dot{\gamma} = (V_f - V_s) / h_{nip}$ | 100 – 1000 s⁻¹ | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | Shear stress | $\tau = \eta_a \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $10^4$ – $10^6$ Pa | Pa |
| 5 | Power per roll | $P = 2\pi N T$ | 5 – 200 kW | W or kW |
| 6 | Net friction power | $P_{net} = (T_f - T_s)\omega_s \cdot FR$ | — | W |
| 7 | Volume output rate | $Q_v = v \cdot L \cdot h_{sheet}$ | 50 – 500 kg/h | m³/s or kg/h |
| 8 | Mass output rate | $\dot{m} = \rho v L h_{sheet}$ | — | kg/s |
| 9 | Batch volume | $V_b = 0.1\text{–}0.5 \times (\pi D^2 L/4)$ | — | m³ |
| 10 | Heat generation | $\dot{Q} = \tau \dot{\gamma} V_{nip}$ | — | W |
| 11 | Roll separating force (RSF) per unit width | $F_w \approx \eta_a \dot{\gamma} \cdot L$ | 50 – 500 N/mm | N/m |
| 12 | Specific energy | $E_{sp} = P / \dot{m}$ | 0.1 – 1.0 kWh/kg | kWh/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT** — In KTU board exams, the **friction ratio** and **shear rate in the nip** are the *most frequently tested formulas* for the two-roll mill topic. Commit these to memory, including the *derivation logic*, not just the final expression.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of the Two-Roll Mill

> [!NOTE]
> Despite being the **oldest compounding equipment**, the two-roll mill remains *irreplaceable* in certain roles:
> - **Small-batch specialty rubber goods** (medical, aerospace) where lot traceability and manual control are paramount.
> - **Laboratory compounding** for recipe development and R&D — the mill is the workhorse for *formulation screening* before scale-up to internal mixers (Banbury, Intermix).
> - **Sheeting of warm polymer** for downstream calendering or extrusion feeding.
> - **Mastication of natural rubber** — historically the only practical way to plasticize NR before Banbury mixers were developed.
> - **Pigment/filler wetting studies** — the transparency of the process (the operator *sees* the dispersion) makes it invaluable for quality diagnosis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala University Examiner's Expectation** — In KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination), derivations are graded for *every* algebraic transition. A derivation that skips a step is penalized 1–2 marks. Below, every logical and algebraic step is spelled out explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Surface Velocity of a Roll

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the linear (tangential) surface velocity of a roll rotating at $N$ rpm with diameter $D$.

**Step 1 — Angular velocity in radians per second:**

The roll makes $N$ revolutions per minute. The angular displacement per revolution is $2\pi$ radians. Therefore, the angular velocity in radians per minute is $2\pi N$. To convert to radians per *second*:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;\; \text{[rad/s]}
$$

**Step 2 — Relationship between tangential and angular velocity:**

For a rigid body in pure rotation, the tangential (linear) velocity at a radius $r$ from the rotation axis is the product of the angular velocity and the radius:

$$
V = \omega \cdot r
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the radius of the roll:**

For a roll of *diameter* $D$, the radius is $r = D/2$. Substituting:

$$
V = \omega \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**

$$
V = \frac{\pi D N}{60} \;\; \text{[m/s]}
$$

This is the **standard linear surface velocity** formula used throughout polymer processing. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Friction Ratio

**Statement:** Derive the friction ratio of a two-roll mill where the front roll has diameter $D_f$ and rotates at $N_f$ rpm, and the back roll has diameter $D_s$ and rotates at $N_s$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Define friction ratio as the ratio of linear surface velocities:**

By definition, the friction ratio is the ratio of the faster roll's surface speed to the slower roll's surface speed:

$$
FR \equiv \frac{V_f}{V_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the linear velocity expression from Derivation 1:**

$$
FR = \frac{\pi D_f N_f / 60}{\pi D_s N_s / 60} = \frac{D_f N_f}{D_s N_s}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplification for equal-diameter rolls:**

In the vast majority of production two-roll mills, $D_f = D_s = D$ (both rolls are manufactured to identical specifications for uniform wear and ease of maintenance). In that special case:

$$
FR = \frac{N_f}{N_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Special-case check (uniform roll, no friction):**

If $N_f = N_s$, then $FR = 1$, which means both rolls move at the same speed → no relative motion of the polymer at the nip → no shear → *the mill does not function as a mixer*. This confirms that **friction ratio is the *cause* of shearing action**. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shear Rate in the Nip Region

**Statement:** Derive the apparent shear rate in the nip region of a two-roll mill with front roll velocity $V_f$, back roll velocity $V_s$, and nip gap $h_{nip}$.

**Step 1 — Definition of shear rate:**

Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ is defined as the velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction:

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the boundary conditions:**

At the surface of the *back (slow) roll*, the polymer moves with the roll surface, so $v(0) = V_s$. At the surface of the *front (fast) roll*, $v(h_{nip}) = V_f$. Here the $y$-axis is perpendicular to the rolls, with $y = 0$ at the back roll and $y = h_{nip}$ at the front roll.

**Step 3 — Assume a linear velocity profile (Newtonian / first-order approximation):**

For an order-of-magnitude estimate, assume the velocity varies linearly from $V_s$ at the back roll to $V_f$ at the front roll across the nip gap $h_{nip}$:

$$
\frac{dv}{dy} \approx \frac{V_f - V_s}{h_{nip} - 0} = \frac{V_f - V_s}{h_{nip}}
$$

**Step 4 — Final expression:**

$$
\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{nip} = \frac{V_f - V_s}{h_{nip}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V_f - V_s = V_s(FR - 1)$:**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{nip} = \frac{V_s(FR - 1)}{h_{nip}}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU-exam form** of the shear rate in the nip. ✅

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Volumetric Throughput of the Sheet

**Statement:** Derive the mass production rate of a two-roll mill sheeting a polymer at linear velocity $v$, sheet thickness $h$, roll face width $L$, polymer density $\rho$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the exiting sheet:**

As the polymer exits the nip, it forms a continuous sheet of thickness $h$ (slightly larger than $h_{nip}$ due to elastic recovery / die swell) and width $L$ (equal to the roll face width in ideal cases).

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of the sheet:**

$$
A = L \cdot h
$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric throughput:**

The volume emerging per unit time is the cross-section times the linear velocity:

$$
Q_v = A \cdot v = L \cdot h \cdot v
$$

**Step 4 — Mass throughput (multiplying by density):**

$$
\boxed{\dot{m} = \rho L h v}
$$

For a mill in steady state, conservation of mass requires that the mass entering the nip per unit time equals the mass exiting per unit time — this is the *continuity principle* applied to the mill. ✅

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Power Consumption by a Rotating Roll

**Statement:** Show that the mechanical power required to drive a single roll rotating at $N$ rpm against a torque $T$ is $P = 2\pi N T$ (with $N$ in rev/s).

**Step 1 — Definition of mechanical power:**

Power is the rate of doing work. For a rotating shaft, the work done per revolution is the torque times the angular displacement per revolution:

$$
W_{rev} = T \cdot (2\pi)
$$

**Step 2 — Power in terms of rev/s:**

If the shaft makes $n$ revolutions per second, then the work done per second (i.e., the power) is:

$$
P = W_{rev} \cdot n = 2\pi n T
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to rpm:**

If $N$ is in revolutions per minute, then $n = N/60$:

$$
P = 2\pi \left(\frac{N}{60}\right) T
$$

**Step 4 — Final form commonly used in industry:**

$$
\boxed{P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{\pi N T}{30} \;\; \text{[W]}}
$$

with $N$ in rpm and $T$ in N·m. ✅

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Net Frictional Power Transmitted to the Polymer

**Statement:** Derive the net power delivered to the polymer as a result of the friction ratio.

**Step 1 — Power supplied to each roll:**

From Derivation 5, the power input to the *fast* (front) roll is $P_f = 2\pi n_f T_f$ and to the *slow* (back) roll is $P_s = 2\pi n_s T_s$.

**Step 2 — The polymer experiences a net tangential force:**

The polymer is dragged along by the fast roll and dragged back by the slow roll. The *net mechanical energy delivered to the polymer per unit time* equals the difference in power inputs (since both rolls are doing work on the polymer).

**Step 3 — Expressing in terms of the slow roll's angular speed (using $n_f = FR \cdot n_s$):**

$$
P_{polymer} = P_f - P_s = 2\pi n_f T_f - 2\pi n_s T_s
$$

$$
P_{polymer} = 2\pi n_s \left( FR \cdot T_f - T_s \right)
$$

**Step 4 — For a thin nip and uniform polymer, an engineering approximation (assuming $T_f \approx T_s = T$):**

$$
P_{polymer} \approx 2\pi n_s T (FR - 1)
$$

This shows that **the net power delivered to the polymer is directly proportional to (FR − 1)** — hence a *higher friction ratio means more intense mixing but more heat generation and power demand*. ✅

---

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A two-roll mill has rolls of diameter **250 mm** and face width **500 mm**. The slow roll rotates at **18 rpm** and the fast roll at **24 rpm**. The nip gap is set to **2 mm**. The polymer melt has an apparent viscosity of **5 × 10⁴ Pa·s** and a density of **1000 kg/m³**. The final sheet thickness is **3 mm**.
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Friction ratio
> (b) Shear rate in the nip
> (c) Shear stress in the nip
> (d) Volumetric production rate
> (e) Mass production rate
> (f) Power input to the fast roll (assume torque on fast roll is 600 N·m)

### Solution:

**(a) Friction ratio:**

$$
FR = \frac{N_f}{N_s} = \frac{24}{18} = 1.333
$$

**(b) Linear surface velocity of the slow roll:**

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi D N_s}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.250 \times 18}{60}
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi \times 4.5}{60} = \frac{14.137}{60} = 0.2356 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$

**(c) Linear surface velocity of the fast roll:**

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi D N_f}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.250 \times 24}{60}
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi \times 6.0}{60} = \frac{18.850}{60} = 0.3142 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$

**(d) Shear rate in the nip:**

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \frac{V_f - V_s}{h_{nip}} = \frac{0.3142 - 0.2356}{0.002}
$$

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \frac{0.0786}{0.002} = 39.3 \;\; \text{s}^{-1}
$$

**(e) Shear stress in the nip:**

$$
\tau = \eta_a \cdot \dot{\gamma} = 5 \times 10^4 \times 39.3
$$

$$
\tau = 1.965 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{Pa} = 1.965 \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**(f) Mass production rate:**

The sheet exits at approximately the fast roll velocity. Using the given thickness and roll face width:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot V_f \cdot L \cdot h_{sheet}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 1000 \times 0.3142 \times 0.500 \times 0.003
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 1000 \times 4.713 \times 10^{-4} = 0.4713 \;\; \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.4713 \times 3600 = 1696.7 \;\; \text{kg/h}
$$

**(g) Power to the fast roll:**

Convert 24 rpm to rev/s: $n_f = 24/60 = 0.4$ rev/s.

$$
P_f = 2\pi n_f T_f = 2\pi \times 0.4 \times 600
$$

$$
P_f = 1507.96 \;\; \text{W} \approx 1.51 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**All seven sub-results verified. ✅**

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| If the question asks for… | Use the formula… | Common trap to avoid |
|---|---|---|
| Speed ratio between rolls | $FR = N_f/N_s$ | Do not confuse with the *velocity ratio* — if diameters differ, $V_f/V_s \neq N_f/N_s$ |
| Shear rate in the nip | $\dot{\gamma} = (V_f - V_s)/h_{nip}$ | Students often forget the minus sign and write $(V_f + V_s)$ |
| Throughput / production rate | $\dot{m} = \rho L h v$ | Do not confuse $h$ (sheet thickness) with $h_{nip}$ (gap) |
| Power consumption | $P = 2\pi n T$ | Convert $N$ from rpm to rev/s before substituting |
| Heat generation | $\dot{Q} = \tau \dot{\gamma} V_{nip}$ | $V_{nip}$ is the volume *of the nip itself*, not the batch volume |
| Batch size | $V_b \approx 0.2 \times \pi D^2 L / 4$ | Coefficient varies (0.1–0.5) — state assumption explicitly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for KTU Students:** The two-roll mill is a *physical machine* — the canonical drawing is a side-elevation showing the two counter-rotating rolls, the bank, the nip, the sheet take-off, and the temperature control. Below are two **Mermaid block diagrams** capturing the *process flow* and the *functional architecture* of the mill, in lieu of a hand-drawn schematic (which cannot be rendered in text).

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Two-Roll Mill

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator<br/>Loading Zone] --> B[Polymer Mass<br/>Initial Charge]
    B --> C[Back Roll<br/>Slow Rotation<br/>Ns rpm]
    C --> D{Nip Region<br/>h nip gap}
    D --> E[Front Roll<br/>Fast Rotation<br/>Nf rpm]
    E --> F[Sheeting Blade<br/>Doctor Blade]
    F --> G[Continuous Sheet<br/>Thickness h]
    G --> H[Take-Off Conveyor<br/>or Wind-Up]
    H -.Returns trim.-> C
    D -.High Shear.-> I[Dispersive<br/>Mixing Zone]
    I --> J[Filler / Carbon Black<br/>Wetting and Dispersion]
    D -.Repeated Cycles.-> K[Distributive<br/>Mixing Zone]
    K --> L[Compositional<br/>Homogenization]
    M[Heating / Cooling<br/>Fluid Circuit] -.Temperature Control.-> C
    M -.Temperature Control.-> E
    N[Drive Motor<br/>and Gearbox] -.Powers.-> C
    N -.Powers.-> E
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of a Typical Rubber Compounding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Load Raw<br/>Polymer NR] --> S2[Banded on<br/>Front Roll]
    S2 --> S3[Mastication<br/>2-5 minutes]
    S3 --> S4[Add Activators<br/>ZnO, Stearic Acid]
    S4 --> S5[Add Antioxidants<br/>and Process Aids]
    S5 --> S6[Add Fillers<br/>Carbon Black, Silica<br/>in 2-3 portions]
    S6 --> S7[Add Plasticizers<br/>and Oils]
    S7 --> S8[Add Curatives<br/>Sulfur, Accelerators]
    S8 --> S9[Final Mixing<br/>and Homogenization]
    S9 --> S10[Sheet Off<br/>at target thickness]
    S10 --> S11[End: Batch Ready<br/>for Curing]
    style S6 fill:#f9d6d6
    style S8 fill:#d6e0f9
    style S10 fill:#d6f9d6
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram of the Shear Field in the Nip

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NipZone[ NIP REGION - h nip gap ]
        direction LR
        R1[Back Roll Surface<br/>Velocity Vs] -->|Polymer Adheres| N1[Velocity = Vs]
        N1 -->|Linear velocity<br/>profile assumed| N2[Velocity = Vf]
        N2 -->|Polymer Adheres| R2[Front Roll Surface<br/>Velocity Vf]
    end
    NipZone -.generates.-> SH[Shear Rate gamma dot<br/>equals Vf minus Vs<br/>divided by h nip]
    SH -.combined with.-> VIS[Apparent Viscosity<br/>eta a of polymer]
    VIS -.produces.-> ST[Shear Stress tau<br/>acting on polymer]
    ST -.does.-> W[Mechanical Work<br/>Dispersive Mixing<br/>Filler Wetting<br/>Heat Generation]
    style NipZone fill:#fff4cc
    style W fill:#ffd6a5
```

## 4.4 Operator Control Loop on a Two-Roll Mill

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OP[Operator Skill] -->|Cutting, Folding,<br/>Re-feeding| BANK[Bank Region]
    BANK -->|Controlled by| FR[Friction Ratio<br/>Setting]
    BANK -->|Controlled by| NIP[Nip Gap<br/>Adjustment]
    BANK -->|Controlled by| TEMP[Roll Temperature<br/>Profile]
    FR --> FEEDBACK1[Mixing Quality]
    NIP --> FEEDBACK1
    TEMP --> FEEDBACK1
    FEEDBACK1 -.Operator observes<br/>and adjusts.-> OP
    style OP fill:#cce5ff
    style FEEDBACK1 fill:#ffcccc
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical takeaway from the diagrams:** The two-roll mill is fundamentally a *closed-loop* process where the **operator IS the control system**. Modern internal mixers (Banbury) have replaced the operator's brain with programmed PLCs — but the underlying physics (friction, shear, nip dynamics) is identical. This is why mastery of the two-roll mill is the foundation of *all* polymer processing education.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based or one-line derivation problems. The model answer should be *concise but complete* — typically 4–6 lines of written text plus one essential formula.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define friction ratio. What is its typical range in rubber compounding?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **friction ratio (FR)** of a two-roll mill is the ratio of the *linear surface velocity* of the *front (fast) roll* to that of the *back (slow) roll*:
>
> $FR = V_f / V_s = (D_f N_f)/(D_s N_s)$. For rolls of equal diameter, this simplifies to $FR = N_f / N_s$.
>
> For **rubber compounding**, the typical friction ratio is **1.10 to 1.40**, with **1.20 being a common industrial value**. For plastic compounding, FR is closer to **1.0–1.1**. A higher friction ratio produces *more shear* and hence *more dispersive mixing*, but also generates *more frictional heat* and increases *roll separating force*.

**[3 marks]** ✅

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a "bank" in a two-roll mill? Explain its role in the mixing process.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **bank** is the *rolling reserve mass of polymer compound* that accumulates *upstream of the nip*, between the two rolls, held by friction against the roll surfaces.
>
> Its roles are:
> 1. **Reservoir function** — supplies material continuously to the nip as the polymer is drawn in and sheeted out.
> 2. **Pre-conditioning zone** — the bank undergoes *bulk folding and re-orientation* before entering the high-shear nip, aiding **distributive mixing**.
> 3. **Heat exchange** — the bank is in contact with the roll surfaces, allowing **heat transfer** to/from the polymer.
>
> A well-controlled bank is hemispherical and rotates slowly with the rolls; an excessively large or unstable bank indicates a *mis-set* nip or temperature.

**[3 marks]** ✅

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always state assumptions** explicitly (e.g., "assume rolls are of equal diameter", "neglecting elastic recovery").
> 2. **Always include units** in the final answer; an answer like "1.5" without "kW" or "m/s" loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Show the substitution step** before computing; "plugging in numbers without the formula" is penalized.
> 4. **Diagram marks** — if the question asks to "explain with a neat sketch", a diagram is worth *at least 2–3 marks*. Even a hand-drawn block schematic earns partial credit.
> 5. **Don't confuse** the *friction ratio* (dimensionless) with *frictional force* (N) — this is a top-3 mark-losing error in KTU valuations.

---

### **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a two-roll mill used in polymer processing. Label all key components and identify the locations of dispersive and distributive mixing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-roll mill has rolls of diameter **300 mm**, face width **600 mm**, with the front roll rotating at **28 rpm** and the back roll at **20 rpm**. The nip gap is **1.5 mm** and the apparent viscosity of the polymer is **3 × 10⁴ Pa·s**. The final sheet thickness is **2.5 mm** and the polymer density is **950 kg/m³**. Calculate:
   (i) Friction ratio
   (ii) Shear rate in the nip
   (iii) Shear stress in the nip
   (iv) Mass production rate in kg/h **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q3A**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

A two-roll mill consists of **two horizontal, hollow, chrome-plated steel rolls** mounted in heavy-duty bearings, capable of being driven at slightly different speeds to create a **friction ratio**. The construction includes:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Hollow steel rolls** (typically $D = 150$ to $850$ mm, $L = 300$ to $2500$ mm) | Provide the working surface and allow internal heating/cooling fluid circulation |
| **Bearing housings with eccentric adjustment** | Permit precise control of the **nip gap** (0.5–5 mm) |
| **Independent drive motors / differential gearbox** | Enforce *different* rotational speeds → **friction ratio** |
| **Rotary joints and TCUs** | Circulate steam/hot oil/water to control roll temperature (often *differentially* — front roll hotter than back roll) |
| **Hopper / feed tray** | Where polymer and additives are introduced |
| **Operator platform and cutting blade** | For manual cutting, folding, and re-feeding — completes the distributive mixing loop |
| **Safety devices** | Emergency stop bar, roll-separating mechanism, light curtain |

**Working Principle:** Polymer is fed into the nip from the top. The fast roll drags the polymer through the nip at higher velocity than the slow roll. The **velocity gradient across the nip** generates a **shear field**. The polymer exits the nip as a thin sheet on the fast roll side; an *operator* (or in modern mills, an automatic blender) cuts the sheet, folds it, and returns it to the nip, ensuring **every portion of the batch experiences the high-shear nip repeatedly**.

**Locations of mixing:**
- **Dispersive mixing** occurs **inside the nip** where local shear stresses break up agglomerates.
- **Distributive mixing** occurs **at the bank** and via **operator cutting and folding** outside the nip.

A neat block diagram has been provided in **Section 4.1** of these notes — students may reproduce it in the answer script for full credit. **[7 marks]** ✅

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 300$ mm $= 0.300$ m
- $L = 600$ mm $= 0.600$ m
- $N_f = 28$ rpm
- $N_s = 20$ rpm
- $h_{nip} = 1.5$ mm $= 0.0015$ m
- $\eta_a = 3 \times 10^4$ Pa·s
- $h_{sheet} = 2.5$ mm $= 0.0025$ m
- $\rho = 950$ kg/m³

**(i) Friction ratio:**

$$
FR = \frac{N_f}{N_s} = \frac{28}{20} = 1.4 \quad \text{(dimensionless)} \;\; \textbf{[1.5 marks]}
$$

**(ii) Shear rate in the nip:**

First, linear surface velocities:

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi D N_f}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.300 \times 28}{60} = \frac{26.389}{60} = 0.4398 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi D N_s}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.300 \times 20}{60} = \frac{18.850}{60} = 0.3142 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$

Shear rate:

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \frac{V_f - V_s}{h_{nip}} = \frac{0.4398 - 0.3142}{0.0015} = \frac{0.1256}{0.0015}
$$

$$
\dot{\gamma} = 83.76 \;\; \text{s}^{-1} \;\; \textbf{[1.5 marks]}
$$

**(iii) Shear stress in the nip:**

$$
\tau = \eta_a \cdot \dot{\gamma} = 3 \times 10^4 \times 83.76 = 2.513 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\tau \approx 2.51 \;\; \text{MPa} \;\; \textbf{[1.5 marks]}
$$

**(iv) Mass production rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot V_f \cdot L \cdot h_{sheet} = 950 \times 0.4398 \times 0.600 \times 0.0025
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 950 \times 6.597 \times 10^{-4} = 0.6267 \;\; \text{kg/s}
$$

Converting to kg/h:

$$
\dot{m} = 0.6267 \times 3600 = 2256.2 \;\; \text{kg/h} \;\; \textbf{[2.5 marks]}
$$

**Total: 7 marks for Part (b).** ✅

---

### **Q3B. [Internal Choice Alternative — 14 Marks]**
**(a)** Define the following terms with respect to a two-roll mill: (i) Friction ratio, (ii) Nip, (iii) Bank, (iv) Mastication, (v) Banding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the two modes of mixing (dispersive and distributive) achieved in a two-roll mill. Describe how operating parameters (friction ratio, nip gap, roll temperature, and batch size) affect mixing quality. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q3B**

**Part (a) — Definitions (7 marks, 1.4 each):**

- **(i) Friction ratio** — Dimensionless ratio of the *linear surface velocity of the fast (front) roll* to the *slow (back) roll*. Typical range: **1.1 to 1.4** for rubber.
- **(ii) Nip** — The *smallest gap* between the two counter-rotating rolls; the location of maximum shear rate.
- **(iii) Bank** — The *reserve mass of polymer* that rolls between the two rolls upstream of the nip, behaving as a temporary reservoir.
- **(iv) Mastication** — The *preliminary mechanical breakdown* of high-molecular-weight polymer (especially natural rubber) by repeated shearing to reduce its viscosity and improve processability.
- **(v) Banding** — The condition in which the polymer sheet *wraps around the front roll as a continuous band*; this is the desired stable operating condition indicating well-controlled viscosity, temperature, and friction ratio. **[7 marks]** ✅

**Part (b) — Modes of Mixing & Parameter Effects (7 marks):**

**Dispersive mixing** — Achieved by **high local shear stress** in the nip that breaks filler agglomerates (e.g., carbon black pellets) into primary particles and *wets* them with polymer. The efficiency depends on the **local shear stress** $\tau = \eta_a \dot{\gamma}$, which is increased by a *larger friction ratio* and *smaller nip gap*.

**Distributive mixing** — Achieved by **repeated cutting, folding, and re-orientation** of the polymer mass on the bank and by the operator. The goal is *spatial uniformity* of composition, not particle size reduction. Distributive mixing improves with *longer mixing time*, *larger batch turn-over*, and *more aggressive operator manipulation*.

**Parameter effects:**

| Parameter | Effect on Mixing Quality |
|---|---|
| **Friction ratio ↑** | Higher shear rate → better dispersive mixing; but more heat, more wear |
| **Nip gap ↓** | Higher shear rate → better dispersive mixing; but risk of roll damage, throughput ↓ |
| **Roll temperature ↑** | Viscosity ↓ → easier processing; but scorch risk for rubber, may reduce shear stress |
| **Batch size ↑** | More uniform mixing in time, but harder to handle; rule of thumb 0.1–0.5 × roll volume |
| **Mixing time ↑** | Better distributive mixing; diminishing returns past optimum |

**[7 marks]** ✅

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Rapid Revision Checklist for Two-Roll Mill Mixing**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- ☐ Two-roll mill is a **batch-type, primary compounding device** with two **counter-rotating** rolls.
- ☐ The **friction ratio (FR)** is the cause of shearing; defined as $FR = V_f / V_s = N_f / N_s$ (for equal-diameter rolls).
- ☐ Typical **friction ratio**: **1.1–1.4 (rubber), 1.0–1.1 (plastics)**.
- ☐ The **nip** is the smallest gap and the *seat of dispersive mixing*.
- ☐ The **bank** is the reserve mass and the *seat of distributive mixing*.
- ☐ **Dispersive mixing** = breakup of agglomerates (nip region, high shear).
- ☐ **Distributive mixing** = spatial homogenization (operator action, bank).
- ☐ **Banding** is the desired stable operating state; **tearing** indicates a problem.

### Key Formulas (Must Know for KTU Board Exam)
- ☐ $V = \pi D N / 60$ (linear surface velocity)
- ☐ $FR = N_f / N_s$ (friction ratio, equal-diameter rolls)
- ☐ $\dot{\gamma}_{nip} = (V_f - V_s) / h_{nip}$ (shear rate in the nip)
- ☐ $\tau = \eta_a \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ (shear stress)
- ☐ $P = 2\pi n T$ (power per roll, with $n$ in rev/s)
- ☐ $P_{polymer} \approx 2\pi n_s T (FR - 1)$ (net frictional power)
- ☐ $\dot{m} = \rho v L h$ (mass production rate)
- ☐ $V_{batch} \approx (0.1 \text{ to } 0.5) \times \pi D^2 L / 4$

### Engineering Judgement Points
- ☐ A **larger FR** → more shear → better mixing BUT more heat generation and higher roll wear.
- ☐ A **smaller nip gap** → higher shear rate → better dispersive mixing BUT risk of metal-to-metal contact.
- ☐ **Differential roll temperatures** (front hotter than back) are used to *direct* the polymer to the front roll and encourage banding.
- ☐ The two-roll mill is **operator-intensive** — modern industry prefers internal mixers (Banbury, Intermix) for production scale, but the mill remains *unbeatable* for small batches, R&D, and color/quality-sensitive products.
- ☐ **Mastication** of natural rubber on a two-roll mill reduces its Mooney viscosity by oxidative chain scission — historically performed at 60–80 °C with peptizers.

### Common KTU Board Exam Traps
- ☐ Mixing up $V_f$ and $V_s$ in the shear rate formula (sign error).
- ☐ Forgetting to convert rpm → rev/s in the power formula.
- ☐ Using $h_{nip}$ instead of $h_{sheet}$ in the production rate formula.
- ☐ Stating $FR$ without specifying that it assumes equal-diameter rolls.
- ☐ Confusing the *front roll* (fast, where sheet is taken off) with the *back roll* (slow, where bank sits).
- ☐ Not labelling axes/units in any graphical sketch — easy 0.5–1 mark loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Mixers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Internal Mixers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Internal Mixer** is a heavy-duty, batch-type, intensive compounding machine in which the entire mixing operation—incorporation, dispersion, and distribution of compounding ingredients—takes place inside a **closed, heated/cooled chamber** using two counter-rotating, non-intermeshing (or intermeshing) rotors. The polymer mass never leaves the mixing enclosure; hence the qualifier *"internal."* In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCPOT502, Module 2), the Internal Mixer is positioned as the **workhorse of the rubber and thermoplastic compounding industry** and forms the conceptual bridge between simple extruder-based mixing and the broader field of polymer blending. The most industrially iconic variant is the **Banbury Mixer**, designed by **Fernley H. Banbury in 1916**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Anchor:** Internal mixers are studied under "Compounding & Mixing Equipment" and must be understood alongside two-roll mills, extruders, and continuous mixers. The examination frequently tests the **construction, working, and design features of the Banbury-type internal mixer.** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Industrial Kitchen Mixer" Imagine preparing a stiff dough (atta + water + salt + oil) by hand. The dough is thick and resistant. A spoon barely moves it. What you instinctively do is: 1. Compress it (downward force). 2. Fold it (shear). 3. Tear it apart (dispersion of lumps). 4. Repeat cyclically until uniform. A domestic stand mixer does this with two planetary beaters. Now **scale that idea up 1000×**, replace the beaters with **massive, contoured, steel rotors spinning at 30–60 rpm inside a dust-tight water-cooled trough**, and you have an internal mixer. The walls push the material inward, the rotors push it outward, and the ram on top provides the **third compressive force**—creating a "sandwich of shear" that the polymer cannot escape. ## 1.3 Salient Engineering Metrics | Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | |---|---| | **Chamber Volume** | 30 L to 600 L (lab to production) | | **Rotor Speed** | 20 – 80 rpm (variable) | | **Specific Energy Input** | **0.3 – 0.8 kWh/kg** of batch | | **Batch Cycle Time** | 2 – 6 minutes | | **Operating Temperature** | 50 °C – 200 °C | | **Ram Pressure** | **0.3 – 0.7 MPa** | | **Filling Factor (η)** | **0.55 – 0.75** | > [!NOTE] > **Filling Factor (η)** is defined as the ratio of the actual batch volume to the free volume of the mixing chamber. Values below 0.5 lead to poor shear; values above 0.8 cause material overflow and poor dispersion. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of an Internal Mixer A modern internal mixer (e.g., Banbury, Krupp, Shaw, Bolling) comprises the following sub-assemblies: 1. **Mixing Chamber (Trough / Body)** - A heavy fabricated steel casing, often lined with replaceable **hardened wear-resistant liners** (e.g., tungsten carbide or chrome-plated steel). - The chamber is **jacketed** to circulate either cooling water (for elastomers that heat up) or hot oil/steam (for thermoplastics that need melting). 2. **Two Counter-Rotating Rotors** - Mounted on parallel horizontal shafts, driven by a common gear train or independent variable-speed drives. - **Rotor speed ratio (friction ratio)** is typically **1.0 : 1.1 to 1.0 : 1.25**—the differential speed generates *inter-rotor shear*. 3. **Floating Weight (Ram) + Hopper** - A pneumatically or hydraulically actuated piston (the "floating weight") sits above the chamber opening, with a charging hopper on top. - Maintains a constant **ram pressure** of 0.3–0.7 MPa on the batch—essential for dispersive mixing. 4. **Discharge Door (Drop Door)** - A pneumatically actuated bottom door that opens to drop the batch onto a two-roll mill or into an extruder for pelletization. 5. **Drive System** - Heavy-duty gear reducer, often with **variable frequency drive (VFD)** for speed control. - Rated power ranges from **50 kW (lab)** to **5000 kW (production)**. 6. **Dust Stops & Seals** - Seal the rotor ends to prevent material leakage and lubricant ingress. ## 2.2 Types of Rotors — The Heart of the Mixer Rotor geometry directly determines shear type, mix quality, and power draw. Two broad families exist: ### A. Non-Intermeshing (Tangential) Rotors - **Classic Banbury rotors** (two-wing or four-wing design). - Rotors do *not* mesh; clearance is large (typically 3–10 mm). - Operate at **high friction ratios (1:1.1 to 1:1.25)**. - Generate **strong inter-rotor shear** but high local heating. - Best for **rubber compounding**. ### B. Intermeshing (Interlocking) Rotors - The rotors are **gear-like and mesh with each other**. - Operate at **synchronized 1:1 ratio** (no friction). - Provide **gentler, more uniform distributive mixing** with less heat build-up. - Best for **heat-sensitive thermoplastics and PVC**. > [!NOTE] > A **friction ratio ≠ gear ratio** in internal mixers. Friction ratio describes the *differential surface speed* between the two rotors to introduce shear. It is *not* the tooth-contact ratio of a gearbox. ## 2.3 The Five-Stage Mixing Cycle A typical rubber compounding cycle in a Banbury mixer proceeds in five stages: | Stage | Time (s) | Phenomena | |---|---|---| | 1. Incorporation | 0 – 30 | Polymer chunks added; ram descends; voids filled. | | 2. Plasticization | 30 – 90 | Polymer softens due to mechanical + thermal input. | | 3. Addition of fillers/plasticizers | 90 – 150 | Carbon black, oil added under ram pressure. | | 4. Dispersion | 150 – 240 | Agglomerates broken; additives wetted out. | | 5. Vulcanization ingredients (if rubber) | 240 – 300 | Curatives added at lower T to prevent scorch. | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $v_{tip} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | $v_{tip}$ = tip speed (m/s); $D$ = rotor diameter (m); $N$ = rotor speed (rev/s) | Predicts shear rate; tip speed range **5–30 m/s**. | | 2 | $\dot{\gamma} \approx \dfrac{v_{tip}}{\delta}$ | $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹); $\delta$ = clearance (~5 mm) | Quantifies dispersive shear; typical $\dot{\gamma}$ = **1000–5000 s⁻¹**. | | 3 | $P = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T$ | $P$ = power (W); $T$ = torque (N·m) | Determines drive motor rating. | | 4 | $E_{spec} = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{m}$ | $E_{spec}$ = specific energy (J/kg); $t$ = batch time; $m$ = batch mass | **SCADA-controlled** in modern mixers for batch repeatability; target **1–3 MJ/kg**. | | 5 | $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat removed by jacket; $h$ = HTC (~200 W/m²K); $A$ = chamber area | Design of cooling system. | | 6 | $N_{Po} = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}}$ | Power number (dimensionless) | **Scales mixers geometrically**—same $N_{Po}$ ⇒ same hydrodynamic regime. | | 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^{2}}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number of mixing | Predicts flow regime: $Re < 10$ ⇒ laminar (typical). | | 8 | $V_{batch} = \eta \cdot V_{chamber}$ | $\eta$ = filling factor (0.55–0.75); $V_{chamber}$ = free chamber volume | Sets batch size. | | 9 | $F_{ram} = P_{ram} \cdot A_{ram}$ | Hydraulic force on batch | Controls dispersive efficiency. | | 10 | $\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \dfrac{E_{spec}}{C_{p}}$ | Bulk temperature rise if no cooling | Worst-case temperature spike (~30–80 °C). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scaling Law (KTU favourite!):** Two geometrically similar internal mixers operating at the *same power number* and *same filling factor* will produce *equivalent mix quality*. This is the basis of lab-to-plant scale-up. ## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility - **Tyre industry:** All major tyre manufacturers (MRF, Apollo, Bridgestone) compound natural/synthetic rubber with carbon black, silica, sulphur, and accelerators in Banbury mixers. - **PVC compounding:** Intermeshing rotor mixers compound PVC with plasticizers, stabilizers, and impact modifiers. - **Masterbatch production:** Used to disperse 30–60% pigment into polymer carrier. - **Recycling:** Re-compounding of post-industrial scrap with property-restoring additives. - **Thermoplastic elastomers (TPE):** Dynamic vulcanization of EPDM/PP blends. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Rotor Tip Speed and Shear Rate The rotor is a cylinder of diameter $D$ rotating at $N$ revolutions per second. A point on the outer circumference of the rotor traces a circular path of length $\pi D$ per revolution. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Distance per revolution} &= \pi \cdot D \\[4pt] \text{Distance per second} &= N \cdot \pi \cdot D \\[4pt] \therefore \quad v_{tip} &= \pi \cdot D \cdot N \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering interpretation:** Doubling the rotor diameter doubles the tip speed and hence the shear rate—*provided the clearance remains unchanged*. This is why large production mixers can be run at *lower* rpm than lab mixers and still deliver equivalent shear. The shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ in the clearance gap $\delta$ between the rotor and the chamber wall is given by the velocity gradient: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{\gamma} &= \frac{dv}{dr} \approx \frac{v_{tip} - 0}{\delta} \\[4pt] \dot{\gamma} &= \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{\delta} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation — Power Number (Newton's Scaling Law) For a geometrically similar family of internal mixers operating in the **laminar regime** (which is the case for highly viscous polymers), dimensional analysis yields a power-law relationship between power and the operating variables: $$ P \propto \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5} $$ Dividing both sides by the proportionality constant group $\rho N^{3} D^{5}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{P}{\rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}} &= \text{constant} \\[4pt] N_{Po} &= \frac{P}{\rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. The Buckingham π-theorem with variables $(P, \rho, N, D, \mu)$ yields **two dimensionless groups**: $N_{Po}$ and $Re$. 2. In the **fully-filled, laminar regime**, $N_{Po}$ becomes independent of $Re$ (a plateau region on a log-log plot of $N_{Po}$ vs $Re$). 3. Therefore, geometrically similar mixers at the same filling factor and same $N_{Po}$ will draw the same *specific* power per unit volume. This is the **practical scale-up equation** used by Krupp, Farrel, and POMINI engineers worldwide. ## 3.3 Derivation — Specific Energy Input The mechanical work done on a batch of mass $m$ in time $t$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} W &= P \cdot t \\[4pt] E_{spec} &= \frac{W}{m} = \frac{P \cdot t}{m} \end{aligned} $$ Modern mixers integrate $\int P \, dt$ electronically and stop the cycle once a *target specific energy* is reached—ensuring batch-to-batch consistency irrespective of minor input variations. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A Banbury mixer with rotor diameter $D = 0.45$ m rotates at $N = 1.2$ rev/s. The chamber wall-to-rotor clearance is $\delta = 6$ mm. The chamber volume is $0.18$ m³, and the filling factor is $\eta = 0.65$. Calculate: (a) Rotor tip speed (b) Average shear rate in the clearance (c) Batch mass (assume polymer density $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³) ### Solution **(a) Rotor tip speed:** $$ \begin{aligned} v_{tip} &= \pi \cdot D \cdot N \\[4pt] v_{tip} &= \pi \cdot 0.45 \cdot 1.2 \\[4pt] v_{tip} &= 1.697 \; \text{m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Average shear rate in the clearance:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{\gamma} &= \frac{v_{tip}}{\delta} = \frac{1.697}{0.006} \\[4pt] \dot{\gamma} &= 282.8 \; \text{s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **(c) Batch mass:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{batch} &= \eta \cdot V_{chamber} = 0.65 \cdot 0.18 = 0.117 \; \text{m}^{3} \\[4pt] m &= \rho \cdot V_{batch} = 1100 \cdot 0.117 \\[4pt] m &= 128.7 \; \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > A production Banbury mixer typically processes ~130 kg per batch—matching our calculation. This validates the typical industrial range cited in §1.3. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tangential vs Intermeshing Rotors | Attribute | Tangential (Banbury) | Intermeshing | |---|---|---| | Friction ratio | 1 : 1.1 to 1 : 1.25 | 1 : 1 (synchronized) | | Inter-rotor clearance | Large (3–10 mm) | Small (1–2 mm) | | Shear intensity | **Very high** | Moderate | | Heat generation | High | Low | | Distributive mixing | Good | **Excellent** | | Dispersive mixing | **Excellent** | Good | | Best for | Rubber, high-viscosity compounds | Heat-sensitive thermoplastics, PVC | | Power draw | Higher | Lower | | Maintenance | Easier (rotors independent) | More complex (timing required) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Constructional Block Diagram of an Internal Mixer (Banbury Type) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Charging Hopper (Top)"] --> B["Floating Weight and Ram
Pneumatic Cylinder 0.3 to 0.7 MPa"] B --> C["Mixing Chamber
Water Cooled Jacket"] C --> D["Front Rotor
Driven Shaft N1 rpm"] C --> E["Rear Rotor
Driven Shaft N2 rpm
Friction Ratio 1 to 1.1"] D --> F["Inter Rotor Shear Zone"] E --> F F --> G["Discharge Drop Door
Pneumatic Actuation"] G --> H["Batch Discharge to Two Roll Mill
or Pelletizing Extruder"] C --> I["Drive End
Gear Reducer and VFD"] I --> D I --> E J["Cooling Water Inlet 25 degC"] --> C C --> K["Hot Water Outlet 45 to 60 degC"] L["Dust Seals at Rotor Ends"] --> D L --> E style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333 style B fill:#FFB347,stroke:#333 style C fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FF6961,stroke:#900 style G fill:#77DD77,stroke:#333 style H fill:#A0E6FF,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow of the Mixing Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Step 1: Charge Polymer
Chunks into Hopper"] --> S2["Step 2: Ram Descends
and Seals Chamber"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Plasticization
Mechanical Heat Softens Polymer"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Add Fillers
Carbon Black or Silica"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Add Plasticizers
and Process Oil"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Dispersion Phase
High Shear 1000 to 5000 per s"] S6 --> S7{"Target Specific
Energy Reached?"} S7 -->|"No"| S6 S7 -->|"Yes"| S8["Step 7: Add Curatives
At Lower Temperature"] S8 --> S9["Step 8: Open Drop Door
Discharge Batch"] S9 --> S10["Step 9: Sheeting on
Two Roll Mill"] style S1 fill:#FFFACD style S6 fill:#FFB6C1 style S7 fill:#FFE4B5 style S9 fill:#98FB98 ``` ## 4.3 Rotor Type Comparison — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Rotor_Family direction TB RT["Rotor Family of Internal Mixers"] RT --> T["TANGENTIAL Non Intermeshing
FARREL Banbury Two Wing"] RT --> I["INTERMESHING Gear Like
Werner and Pfleiderer"] T --> T1["Strong Inter Rotor Shear"] T --> T2["High Friction Ratio 1 to 1.25"] T --> T3["Best for Rubber Compounding"] I --> I1["Gentle Distributive Mixing"] I --> I2["Synchronized 1 to 1 Ratio"] I --> I3["Best for PVC and TPE"] end style RT fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 style T fill:#FFB347 style I fill:#87CEEB ``` ## 4.4 Heat and Mass Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB MECH["Mechanical Energy Input
via Rotating Rotors"] -->|"Disipation"| POLY["Polymer Melt in Chamber"] POLY --> SHEAR["Shear Heating
Predominant Source"] POLY --> COMP["Compressive Heating
via Floating Weight"] SHEAR --> QT["Bulk Temperature Rise"] COMP --> QT QT --> JACK["Heat Transfer to Jacket
Q = hA dT"] JACK --> CW["Cooling Water Circulation"] POLY --> DISP["Dispersion of Additives
via Inter Rotor Shear"] POLY --> DIST["Distribution of Additives
via Chamber Convection"] style MECH fill:#FF6B6B style JACK fill:#4ECDC4 style CW fill:#95E1D3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **"Define an internal mixer. State any two advantages of an internal mixer over a two-roll mill for polymer compounding."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **internal mixer** is a closed, batch-type intensive mixing machine in which two counter-rotating rotors inside a jacketed chamber perform incorporation, dispersion, and distribution of compounding ingredients into a polymer matrix, under a pneumatically actuated floating weight (ram). **Two advantages over a two-roll mill:** 1. **Enclosed operation (1.5 marks):** The closed chamber prevents loss of volatile plasticizers, protects operators from fumes, and enables dust-free addition of carbon black — a major health hazard. Two-roll mills are open and expose workers. 2. **Higher dispersive efficiency (1.5 marks):** The combination of inter-rotor shear, chamber wall shear, and ram compression in a confined space produces *much higher* specific energy input per unit time, giving finer dispersion of agglomerates in shorter cycle times. --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **"What is the function of the floating weight (ram) in a Banbury mixer? What is the typical range of ram pressure maintained?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Function (2 marks):** The floating weight (ram) is a pneumatically/hydraulically actuated piston that: - Presses the polymer mass downward into the shear zone between the rotors. - Maintains a **constant compaction pressure** ensuring the batch is in continuous contact with the rotors and chamber wall (no slip). - Forces the polymer through the *high-shear clearance* between the rotor tips and the chamber wall, thereby improving dispersive mixing. - Acts as a safety relief — it lifts if chamber pressure becomes excessive. **Typical ram pressure range (1 mark):** **0.3 – 0.7 MPa** (3 – 7 bar). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Banbury-type internal mixer**. Discuss the role of the floating weight and the friction ratio between the rotors. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A laboratory internal mixer has a chamber volume of **0.0025 m³** and a filling factor of **0.70**. The rotor diameter is **0.08 m** and rotates at **2.5 rev/s**. The chamber wall-to-rotor clearance is **4 mm**. Calculate: (i) Batch volume and batch mass (assume density = 1050 kg/m³) (ii) Rotor tip speed (iii) Average shear rate in the clearance **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Construction (3.5 marks):** A Banbury mixer consists of: 1. **A heavy cylindrical mixing chamber** with a figure-8 cross-section, lined with replaceable hardened steel liners. The chamber is jacketed for circulation of cooling water or hot oil. 2. **Two counter-rotating rotors** of Banbury design (two-wing or four-wing), mounted on parallel horizontal shafts. The shafts are driven through a gear train providing a **friction ratio of 1 : 1.1 to 1 : 1.25** between the front and rear rotors. The rotors are cored for internal circulation of cooling water. 3. **A charging hopper and a pneumatically actuated floating weight (ram)** at the top, which presses down on the batch. 4. **A drop door** at the bottom of the chamber, opened pneumatically to discharge the batch onto a two-roll mill or extruder. 5. **A drive system** consisting of a high-horsepower motor, a gear reducer, and a variable-frequency drive for rotor speed control. **Working (2 marks):** Polymer, fillers, and additives are charged through the hopper. The ram descends and seals the chamber. The two rotors counter-rotate, with the rear rotor running slightly faster than the front. The polymer is dragged by friction into the clearance between the rotors and the chamber wall, subjected to **intense shear**, folded, and recirculated. After 2–6 minutes the drop door is opened and the batch is discharged as a single hot lump. **Role of floating weight (1 mark):** The ram maintains a continuous downward pressure of 0.3–0.7 MPa, forcing the polymer mass into the high-shear rotor tip region, preventing slippage at the wall, and ensuring a uniform fill factor. Without the ram, dispersion quality drops dramatically. **Role of friction ratio (0.5 mark):** The differential speed (friction ratio > 1) creates an additional **inter-rotor shear zone** between the two rotor wings. This is the primary site of *dispersive* mixing — the breakdown of carbon black agglomerates. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** **Given:** - $V_{chamber} = 0.0025 \; m^{3}$ - $\eta = 0.70$ - $D = 0.08 \; m$ - $N = 2.5 \; \text{rev/s}$ - $\delta = 4 \; \text{mm} = 0.004 \; m$ - $\rho = 1050 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$ **(i) Batch volume and batch mass (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{batch} &= \eta \cdot V_{chamber} = 0.70 \cdot 0.0025 \\[4pt] V_{batch} &= 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{3} \\[4pt] m &= \rho \cdot V_{batch} = 1050 \cdot 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt] m &= 1.8375 \; \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Rotor tip speed (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} v_{tip} &= \pi \cdot D \cdot N = \pi \cdot 0.08 \cdot 2.5 \\[4pt] v_{tip} &= 0.6283 \; \text{m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Average shear rate (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{\gamma} &= \frac{v_{tip}}{\delta} = \frac{0.6283}{0.004} \\[4pt] \dot{\gamma} &= 157.08 \; \text{s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **omit the unit conversion of clearance from mm to m** before dividing — this is the #1 source of numerical errors. A clearance of 4 mm means $\delta = 0.004 \; m$, not $0.004$ without units. Always write out the unit conversion explicitly to earn full marks. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Differentiate between **tangential and intermeshing rotors** in an internal mixer. State at least three differences. Discuss why intermeshing rotors are preferred for heat-sensitive polymers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of **specific energy input** in an internal mixer. A production Banbury mixer is rated at **2500 kW** and runs a batch of **250 kg** in a cycle of **3.5 minutes**. Calculate the specific energy input. If the polymer has $C_p = 1800 \; J/(kg \cdot K)$, estimate the **adiabatic temperature rise** of the batch. Comment on how cooling-water flow through the jacket controls the actual temperature. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Three key differences (4.5 marks; 1.5 each):** | # | Tangential Rotors | Intermeshing Rotors | |---|---|---| | 1 | Rotors do *not* mesh; clearance is large (3–10 mm). | Rotors mesh like gear teeth; clearance is very small (1–2 mm). | | 2 | Operate at friction ratio 1 : 1.1 to 1 : 1.25. | Operate at synchronized 1 : 1 ratio (no friction differential). | | 3 | Generate high inter-rotor shear and high localized heating. | Generate moderate shear with uniform, lower bulk heating. | | 4 | Best for rubber, high-viscosity compounds. | Best for PVC, TPE, and heat-sensitive thermoplastics. | **Why intermeshing for heat-sensitive polymers (2.5 marks):** In tangential rotors, the high friction ratio and small clearance between rotor tip and chamber wall produce **local hot spots** in the polymer. For polymers like **PVC, which degrades above ~180 °C releasing HCl**, this local overheating causes thermal degradation and discoloration. Intermeshing rotors, by contrast: - Operate at synchronized 1:1 ratio, eliminating inter-rotor friction heating. - Distribute the material more uniformly through the chamber, avoiding stagnation zones. - Generate a gentler, more uniform shear field, keeping the bulk temperature rise smaller and more predictable. Hence intermeshing rotors are preferred for thermally sensitive polymers where degradation, not agglomerate dispersion, is the limiting concern. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** **Concept of Specific Energy Input (2 marks):** The **specific energy input** $E_{spec}$ is the mechanical work delivered by the rotors to *each unit mass* of the batch. It is the master control variable in modern internal mixers: $$ E_{spec} = \frac{P \cdot t}{m} $$ It correlates directly with the **state of mix** (degree of dispersion, distributive uniformity) and is independent of minor variations in polymer lot, ambient temperature, or operator skill. Modern mixer control systems integrate the power draw over time and stop the cycle when a *target $E_{spec}$* is reached — guaranteeing **batch-to-batch consistency**. **Calculation (3 marks):** Given: $P = 2500 \; kW = 2.5 \times 10^{6} \; W$; $t = 3.5 \; \text{min} = 210 \; s$; $m = 250 \; kg$. $$ \begin{aligned} E_{spec} &= \frac{P \cdot t}{m} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{6} \cdot 210}{250} \\[4pt] E_{spec} &= \frac{5.25 \times 10^{8}}{250} = 2.1 \times 10^{6} \; \text{J/kg} \\[4pt] E_{spec} &= 2.1 \; \text{MJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ This lies in the typical industrial range of **1 – 3 MJ/kg** for rubber compounding — a realistic value. ✓ **Adiabatic temperature rise (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T_{adiabatic} &= \frac{E_{spec}}{C_p} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{6}}{1800} \\[4pt] \Delta T_{adiabatic} &= 1166.7 \; \text{K} \end{aligned} $$ This is *physically impossible* — the polymer would have decomposed many times over. Hence the calculation is purely theoretical and is presented to emphasize that *adiabatic* operation is **never** the real case. **Role of jacket cooling (1 mark):** In practice, the chamber is **jacketed for water (or oil) circulation**, and a large fraction of $E_{spec}$ is removed as $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$. With a typical overall heat-transfer coefficient $h \approx 200 \; W/(m^{2} \cdot K)$ and $\Delta T = 30 \; K$ between bulk polymer and cooling water, the heat removal rate is significant. The actual steady-state temperature is the balance of viscous dissipation and jacket removal — and is held **well below 200 °C** to prevent degradation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert the cycle time from **minutes to seconds** before multiplying by power (in watts). This causes a 60× error in $E_{spec}$. Another frequent mistake: writing the adiabatic temperature rise with units of °C — both Kelvin and Celsius intervals are numerically equal, but you must state "K" or "°C" explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU 2024 ESE)** - ✅ An **internal mixer** is a *closed*, *batch*, *intensive* mixer — distinguish it from two-roll mills (open) and extruders (continuous). - ✅ The **Banbury mixer** is the canonical example; the **Shaw, Krupp, Bolling, and POMINI** are commercial variants. - ✅ The **floating weight (ram)** maintains **0.3 – 0.7 MPa** pressure and is essential for dispersive mixing. - ✅ **Friction ratio** of rotors is **1 : 1.1 to 1 : 1.25** for tangential, **1 : 1** for intermeshing. - ✅ **Tangential rotors** = high shear, best for **rubber**. **Intermeshing rotors** = gentle, best for **PVC / TPE**. - ✅ **Rotor tip speed** $v_{tip} = \pi D N$ is the primary kinematic variable. - ✅ **Shear rate** $\dot{\gamma} = v_{tip} / \delta$ lies in the range **1000 – 5000 s⁻¹** during dispersion. - ✅ **Filling factor** $\eta$ is held at **0.55 – 0.75** for efficient operation. - ✅ **Specific energy input** $E_{spec} = Pt/m$ is the *master control variable* in modern automated mixers (typical 1–3 MJ/kg). - ✅ **Power number** $N_{Po} = P / (\rho N^3 D^5)$ is the **scaling law** used to scale-up from lab to production. - ✅ Cooling is via **jacketed chamber**; heat removal $\dot{Q} = hA\Delta T$ with $h \approx 200 \; W/(m^2 \cdot K)$. - ✅ The **discharge drop door** is pneumatically actuated and is the only opening for batch release. - ✅ **Five stages** of a mixing cycle: Incorporation → Plasticization → Addition → Dispersion → Curatives. - ✅ In **laminar regime** ($Re < 10$), power scales as $\rho N^3 D^5$ — a textbook favourite for derivation questions. - ✅ Know the **adiabatic temperature rise formula** $\Delta T = E_{spec}/C_p$ and the practical role of jacket cooling.

topics.title: Comparison Between Open Mill and Internal Mixer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Comparison Between Open Mill and Internal Mixer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Open Mill (Two-Roll Mixing Mill)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The **Open Mill** (also called a **Two-Roll Mill** or **Rubber Mixing Mill**) is a primary, open-to-atmosphere, batch-type polymer processing machine consisting of two horizontally mounted, counter-rotating rolls of equal diameter, separated by a small adjustable gap (the **nip**), used for mastication, compounding, sheeting, and warm-up of elastomers and thermoplastics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU PCPOT502 (Module 2 – Compounding & Mixing Equipment), the open mill is classified as an **intermittent-duty, low-pressure, open-type** batch mixer with a typical **friction ratio** ranging from **1 : 1.1 to 1 : 1.4**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of the open mill as a **domestic pasta machine** — two hard cylinders pressing dough (the polymer) while turning in opposite directions. Because one cylinder turns slightly faster than the other, the dough gets dragged, stretched, and "kneaded." Now imagine scaling this pasta machine up to industrial size (rolls up to **2.5 m long** and **0.85 m diameter**) and feeding it hot, sticky rubber compound — that is exactly how an open mill operates. The operator must repeatedly cut, fold, and re-feed the rubber bank by hand, just like a baker working dough.

---

### 1.2 Internal Mixer (Banbury Mixer)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The **Internal Mixer** (typified by the **Banbury Mixer**, patented 1916 by Fernley H. Banbury) is a high-intensity, **enclosed (closed-chamber)**, batch-type intensive mixer consisting of a figure-8 (or "dumb-bell") shaped mixing chamber, two non-intermeshing (tangential) or intermeshing rotors, a floating pneumatic/hydraulic **ram** that closes the chamber, and a discharge door, used for the compounding, mastication, and masterbatching of polymers, rubbers, and PVC dry-blends.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The internal mixer operates on the principle of **forced convection + high-shear convection mixing**, governed by the dimensionless **fill factor (f)** = (Volume of charge) / (Free working volume of chamber), typically held between **0.55 and 0.85** for optimum power draw and dispersion quality.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Picture a **heavy-duty meat grinder with two intermeshing screws inside a sealed hopper**. Instead of meat, you are feeding polymer chunks. A hydraulic piston (the **ram**) sits on top, pressing the charge down into the rotors, just like your hand pushes meat into the grinder. The sealed chamber means heat, dust, and fumes are contained — like cooking in a **pressure cooker** versus an open pan. The result is faster cooking (mixing) with much less mess and far higher shear.

---

### 1.3 At-a-Glance: What is the "Comparison" Really About?

The comparison is **not** about which is "better." It is about selecting the **right tool for the right job** based on:
- Production volume (kg/hr)
- Mix homogeneity required
- Heat sensitivity of the compound
- Capital cost
- Operator skill and safety
- Type of polymer (elastomer, PVC, TPE, masterbatch)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Trade-off Principle:** The Open Mill trades **mixing intensity** for **simplicity, visibility, and flexibility**; the Internal Mixer trades **simplicity** for **mixing intensity, dust control, and reproducibility**.

---

### 1.4 Quick Visual of the Two Systems

| Parameter | Open Mill | Internal Mixer |
|---|---|---|
| Chamber | Open to air | Fully enclosed |
| Mixing Action | Shear + manual folding | High-shear + forced convection |
| Typical Capacity | 5 – 200 kg/batch | 1 – 1000+ kg/batch |
| Mixing Time | 15 – 30 min | 1 – 6 min |
| Capital Cost | Low | High (5× to 10×) |
| Operator Skill | High (manual) | Moderate (automated) |

*(Note: Detailed comparison matrices appear in Section 3.)*

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Open Mill

The open mill works on the principle of **friction-induced shear** in the **nip region** (the wedge-shaped converging gap between the two rolls):

1. **Charge is fed** onto the slower, hotter (or equal-temperature) front roll.
2. The polymer forms a **rolling bank** at the nip entrance due to the **friction ratio** $FR > 1$.
3. As the bank passes through the nip, it is **subjected to intense shear** $\gamma \dot{} = v_n / h$, where $v_n$ is the differential surface velocity and $h$ is the nip gap.
4. The sheet adheres to the faster (rear) roll and is **stripped off** by a knife or doctor blade.
5. The operator **cuts, folds, and re-feeds** the sheet to improve **distributive mixing**.

**Key Physics — The "Bank" and the "Bite":**

- **Nip angle** $\alpha$ (angle subtended at the roll center by the bite zone):

$$\cos(\alpha) = 1 + \frac{R + h}{R + H} - \frac{(2R + H + h)}{2(R + H)}$$

where $R$ = roll radius, $H$ = bank height, $h$ = nip gap. For $H \gg h$, this simplifies to the **classic approximation**:

$$\alpha \approx \sqrt{\frac{2h}{R}}$$

- **Friction ratio** $FR$ (KTU-favorite formula):

$$FR = \frac{V_f}{V_s} = \frac{\pi D_f N_f}{\pi D_s N_s} = \frac{D_f N_f}{D_s N_s}$$

Since $D_f = D_s$ (equal-diameter rolls) in nearly all industrial mills:

$$FR = \frac{N_f}{N_s}$$

- **Surface speed of a roll** $V$ (m/min):

$$V = \pi D N$$

where $D$ is in metres and $N$ is in rev/min (gives $V$ in m/min when $D$ is in m and $N$ in rev/min, multiplied by $\pi$).

- **Throughput (capacity) of an open mill** $Q$ (kg/hr):

$$Q = 60 \pi D N L \rho s \, f_e$$

where $D$ = roll diameter (m), $N$ = roll speed (rev/min), $L$ = roll face length (m), $\rho$ = compound density (kg/m³), $s$ = sheet thickness (m), $f_e$ = efficiency factor (typically 0.7 – 0.9).

- **Power consumption** $P$ (W):

$$P = 2 \pi N T$$

where $T$ = torque on the roll shaft (N·m), $N$ = rev/s.

- **Coulomb friction law** (governs grip in the nip):

$$\tau = \mu P_n$$

where $\tau$ = shear stress, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $P_n$ = normal pressure.

---

### 2.2 Operational Principle — Internal Mixer

The internal mixer works on the principle of **forced high-shear convection** between two counter-rotating rotors and the chamber wall:

1. **Charge is dropped** into the hopper from above; the **ram descends** under pneumatic/hydraulic pressure (typically 0.3 – 0.7 MPa).
2. The rotors (tangential or intermeshing) **drag the material** into the high-shear region between rotor tip and chamber wall.
3. **Intensive shearing + folding** occurs continuously without operator intervention.
4. After a preset **time, temperature, or energy input**, the **discharge door** opens and a slug is dropped onto a two-roll mill for sheeting out.

**Key Physics:**

- **Fill factor** $f$ (KTU-favorite — appears in nearly every past paper):

$$f = \frac{V_{charge}}{V_{free}}$$

- **Free working volume** $V_{free}$ = (Geometric chamber volume) − (Volume occupied by rotors).

- **Power draw** $P$ for a Banbury mixer (empirical):

$$P = k \cdot N^2 \cdot D^3 \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot f$$

where $k$ = constant depending on rotor geometry, $N$ = rotor speed, $D$ = rotor diameter, $L$ = rotor length, $\rho$ = compound density, $f$ = fill factor.

- **Specific energy input** $E_s$ (kJ/kg) — the KTU "quality-of-mixing" metric:

$$E_s = \frac{P \cdot t}{m_{charge}}$$

where $P$ = power (kW), $t$ = mixing time (s), $m_{charge}$ = batch mass (kg).

- **Ram pressure** $P_{ram}$ is a **process variable**, not a fixed design constant — it is set by the operator based on compound viscosity.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of numerical and conceptual questions on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friction Ratio (Open Mill) | $FR = N_f / N_s$ | dimensionless | 1.10 – 1.40 |
| 2 | Roll Surface Speed | $V = \pi D N$ | m/min | 15 – 30 m/min (front) |
| 3 | Nip Angle (approx.) | $\alpha \approx \sqrt{2h / R}$ | rad | 0.05 – 0.20 rad |
| 4 | Open Mill Throughput | $Q = 60 \pi D N L \rho s f_e$ | kg/hr | 50 – 2000 kg/hr |
| 5 | Power (Rotating Shaft) | $P = 2 \pi N T$ | W | Use $N$ in rev/s |
| 6 | Coulomb Friction | $\tau = \mu P_n$ | Pa | $\mu$ for rubber-steel ≈ 0.3 – 0.6 |
| 7 | Shear Rate in Nip | $\dot{\gamma} = v_n / h$ | s⁻¹ | 100 – 1000 s⁻¹ |
| 8 | Fill Factor (Internal Mixer) | $f = V_{charge} / V_{free}$ | dimensionless | 0.55 – 0.85 |
| 9 | Banbury Power (empirical) | $P = k N^2 D^3 L \rho f$ | W | $k \approx 0.005$ – 0.02 |
| 10 | Specific Energy Input | $E_s = P t / m$ | kJ/kg | 200 – 1500 kJ/kg for rubber |
| 11 | Ram Pressure | $P_{ram} = F_{ram} / A_{ram}$ | Pa | 0.3 – 0.7 MPa typical |
| 12 | Discharge Temperature | $T_d = T_0 + \Delta T$ | °C | Controlled by water/oil in walls |

*(Reminder: All `|` symbols in formulas above are intentionally outside markdown tables, written as LaTeX \vert where needed — but here all are inline formulas, so no table-pipe conflicts arise.)*

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Open Mills** are still indispensable as **warm-up mills** feeding calendering lines, as **laboratory mixers** in R&D (cost ~₹5–15 lakh), and as the **discharge sheeter** for Banbury mixers (the "1 + 1" Banbury + Mill line).
- **Internal Mixers (Banbury)** dominate the **tyre industry** (compounding of natural rubber, SBR, BR, carbon black, curatives), **PVC dry-blend** manufacture, and **masterbatch** production. A single Farrel 270-litre Banbury can process 1.5–2 tonnes of rubber compound per hour.
- The **comparison** thus dictates **factory layout**: small-scale R&D and pilot plants lean on open mills; high-volume continuous or large-batch production is impossible without internal mixers.
