## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Friction Ratio and its Role

**Statement:** Prove that for an open mill with equal-diameter rolls rotating at different speeds, the friction ratio is simply the ratio of rotational speeds.

**Step 1 — Define the surface velocity of any roll.**
A point on the surface of a roll of diameter $D$ rotating at $N$ revolutions per unit time travels a linear distance of $\pi D$ per revolution. Therefore, the surface (linear) velocity is:

$$V = \pi D N$$

**Step 2 — Apply to the two rolls.**
Let the front (slower) roll have $D_s, N_s$ and the rear (faster) roll have $D_f, N_f$. Their surface velocities are:

$$V_s = \pi D_s N_s, \qquad V_f = \pi D_f N_f$$

**Step 3 — Apply the friction ratio definition.**

$$FR = \frac{V_f}{V_s} = \frac{\pi D_f N_f}{\pi D_s N_s} = \frac{D_f N_f}{D_s N_s}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the equal-diameter condition.**
For an open (two-roll) mill, by design $D_f = D_s = D$. The $\pi$ and $D$ cancel:

$$FR = \frac{N_f}{N_s}$$

This is the **standard KTU answer**. A typical value of $FR = 1.25$ means the rear roll rotates 25% faster than the front roll, producing the **frictional drag** that creates the rolling bank and the shearing action in the nip.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Open-Mill Throughput

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the mass throughput of a two-roll mill producing a continuous sheet.

**Step 1 — Geometric delivery rate (volume per unit time).**
The faster roll carries away a sheet of thickness $s$, width $L$, at surface velocity $V_f$. The volume rate of the leaving sheet is:

$$\dot{V} = V_f \cdot L \cdot s = \pi D_f N_f L s$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mass rate using density.**

$$\dot{m}_{ideal} = \rho \cdot \pi D_f N_f L s$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the efficiency factor.**
In practice, the operator must stop, cut, fold, and re-feed the sheet, so the *continuous* mass rate is reduced:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \pi D_f N_f L s \cdot f_e$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/hr.**
Since the formula above gives kg/sec when using SI consistently, multiply by 3600, or by 60 if $N$ is in rev/min and the result is desired in kg/min. The common KTU form (kg/hr) is:

$$Q = 60 \pi D N L \rho s f_e$$

**Step 5 — Typical worked example.**
$D = 0.55$ m, $N = 0.4$ rev/s (24 rpm), $L = 1.5$ m, $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³, $s = 0.005$ m, $f_e = 0.8$:

$$Q = 60 \times \pi \times 0.55 \times 0.4 \times 1.5 \times 1100 \times 0.005 \times 0.8$$

$$Q = 60 \times \pi \times 0.55 \times 0.4 \times 1.5 \times 1100 \times 0.005 \times 0.8 \approx 273.6 \text{ kg/hr}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Nip Angle

**Statement:** Derive the simplified nip-angle expression $\alpha \approx \sqrt{2h / R}$.

**Step 1 — Geometric setup.**
The polymer bank of height $H$ contacts both rolls across a chord subtending angle $2\alpha$ at the roll centers. The minimum gap is $h$ (the nip setting). The maximum gap (at the bank entrance) is approximately $H$.

**Step 2 — Chord-gap relationship.**
For a circle of radius $R$, the perpendicular distance from the center to a chord of half-angle $\alpha$ is $R \cos(\alpha)$. The sagitta (gap) is:

$$h = R (1 - \cos \alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\alpha$.**

$$1 - \cos \alpha = \frac{h}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos \alpha = 1 - \frac{h}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Small-angle approximation.**
For $h \ll R$, expand $\cos \alpha \approx 1 - \alpha^2 / 2$:

$$1 - \frac{\alpha^2}{2} = 1 - \frac{h}{R}$$

$$\frac{\alpha^2}{2} = \frac{h}{R}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx \sqrt{\frac{2h}{R}}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
For $R = 0.25$ m, $h = 0.002$ m (2 mm):

$$\alpha = \sqrt{2 \times 0.002 / 0.25} = \sqrt{0.016} = 0.1265 \text{ rad} = 7.25°$$

This is the **bite angle** — the maximum angle at which the bank can still be "gripped" and dragged into the nip. If the natural angle of the bank exceeds $\alpha$, the rolls will **refuse the bite** and the polymer rides on top.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Fill Factor and Working Volume in an Internal Mixer

**Step 1 — Define the geometric chamber volume.**
For a Banbury with chamber length $L_c$, width $W_c$, and average depth $H_c$ (approximating as a rectangular box for simplicity):

$$V_{geom} = L_c \cdot W_c \cdot H_c$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the rotor volume.**

$$V_{free} = V_{geom} - V_{rotors}$$

**Step 3 — Define the fill factor.**

$$f = \frac{V_{charge}}{V_{free}}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to find optimum charge volume.**

$$V_{charge}^{opt} = f_{opt} \cdot V_{free}, \quad f_{opt} \in [0.55, 0.85]$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.**
- If $f < 0.55$: insufficient material → rotors "slip," low power draw, poor dispersion.
- If $f > 0.85$: chamber is **starved of free space** → rotor stalls, motor overload, no distributive mixing.
- **Why $f_{opt}$ matters (KTU classic):** Power draw is a **strong function** of $f$ (often $P \propto f^{1.5}$). Most plants set $f$ by trial to give the target temperature rise in the target cycle time.

---

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix (Analytical)

| # | Comparison Parameter | Open Mill | Internal Mixer | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mixing mechanism | Friction + manual folding | Forced high-shear + convection | Rotor tip-wall gap = 1–5 mm |
| 2 | Mixing time | 15 – 30 min | 1 – 6 min | Sealed chamber retains heat, no air-cooling loss |
| 3 | Temperature control | Poor (heat loss to air) | Excellent (water/oil jacket) | Enclosed walls enable jacketing |
| 4 | Dust/fume emission | High (open) | Negligible (sealed) | Health & safety, OSHA compliance |
| 5 | Operator skill required | Very high | Moderate | Manual cutting and folding are skilled tasks |
| 6 | Capital cost | ₹5 – 25 lakh | ₹50 lakh – 5+ crore | Rotor machining, ram hydraulics, drives |
| 7 | Batch size | 5 – 200 kg | 1 – 1000+ kg | Chamber volume scales 1 L to 1000+ L |
| 8 | Filler loading capacity | Limited (poor dispersion) | Excellent (high-shear) | Shear stress in Banbury > Mill by 10× |
| 9 | Colour/compound changeover | Easy (visible) | Hard (cleaning required) | Open mill = "see what you mix" |
| 10 | Energy efficiency | Low (heat loss dominates) | High (adiabatic-like) | Internal mixer uses >80% of motor input for mixing |
| 11 | Safety | Higher injury risk (roll nip) | Lower (enclosed) | Modern mills have trip wires, palm bars |
| 12 | Final sheet quality | Excellent (direct sheet-out) | Poor (must be sheeted on a mill downstream) | Banbury discharges a "slug" |
| 13 | Masterbatch / carbon black dispersion | Poor | Excellent | High local shear breaks agglomerates |
| 14 | PVC dry-blend suitability | Not used | Industry standard | Heated/cooled high-shear needed |
| 15 | Automation compatibility | Low | High | Sensors (T, P, E) easily retrofitted |
| 16 | Process repeatability | Operator-dependent | Recipe-controlled | Banbury data-logged in modern plants |

---

### 3.6 Numerical Example: Specific Energy

A Banbury mixer with rotor tip speed $V_t = 25$ m/s, charge mass $m = 50$ kg, mixing time $t = 4$ min, motor power $P = 250$ kW.

**Step 1 — Compute energy delivered.**

$$E = P \cdot t = 250 \text{ kW} \times (4 \times 60) \text{ s} = 250 \times 240 = 60{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Divide by mass to get specific energy.**

$$E_s = \frac{60{,}000}{50} = 1200 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with target.**
For good carbon-black dispersion in NR, the industry target is $E_s = 600$ – 1500 kJ/kg. Hence this batch meets the target.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

**Reading the diagram:** Charge enters, forms a bank at the nip (where the shear happens), sticks to the faster roll, is stripped as a sheet, and the operator cuts/folds/refeeds for distributive mixing. A safety trip interrupts the motor instantly if the operator's hand approaches the nip.

### 4.3 Internal Mixer (Banbury) — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[POLYMER CHARGE] --> B2[HOPPER]
    B2 --> B3[CHAMBER FIGURE 8]
    B4[FLOATING PNEUMATIC RAM] --> B3
    B5[ROTOR A TANGENTIAL] --> B3
    B6[ROTOR B TANGENTIAL] --> B3
    B3 --> B7[HIGH SHEAR ZONE TIP WALL GAP]
    B3 --> B8[JACKET FOR WATER OR OIL]
    B8 --> B9[TEMPERATURE CONTROL UNIT]
    B5 --> B10[GEARBOX]
    B6 --> B10
    B10 --> B11[DRIVE MOTOR HIGH POWER]
    B7 --> B12[DISCHARGE DOOR]
    B12 --> B13[SLUG TO TWO ROLL MILL]
    B13 --> B14[SHEET FOR PELLETIZER OR CALENDER]
    B3 --> B15[INSTRUMENTATION T P E SENSORS]
    B15 --> B16[CONTROL PLC HMI]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Charge enters the chamber, the ram closes the system, two counter-rotating rotors create high-shear zones, the jacketed walls remove heat, sensors (T, P, energy) feed back to the PLC which opens the discharge door at the set point, and the slug falls onto a downstream two-roll mill for sheeting.

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OM[OPEN MILL LINE]
        O1[CHARGE] --> O2[OPEN MILL]
```

**Key observation:** The Internal Mixer line is *always* followed by an open mill (the famous **1+1 Banbury-mill setup**). The open mill, used alone, is a self-contained line.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
Define **friction ratio** of a two-roll mill. Why is it always kept greater than 1 in rubber processing? Mention the typical range used in industry.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Friction ratio $FR$ of a two-roll mill is the ratio of the surface speeds of the two rolls, $FR = V_f / V_s = N_f / N_s$ (for equal-diameter rolls).
- **(1 mark) Reason:** A value greater than 1 ensures that the polymer is **dragged into the nip** by friction and is **sheared** in the nip region; without it, the bank would rotate with the front roll and no mixing would occur.
- **(1 mark) Typical range:** Industry uses $FR = 1.10$ to $1.40$ (commonly 1.25); higher values give more shear but risk roll slippage and overheating.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Repeated Concept]**
What is **fill factor** in an internal mixer? State its typical range and explain what happens if the fill factor is too low or too high.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Fill factor $f = V_{charge} / V_{free}$, the ratio of the volume of polymer charge to the free working volume of the mixer chamber.
- **(1 mark) Typical range:** $f = 0.55$ to $0.85$.
- **(1 mark) Consequences:**
  - *Too low* ($f < 0.55$): Rotor slip, insufficient shear, poor dispersion, low power draw.
  - *Too high* ($f > 0.85$): Rotor stalls, motor overload, poor distributive mixing, risk of equipment damage.

---

### 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Many students **omit units** in numerical answers — KTU deducts **0.5 to 1 mark per question** for missing units.
> 2. When asked to "compare," students often write a one-sided essay. KTU **expects a tabular or point-wise side-by-side** — allocate at least 5 distinct comparison points.
> 3. In derivations, students skip the **small-angle approximation justification** for the nip angle — this costs the "step" mark.
> 4. Confusing **friction ratio** with **gear ratio** is a recurring error.
> 5. Forgetting that open mill is used as the **downstream sheeter** for the Banbury loses the "process linkage" mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an **open mill**. Explain the role of the **friction ratio** and the **rolling bank** in the mixing process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Construction (3 marks):*
- Two horizontal chilled-cast-iron or hardened-steel rolls of equal diameter ($D = 0.25$ – 0.85 m), mounted in heavy bearing housings in a robust cast-iron frame.
- Rolls are driven through a **gearbox** by an electric motor; the gear train provides the **friction ratio** (1.1 – 1.4) — usually by using gears of different sizes on the two roll shafts.
- The **nip gap** between the rolls is adjustable (typically 0.5 – 25 mm) by a **screw-down mechanism** with hand-wheel or hydraulic actuation.
- One or both rolls have **internal bores** for circulation of heating/cooling water or steam.
- A **scraper blade (doctor blade)** strips the sheet from the faster roll.
- **Safety devices**: trip wire, palm bar, emergency stop — all designed to halt the mill within 0.1 s if the operator's hand approaches the nip.

*Working (2 marks):*
1. Compound is fed onto the slower (front) roll.
2. Due to $FR > 1$, the faster (rear) roll drags the material through the nip, generating shear.
3. The polymer forms a **rolling bank** at the nip entrance.
4. The sheet sticks to the rear roll and is stripped by the doctor blade.
5. The operator **cuts, folds, and re-feeds** the sheet 4–8 times for distributive mixing.

*Role of friction ratio and bank (2 marks):*
- Friction ratio creates the **velocity gradient** in the nip, producing the **shear stress** $\tau = \mu P_n$ that breaks down agglomerates and disperses fillers.
- The rolling bank acts as a **reservoir** that ensures continuous feeding of material into the nip — without it, the nip would starve and the mill would "chatter."

---

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **approximate nip angle** of a two-roll mill. Given: roll diameter $D = 0.5$ m, nip setting $h = 3$ mm. Calculate the nip angle in degrees. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Derivation (5 marks):*

For a chord of half-angle $\alpha$ at the roll surface, the sagitta (gap) $h$ is related to the roll radius $R$ by:

$$h = R (1 - \cos \alpha)$$

Solving for $\cos \alpha$:

$$\cos \alpha = 1 - \frac{h}{R}$$

For small $\alpha$, using the Taylor expansion $\cos \alpha \approx 1 - \alpha^2 / 2$:

$$1 - \frac{\alpha^2}{2} \approx 1 - \frac{h}{R}$$

$$\frac{\alpha^2}{2} = \frac{h}{R}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx \sqrt{\frac{2h}{R}}}$$

*Numerical substitution (2 marks):*

Given $D = 0.5$ m → $R = 0.25$ m, $h = 0.003$ m:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.003}{0.25}} = \sqrt{0.024} = 0.1549 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\alpha = 0.1549 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 8.88°$$

**Answer:** Nip angle $\alpha \approx 8.88°$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an **Internal (Banbury) Mixer**. Explain the functions of the **rotors**, the **ram**, and the **discharge door**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Construction (3 marks):*
- **Chamber**: Figure-8 (or dumb-bell) shaped, jacketed for water/oil circulation, made of wear-resistant alloy steel.
- **Two rotors**: Mounted on parallel shafts, rotating at slightly different speeds (typically 1:1.05 to 1:1.15), either **tangential** (non-intermeshing, most common) or **intermeshing**. Each rotor has helical flights ("wings" or "blades") to push material axially and provide kneading.
- **Hopper and floating ram**: A pneumatically/hydraulically actuated piston that descends into the chamber, sealing it and applying pressure (0.3 – 0.7 MPa) on the charge.
- **Discharge door**: At the bottom, hydraulically operated; drops the mixed "slug" onto a downstream two-roll mill.
- **Drive system**: High-torque, low-speed (30 – 60 rpm) drive motor coupled through a reduction gearbox.
- **Instrumentation**: Temperature, pressure, and energy sensors feeding back to a PLC for recipe control.

*Working (2 marks):*
1. Pre-weighed polymer and additives are charged into the hopper.
2. The ram descends, sealing and pressurizing the chamber.
3. Rotors counter-rotate, dragging material into the small gap (1–5 mm) between the rotor tip and the chamber wall — this is where **intensive shear** occurs.
4. The compound is continuously folded, sheared, and mixed.
5. When the preset time/temperature/energy is reached, the discharge door opens and the slug drops out.

*Functions (2 marks):*
- **Rotors**: Provide the high-shear and kneading action; the helical flights give axial conveying and prevent dead zones.
- **Ram**: Seals the chamber (dust/fume control), compresses the charge against the rotors (ensures bite), and allows pressure-based process control.
- **Discharge door**: Provides rapid, complete emptying of the chamber to avoid thermal degradation; also enables the slug to fall directly onto a downstream mill.

---

**(b)** Define **fill factor** and **specific energy input** for an internal mixer. A Banbury mixer is charged with 80 kg of compound. The motor draws 200 kW during a 5-minute cycle. Calculate the **specific energy input** and comment on whether the value is suitable for good carbon-black dispersion in natural rubber. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Definitions (3 marks):*
- **Fill factor** $f$ is the ratio of the volume of polymer charge to the free working volume of the mixing chamber: $f = V_{charge} / V_{free}$. Typical range: 0.55 – 0.85.
- **Specific energy input** $E_s$ is the mechanical energy delivered per unit mass of compound: $E_s = P \cdot t / m$. It is a **scale-up invariant** and is the most reliable indicator of mix quality across different mixer sizes.

*Calculation (3 marks):*

Given: $P = 200$ kW, $t = 5$ min $= 300$ s, $m = 80$ kg.

Total energy delivered:

$$E = P \times t = 200 \times 300 = 60{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

Specific energy:

$$E_s = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{60{,}000}{80} = 750 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

*Comment (1 mark):*
The typical range for good carbon-black dispersion in natural rubber is **600 – 1500 kJ/kg**. The calculated value of **750 kJ/kg** falls comfortably within this range. The batch is therefore expected to achieve satisfactory dispersion quality.

**Answer:** $E_s = 750$ kJ/kg — **suitable** for good carbon-black dispersion in NR.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this checklist — read it 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **Open Mill** = open, two rolls, manual, low intensity, low cost, good for warm-up and R&D.
- ☐ **Internal Mixer (Banbury)** = enclosed, two rotors, automatic, high intensity, high cost, industry standard for high-volume compounding.
- ☐ **Friction ratio** $FR = N_f / N_s$ (equal diameters); typical 1.10 – 1.40; **never 1.0** in rubber processing.
- ☐ **Nip angle** $\alpha \approx \sqrt{2h / R}$ — small-angle approximation valid for $h \ll R$.
- ☐ **Coulomb's friction law** $\tau = \mu P_n$ governs the grip in the nip region.
- ☐ **Fill factor** $f = V_{charge} / V_{free}$ — optimum 0.55 – 0.85; too low or too high ruins the mix.
- ☐ **Specific energy input** $E_s = Pt/m$ is the **gold standard** for comparing mixers and scaling up.
- ☐ **Banbury line = Banbury + Mill** (1+1 configuration) — the mill is always needed downstream for sheeting.
- ☐ **Open mill safety**: nip is the most dangerous region in any polymer plant — always use the trip wire and palm bar.
- ☐ **Masterbatch and carbon black** require high-shear → use Banbury, not mill.
- ☐ **PVC dry-blend** → only internal mixer (high-shear heated/cooled chamber).
- ☐ **Quick comparison trigger words** (use in exams): *open vs. closed*, *manual vs. automatic*, *low vs. high shear*, *lab vs. production*, *small batch vs. large batch*.
- ☐ **Examiner loves**: tabular comparison, neat sketches (even hand-drawn is fine), correct units, and the **specific energy concept**.

---

### 5.4 One-Line Memory Hooks (For Last-Minute Recall)

- *"**Mill** = **M**anual, **I**nexpensive, **L**ow-shear, **L**ab-friendly."*
- *"**Banbury** = **B**ig, **A**utomated, **N**on-stop, **B**atch, **U**niform, **R**obust, **Y**ield."*
- *"**FR > 1** = **F**riction **R**atio drags material in."*
- *"**f between 0.55 and 0.85** = **F**ill factor safe zone."*
- *"**E_s** = Energy per kg = the **G**olden **Q**uality **N**umber."*

---

*End of KTU PCPOT502 – Module 2 – Comparison Between Open Mill and Internal Mixer*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Banbury Mixing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Banbury Mixing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Banbury Mixer** (also called an *Internal Batch Mixer* or *Intensive Mixer*) is a high-shear, high-energy, batch-type mixing machine used in polymer and rubber processing. It was patented by **Fernley H. Banbury** in **1916** and is manufactured by Farrel Corporation. It consists of a closed mixing chamber housing two **counter-rotating, non-intermeshing rotors** that masticate, disperse, and homogenize the polymer compound against a heavy floating ram under controlled temperature conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Banbury Mixer is the *workhorse of discontinuous (batch) compounding*. It is rated as a **high-intensity internal mixer** because mixing energy is delivered directly *inside* the material, unlike external mixers (two-roll mills).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to mix chocolate chips uniformly into a thick dough using a spoon in a deep bowl. A spoon (extruder-like slow mixing) is gentle but takes ages and gives poor distribution. Now imagine plunging a powerful hand blender (rotors) into the bowl while pressing the dough down with your palm (ram). The blades tear, fold, and squeeze the dough against the bowl walls (chamber walls) — that is exactly what a Banbury mixer does to polymer dough, but on an industrial, thermostated scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mental Model:**
> - The **rotors** = your hand blender blades
> - The **mixing chamber** = the deep bowl
> - The **pneumatic/hydraulic ram** = your pressing palm
> - The **discharge door** = the bottom flap you open to dump dough

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

- **Fill Factor (F):** typically **0.55 – 0.75** (fraction of chamber volume occupied by material)
- **Batch Size:** **40 – 600 liters** chamber volume (laboratory to industrial)
- **Rotor Speed:** **30 – 100 rpm** (some high-speed units up to **200 rpm**)
- **Ram Pressure:** **0.2 – 0.5 MPa** (pneumatic), up to **1.0 MPa** (hydraulic)
- **Mixing Cycle:** **2 – 10 minutes** per batch
- **Specific Energy Input:** **0.3 – 1.2 kWh/kg** for thermoplastics
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Banbury mixer operates on a **discrete batch cycle** with four distinct stages:

1. **Charging (Loading):** Polymer resin, plasticizers, stabilizers, fillers, and curatives are introduced through the feed chute. Order of addition is critical: typically polymer first, then fillers, then curatives last.
2. **Plasticization & Mastication:** The rotors shearing the bulk compound convert the polymer from a granular/powdery state into a coherent viscoelastic mass. Frictional heat raises the temperature toward melt-processing conditions.
3. **Dispersion & Distribution:** As temperature rises, viscosity drops. The rotors now break down agglomerates (filler agglomerates, carbon black clusters) into primary particles (**dispersion**) and spread them uniformly throughout the melt (**distribution**).
4. **Discharge:** The lower sliding door is opened, and the entire batch is ejected as a single mass (typically dropped onto a two-roll mill for sheeting or fed to an extruder for pelletizing).

### The "Why" Behind the Process

- The **floating ram** maintains pressure on the charge, ensuring the material stays within the high-shear zone between the rotors and the chamber wall.
- **Counter-rotating rotors** create a *figure-eight* flow pattern, forcing the material from one rotor to the other — this is the key to distributive mixing.
- **Rotor tip speed** (not RPM) determines shear rate: $ \dot{\gamma} \approx v_{tip} / h $, where $ h $ is the clearance.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Rotor Tip Speed | $v_{tip} = \pi \, D \, N$ | m/s | D = rotor diameter, N = rotational speed (rev/s) |
| 2 | Average Shear Rate (Banbury) | $\dot{\gamma}_{avg} = \dfrac{\pi \, D \, N}{h}$ | s⁻¹ | h = clearance between rotor and wall |
| 3 | Fill Factor | $F = \dfrac{V_{charge}}{V_{chamber}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.55–0.75 |
| 4 | Batch Production Rate | $Q_{b} = \dfrac{\rho \, V_{chamber} \, F}{t_{cycle}}$ | kg/s | $t_{cycle}$ = charge + mix + discharge time |
| 5 | Specific Energy Input | $E_{s} = \dfrac{P \cdot t_{mix}}{m_{batch}}$ | kJ/kg or kWh/kg | Critical scale-up index |
| 6 | Power Number (Newtonian) | $N_{p} = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}}$ | dimensionless | Used for scale-up |
| 7 | Discharge Temperature Rise | $\Delta T = \dfrac{E_{s}}{C_{p}}$ | °C or K | $C_{p}$ = specific heat of compound |
| 8 | Ram Downward Force | $F_{ram} = P_{ram} \cdot A_{ram}$ | N | $A_{ram}$ = ram cross-sectional area |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Trick Question:** The Banbury mixer is *not* a continuous mixer. If asked for "throughput," the answer is a **batch production rate** with units **kg/batch** or **kg/hr** (after dividing by cycle time).

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

The Banbury mixer is the *backbone of the rubber and PVC industries*. It is the standard compounder for:
- Tire tread compounds (natural rubber + carbon black + sulfur)
- PVC plastisols and dry blends
- Masterbatches (color and additive concentrates)
- Thermoset preforms (phenolic, melamine molding powders)
- High-viscosity polymer blends and alloys
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rotor Tip Speed and Shear Rate

**Problem Setup:** A Banbury mixer has rotors of diameter $D = 0.45$ m rotating at $N = 60$ rpm. The clearance between the rotor tip and chamber wall is $h = 5$ mm. Calculate (i) the tip speed, (ii) the average shear rate, and (iii) the average shear stress for a rubber compound of viscosity $\mu = 10^{4}$ Pa·s at processing temperature.

### Step 1: Convert rotational speed to revolutions per second

$$
N_{rps} = \dfrac{60 \text{ rev/min}}{60 \text{ s/min}} = 1.0 \text{ rev/s}
$$

*Reasoning:* Standard SI unit for rotational frequency in derived formulas is rev/s (Hz). One full revolution per second corresponds to 1.0 Hz.

### Step 2: Calculate the rotor tip speed

$$
v_{tip} = \pi \, D \, N_{rps} = \pi \times 0.45 \times 1.0 = 1.4137 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Reasoning:* Each point on the rotor tip travels a circumferential distance $\pi D$ per revolution. Multiplying by revolutions per second gives the linear velocity at the tip.

### Step 3: Calculate the average shear rate in the clearance

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{avg} = \dfrac{v_{tip}}{h} = \dfrac{1.4137}{0.005} = 282.74 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

*Reasoning:* In the narrow gap between the rotor tip and the chamber wall, the velocity drops from $v_{tip}$ at the rotor surface to approximately zero at the stationary wall. This is analogous to Couette flow, where shear rate equals velocity gradient.

### Step 4: Calculate the average shear stress (Newtonian assumption)

$$
\tau = \mu \, \dot{\gamma}_{avg} = 10^{4} \times 282.74 = 2.827 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 2.827 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Reasoning:* For a Newtonian fluid, shear stress equals viscosity multiplied by shear rate. Real polymer melts are non-Newtonian (shear-thinning), so this value represents an upper-bound estimate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A shear stress of 2.8 MPa in the clearance region explains why the Banbury mixer can break down carbon black agglomerates — the cohesive strength of typical carbon black clusters is in the 1–5 MPa range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Batch Production Rate

**Problem Setup:** A Banbury mixer with an effective chamber volume of $V_{chamber} = 250$ L is loaded with a PVC dry-blend of bulk density $\rho_{bulk} = 0.55$ g/cm³ at a fill factor $F = 0.65$. The total cycle consists of: charge time = 30 s, mixing time = 240 s, discharge time = 30 s. Calculate the production rate in kg/hr.

### Step 1: Determine the charge volume

$$
V_{charge} = F \times V_{chamber} = 0.65 \times 250 = 162.5 \text{ L} = 0.1625 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

*Reasoning:* The fill factor defines what fraction of the chamber's free volume is filled with material. Empty space above the material is required for the ram stroke and for material expansion during plasticization.

### Step 2: Calculate the batch mass

$$
m_{batch} = \rho_{bulk} \times V_{charge} = 550 \text{ kg/m}^{3} \times 0.1625 \text{ m}^{3} = 89.375 \text{ kg}
$$

*Reasoning:* Bulk density is appropriate here because the PVC blend is initially in powder form (not yet molten). Once melted, the material density would be higher.

### Step 3: Calculate the total cycle time

$$
t_{cycle} = t_{charge} + t_{mix} + t_{discharge} = 30 + 240 + 30 = 300 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ min}
$$

*Reasoning:* Total cycle time includes all non-productive and productive intervals. Mixing time is the only productive interval, but the others still limit throughput.

### Step 4: Calculate the production rate in kg/s and convert to kg/hr

$$
Q_{b} = \dfrac{m_{batch}}{t_{cycle}} = \dfrac{89.375}{300} = 0.2979 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_{b,hr} = 0.2979 \times 3600 = 1072.5 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

*Reasoning:* 3600 seconds per hour. The result is the *average* throughput including all dead time.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Outcomes

| Process Variable | Increase Effect on Mix Quality | Increase Effect on Throughput | Risk if Overdone |
|------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| Rotor Speed (N) | Better dispersion (higher shear) | Slightly higher | Material degradation, scorch |
| Fill Factor (F) | Optimal around 0.65 | Higher mass per batch | Poor mixing, motor overload |
| Ram Pressure | Better ram efficiency | Marginal | Ram stalling, motor trip |
| Mixing Time | Better dispersion | Lower (longer cycle) | Thermal degradation, scorch |
| Jacket Temperature | Lower melt viscosity (better flow) | Faster cycle | Premature cure in rubber |
| Rotor Clearance (h) | Higher shear (smaller h) | Unchanged | Mechanical damage |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Banbury Mixer — Cross-Sectional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the functional architecture of a Banbury mixer, showing the major subsystems and their interactions. This serves as a substitute for a physical cross-section drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FeedSystem["FEEDING SYSTEM"]
        FeedHopperA["Feed Chute (Top Entry)"]
        WeighUnit["Batching & Weighing Unit"]
    end

    subgraph RamAssembly["RAM ASSEMBLY"]
        RamCyl["Hydraulic / Pneumatic Cylinder"]
        RamPiston["Floating Piston (Ram)"]
        RamGauge["Pressure Gauge"]
    end

    subgraph MixingCore["MIXING CHAMBER CORE"]
        ChamberWall["Water / Oil Jacketed Chamber Wall"]
        RotorFront["Front Rotor (Driven)"]
        RotorRear["Rear Rotor (Driven)"]
        DustStopA["Front Dust Stop"]
        DustStopB["Rear Dust Stop"]
        DischargeDoor["Sliding Discharge Door (Bottom)"]
    end

    subgraph DriveUnit["DRIVE & POWER UNIT"]
        Motor["Electric Motor"]
        Gearbox["Reduction Gearbox"]
        Coupling["Flexible Coupling"]
    end

    subgraph ThermalUnit["THERMAL CONTROL UNIT"]
        Heater["Steam / Oil Heater"]
        Chiller["Chilled Water Circulator"]
        TC["Temperature Sensors (Wall + Melt)"]
    end

    subgraph ControlPanel["CONTROL PANEL"]
        PLC["PLC / DCS Controller"]
        HMI["Operator HMI Screen"]
    end

    FeedHopperA --> ChamberWall
    WeighUnit --> FeedHopperA
    RamCyl --> RamPiston
    RamPiston --> ChamberWall
    RamGauge --> RamCyl
    Motor --> Gearbox
    Gearbox --> Coupling
    Coupling --> RotorFront
    Coupling --> RotorRear
    Heater --> ChamberWall
    Chiller --> ChamberWall
    TC --> PLC
    RamGauge --> PLC
    DischargeDoor --> ChamberWall
    PLC --> HMI
    PLC --> Motor
    PLC --> RamCyl
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram (PFD) — Single Batch Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    state1(["START"]) --> step1["Charge: Polymer + Additives via Feed Chute"]
    step1 --> step2["Lower Ram → Apply Pressure"]
    step2 --> step3["Start Rotors → Begin Shear Mastication"]
    step3 --> step4["Monitor: Torque, Temp, Power"]
    step4 --> decision1{"Mix Quality\nAchieved?"}
    decision1 -- No --> step3
    decision1 -- Yes --> step5["Stop Rotors → Open Discharge Door"]
    step5 --> step6["Eject Batch → 2-Roll Mill / Extruder"]
    step6 --> step7["Close Door → Ready for Next Batch"]
    step7 --> step1
```

## 4.3 Rotor Geometry — Two Common Designs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Tangential["TANGENTIAL ROTOR (Standard Banbury)"]
        TT["Two rotors side by side, tips trace SEPARATE circles"]
        TAdv["High free volume → larger batches"]
        TDis["Lower dispersion efficiency vs. intermeshing"]
    end

    subgraph Intermeshing["INTERMESHING ROTOR (FCM / Farrel Continuous Mixer)"]
        IT["Two rotors interlock; tips trace OVERLAPPING circles"]
        IAdv["Excellent distributive mixing"]
        IDis["Lower free volume → smaller batches"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The classic "Banbury" trademark refers specifically to *tangential rotors*. *Intermeshing rotors* are typically called by other names (e.g., Farrel FCM in continuous mode, or "Intermix" by Shaw). KTU examiners may treat both as "Banbury-type" mixers.

## 4.4 Component Identification Table

| Label in Diagram | Physical Component | Function |
|------------------|-------------------|----------|
| Feed Chute | Vertical entry port | Receives polymer + additives |
| Floating Ram | Piston driven by cylinder | Applies pressure, holds material in shear zone |
| Rotor (Front/Rear) | Helical/flighted steel shaft | Provides shear and kneading |
| Chamber Wall | Heavy steel body with jacket | Contains compound, transfers heat |
| Dust Stops | Spring-loaded seals at chamber ends | Prevents material leakage past rotor ends |
| Discharge Door | Sliding/hinged bottom plate | Releases batched compound |
| Jacket | Internal channels in wall | Circulates heating/cooling fluid |
| Gearbox | Reduction gearing | Reduces motor RPM to rotor RPM |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Define a Banbury mixer and list its four main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Banbury mixer is a high-shear, batch-type internal mixer used for compounding polymers and rubber. Its four main components are:
1. **Mixing chamber** (jacketed body) – contains the compound
2. **Two counter-rotating rotors** – provide shear and kneading
3. **Floating ram (piston)** – applies pressure on the charge
4. **Discharge door** – ejects the finished batch

*(1 mark for definition; 2 marks for listing and briefly stating the function of each component.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]`
**What is the meaning of "fill factor" in a Banbury mixer? Why is it typically maintained between 0.55 and 0.75?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The fill factor $F$ is the ratio of charge volume to the free volume of the mixing chamber: $F = V_{charge} / V_{chamber}$.
- **Below 0.55:** Insufficient material — rotors cannot develop adequate pressure; poor shear and dispersion.
- **Above 0.75:** Overloading — material cannot flow freely; motor overload, poor mixing, and risk of ram stalling.
- **Optimum 0.55–0.75:** Balances batch size with effective material flow in the rotor-chamber clearance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working principle of a Banbury mixer with a neat labeled diagram. Discuss the role of rotors and the floating ram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Banbury mixer has a chamber volume of 200 L, operates at a fill factor of 0.65, and processes a polymer of bulk density 0.60 g/cm³. The cycle time is 6 minutes. Calculate (i) the batch mass, (ii) the production rate in kg/hr, and (iii) the specific energy input if the motor draws an average power of 90 kW during the 4-minute mixing interval.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
The Banbury mixer consists of a heavy, double-walled (jacketed) steel mixing chamber, a pair of rotors mounted horizontally on parallel axes, a feed chute at the top, a pneumatically/hydraulically driven floating ram, and a sliding discharge door at the bottom. The chamber is jacketed for circulation of heating or cooling fluid. The rotors are driven through a reduction gearbox by an electric motor.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
The compound is charged through the feed chute, the ram is lowered, and the rotors are started. The rotors rotate at different speeds (friction ratio typically 1.1:1 to 1.2:1) in opposite directions. Material is drawn into the high-shear clearance between the rotors and the chamber wall, then pushed axially and around in a figure-eight flow. The combination of compression (from the ram) and shearing (from the rotors) gives both **distributive** and **dispersive** mixing. After the cycle, the rotors are stopped, the discharge door is opened, and the batch is dropped onto a two-roll mill or extruder.

**Role of Rotors and Ram (1 mark):**
- *Rotors:* Provide shear (dispersive) and convective (distributive) mixing.
- *Floating Ram:* Maintains pressure on the compound, keeping it in the high-shear zone; the position of the ram serves as an indicator of mix state (drops as material fluxionalizes).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $V_{chamber} = 200$ L $= 0.200$ m³
- $F = 0.65$
- $\rho_{bulk} = 0.60$ g/cm³ $= 600$ kg/m³
- $t_{cycle} = 6$ min $= 360$ s
- $P = 90$ kW
- $t_{mix} = 4$ min $= 240$ s

**(i) Batch mass (2 marks):**

$$
V_{charge} = F \times V_{chamber} = 0.65 \times 0.200 = 0.130 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

$$
m_{batch} = \rho_{bulk} \times V_{charge} = 600 \times 0.130 = 78.0 \text{ kg}
$$

**(ii) Production rate (2 marks):**

$$
Q_{b} = \dfrac{m_{batch}}{t_{cycle}} = \dfrac{78.0}{360} = 0.2167 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_{b,hr} = 0.2167 \times 3600 = 780 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**(iii) Specific energy input (3 marks):**

$$
E_{s} = \dfrac{P \times t_{mix}}{m_{batch}} = \dfrac{90{,}000 \text{ W} \times 240 \text{ s}}{78.0 \text{ kg}}
$$

$$
E_{s} = \dfrac{21{,}600{,}000}{78.0} = 276{,}923 \text{ J/kg} \approx 277 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Equivalently, $E_s = 277 / 3600 = 0.0768$ kWh/kg.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the process variables that affect the quality of mixing in a Banbury mixer. Explain how rotor speed, fill factor, ram pressure, and mixing time influence the final compound.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Banbury mixer with rotor diameter 0.40 m and tip-to-wall clearance of 4 mm operates at 80 rpm. The compound has a viscosity of $8 \times 10^{3}$ Pa·s at processing temperature. Calculate (i) the rotor tip speed, (ii) the average shear rate in the clearance, and (iii) the shear power dissipated in the clearance if the effective shearing area is 0.04 m².

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Process Variables (7 marks):**

The quality of mixing in a Banbury mixer is governed by the following four key process variables:

1. **Rotor Speed (N):** Higher rotor speed increases tip speed and shear rate in the clearance. This improves **dispersive mixing** (breakdown of agglomerates) and shortens mixing time. However, excessive speed raises temperature rapidly, risking thermal degradation or scorch in rubber.

2. **Fill Factor (F):** An optimal fill factor (0.55–0.75) ensures that the charge is large enough to be continuously pulled into the rotor clearance, but small enough to allow free material circulation. Under-filling leads to poor ram contact and starved mixing; over-filling chokes the rotors and overloads the motor.

3. **Ram Pressure ($P_{ram}$):** The ram forces the compound into the shearing zone between the rotors and the wall. Higher ram pressure enhances material throughput through the clearance, improving dispersion uniformity. Excess pressure increases motor load without proportional quality gain.

4. **Mixing Time ($t_{mix}$):** Longer mixing time improves both dispersion and distribution. Beyond an optimum, the compound may over-mix, causing filler breakdown, polymer degradation, or premature crosslinking. The optimum is determined by torque/temperature curves and tested end-properties.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $D = 0.40$ m
- $N = 80$ rpm
- $h = 4$ mm $= 0.004$ m
- $\mu = 8 \times 10^{3}$ Pa·s
- $A_{shear} = 0.04$ m²

**(i) Rotor tip speed (2 marks):**

Convert N to rev/s: $N = 80/60 = 1.333$ rev/s

$$
v_{tip} = \pi \, D \, N = \pi \times 0.40 \times 1.333 = 1.6755 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Average shear rate (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{avg} = \dfrac{v_{tip}}{h} = \dfrac{1.6755}{0.004} = 418.88 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**(iii) Shear power dissipated (3 marks):**

First, calculate the shear stress:

$$
\tau = \mu \, \dot{\gamma}_{avg} = 8 \times 10^{3} \times 418.88 = 3.351 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}
$$

Then, the shear force on the effective area:

$$
F_{shear} = \tau \times A_{shear} = 3.351 \times 10^{6} \times 0.04 = 1.340 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}
$$

Finally, the shear power:

$$
P_{shear} = F_{shear} \times v_{tip} = 1.340 \times 10^{5} \times 1.6755 = 2.245 \times 10^{5} \text{ W} \approx 224.5 \text{ kW}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Banbury Mixer Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing rotor speed (rpm) with rev/s** — Banbury tip speed formulas require N in rev/s. Many students forget the /60 conversion and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Using melt density instead of bulk density** — For charging/loading calculations (fill factor, batch mass), use **bulk density** of the powder. Melt density applies only to fully plasticized material in the discharge state.
> 3. **Mixing time vs. cycle time** — Specific energy input uses **mixing time** (when rotors are running), but production rate uses **total cycle time** (charge + mix + discharge).
> 4. **Fill factor units** — It is dimensionless; do not state it in "%" unless explicitly asked.
> 5. **Newtonian assumption** — Real polymer melts are shear-thinning. State the Newtonian assumption clearly when using $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$.
> 6. **Confusing "dispersive" and "distributive" mixing** — Dispersive = breaking agglomerates; Distributive = spreading uniformly. Examiners award marks for correct use of these terms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Banbury Mixing**

- ⭐ **Banbury mixer = high-shear, batch-type, internal mixer** (patented 1916 by F.H. Banbury).
- ⭐ **Two counter-rotating rotors** (tangential, friction ratio 1.1:1 to 1.2:1) generate figure-eight flow.
- ⭐ **Floating ram** maintains pressure on charge; its vertical position is a qualitative mix indicator.
- ⭐ **Fill factor optimum:** $F = 0.55$ to $0.75$ (dimensionless).
- ⭐ **Rotor tip speed formula:** $v_{tip} = \pi D N$ (with N in rev/s).
- ⭐ **Shear rate in clearance:** $\dot{\gamma} = v_{tip} / h$ (Couette-flow analogy).
- ⭐ **Batch production rate:** $Q_b = \rho V_{chamber} F / t_{cycle}$ (kg/hr).
- ⭐ **Specific energy input:** $E_s = P t_{mix} / m_{batch}$ — the most important scale-up parameter.
- ⭐ **Process variables to control:** rotor speed, fill factor, ram pressure, mixing time, jacket temperature.
- ⭐ **Discharge:** Drop batch onto two-roll mill or feed to extruder for pelletizing.
- ⭐ **Typical applications:** Tire compounds, PVC dry-blend, color masterbatches, thermoset preforms, polymer blends.
- ⭐ **Newtonian shear stress:** $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ (state assumption; real melts are shear-thinning).
- ⭐ **Tangential vs. intermeshing rotors:** Tangential = larger batches; intermeshing = better distributive mixing.
- ⭐ **Continuous variant:** Farrel Continuous Mixer (FCM) — based on intermeshing rotor principle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Mixing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Continuous Mixing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Continuous Mixing** is a polymer processing operation in which the components of a polymer formulation (resin, additives, fillers, stabilizers, colorants, reinforcements) are fed into a processing machine at a constant mass flow rate, are progressively combined, melted, homogenized, and discharged as a uniformly blended melt stream on a continuous, uninterrupted basis. The two most industrially significant continuous mixing devices are the **Single-Screw Extruder (with mixing elements)** and the **Intermeshing Twin-Screw Extruder (co-rotating and counter-rotating)**, along with dedicated machines such as the **Farrel Continuous Mixer (FCM)** and the **Buss Ko-Kneader**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Continuous mixing is differentiated from **Batch Mixing** (e.g., internal mixers like Banbury) by its steady-state operation, throughput-based design, and use of **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** rather than a single cycle time as a performance metric. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long, spiraling escalator carrying ingredients for a cake — flour, eggs, sugar, and chocolate chips — all entering at the top at a steady rate. As the escalator moves downward, rotating paddles (the *screw flights*) push the ingredients forward while simultaneously smearing, folding, and stretching them against the walls. By the time the mixture reaches the bottom, it is a perfectly uniform batter. The "escalator" never stops; ingredients keep entering and batter keeps exiting at the same rate. - The **flour + chocolate chips** represent the **polymer melt + solid additives/fillers**. - The **rotating paddles** represent the **rotor/screw elements**. - The **time on the escalator** represents the **Mean Residence Time ($\bar{t}$)**. - The **uniform batter** represents a **homogenized compound** with a target **Degree of Mixedness**. ## 1.3 Why Continuous Mixing Matters in Polymer Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Industrial Scale:** Production rates of $\mathbf{100 \text{ kg/h}}$ to over $\mathbf{10{,}000 \text{ kg/h}}$ are routinely achieved — batch mixers cannot match this throughput. > - **Product Consistency:** Steady-state operation eliminates batch-to-batch variability. > - **In-line Compounding:** Reactive extrusion, devolatilization, and masterbatch let-down are all possible within a single continuous machine. > - **Energy Efficiency:** Specific Energy Input (SEI / SME) is more tightly controlled and lower per kg in optimized continuous systems. ## 1.4 Classification of Continuous Mixers | Family | Examples | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Rotating-shaft, single-shaft** | Single-screw extruder + Maddock / Saxton / pineapple / pin mixers | Compounding, profile extrusion | | **Rotating-shaft, twin-shaft** | Co-rotating TSE (e.g., ZSK), Counter-rotating TSE (e.g., CMG) | Compounding, reactive extrusion | | **Non-intermeshing twin-rotor** | Farrel Continuous Mixer (FCM), Continuous Banbury | Masterbatch, PVC, rubber | | **Reciprocating single-screw** | Buss Ko-Kneader | PVC, cable insulation, TPU | | **Static (no moving parts)** | Kenics, Sulzer SMV static mixers | Final homogenization of low-viscosity melts | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Mechanisms of Mixing Every continuous mixer achieves its output through a combination of two fundamentally different physical mechanisms: ### 2.1.1 Distributive (Extensive) Mixing - **Definition:** The spatial distribution of the minor component throughout the major component, **without changing the size of the dispersed particles**. - **Physics:** Achieved by repeated **lamination, folding, and re-orientation** of the melt stream. - **Analogy:** Folding a chocolate-chip dough with a rolling pin — chips move around but do not break. - **Performance Metric:** **Scale of Segregation**, $S$ (the striation thickness between regions of differing concentration). - **Key Geometric Action:** Screw flights, kneading discs staggered at 90°, reverse elements. ### 2.1.2 Dispersive (Intensive) Mixing - **Definition:** The **reduction in size** of agglomerates, droplets, or filler clusters down to the target particle/domain size. - **Physics:** Requires **high local shear stress** exceeding the cohesive (or interfacial) strength of the agglomerate. - **Analogy:** Crushing a clump of cocoa powder between the rollers. - **Key Equation (Mohr–Coulomb Breakage Criterion):** - The agglomerate breaks when the **local shear stress** $\tau$ exceeds the **cohesive strength** $\sigma_c$: - $$\tau \ge \sigma_c$$ - **Design Implication:** Dispersive mixing zones need high-shear elements (e.g., **kneading blocks at 30°/60°/90° stagger**, **gear mixers**). > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Pearl:** When asked *"Compare distributive and dispersive mixing,"* the marker always expects: **mechanism (strain vs stress), geometry (laminar stretching vs shear rupture), and additive type (soluble dyes / liquids vs fillers / pigments).** ## 2.2 Operational Theory: From Feed to Discharge The screw channel of a continuous mixer (or twin-screw barrel) can be analyzed as a sequence of functional zones: 1. **Conveying (Solids Conveying + Drag Conveying) Zone** - Solid polymer pellets are compacted against the barrel wall. - Frictional drag of the melt film transports material forward. - Primary engineering parameter: **conveying angle $\theta$** of the flight relative to the perpendicular to the axis. - Forward conveying capacity $\propto \sin\theta \cos\theta$. 2. **Melting (Phase Change) Zone** - The polymer melts against the hot barrel by **Tadmor's melting model**. - A melt film of thickness $\delta$ forms; the solid bed width $X(s)$ decreases along the screw length $s$ until $X=0$ (melt completion). - **Tadmor's Melting Equation:** - $$\frac{dX}{ds} \;=\; \frac{\Phi \, \sqrt{W_e \, H(s)}}{\,G \, c_p \, (T_m - T_b)\, X(s)\,}$$ with boundary condition $X(0) = W_0$ (initial solid-bed width). 3. **Mixing (Kneading) Zone** - Molten polymer is subjected to: - **Convective mixing** — bulk flow patterns (8-figure, helix, circulating). - **Dispersive mixing** — high-shear kneading discs. - **Distributive mixing** — toothed, gear, or pin elements. 4. **Metering / Discharge Zone** - Acts as a **pump** generating die pressure. - **Newtonian drag-flow approximation (simplified):** - $$Q_{drag} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, v_{bx}\, W\, H$$ - **Pressure flow (from die resistance):** - $$Q_{pressure} \;=\; -\,\frac{W\, H^{3}}{12\,\mu}\,\frac{dp}{dz}$$ - **Net throughput:** - $$Q \;=\; Q_{drag} \;-\; Q_{pressure} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,v_{bx}\,W\,H \left[1 - \frac{H^{2}}{6\mu\,v_{bx}}\frac{dp}{dz}\right]$$ ## 2.3 Residence Time Distribution (RTD) — The Heart of Continuous Mixing Because continuous mixers are not plug-flow reactors, different fluid elements spend **different times** inside. The RTD function $E(t)$ quantifies this. - **Definition:** $E(t)\,dt$ = fraction of exit stream that has resided in the mixer for a time between $t$ and $t+dt$. - **Idealized RTD (perfectly back-mixed, CSTR analogy):** - $$E(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{\bar{t}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\bar{t}}\right)$$ - **Mean Residence Time:** - $$\bar{t} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,E(t)\,dt \;=\; \frac{V}{Q}$$ - **Variance (spread of RTD):** - $$\sigma^{2} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty}(t-\bar{t})^{2}\,E(t)\,dt$$ - **Dimensionless Variance:** - $$\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \;=\; \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\bar{t}^{2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** A **narrow RTD ($\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \to 0$)** indicates behavior approaching **plug flow** — desirable for **reactive extrusion** (residence time must be uniform to control conversion). A **broad RTD** indicates **back-mixing** — desirable for **thermal homogenization** but disastrous for **uniform reaction**. ## 2.4 Degree of Mixedness and Mixing Index A measurable engineering quantity that students are expected to derive: - **Concentration variance after mixing:** - $$\sigma^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl(c_i - \bar{c}\bigr)^{2}$$ - **Initial (segregated) variance** for two components of volume fractions $a$ and $b$: - $$\sigma_{0}^{2} \;=\; a\,b\,(c_A - c_B)^{2}$$ - **Degree of Mixedness (I.C. Mohr–Saxton–Paddock, 1957):** - $$M \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}^{2} - \sigma^{2}}{\sigma_{0}^{2} - \sigma_{\infty}^{2}}$$ - $M = 1 \rightarrow$ perfectly mixed. - $M = 0 \rightarrow$ completely segregated. - For identical density, $\sigma_{\infty}^{2} \to 0$ and the expression simplifies to $M = 1 - \sigma^{2}/\sigma_{0}^{2}$. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Exam-Safe) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mean Residence Time | $\bar{t} = V/Q$ | s | | 2 | RTD for CSTR-like | $E(t) = (1/\bar{t})\exp(-t/\bar{t})$ | s⁻¹ | | 3 | Dimensionless RTD | $E_{\theta}(\theta) = \bar{t}\,E(t)$ with $\theta = t/\bar{t}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Variance of RTD | $\sigma^{2} = \int (t-\bar{t})^{2}E(t)dt$ | s² | | 5 | Peclet Number (axial) | $Pe = v\,L/D_{ax}$ | dimensionless, $D_{ax}$ = axial diffusivity | | 6 | Dispersion Model | $E_{\theta} = \sqrt{Pe/(4\pi\theta)}\exp\!\big[-Pe(1-\theta)^{2}/(4\theta)\big]$ | valid for large $Pe$ | | 7 | Specific Energy Input | $SEI = P/\dot{m} = 2\pi\,N\,\tau/\dot{m}$ | kWh/kg or J/kg | | 8 | Shear rate in screw | $\dot{\gamma} = \pi D N / H$ | s⁻¹ | | 9 | Drag flow rate | $Q_{d} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,v_{bx}\,W\,H$ | m³/s | | 10 | Pressure flow rate | $Q_{p} = -W H^{3}\,(dp/dz)/(12\mu)$ | m³/s | | 11 | Power number (mixer impeller) | $Po = P/(\rho\,N^{3}\,D^{5})$ | dimensionless | | 12 | Degree of Mixedness | $M = (\sigma_{0}^{2}-\sigma^{2})/(\sigma_{0}^{2}-\sigma_{\infty}^{2})$ | 0 → 1 | | 13 | Scale of segregation | $S = \int_0^{\infty} R(r)\,dr$ | m (where $R(r)$ is correlation function) | | 14 | Tadmor melting | $dX/ds = \Phi\sqrt{W_e H}/[Gc_p(T_m-T_b)X]$ | $X$ = solid-bed width | | 15 | Fill factor | $FF = m_{\text{actual}}/m_{\text{flooded}}$ | 0 → 1 (typically 0.2–0.5 for TSE) | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Twin-screw extruders in masterbatch production:** A $40:1$ L/D co-rotating TSE with a kneading block at 60°/30° stagger achieves dispersive mixing of $70\%$ TiO₂ in PP at throughputs of 1500 kg/h, with $\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \approx 0.18$. - **Reactive extrusion (PA-6 anionic polymerization):** Narrow RTD ($\sigma_{\theta}^{2} < 0.05$) is essential to control molecular weight; hence, long, partially filled kneading zones are avoided. - **Color masterbatch let-down:** Distributive mixing dominates; pin mixers and cavity-transfer mixers are used. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Residence Time from the Mass Balance **Starting point:** A continuous mixer operating at steady state with internal volume $V$ (m³) and volumetric throughput $Q$ (m³/s). Apply a Dirac-delta tracer pulse at $t = 0$ and record the exit concentration $C(t)$. **Step 1 — Conservation of tracer mass:** The total tracer that exits equals the total tracer injected: $$\int_{0}^{\infty} Q\,C(t)\,dt \;=\; m_{tracer}$$ **Step 2 — Define the exit-age distribution (RTD) function:** $$E(t) \;\equiv\; \frac{C(t)}{\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt}$$ By construction, $\int_0^{\infty} E(t)\,dt = 1$. **Step 3 — Mean (first moment) of the distribution:** $$\bar{t} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,E(t)\,dt$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $C(t) = m_{tracer}\,E(t)/Q$:** $$\bar{t} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,\frac{Q\,C(t)}{m_{tracer}}\,dt \;=\; \frac{Q}{m_{tracer}}\int_{0}^{\infty} t\,C(t)\,dt$$ **Step 5 — Compare to a CSTR (well-mixed tank) at steady state, where $V\,dC/dt = -Q\,C$, giving $C(t) = C_0\exp(-t/\tau)$ with $\tau = V/Q$.** The integral then yields: $$\bar{t} \;=\; \frac{V}{Q} \;=\; \tau$$ **Conclusion:** $$\boxed{\bar{t} \;=\; \frac{V}{Q} \quad\text{(mean residence time)}}$$ This is the *universal* relationship for any continuous mixer regardless of internal flow pattern. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variance of RTD for a Co-rotating Twin-Screw Extruder (Axial Dispersion Model) The axial dispersion model treats the real flow as plug flow with a superimposed Fickian diffusion in the axial direction: $$D_{ax}\,\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial z^{2}} \;-\; v\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial z} \;=\; \frac{\partial C}{\partial t}$$ **Step 1 — Non-dimensionalize** using $\theta = t/\bar{t}$ and $Z = z/L$: $$\frac{1}{Pe}\,\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial Z^{2}} \;-\; \frac{\partial C}{\partial Z} \;=\; \frac{\partial C}{\partial \theta}$$ where $Pe = vL/D_{ax}$ is the **Peclet number**. **Step 2 — Solve for an open-open boundary condition** (Danckwerts) with a Dirac pulse input. The exit-age density is: $$E_{\theta}(\theta) \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{Pe}{4\pi\,\theta}}\;\exp\!\left[-\frac{Pe\,(1-\theta)^{2}}{4\,\theta}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Compute the variance.** Using standard moment relations of the dispersion model: $$\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \;=\; \frac{2}{Pe} \;-\; 2\!\left(\frac{1}{Pe}\right)^{\!2}\!\left(1 - e^{-Pe}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Take two engineering limits:** - **Large $Pe$ (near plug flow):** $\exp(-Pe) \to 0$ - **Small $Pe$ (heavy back-mixing):** expanding $e^{-Pe} \approx 1 - Pe + Pe^{2}/2$: $$\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \;\approx\; 1 \;-\; \frac{2}{Pe} \;+\; \cdots \;\to\; 1 \quad (Pe \to 0)$$ This limit matches a single CSTR, whose dimensionless variance is **exactly $1$**. **Step 5 — Engineering application:** For a typical co-rotating TSE, $Pe \approx 10$ to $50$ (deep kneading channels give back-mixing; long fully-filled sections push $Pe$ upward). The corresponding $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ is roughly $0.04$–$0.2$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Mechanical Energy (SME / SEI) **Step 1 — Mechanical power delivered to the polymer** by a rotating shaft of torque $\tau$ (N·m) at speed $N$ (rev/s): $$P \;=\; 2\pi\,N\,\tau \quad [W]$$ **Step 2 — Mass throughput** is $\dot{m}$ (kg/s). The Specific Energy Input per unit mass is: $$SME \;=\; \frac{P}{\dot{m}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi\,N\,\tau}{\dot{m}} \quad [J/kg]$$ **Step 3 — Convert to kWh/kg** (the unit typically used in compounding specifications): $$SME \, [kWh/kg] \;=\; \frac{2\pi\,N\,\tau}{3.6\times 10^{6}\,\dot{m}}$$ **Step 4 — Typical values:** - Twin-screw compounding of glass-filled PA: $SME = 0.25$–$0.45$ kWh/kg. - Reactive extrusion of PET: $SME = 0.15$–$0.30$ kWh/kg. - Color masterbatch (high pigment): $SME = 0.6$–$1.2$ kWh/kg. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Shear Rate in a Screw Channel (Newtonian) **Step 1 — Geometry:** barrel radius $R$, screw radius $R_s = R-H$ where $H$ is the channel depth. Barrel tangential velocity at the wall is $v_b = \pi D N$ where $D = 2R$. **Step 2 — In the unwound channel** (Mohr–Paddock–Tadmor assumption), the relative velocity between barrel and screw varies linearly across the depth $H$: $$v(y) \;=\; v_b\,\frac{y}{H}$$ **Step 3 — Shear rate** is the velocity gradient: $$\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \frac{dv}{dy} \;=\; \frac{v_b}{H} \;=\; \frac{\pi D N}{H} \quad [s^{-1}]$$ **Step 4 — Engineering rule of thumb:** For $D = 30$ mm, $N = 100$ rpm $= 1.67$ rev/s, $H = 3$ mm: $$\dot{\gamma} \;\approx\; \frac{\pi\,(0.03)\,(1.67)}{0.003} \;\approx\; 52\,s^{-1}$$ In kneading blocks, the local shear rate can be 5–10× higher due to the small gap between kneading disc tip and barrel wall. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Why Each Design Parameter Matters | Design Lever | Effect on Dispersive Mixing | Effect on Distributive Mixing | Effect on RTD | Risk if Overdone | |---|---|---|---|---| | ↑ Kneading-disc stagger angle | ↑ Shear stress at gap | ↓ | Slight broadening | Melt temperature rise, degradation | | ↑ Screw speed (N) | ↑ Shear rate (linear) | ↑ strain rate | Slight narrowing of $\bar{t}$ | Resin degradation, torque limit | | ↑ Throughput (Q) | ↓ (less time in shear zone) | ↓ | Narrower $\bar{t}$ | Incomplete melting | | ↑ L/D ratio | More mixing zones | More lamination | Increases $\bar{t}$ | Residence time too long for reactive systems | | Reverse element | Forces back-flow | ↑ local lamination | Strongly broadens RTD | Melt temperature spike, surging | | Lower fill factor | Less compression | ↑ free surface, lamination | Broadens RTD | Air entrapment, oxidation | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Continuous Mixing Line The following Mermaid block maps the *process architecture* of a typical compounding line centered on a co-rotating twin-screw extruder — the industrial workhorse for continuous mixing. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Feed Hopper
Polymer Pellets] --> B1[Volumetric Feeder 1
Loss-in-Weight] A2[Side Feeder
Glass Fibre] --> B1 A3[Liquid Injector
Plasticiser or Oil] --> B1 B1 --> C1[Conveying Zone 1
Solids Transport] C1 --> C2[Melting Zone
Barrel Heaters and Kneading] C2 --> C3[Dispersive Mixing
Kneading Blocks 60 Degree] C3 --> C4[Distributive Mixing
Pineapple Element and Gear] C4 --> C5[Devolatilisation
Vacuum Vent] C5 --> C6[Metering Zone
Reverse Element if Needed] C6 --> C7[Die and Strand Pelletiser] C7 --> D1[Final Compound
Uniform Melt] D1 --> E1[Quality Lab
MFI, Colour, Filler Dispersion] E1 --> F1[Feedback to Screw
Profile and RPM] F1 -.-> C3 F1 -.-> C6 ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of Twin-Screw Configurations ```mermaid flowchart TB G1[Configuration Type] --> H1[Co-Rotating Intermeshing] G1 --> H2[Counter-Rotating Intermeshing] G1 --> H3[Counter-Rotating Non-Intermeshing] H1 --> I1[Self Wiping Action] H1 --> I2[High Free Volume 0.4L cubed per rev] H1 --> I3[Used in Compounding] H2 --> J1[Calendar Effect at Crossover] H2 --> J2[Low Free Volume] H2 --> J3[Used in PVC and Profile] H3 --> K1[FCM Type] H3 --> K2[Rotor Like Internal Mixer] H3 --> K3[Used in Masterbatch and Rubber] ``` ## 4.3 RTD Curve Interpretation (Textual Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Tracer Pulse
at t equals 0] --> L2[Mode 1: Narrow RTD
High Peclet Number] L3[Tracer Pulse
at t equals 0] --> L4[Mode 2: Broad RTD
Low Peclet Number] L2 --> M1[Sharp Peak near t equals t bar] L2 --> M2[Small Tail] L4 --> N1[Long Tail Starting Early] L4 --> N2[Mode Shifted toward Low t] M1 --> O1[Best for Reactive Extrusion] N1 --> O2[Best for Thermal Homogenisation] ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Block: Continuous vs Batch ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Polymer Mixing Type] --> Q1[Batch] P1 --> Q2[Continuous] Q1 --> R1[Banbury Internal Mixer] Q1 --> R2[Sigma Blade Mixer] Q1 --> R3[Cycle Time 5 to 15 min] Q1 --> R4[Low Throughput High Flexibility] Q2 --> S1[Twin Screw Extruder] Q2 --> S2[Farrel Continuous Mixer] Q2 --> S3[Steady State Operation] Q2 --> S4[High Throughput 100 to 10000 kg per h] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block in the Mermaid charts represents a **physical unit operation or functional zone**. Arrows indicate the **direction of material flow**. Dashed arrows in the first block (lab → screw profile) represent the **feedback loop** by which the rheology and quality lab re-tunes screw geometry or RPM — a hallmark of modern Industry-4.0 compounding lines. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]** **Differentiate between distributive and dispersive mixing in polymer processing.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Distributive Mixing | Dispersive Mixing | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Spatial re-distribution of components | Size reduction of agglomerates/droplets | | **Driving force** | Strain (laminar stretching) | Stress (shear must exceed cohesive strength) | | **Typical action** | Folding, lamination, splitting | High-shear kneading, gap rupture | | **Geometric element** | Pin mixers, gear mixers, pineapple | Kneading blocks at 30°/60°/90° stagger | | **Additives benefiting** | Soluble dyes, low-viscosity liquids | Pigments, fillers, fibres, rubber domains | | **Performance metric** | Scale of segregation $S$ | Particle/cluster size after the process | | **Energy regime** | Moderate shear, high strain | High local shear stress, high SME | *(Word limit: ~120 words; rubric = definition (1) + mechanism (1) + application/example (1).)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]** **Define mean residence time and state its significance in a continuous mixer.** **Model Answer:** The **mean residence time** $\bar{t}$ of a continuous mixer is the average time a fluid element spends inside the machine. It is given by: $$\bar{t} \;=\; \frac{V}{Q}$$ where $V$ is the internal free volume (m³) and $Q$ is the volumetric throughput (m³/s). **Significance:** - Determines the **time available for melting, mixing, and chemical reaction**. - Sets the **thermal budget** a polymer experiences (long $\bar{t}$ → more degradation). - For **reactive extrusion**, $\bar{t}$ must be ≥ the reaction time; the **variance $\sigma^{2}$** must be small to keep conversion uniform. - For **compounding**, $\bar{t}$ correlates with the **degree of distributive mixing** (number of laminations $n \approx \bar{t} / t_{\text{element}}$). *(Word limit: ~100 words; rubric = definition (1) + formula (1) + significance (1).)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full from the same module. --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2] **(a) Derive the expression for the mean residence time of a continuous mixer using the residence time distribution $E(t)$. Show that for a perfectly back-mixed continuous stirred tank, the RTD is exponential.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Mass balance on a tracer in steady state:* $$\int_0^{\infty} Q\,C(t)\,dt \;=\; m_{tracer}$$ *Step 2 — Define the RTD function as the normalized exit concentration:* $$E(t) \;\equiv\; \frac{C(t)}{\int_0^{\infty} C(t)\,dt} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \int_0^{\infty} E(t)\,dt \;=\; 1$$ *Step 3 — The mean (first moment):* $$\bar{t} \;=\; \int_0^{\infty} t\,E(t)\,dt$$ *Step 4 — For a perfectly back-mixed CSTR, the governing equation is:* $$V\,\frac{dC}{dt} \;=\; -Q\,C(t) \quad\text{with}\quad C(0) \;=\; C_0$$ *Step 5 — Solve the ODE:* $$\frac{dC}{C} \;=\; -\frac{Q}{V}\,dt \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C(t) \;=\; C_0\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$$ where $\tau = V/Q$. *Step 6 — Normalize to obtain the RTD:* $$E(t) \;=\; \frac{C(t)}{\int_0^\infty C(t)\,dt} \;=\; \frac{C_0\,e^{-t/\tau}}{C_0\,\tau} \;=\; \frac{1}{\tau}\,e^{-t/\tau}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{t} \;=\; \int_0^{\infty} t\,\frac{1}{\tau}e^{-t/\tau}\,dt \;=\; \tau \;=\; \frac{V}{Q}}$$ **Mark Split:** Definition (1) + Normalization (2) + ODE setup (1) + ODE solution (1) + Identification $\bar{t} = \tau$ (2). --- **(b) A co-rotating twin-screw extruder has a free volume of 2.4 × 10⁻³ m³ and is operated at a throughput of 80 kg/h of polypropylene (melt density 730 kg/m³). (i) Compute the mean residence time. (ii) If the dimensionless RTD variance is 0.20, estimate the standard deviation. (iii) Comment on whether this machine is suitable for anionic polymerization of caprolactam (reaction time ≈ 4 min, conversion must be uniform within ±10%).** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** *(i) Mean residence time:* $$Q \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} \;=\; \frac{80}{730} \;=\; 0.1096\;m^{3}/h \;=\; 3.045\times 10^{-5}\;m^{3}/s$$ $$\bar{t} \;=\; \frac{V}{Q} \;=\; \frac{2.4\times 10^{-3}}{3.045\times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; 78.8\;s \;\approx\; 1.31\;min$$ *(ii) Standard deviation:* $$\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \;=\; \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\bar{t}^{2}} \;=\; 0.20 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sigma \;=\; \bar{t}\,\sqrt{0.20} \;=\; 78.8\times 0.447 \;\approx\; 35.2\;s$$ *(iii) Suitability for anionic caprolactam polymerization:* - Required reaction time: $4$ min = $240$ s. - Available mean residence time: $1.31$ min = $78.8$ s. - **Conclusion:** $\bar{t} \ll 240$ s → the extruder is **not suitable** in its current configuration. - The **wide spread** ($\sigma \approx 35$ s relative to $\bar{t} = 79$ s) further confirms this: a fluid element at $t = \bar{t} - \sigma = 44$ s would be vastly under-reacted, and one at $t = \bar{t} + 3\sigma = 184$ s would be far over-reacted. Conversion uniformity within ±10 % is **not achievable**. **Recommendations to make it suitable:** (1) Increase L/D from current $\sim 40$ to $\sim 60$; (2) Reduce throughput to ~ 25 kg/h; (3) Use long fully-filled kneading sections (high $Pe$). **Mark Split:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks (judgement + recommendations). --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2] **(a) With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of a Farrel Continuous Mixer (FCM). Compare its dispersive-mixing performance with a co-rotating intermeshing twin-screw extruder.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** *Working Principle of the FCM:* - The FCM consists of **two non-intermeshing rotors** inside a closed, jacketed barrel with a **discharge orifice (or adjustable slide gate)** at one end. - The rotors are **sigma- or cam-shaped** and rotate in the same direction at slightly different speeds (differential rotation). - Material is fed at one end, conveyed forward by the rotors, and is **progressively sheared, folded, and compressed** as it moves along the rotor length. - The discharge opening controls the **fill level and thus the hold-up / mean residence time**. - Steam/water injection ports allow direct heating/cooling. *Comparison with Co-rotating Intermeshing TSE:* | Feature | FCM | Co-rotating TSE | |---|---|---| | **Rotor type** | Non-intermeshing, sigma/banbury-like | Intermeshing, self-wiping screws | | **Fill level** | Adjustable (0.2–0.9) | Mostly starved (0.2–0.5) | | **RTD** | Broad, $\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \approx 0.3$–$0.6$ | Narrow, $\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \approx 0.05$–$0.2$ | | **Dispersive mixing** | Excellent (long high-shear residence) | Excellent in kneading zones | | **Throughput** | Moderate (up to ~ 20 t/h) | High (up to ~ 50 t/h) | | **Best for** | Masterbatch, rubber, PVC | Engineering thermoplastics, glass-fibre, reactive extrusion | | **Cleaning / recipe change** | Slower | Fast (segmented screws) | **Mark Split:** Description with sketch (3) + comparison table (3) + judgement (1). --- **(b) A single-screw extruder of diameter $D = 60$ mm, screw speed $N = 80$ rpm, and channel depth $H = 4$ mm is processing LDPE at 200 °C. (i) Estimate the average shear rate in the metering zone. (ii) If the specific energy input is 0.35 kWh/kg at a throughput of 90 kg/h, compute the absorbed power and the shaft torque. (iii) State two design changes you would recommend to improve dispersive mixing of a $20\%$ calcium-carbonate filler.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** *(i) Average shear rate:* $$\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \frac{\pi D N}{H}$$ Convert: $N = 80$ rpm $= 1.333$ rev/s, $D = 0.06$ m, $H = 0.004$ m. $$\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \frac{\pi\,(0.06)\,(1.333)}{0.004} \;\approx\; 62.8\;s^{-1}$$ *(ii) Power and torque:* $$P \;=\; SME \times \dot{m} \;=\; 0.35\;kWh/kg \times 90\;kg/h \;=\; 31.5\;kWh/h \;=\; 31{,}500\;W \;=\; 31.5\;kW$$ Torque: $$\tau_{shaft} \;=\; \frac{P}{2\pi N} \;=\; \frac{31{,}500}{2\pi \times 1.333} \;\approx\; 3760\;N\cdot m$$ *(iii) Two design recommendations for better CaCO₃ dispersion:* 1. **Add a high-shear mixing element** (e.g., a **Maddock mixer** or **Union Carbide pin mixer**) in the metering zone, which creates a $\sim 1$ mm clearance gap generating shear rates of $500$–$1500$ s⁻¹ — roughly 10× higher than the screw channel. 2. **Increase the screw L/D ratio from $\sim 25:1$ to $\sim 33:1$** with a dedicated **kneading-disc section** (stagger 60°) at 8–12 D from the feed throat; this adds a dispersive zone without increasing melt temperature excessively. (Other valid options: increase $N$ to 120 rpm with barrel cooling; use a **Saxton distributive mixer** for separating wetting and dispersion steps.) **Mark Split:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks (justified design changes). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Hotspots in Continuous Mixing Questions:** > 1. **Conflating $\bar{t}$ with $t_{cycle}$.** Batch-mix students wrongly apply cycle time to a continuous machine. **Always** derive $\bar{t} = V/Q$. > 2. **Forgetting the boundary condition in the dispersion model.** A "closed–closed" vs "open–open" boundary changes the RTD formula. The KTU model answer uses **open–open (Danckwerts)**. > 3. **Dropping units in SME problems.** $SME$ is most often required in **kWh/kg**, not J/kg. A factor-of-$3.6 \times 10^{6}$ mistake can cost full marks. > 4. **Stating "more shear = better mixing"** without qualifying *dispersive* vs *distributive*. Markers specifically look for the word **"intensive vs extensive"** or **"stress vs strain"**. > 5. **Wrong Peclet definition.** Some students write $Pe = vL/D_{ax}$ with $D_{ax}$ confused with screw diameter $D$. Use $L$ = barrel length and $D_{ax}$ = axial dispersion coefficient (m²/s). > 6. **Neglecting fill factor in TSE.** A TSE is **starve-fed** — the screw is never fully filled. A statement implying 100 % fill loses credibility marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Continuous mixing = steady-state, throughput-based, RTD-governed** operation. - ✅ Two complementary mechanisms: **Distributive (extensive, strain-based)** and **Dispersive (intensive, stress-based)**. - ✅ **Key geometric levers**: stagger angle of kneading blocks, L/D ratio, reverse elements, fill factor. - ✅ **Mean residence time:** $\bar{t} = V/Q$ — applies to *all* continuous mixers. - ✅ **RTD for ideal CSTR:** $E(t) = (1/\bar{t})\,e^{-t/\bar{t}}$; **for plug flow with dispersion:** $E_{\theta}$ is the Gaussian-like Danckwerts form with $Pe$. - ✅ **Reactive extrusion needs narrow RTD** ($\sigma_{\theta}^{2} < 0.1$); **thermal homogenization accepts broad RTD**. - ✅ **Shear rate in screw channel:** $\dot{\gamma} = \pi D N / H$ — independent of viscosity (Newtonian assumption). - ✅ **SME** controls **dispersive mixing quality** and **thermal degradation budget** simultaneously — there is an optimum. - ✅ **Co-rotating TSE** = best general-purpose compounder; **Counter-rotating TSE** = best for PVC/profile; **FCM** = best for masterbatch and rubber. - ✅ **Static mixers** provide final **distributive** homogenization with zero moving parts; placed before the die. - ✅ **Fill factor 0.2–0.5** for starve-fed TSE; > 0.7 indicates surging/instability. - ✅ **Mohr–Coulomb breakage rule:** $\tau \ge \sigma_c$ is the design criterion for dispersive element sizing. - ✅ Always **convert $N$ from rpm to rev/s** before substituting in formulas. - ✅ Remember the **3-tier connection:** *Mechanism (distributive/dispersive) → Equipment (kneading/pin/static) → Performance metric (scale of segregation / particle size)*. Examiners reward answers that explicitly link all three. - ✅ **Common KTU board keywords to memorize verbatim:** *RTD, mean residence time, dispersive mixing, distributive mixing, kneading disc, specific mechanical energy, Peclet number, fill factor, scale of segregation.* ---

topics.title: Master Batching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Master Batching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER BATCHING (MASTERBATCHING)** is a pre-dispersion compounding technique in polymer processing in which a high concentration of an additive (pigment, filler, stabilizer, flame retardant, or other modifier) is incorporated into a carrier polymer resin to form a concentrated intermediate product called a **Masterbatch (MB)**. This concentrate is subsequently let-down (diluted) into a larger volume of the base polymer during final processing (injection molding, extrusion, blow molding, etc.) to achieve the desired final concentration with uniform dispersion.

In the context of **PCPOT502 – Polymer Processing (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2: Additives & Compounding)**, master batching is taught as a critical **industrial scale-up strategy** that overcomes the limitations of direct addition of small-quantity additives into large polymer matrices.

### 1.2 Constituents of a Masterbatch

A typical masterbatch formulation consists of three key components:

| Component | Typical Range (wt%) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Carrier Resin** | 30 – 90 % | Polymer matrix compatible with the base resin; provides melt viscosity for dispersion |
| **Additive / Active Ingredient** | 10 – 70 % | Pigment, filler, UV stabilizer, etc. — the functional concentrate |
| **Dispersing Agent / Wetting Agent** | 1 – 10 % | Surfactant or compatibilizer that reduces interfacial tension between additive and carrier |
| **Processing Aids / Binders** | 0.5 – 5 % | Internal lubricants, wetting agents, anti-caking agents |

> [!NOTE]
> The additive concentration in a masterbatch is typically **5× to 50× higher** than its target concentration in the final product. This is what makes "let-down ratio" the central design parameter.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Instant Coffee Principle"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a masterbatch exactly like **instant coffee granules**.
> - You do not grind coffee beans directly into a cup of milk (impractical, lumpy, inconsistent).
> - Instead, the manufacturer pre-concentrates the coffee solids into a dense, easily-dispersible granule (the **masterbatch**).
> - At home, you simply mix a small spoon of this concentrate into a large cup of milk (the **base polymer**) to get the perfect, uniform strength instantly.
> - The "let-down ratio" is the spoon-to-cup ratio — say 1:10 or 1:20.

**Why manufacturers love masterbatching:**
- **Dust-free handling** of powders (pigments, fillers are messy).
- **Improved dosage accuracy** — easy to meter pellets, not powders.
- **Better dispersion** — twin-screw compounding in concentrate form provides much higher shear than the final processing machine.
- **Lower capital cost** — only a fraction of the polymer needs to be compounded at high intensity.
- **Inventory flexibility** — one white polymer can be tinted into hundreds of colors by adding different MBs.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

- **Let-Down Ratio (LDR):** the ratio of masterbatch mass to total final compound mass. A **1:20 LDR** means 1 part MB is mixed with 19 parts base polymer (plus 1 part MB = 20 total → MB = 5 wt%).
- **Pigment Loading:** typically 20 – 50 % in colour masterbatches.
- **Filler Loading:** can be as high as 80 wt% (calcium carbonate MBs are very common).
- **Standard pellet size:** 2 – 4 mm cylindrical or spherical.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" — Limitations of Direct Additive Addition

Direct addition of additives (especially at < 1 wt%) into molten polymer is **unreliable** because of:

1. **Metering inaccuracy** — feeding 200 g of carbon black into a 1000 kg extruder is mechanically impractical.
