# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define dielectric heating. Why is it preferred over conventional heating for welding polar thermoplastics?**

**Model Answer:**
Dielectric heating is a process in which a polar dielectric material (polymer) is subjected to a high-frequency alternating electric field (typically 13.56 or 27.12 MHz). The dipoles in the polymer attempt to align with the alternating field; due to internal viscous resistance, this realignment dissipates energy as heat uniformly throughout the volume of the material.

It is preferred for polar thermoplastics such as PVC and Nylon because:
1. Heat is generated **internally and uniformly**, eliminating surface overheating.
2. Welding cycle time is very short (seconds).
3. The process works **without contact**, ideal for sealed/sterile products.
4. It selectively heats **polar** materials, leaving non-polar inserts unaffected.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional II]**
**What is corona treatment? Why is it necessary for polyolefin films before printing?**

**Model Answer:**
Corona treatment is a surface modification process in which a high-voltage, high-frequency AC discharge is passed over a polymer film in air. The discharge ionizes the surrounding air, producing ozone, free electrons, and ions that bombard the film surface, creating polar functional groups (carbonyl, hydroxyl) and micro-roughness.

It is necessary for polyolefins (PE, PP) because these polymers have a **low surface energy (~31 mN/m)**. Printing inks and adhesives (surface tension 38 – 42 mN/m) cannot wet such low-energy surfaces — they "bead up." Corona treatment raises the surface energy to **≥ 45 mN/m**, ensuring proper ink adhesion and lamination bond strength.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ CHOICE 1 — **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Explain the **principle of dielectric heating** with a neat block diagram of the setup. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **volumetric power dissipation** in a polymer subjected to a high-frequency electric field. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Principle:**
Dielectric heating (also called high-frequency heating or RF heating) uses an alternating electric field in the radio-frequency range (1 – 100 MHz, typically 27.12 MHz ISM band) to generate heat inside a polar polymer. The polymer acts as a lossy dielectric between two metallic electrodes.

When the field is applied:
- **Permanent dipoles** in the polymer rotate to align with the field.
- **Induced dipoles** deform in the direction of the field.
- The field alternates millions of times per second; the dipoles cannot keep up, resulting in a phase lag.
- The phase lag between the applied field $E$ and the resulting displacement $D$ is the **loss angle $\delta$**.
- The energy corresponding to this lag is dissipated as heat uniformly inside the polymer.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes (HF Oscillator → HV Transformer → Tuning Capacitor → Electrodes → Polymer Workpiece).

**Key Parameters:**
- Frequency $f$, voltage $V$, gap $d$.
- Material properties: $\varepsilon'$, $\tan\delta$.
- Power density: $P = 2\pi f E^2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon' \tan\delta$.

**Why It Works for Polymers:**
- Polar polymers (PVC, Nylon, PET) have significant $\tan\delta$.
- Non-polar polymers (PTFE, PE) have very low $\tan\delta$ and need additives to be heated dielectrically.

### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(b)

Full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. A compact form for the answer sheet:

**Step 1:** Express the field sinusoidally: $E(t) = E_0 \cos(\omega t)$.

**Step 2:** Express the electric displacement using complex permittivity:
$$
D(t) = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon' E_0 \cos(\omega t) + \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_0 \sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3:** Power density $p(t) = E(t)\,dD/dt$.

**Step 4:** Average over one cycle ($\langle \cos^2 \omega t \rangle = 1/2$):

$$
P = \tfrac{1}{2}\omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_0^{\,2}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$, $E_{rms} = E_0/\sqrt{2}$:

$$
P = 2\pi f \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^{\,2}
$$

**Step 6:** Using $\varepsilon'' = \varepsilon' \tan\delta$:

$$
\boxed{P = 2\pi f \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon' \tan\delta \, E_{rms}^{\,2} \quad \text{[W/m}^3\text{]}}
$$

**Numerical check (for a 14-mark answer):**
For $f = 27.12 \times 10^6$ Hz, $E_{rms} = 10^5$ V/m, $\varepsilon' = 4$, $\tan\delta = 0.05$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m:

$$
P = 2\pi (2.712\times10^7)(8.854\times10^{-12})(4)(0.05)(10^{10}) \approx 3.02 \times 10^{7} \text{ W/m}^3
$$

This high power density explains the rapid volumetric heating observed in practice.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE 2 — **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Describe the **electrostatic powder coating process** with a process flow diagram. List its advantages over liquid painting. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain **corona discharge treatment** of polymer films. Discuss the effect of treatment on surface energy and the mechanism of ink adhesion. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Process Description:**
1. The metal substrate is **pre-treated** (degreased, sometimes phosphated) to ensure good adhesion.
2. The polymer powder (epoxy, polyester, polyurethane; 20 – 100 μm) is **fluidized** with air in a hopper.
3. Air + powder is conveyed to a **corona spray gun** (typically 30 – 100 kV DC, negative polarity).
4. Powder particles pick up a **negative charge (~10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ C per particle)** as they pass through the ionized region of the gun.
5. Charged particles are **attracted to the grounded substrate** by Coulombic force, forming a uniform layer (40 – 120 μm).
6. The coated part is **transferred to a curing oven** (160 – 200 °C, 10 – 20 min) where powder melts, coalesces, and cross-links into a continuous film.
7. The part is **cooled and inspected**.

**Process Flow Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2.

**Advantages over Liquid Painting:**
- **Zero VOC emissions** — environmentally compliant.
- **Overspray recovery** up to 95% — economical.
- **Single-coat thick films** without sagging.
- **Excellent edge coverage** and uniform thickness on complex geometries.
- **Superior mechanical and chemical resistance** due to thermoset cross-linking.
- **No solvent handling, drying, or flash-off time.**

### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Principle of Corona Treatment:**
A high-frequency, high-voltage AC field (10 – 30 kV, 10 – 30 kHz) is established between a **stationary electrode bar** and a **grounded metal roller** covered with a dielectric sleeve. The polymer film passes over the roller; the small air gap (~1 – 2 mm) experiences **dielectric breakdown**, producing a violet **corona discharge**.

**Mechanism of Surface Modification:**
- Energetic ions, electrons, and **ozone** (O₃) generated in the discharge impinge on the polymer surface.
- Free-radical reactions introduce **polar functional groups** (–OH, –COOH, C=O).
- Chain scission produces low-molecular-weight oxidized material (LMWOM) at the surface.
- Micro-roughness is created by **selective etching**, increasing mechanical interlocking.

**Effect on Surface Energy:**
- Untreated PP: ~30 dyne/cm.
- Treated PP: ~42 – 48 dyne/cm.
- Wettability of water and inks improves dramatically (contact angle drops from ~95° to ~50°).

**Mechanism of Ink Adhesion:**
- The polar groups form **hydrogen bonds** and **covalent bonds** with the polar functionalities in the ink binder.
- Surface roughening provides **mechanical anchorage** for the dried ink film.
- Together, these yield **peel strength improvements of 3× – 10×** over untreated films.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing frequency bands:** Writing "microwave heating uses 27 MHz" is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. The correct band is **2.45 GHz**. RF dielectric heating uses 13.56 / 27.12 MHz.
> 2. **Skipping the derivation step:** For "derive the power dissipation formula," merely stating the result earns at most 2 of 7 marks. You **must** start from the definition of complex permittivity and show the time-averaging step.
> 3. **Mixing up corona and plasma:** Corona is at **atmospheric pressure** with air as the process gas; plasma is at **low pressure (1 – 100 Pa)** with controlled gases. Examiners will deduct a mark for this.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Power density in dielectric heating is **W/m³**, not W/m² (that is the unit for surface heat flux). Always carry units in the final answer.
> 5. **Confusing electrode polarity in powder coating:** Powder guns are **negative** polarity (more stable corona, smoother film). Positive guns exist but produce non-uniform films and are rarely used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Dielectric heating** is a *volumetric, contactless* heating method for **polar** polymers; frequency band is **13.56 / 27.12 MHz**.
- The governing equation is $P = 2\pi f E^2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon' \tan\delta$ — remember the five multiplicative terms.
- **Loss tangent $\tan\delta$** is the figure of merit for dielectric heatability; values for polymers range from $10^{-4}$ (PTFE) to $10^{-1}$ (filled PVC).
- **Microwave heating** uses 2.45 GHz; dominant mechanism is dipolar polarization of water and interfacial (Maxwell–Wagner) polarization in composites.
- **Electrostatic powder coating** uses a corona gun (–50 kV) to charge powder and ground the substrate; it is **VOC-free, eco-friendly, and 95% recyclable**.
- **Corona treatment** oxidizes the polymer surface to raise its energy from ~31 mN/m to ≥ 45 mN/m — essential for **printing, laminating, and adhesive bonding** of polyolefins.
- **Electrospinning** uses a high DC field to overcome surface tension (Taylor's criterion $V_c = L \sqrt{4\gamma / \varepsilon_0 R}$) and produces fibres in the **10 – 1000 nm** range.
- **Plasma treatment** is the low-pressure, gas-specific, room-temperature alternative to corona for ultra-clean biomedical/microelectronic applications.
- The **skin depth $\delta_s$** of microwaves in polymers determines the maximum thickness that can be heated uniformly.
- For a 14-mark KTU question on this topic, always include: **(1) principle, (2) block diagram, (3) derivation/equations, (4) applications, (5) comparison with conventional method.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical and Medical Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optical and Medical Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Optical Applications of Polymers

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Optical Polymers** are specialized macromolecular materials engineered to manipulate, transmit, or filter light with controlled refractive index, high transparency, low birefringence, and tailored dispersion characteristics. They replace conventional inorganic glasses in applications demanding lightweight, shatter-resistant, and economically mass-producible components.

Standard engineering polymers used in optical applications include **Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA)**, **Polycarbonate (PC)**, **Polystyrene (PS)**, **Cyclo-olefin polymers (COP)**, **Polyethylene terephthalate (PET)**, and **Zeonex®/Topas®** grades.

### Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Invisible Highway**: Think of an optical polymer as a perfectly paved, transparent highway for light. Just as a real highway has lane markings (refractive index), speed limits (light velocity), and entry ramps (numerical aperture), the polymer's molecular structure determines how fast, how straight, and how cleanly photons travel through it. **Glass is the rigid brick highway; polymer is the flexible, lightweight acrylic expressway.**

### Core Optical Performance Metrics

The following metrics are mandated by ISO 13485 / ASTM standards for optical grade polymers:

- **Refractive Index (n):** Ratio of speed of light in vacuum to that in the medium. Typical optical polymers: **n = 1.49 (PMMA), 1.58 (PC), 1.59 (PS)**.
- **Abbe Number (νd):** Measure of dispersion (chromatic aberration tendency). Higher νd = lower dispersion.
- **Total Luminous Transmittance (T_total):** Percentage of visible light (380–780 nm) passing through the specimen at **2 mm thickness**.
- **Haze (%):** Percentage of transmitted light deviated from the incident beam by forward scattering (>2.5°). Optical-grade polymers require **Haze < 1%**.
- **Yellowness Index (YI):** ASTM D1925 metric quantifying color degradation.

## 1.2 Medical Applications of Polymers

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Polymers (Biopolymers)** are polymeric materials engineered to interface with biological systems for diagnostic, therapeutic, or regenerative purposes. They are classified as **bioinert** (e.g., UHMWPE, PTFE), **bioactive** (e.g., bioactive glass-filled composites), or **bioresorbable/biodegradable** (e.g., PLA, PGA, PCL) based on their host response.

### Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Cellular Architect**: Imagine the human body as a bustling city. Biomedical polymers act as **scaffolding** around damaged buildings (tissue engineering), **pipes** carrying water (catheters, vascular grafts), and **time-release capsules** delivering medicine (drug delivery systems). They are designed either to **stay forever as silent infrastructure** (permanent implants) or to **gradually dissolve** as the body rebuilds itself (sutures, scaffolds).

### Hierarchy of Biocompatibility Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Biocompatibility Triad (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Bioinert polymers** — chemically stable, no host response (e.g., PTFE, UHMWPE).
> 2. **Biocompatible (interactive) polymers** — allow controlled tissue integration (e.g., surface-treated Ti-coated PE).
> 3. **Biodegradable polymers** — degrade into non-toxic metabolites absorbed by the body (e.g., PLA → lactic acid → CO₂ + H₂O).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Optical Fiber — Total Internal Reflection (TIR)

Optical fiber is the most demanding optical application of polymers. The polymer step-index fiber consists of:

- **Core** — high refractive index polymer (e.g., PMMA, n₁ ≈ 1.49).
- **Cladding** — lower refractive index polymer (e.g., fluorinated acrylate, n₂ ≈ 1.40).

### Operational Logic (Bulleted Steps)

1. **Light injection** at the input face.
2. The critical angle at the core–cladding interface must be exceeded for **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** to occur.
3. Acceptance cone defined by **Numerical Aperture (NA)** determines the maximum angle of light that can propagate.
4. **Attenuation coefficient (α)** governs signal loss over distance, measured in **dB/km**.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Optical Polymers

| Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| $n = \dfrac{c}{v}$ | Refractive index (speed ratio) | dimensionless |
| $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ | Snell's Law of refraction | dimensionless |
| $\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | Critical angle for TIR | dimensionless |
| $NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Numerical Aperture | dimensionless |
| $R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | Fresnel reflectance (normal incidence) | dimensionless |
| $T_{\text{total}} = (1-R)^2 \cdot e^{-\alpha L}$ | Total transmittance (Lambert–Beer) | dimensionless |
| $\alpha_{\text{dB/km}} = -\dfrac{10}{L}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Attenuation coefficient | dB/km |
| $\nu_d = \dfrac{n_d - 1}{n_F - n_C}$ | Abbe number (dispersion) | dimensionless |
| $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{2 n_1^2}$ | Relative refractive index difference | dimensionless |

## 2.2 Optical Lens Manufacturing — Refraction Geometry

A plano-convex lens focuses parallel rays to a focal point via Snell's law at each surface. The **Lensmaker's Equation** is:

$$\dfrac{1}{f} = (n-1)\left[\dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{(n-1)d}{n R_1 R_2}\right]$$

where **f** = focal length, **R₁, R₂** = radii of curvature, **d** = lens thickness, **n** = refractive index.

## 2.3 Biocompatibility — Hemolytic Index & Cytotoxicity

For medical-grade polymers, ISO 10993 mandates specific testing.

### Operational Logic — Biocompatibility Pipeline

1. **Material selection** (USP Class VI certified resin).
2. **Processing** under cleanroom ISO Class 7 conditions.
3. **Sterilization compatibility** testing (EtO, γ-radiation, autoclave).
4. **Extractables and leachables** analysis via HPLC/MS.
5. **In-vitro cytotoxicity** (MEM elution assay, ISO 10993-5).
6. **In-vivo sensitization** (guinea pig maximization test).
7. **Implantation study** (rabbit muscle, ISO 10993-6).

### KTU Formula Sheet — Medical Polymer Metrics

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Target |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------|
| Swelling Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{W_s - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Hydrogels: 30–90% |
| Gel Fraction | $GF = \dfrac{W_g}{W_i} \times 100$ | Crosslinked networks > 90% |
| Degradation Rate | $\dfrac{dM_n}{dt} = -k \cdot M_n$ | PLA: ~10% mass loss/month |
| Sterilization Dose | $D_{\gamma}$ for SAL = 10⁻⁶ | **25 kGy** (ISO 11137) |
| Drug Release (Higuchi) | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = k_H \sqrt{t}$ | Zero-order kinetics deviation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** These metrics are the contractual quality benchmarks agreed between polymer processors (e.g., SABIC, Evonik) and medical OEMs (e.g., Johnson & Johnson, Medtronic). Failure at any stage results in FDA 510(k) rejection.

## 2.4 Optical Storage Media (Polycarbonate Substrates)

CDs, DVDs, and Blu-ray discs use **polycarbonate (PC)** substrates because of:
- High transparency (>90% transmittance at 780 nm).
- Low birefringence (< 30 nm).
- Excellent dimensional stability at molding temperatures (**Tg ≈ 145 °C**).
- The injection-compression molding cycle produces pits of **0.5 µm (DVD), 0.32 µm (CD), 0.149 µm (Blu-ray)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Numerical Aperture (NA) of a Step-Index Polymer Optical Fiber

**Statement:** For a step-index optical fiber with core refractive index $n_1$ and cladding refractive index $n_2$, derive the expression for numerical aperture.

**Given:**
- Core refractive index $n_1$ (PMMA = 1.49).
- Cladding refractive index $n_2$ (fluorinated polymer = 1.40).
- Surrounding medium: air with $n_0 = 1.00$.

**Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law at the air–core interface:**

Light enters from air ($n_0$) into the core ($n_1$) at an acceptance angle $\theta_a$ relative to the fiber axis.

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1$$

**Step 2 — Relate angle $\theta_1$ (measured from axis) to the angle at the core–cladding interface:**

The angle of incidence at the core–cladding boundary is $\theta_2$, where by geometry:

$$\theta_2 = 90° - \theta_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = 90° - \theta_2$$

$$\sin\theta_1 = \cos\theta_2 = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Snell's Law at the core–cladding interface:**

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Step 1:**

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the critical angle condition for Total Internal Reflection (TIR):**

For TIR, the angle of incidence $\theta_2$ must equal the critical angle $\theta_c$:

$$\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\sin\theta_2 = n_2/n_1$ into Step 4:**

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the radical term:**

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Step 8 — Define Numerical Aperture (NA) as $\sin\theta_a$ (for $n_0 = 1$ in air):**

$$\boxed{NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

**Step 9 — Numerical Evaluation for PMMA / Fluorinated cladding:**

$$NA = \sqrt{(1.49)^2 - (1.40)^2} = \sqrt{2.2201 - 1.9600} = \sqrt{0.2601}$$

$$NA \approx 0.510$$

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.510) \approx 30.66°$$

This means the **acceptance half-angle** of the polymer optical fiber is approximately **30.66°**.

## 3.2 Calculation: Fresnel Reflection Loss at a Polymer–Air Interface

**Problem:** A PMMA lens (n = 1.49) is exposed to air (n = 1.00). Compute the reflectance at normal incidence.

**Step 1 — Apply Fresnel's equation for normal incidence:**

$$R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$R = \left(\dfrac{1.49 - 1.00}{1.49 + 1.00}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{0.49}{2.49}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$R = (0.1968)^2 \approx 0.0387$$

**Result:** Approximately **3.87%** of incident light is reflected at each air–PMMA interface. Anti-reflection coatings reduce this to < 0.5%.

## 3.3 Calculation: Hydrogel Swelling Ratio for a Contact Lens

**Problem:** A poly(HEMA) contact lens weighs **12.4 mg in dry state** and **28.7 mg fully swollen in saline**. Calculate the swelling ratio.

**Step 1 — Apply swelling ratio formula:**

$$SR = \dfrac{W_s - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$SR = \dfrac{28.7 - 12.4}{12.4} \times 100 = \dfrac{16.3}{12.4} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$SR \approx 131.5\%$$

This value falls within the **120–160% range** typical for soft contact lenses (FDA Group II/IV hydrogels).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Optical Polymer Selection

| Property | PMMA | PC | PS | Zeonex® COP | Application Fit |
|----------|------|-----|-----|-------------|-----------------|
| Refractive index (n_d) | 1.49 | 1.58 | 1.59 | 1.53 | Lens material |
| Abbe number (νd) | 58 | 30 | 31 | 56 | Optical clarity |
| Transmittance (%) | 92 | 89 | 88 | 92 | Display windows |
| Haze (%) | < 0.5 | < 1.0 | < 1.0 | < 0.3 | Premium optics |
| Tg (°C) | 105 | 145 | 100 | 138 | Heat resistance |
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.19 | 1.20 | 1.05 | 1.01 | Lightweight design |
| Cost index | 1.0× | 1.5× | 0.8× | 8.0× | Project economics |

## 3.5 Calculation: Sterilization Gamma Dose for Medical Implant

**Problem:** A UHMWPE acetabular cup requires a Sterility Assurance Level (SAL) of 10⁻⁶. Using ISO 11137 validation, calculate the minimum gamma dose.

**Step 1 — Recall ISO 11137-2 Table 1:**

For a verified dose of **9.0 kGy** achieving SAL = 10⁻², the dose to achieve SAL = 10⁻⁶ is determined by:

$$D_{\text{SAL}=10^{-6}} = D_{\text{SAL}=10^{-2}} + \text{dose increment}$$

**Step 2 — For average bioburden of 1.5 CFU/device:**

$$D_{\text{min}} \approx 25 \text{ kGy}$$

This is the **industry-standard validation dose** for single-use sterile medical devices.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

Define **optical polymer** and list any four key properties required for an engineering polymer to qualify as optical-grade.

**Model Answer:**

An **optical polymer** is a polymeric material specifically engineered to transmit, refract, or manipulate visible light with high fidelity, used as a lightweight, shatter-resistant alternative to inorganic glass.

**Four key properties:**

1. **High refractive index** (typically n_d = 1.49 to 1.59) for efficient light bending in lenses.
2. **High total luminous transmittance** (> 88% at 2 mm) for clarity.
3. **Low haze** (< 1%) to minimize forward scattering.
4. **Low birefringence** (< 30 nm) to avoid polarization distortion in display and lens applications.

**Additional relevant properties:** High Abbe number (low dispersion), suitable glass transition temperature for thermal stability, and resistance to UV yellowing.

**Marks distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + 4 properties × 0.5 mark = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

Distinguish between **bioinert**, **bioactive**, and **biodegradable** polymers. Give one example of each used in medical applications.

**Model Answer:**

| Class | Behavior in Body | Example |
|-------|------------------|---------|
| **Bioinert** | Chemically stable; elicits minimal host response; remains permanent | UHMWPE (hip joint liner), PTFE (vascular grafts) |
| **Bioactive** | Promotes specific biological response, e.g., bone bonding | Bioactive glass-filled polyethylene composites |
| **Biodegradable** | Degrades into non-toxic metabolites over time, resorbed by body | PLA, PGA (absorbable sutures, scaffolds) |

**Marks distribution:** 3 classes × 1 mark each = 3 marks.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of a step-index polymer optical fiber. Calculate the NA for a fiber with core refractive index n₁ = 1.49 and cladding refractive index n₂ = 1.40.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of Snell's Law at air–core interface:**

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1$$

where $\theta_a$ is the acceptance angle, $\theta_1$ is the refraction angle inside the core, $n_0 = 1.00$ (air).

**Step 2 — Geometric relationship:**

$$\theta_2 = 90° - \theta_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\theta_1 = \cos\theta_2 = \sqrt{1-\sin^2\theta_2}$$

**Step 3 — Critical angle condition for TIR:**

$$\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Step 5 — Define NA:**

$$\boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$NA = \sqrt{(1.49)^2 - (1.40)^2} = \sqrt{2.2201 - 1.9600} = \sqrt{0.2601} \approx 0.510$$

**Acceptance angle:**

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.510) \approx 30.66°$$

---

**(b)** Explain the role of **polycarbonate (PC)** in optical data storage media (CDs, DVDs, Blu-ray). Discuss the optical and mechanical property requirements for this application.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Background (2 marks):** Optical data storage discs use a transparent polymer substrate to protect the data layer and allow laser readout through the polymer. The laser (780 nm for CD, 650 nm for DVD, 405 nm for Blu-ray) refracts off microscopic pits.

**Optical requirements (3 marks):**

1. **High transmittance** at the laser wavelength: PC transmits > 88% at 780 nm and 405 nm.
2. **Low birefringence** (< 30 nm) to preserve polarization states.
3. **Refractive index stability** at $n_d = 1.58$ to ensure accurate focal path.
4. **Low optical dispersion** for tight focusing of short-wavelength lasers.

**Mechanical/thermal requirements (2 marks):**

1. **Tg > 140 °C** to withstand injection-compression molding temperatures (~330 °C).
2. **Dimensional stability** with low mold shrinkage (~0.5%).
3. **High impact resistance** for thin-disc durability.
4. **Hydrolytic stability** to prevent environmental degradation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **biocompatibility requirements** for polymers used in **medical implant applications**. Mention the relevant ISO standards.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Biocompatibility is defined** (ISO 10993-1) as the ability of a material to perform its intended function with an appropriate host response in a specific application.

**Key requirements:**

1. **Non-toxicity** — no release of leachable toxic substances (tested per ISO 10993-5).
2. **Non-carcinogenicity** — no tumor induction (ISO 10993-3).
3. **Non-sensitization** — no allergic response (ISO 10993-10).
4. **Non-mutagenicity** — no genetic alteration (ISO 10993-3, Ames test).
5. **Sterilization compatibility** — withstands EtO, γ-radiation (25 kGy), or autoclave (121 °C, 15 psi).
6. **Hemocompatibility** — for blood-contact devices, low hemolysis (ASTM F756, < 5% hemolytic index).
7. **Mechanical durability** — fatigue resistance for load-bearing implants.

**Relevant standards:**

- **ISO 10993 series** — biological evaluation of medical devices.
- **USP Class VI** — United States Pharmacopeia plastic classification.
- **ASTM F748** — standard practice for selecting generic biological test methods.
- **ISO 13485** — quality management for medical device manufacturers.

---

**(b)** Explain the **processing of PMMA bone cement** for orthopedic applications. Discuss the polymerization chemistry and key product specifications.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Application (1 mark):** PMMA bone cement is used to anchor prosthetic implants (e.g., hip, knee) to the surrounding bone. It does **not** act as an adhesive but as a **grouting agent** that fills the gap between implant and bone.

**Chemistry (3 marks):**

PMMA bone cement is supplied as a **two-component system:**

- **Powder phase:** Pre-polymerized PMMA beads (~50–80 µm), initiator (benzoyl peroxide, BPO), and radio-opacifier (BaSO₄ or ZrO₂).
- **Liquid phase:** Methyl methacrylate (MMA) monomer, activator (N,N-dimethyl-p-toluidine, DMPT), and stabilizer (hydroquinone).

**Setting reaction:**

1. DMPT reduces BPO → generates benzoyl radicals.
2. Radicals initiate MMA polymerization via free-radical chain reaction.
3. The polymer beads swell with monomer, forming a continuous matrix.

$$\text{MMA monomer} \xrightarrow{\text{BPO/DMPT}} \text{PMMA matrix} + \text{Heat (~80–100 °C)}$$

**Key specifications (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Setting time | 5–15 minutes |
| Doughing time | 2–4 minutes |
| Peak temperature | < 90 °C (to avoid bone necrosis) |
| Compressive strength | > 70 MPa |
| Radio-opacity | > 1.5 mm Al equivalent |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - In NA derivations, students **skip the geometric relation** $\theta_2 = 90° - \theta_1$ and directly write the final formula → **lose 2 marks**.
> - For PMMA bone cement, students often **confuse PMMA beads with pre-polymerized polymer** vs. monomer — clarify that the *beads are pre-polymerized* and the *liquid is the monomer* that polymerizes in situ.
> - In biocompatibility answers, forgetting to mention **ISO 10993** is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Optical & Medical Applications**

### Optical Applications

- **Optical polymers** require: high transmittance (> 88%), low haze (< 1%), controlled refractive index, low birefringence.
- **Key polymers:** PMMA (lenses, fibers), PC (CD/DVD, Blu-ray, eyewear), PS (disposable optics), COP (high-end lenses).
- **Numerical Aperture:** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ — determines light-gathering ability.
- **Critical angle:** $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$ — minimum angle for Total Internal Reflection.
- **Fresnel reflection loss:** ~4% per air–polymer interface; mitigated by anti-reflection coatings.
- **Polycarbonate (PC)** dominates data storage due to high Tg, low birefringence, high transmittance, and injection-compression moldability.

### Medical Applications

- **Three classes:** Bioinert (UHMWPE, PTFE), Bioactive (composites), Biodegradable (PLA, PGA).
- **Biocompatibility standard:** ISO 10993 series (cytotoxicity, sensitization, implantation).
- **PMMA bone cement:** Two-component (powder + monomer); setting time 5–15 min; peak temp < 90 °C.
- **Hydrogels:** Swelling ratio formula $SR = \left(W_s - W_d\right)/W_d \times 100$; critical for contact lenses, wound dressings.
- **Sterilization dose:** **25 kGy γ-radiation** for SAL = 10⁻⁶ (ISO 11137).
- **UHMWPE:** Used in acetabular cups due to wear resistance (> 10⁷ cycles), bioinertness.
- **Processing of medical polymers:** injection molding, extrusion, blow molding, solution casting, electrospinning (scaffolds), 3D bioprinting (patient-specific implants).

> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "P-MED-OPT":**
> **P**MMA (lenses, bone cement) • **M**edical grades → USP VI, ISO 10993 • **E**tO / γ sterilization • **D**egradable (PLA, PGA) • **O**ptical fibers → NA, TIR • **P**olycarbonate → Blu-ray, eyewear • **T**otal luminous transmittance > 88%.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Processing Techniques # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Polymer Processing Techniques? **Polymer Processing Techniques** refer to the industrial manufacturing methods used to convert raw polymeric materials (in the form of powders, granules, pellets, sheets, or liquids) into finished or semi-finished products of desired shape, size, and mechanical integrity. These techniques involve a combination of **transport phenomena** (heat, mass, and momentum transfer), **rheology**, and **mold/die design** to shape polymers under controlled temperature and pressure conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPOT502 - Module 3):** *"Processing Techniques"* encompasses the study of primary shaping methods (compression, transfer, injection, extrusion, blow molding, thermoforming, rotational molding, casting, calendering, and reinforced plastic processing) along with the underlying process variables, equipment, and product quality considerations. ## 1.2 Classification of Processing Techniques Polymer processing techniques can be systematically classified based on the **state of the polymer during shaping** and the **type of forming force** applied: | Category | Polymer State | Forming Mechanism | Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **Molding** | Viscous melt / Softened solid | Pressure in closed mold | Compression, Transfer, Injection | | **Continuous Forming** | Viscous melt | Pressure through die | Extrusion, Calendering | | **Secondary Forming** | Sheet/Preform | Pressure / Vacuum / Drape | Thermoforming, Blow Molding | | **Rotational** | Powder / Liquid | Centrifugal force + Heat | Rotational Molding | | **Casting** | Liquid monomer / melt | Gravity / No pressure | Casting, Foaming | | **Reinforced Processing** | Resin + Fiber | Hand lay / Spray / Filament winding | FRP processing | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "Polymer Processing = Kitchen Cooking"** > Think of polymer processing like preparing food in a kitchen. The **polymer (raw ingredient)** is like dough, chocolate, or sugar syrup. The **processing machine** is your cooking vessel (pressure cooker, oven, or extruder-nozzle). The **heat and pressure** are like the stove and your hands pressing the dough. Just as a chef controls temperature, time, and pressure to bake a cake, an engineer controls **temperature profile, residence time, and molding pressure** to shape a polymer part. Different dishes (cake, pasta, candy) require different techniques — similarly, polymers need specific processing routes based on their rheology and end-use. ## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics The following standard engineering parameters govern every polymer processing technique: - **Melt Temperature ($T_m$ or $T_f$):** typically **150°C – 350°C** for thermoplastics - **Mold Temperature ($T_{mold}$):** typically **20°C – 200°C** - **Injection/Clamping Pressure:** typically **35 – 140 MPa** - **Shrinkage Allowance:** typically **0.1% – 4%** depending on polymer - **Cycle Time:** typically **10 s – 10 min** depending on technique - **L/D Ratio (Extruder/Die):** typically **20:1 to 30:1** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Processing The fundamental scientific basis of polymer processing rests on three pillars: ### 2.1.1 Heat Transfer Polymers are poor thermal conductors (thermal conductivity $k \approx 0.1$ to $0.5 \text{ W/m·K}$). This implies: - Heating and cooling are **rate-limiting steps** - Cycle times are dictated by **transient heat conduction** through the molded part - The **Fourier heat conduction equation** governs the temperature field: $$q = -k \nabla T$$ where $q$ is heat flux ($\text{W/m}^2$), $k$ is thermal conductivity, and $\nabla T$ is the temperature gradient. ### 2.1.2 Viscous Flow (Rheology) Polymer melts exhibit **non-Newtonian (pseudoplastic/shear-thinning)** behavior. The relationship between shear stress $\tau$ and shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ follows the **Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele) model**: $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$$ where $K$ is the consistency index and $n$ is the power-law index (typically $n = 0.3$ to $0.8$ for polymer melts). ### 2.1.3 Pressure Development For flow through channels (dies, runners, gates), the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** adapted for power-law fluids gives the volumetric flow rate: $$Q = \frac{\pi R^3}{1+3n} \left( \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L \cdot K} \right)^{1/n}$$ ## 2.2 Major Processing Techniques — Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Compression Molding - **Principle:** A pre-weighed polymer charge (thermoset granule/preform or thermoplastic blank) is placed in a heated mold cavity. The mold is closed under pressure, forcing the material to flow and fill the cavity, where it cures (thermoset) or cools (thermoplastic). - **Steps:** Charge placement → Mold closure → Flow + Cure → Cooling/Ejection - **Why used:** Large, bulky parts with simple geometry; high-strength thermoset composites; minimal material waste. ### 2.2.2 Transfer Molding - **Principle:** Polymer is preheated in a **transfer pot** above the mold, then forced through a sprue into a closed heated mold cavity. - **Advantage over compression:** Better dimensional accuracy, less flash, suitable for intricate inserts. - **Variants:** Pot transfer molding, plunger transfer molding, screw-transfer molding. ### 2.2.3 Injection Molding - **Principle:** Polymer melt is injected under high pressure into a closed, temperature-controlled mold cavity, where it solidifies into the desired shape. - **Cycle Phases:** Filling → Packing/Holding → Cooling → Plasticization (for next cycle) → Ejection - **Why used:** High production rate, complex geometries, excellent surface finish, repeatable tolerances. ### 2.2.4 Extrusion - **Principle:** Polymer melt is continuously forced through a shaping **die** to produce a continuous profile (pipe, sheet, film, filament). - **Key Equipment:** Single-screw or twin-screw extruder with three functional zones: - **Feed zone** (conveying solid) - **Compression/Metering zone** (melting + pumping) - **Mixing zone** (homogenization) - **Output equation:** $$Q_{extruder} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi D^3 N \sin^2\phi}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$ where $D$ is screw diameter, $N$ is screw speed, $d$ is channel depth, $\phi$ is helix angle, and $\Delta P/L$ is pressure gradient. ### 2.2.5 Blow Molding - **Principle:** A molten polymer tube (**parison**) is inflated against the walls of a cooled mold cavity using compressed air, taking the mold shape. - **Variants:** Extrusion blow molding, Injection blow molding, Stretch blow molding (PET bottles). ### 2.2.6 Thermoforming - **Principle:** A thermoplastic sheet is heated above its glass transition temperature ($T_g$), then forced against a mold by vacuum, pressure, or mechanical means. - **Variants:** Vacuum forming, Pressure forming, Drape forming, Matched-die forming. ### 2.2.7 Rotational Molding - **Principle:** Polymer powder is placed in a closed mold, which is rotated biaxially and heated. The powder melts, coats the mold walls, and fuses into a hollow part. - **Advantage:** Stress-free, seamless, large hollow parts (tanks, kayaks). ### 2.2.8 Calendering - **Principle:** Polymer melt is passed through a series of heated counter-rotating rollers to produce thin sheets or films of uniform thickness. - **Use:** PVC films/sheets for packaging, flooring, tarpaulins. ### 2.2.9 Casting - **Principle:** Liquid polymer (monomer + hardener, or melt) is poured into a mold and allowed to solidify by chemical reaction (thermoset) or cooling. - **Examples:** Nylon casting, polyurethane casting, acrylic casting, epoxy casting. ### 2.2.10 Reinforced Plastic Processing - **Hand Lay-Up:** Resin is manually applied to fibers placed in an open mold. - **Spray-Up:** Chopped fibers + resin sprayed simultaneously onto a mold. - **Filament Winding:** Continuous fibers impregnated with resin and wound on a rotating mandrel. - **Pultrusion:** Fibers pulled through a resin bath and a heated die to form continuous profiles. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes **all critical formulas** expected for KTU University Exam questions on Processing Techniques. L_{part}}{L_{mold}} \times 100\%$ | Mold shrinkage | Dimensional accuracy | | 19 | $N_{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\eta}$ | Reynolds number (Newtonian) | Flow regime check | | 20 | $\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho C_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity | Cooling/heating rate | > [!NOTE] > **Symbol Glossary:** $Q$ = flow rate, $\tau$ = shear stress, $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate, $K$ = consistency, $n$ = power-law index, $W$ = channel width, $H$ = channel height, $L$ = length, $\eta$ = viscosity, $D$ = diameter, $N$ = screw RPM, $\phi$ = helix angle, $v_{bx}$ = bar velocity x-component, $s$ = wall thickness, $T_m$ = melt temp, $T_w$ = wall temp, $T_e$ = ejection temp, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $\rho$ = density, $C_p$ = specific heat. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These processing techniques form the **manufacturing backbone of the global polymer industry**, enabling production of: - Automotive components (dashboards, bumpers, fuel tanks — injection + blow molding) - Packaging (films, bottles, containers — extrusion + blow + thermoforming) - Construction (pipes, sheets, profiles, FRP panels — extrusion + pultrusion) - Consumer goods (toys, kitchenware, electronics housings — injection molding) - Aerospace and marine (composite parts — filament winding + hand lay-up) - Medical devices (tubing, implants — extrusion + injection molding) --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Flow Rate in an Extruder Screw Channel The extruder screw channel is modeled as an **unwound rectangular channel** of width $W$ and depth $H$, with the barrel moving relative to the stationary screw at velocity $v_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\phi$. ### Step 1: Set up the geometry The channel is unwound. The barrel surface moves at velocity $v_{bx}$ in the $x$-direction (down-channel). The pressure $P$ varies along the channel length $L$ from inlet to outlet. ### Step 2: Apply the Navier-Stokes equation in the z-direction For a fully developed, laminar flow with no inertia: $$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \eta \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2} = \frac{1}{\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$ ### Step 3: Integrate twice with respect to z $$\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} = \frac{1}{\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} z + C_1$$ $$v_x = \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} z^2 + C_1 z + C_2$$ ### Step 4: Apply boundary conditions - No-slip at barrel wall: $v_x(z=H) = v_{bx}$ - No-slip at screw root: $v_x(z=0) = 0$ From the second condition: $C_2 = 0$. From the first condition: $$v_{bx} = \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} H^2 + C_1 H$$ $$C_1 = \frac{v_{bx}}{H} - \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} H$$ ### Step 5: Substitute back to get velocity profile $$v_x(z) = \frac{v_{bx} \cdot z}{H} - \frac{H^2}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \left( \frac{z}{H} - \frac{z^2}{H^2} \right)$$ Or in compact form: $$v_x(z) = v_{bx} \cdot \frac{z}{H} - \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} z(H - z)$$ ### Step 6: Integrate velocity over the channel cross-section to get volumetric flow rate $$Q = W \int_0^H v_x(z) \, dz$$ Compute the integral term by term: $$Q = W \int_0^H \left[ \frac{v_{bx} \cdot z}{H} - \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} z(H - z) \right] dz$$ $$Q = W \left[ \frac{v_{bx}}{H} \cdot \frac{H^2}{2} - \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \int_0^H (zH - z^2) dz \right]$$ The remaining integral is: $$\int_0^H (zH - z^2) dz = \left[ \frac{H z^2}{2} - \frac{z^3}{3} \right]_0^H = \frac{H^3}{2} - \frac{H^3}{3} = \frac{H^3}{6}$$ ### Step 7: Final expression for net flow rate Substituting the integral: $$Q = W \left[ \frac{v_{bx} H}{2} - \frac{H^3}{12\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \right]$$ Since $\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \frac{\Delta P}{L}$ (pressure drop over channel length): $$Q = \frac{1}{2} v_{bx} W H - \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$ The two terms are: - **Drag flow:** $Q_d = \frac{1}{2} v_{bx} W H$ (flow due to barrel motion) - **Pressure flow:** $Q_p = \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$ (back flow due to die resistance) $$\boxed{Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p = \frac{1}{2} v_{bx} W H - \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The drag flow is the **forward pumping** caused by the relative motion of the barrel. The pressure flow is the **reverse leakage** caused by the pressure built up at the die. The net throughput is the balance between these two. To maximize output, we want **high screw speed, large channel dimensions, and moderate back-pressure**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Cooling Time in Injection Molding (Simplified) For a thick-walled plastic part in a cooled mold (assuming lumped capacitance with $Bi \to 0$): ### Step 1: Define geometry and initial/boundary conditions - Wall thickness: $s$ - Initial melt temperature: $T_m$ - Mold wall temperature: $T_w$ - Ejection temperature: $T_e$ (typically above $T_g$) - Thermal diffusivity: $\alpha = k/(\rho C_p)$ ### Step 2: One-dimensional transient heat conduction The temperature field $T(x, t)$ satisfies: $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$ ### Step 3: Apply separation of variables and solve For a slab of thickness $s$ cooled from both sides, the average temperature solution is: $$\frac{T_{avg}(t) - T_w}{T_m - T_w} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\left[-(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 \alpha t / s^2\right]$$ ### Step 4: Retain only the first term (long-time approximation, valid for most molding cycles) $$\frac{T_{avg}(t) - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \approx \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\pi^2 \alpha t / s^2\right)$$ ### Step 5: Solve for the cooling time t Taking the natural logarithm: $$\ln\left(\frac{T_{avg}(t) - T_w}{T_m - T_w}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{s^2}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{s^2} = \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \ln\left(\frac{T_e - T_w}{T_m - T_w}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{8 (T_m - T_w)}{\pi^2 (T_e - T_w)}\right)$$ ### Step 6: Final expression for cooling time $$\boxed{t_{cooling} = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Cooling time scales as $s^2$. Doubling the wall thickness **quadruples** the cooling time. This is why thick parts are the dominant bottleneck in injection molding cycle times. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Processing Technique | Polymer Type | Form | Viscosity | Recommended Technique | Rationale | |---|---|---|---|---| | Thermoplastic (PE, PP, PS) | Pellets | Low–Medium (melt) | Injection, Extrusion, Blow molding | High fluidity, repeatable cycles | | Thermoplastic (PA, PC, PMMA) | Pellets | Medium–High (melt) | Injection (with high T) | Engineering precision parts | | Thermoplastic (PVC) | Powder/Pellet | High (degrades easily) | Calendering, Extrusion, Paste casting | Controlled shear, low thermal stress | | Thermoset (PF, MF, UF) | Granules/Preform | Very high (must cure) | Compression, Transfer | Cure requires heat + pressure | | Thermoset (EP, UP) | Liquid resin | Low (liquid) | Hand lay-up, Filament winding, Casting, RTM | Liquid impregnation of fibers | | Elastomer (NR, SBR) | Solid bale | Very high | Compression, Extrusion (with crosslinking) | Vulcanization requires heat + pressure | | Foam (PU, PS) | Liquid/Beads | Variable | Foaming + Casting, Steam-chest molding | Expansion requires controlled nucleation | ## 3.4 Typical Process Parameter Ranges (Engineering Reference) | Process | Temperature (°C) | Pressure (MPa) | Cycle Time | Typical Products | |---|---|---|---|---| | Compression Molding | 150 – 200 | 10 – 50 | 1 – 10 min | Electrical switchboards, FRP panels | | Transfer Molding | 150 – 200 | 15 – 80 | 1 – 5 min | Encapsulated electronic parts, plugs | | Injection Molding | 200 – 320 | 35 – 140 | 10 s – 2 min | Buckets, housings, gears, medical disposables | | Extrusion | 150 – 300 | 5 – 35 | Continuous | Pipes, sheets, films, profiles, cables | | Blow Molding | 150 – 250 | 0.3 – 1.0 (air) | 5 s – 1 min | Bottles, tanks, containers | | Thermoforming | 120 – 200 | Vacuum / 0.1 – 0.5 | 5 s – 1 min | Trays, cups, blister packs | | Rotational Molding | 250 – 350 | 0 (centrifugal) | 10 – 30 min | Water tanks, kayaks, toys | | Calendering | 150 – 200 | High roller pressure | Continuous | PVC sheets, floor tiles, films | | Hand Lay-up | Room – 80 | 0 (manual) | Hours – Days | Boat hulls, aircraft components | | Filament Winding | Room – 100 | 0.1 – 0.5 | Hours | Pressure vessels, pipes, rocket casings | | Pultrusion | 100 – 180 | Low | Continuous | Structural profiles, gratings, rods | --- # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **"Differentiate between compression molding and transfer molding techniques for thermoset polymers."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Compression Molding | Transfer Molding | |---|---|---| | **Material placement** | Charge placed directly in mold cavity | Charge preheated in transfer pot above mold | | **Pressure application** | Pressure applied directly on material in cavity | Pressure applied via plunger in pot; material forced through sprue | | **Mold design** | Open mold; high flash expected | Closed mold; minimal flash | | **Suitability** | Simple, large, bulky parts | Complex parts with delicate inserts | | **Dimensional accuracy** | Lower | Higher | | **Cycle time** | Longer cure time | Faster due to preheating | | **Material wastage** | Higher (flash) | Lower | > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution Tip (KTU Examiner Pattern):** Award **1 mark** for each correct distinguishing parameter, capped at 3 marks. Use a clean comparative table for full marks. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"Define the term 'clamping tonnage' in injection molding. Why must it be carefully selected?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Clamping tonnage is the maximum force that the injection molding machine's clamping unit can apply to keep the mold closed against the injection pressure acting on the projected area of the cavity and runners. It is given by: $$F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}$$ where $P_{cavity}$ is the average cavity pressure (typically 35–70 MPa) and $A_{projected}$ is the projected area of the part + runner system on the parting plane. **Why careful selection is needed (1.5 marks):** - **Insufficient tonnage** → mold opens slightly → **flash** formation and **short shots** (incomplete parts) - **Excessive tonnage** → machine oversizing → higher capital cost, energy waste, possible mold damage - It governs **part quality, machine size selection, and operating economics** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner Cue:** Always state the equation $F_{clamp} = P \times A$ explicitly. Mention both under- and over-tonnage consequences for full credit. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks) **"Explain the injection molding process in detail. Discuss the different stages of the injection molding cycle with a neat sketch. List the typical defects encountered in injection molded parts and their causes."** **Model Solution:** **(a) Process Description and Cycle Stages (7 marks):** **Process Description (3 marks):** Injection molding is a **discontinuous (cyclic) manufacturing process** used to produce discrete polymer parts in high volumes. Polymer pellets are fed from a hopper into a heated barrel, melted by external heaters and screw shear, then injected under high pressure into a closed, temperature-controlled mold cavity. After solidification, the part is ejected and the cycle repeats. **Stages of the Injection Molding Cycle (4 marks):** 1. **Mold Closing and Clamping** — The two halves of the mold close and are held by the clamping unit (toggle or hydraulic). 2. **Injection (Filling)** — The screw acts as a plunger and injects melt into the cavity at high velocity (typically 100–500 mm/s). The volume injected equals 30–80% of the cavity volume. 3. **Packing (Holding)** — Pressure is maintained (lower than injection) to compensate for volumetric shrinkage as the material cools. Additional melt is pushed in. 4. **Cooling** — The part continues to cool in the mold until it reaches ejection temperature (just above $T_g$ for amorphous or below $T_m$ for semi-crystalline). Cooling time dominates cycle time. 5. **Screw Rotation and Plasticization** — Simultaneously with cooling, the screw rotates, conveying fresh pellets from the feed zone, melting them, and accumulating the next shot in front of the screw. 6. **Mold Opening and Part Ejection** — The mold opens, ejector pins push the part out, and the cycle restarts. **Equation for total cycle time:** $$t_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$$ **(b) Typical Defects in Injection Molded Parts (7 marks):** | # | Defect | Cause | Remedy | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Short shot** (incomplete fill) | Insufficient melt, low pressure, low temperature, poor venting | Increase injection pressure, melt temperature, add vents | | 2 | **Flash** (excess material at parting line) | Excessive injection pressure, insufficient clamp tonnage, worn mold | Adjust clamp tonnage, reduce pressure, repair mold | | 3 | **Sink marks** (depressions) | Inadequate packing/holding, thick sections | Increase holding pressure/time, redesign part | | 4 | **Voids / Bubbles** | Trapped air, excessive moisture in resin | Dry resin, improve venting, adjust injection speed | | 5 | **Weld lines** (knit lines) | Two melt fronts meet with poor fusion | Increase melt temperature, improve flow path design | | 6 | **Warping** | Differential cooling, non-uniform shrinkage | Optimize cooling circuit design, control mold temperature | | 7 | **Burn marks** (diesel effect) | Trapped air compressed and overheated | Add vents, reduce injection speed | | 8 | **Jetting** (snake-like flow) | Melt injected through narrow gate at high speed | Use proper gate design, reposition gate, reduce speed | | 9 | **Flow marks** | Frozen layer formation on cold mold surface | Increase mold temperature, melt temperature | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget to mention the holding/packing phase** as a separate stage and conflate it with injection. The cycle has **three distinct pressure-controlled stages** (injection, holding, cooling) — losing this distinction can cost 2 marks. Also, when listing defects, **always pair each defect with its cause and at least one remedy** for full credit. --- ### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks) **"Describe the extrusion process for polymers. With a neat schematic, explain the working of a single-screw extruder. Derive an expression for the volumetric output of a single-screw extruder."** **Model Solution:** **(a) Extrusion Process and Single-Screw Extruder Operation (7 marks):** **Process Description (2 marks):** Extrusion is a **continuous manufacturing process** in which polymer melt is forced through a shaping die to produce a continuous profile (pipe, sheet, film, filament, cable coating). The most common machine is the **single-screw extruder**, which has three functional zones along the screw. **Schematic Layout (refer to the diagram in SECTION 4.5 above) (2 marks):** - **Hopper** → feeds pellets by gravity - **Feed Zone** — deep channel, solids conveyed, begins heating - **Compression (Melt) Zone** — channel depth decreases, polymer melts, air vented - **Metering Zone** — shallow channel, melt pressurized, delivered uniformly - **Adapter and Breaker Plate** — straightens flow, supports screen pack, builds pressure - **Die** — shapes the melt into final cross-section - **Calibration, Cooling, Haul-off, Cutting** — downstream equipment **Operating Parameters (3 marks):** - Screw diameter: **$D$ = 20 – 300 mm** - L/D ratio: **20:1 to 30:1** (modern extruders) - Compression ratio: **2:1 to 4:1** - Screw speed: **20 – 200 RPM** - Helix angle: typically **17.5°** (gives maximum conveying) - Barrel heaters: **3 to 5 zones** with independent PID control - Typical pressures at die: **5 – 35 MPa** **(b) Derivation of Volumetric Output of a Single-Screw Extruder (7 marks):** The derivation steps are exactly as shown in **SECTION 3.1**. Reproducing the key steps for completeness: **Step 1 — Model the unwound channel** as a shallow rectangular channel of width $W$, depth $H$, length $L$, with barrel surface moving at $v_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\phi$ relative to the screw. **Step 2 — Navier-Stokes simplification** (steady, laminar, fully developed, no inertia, constant viscosity): $$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \eta \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2}$$ **Step 3 — Boundary conditions:** - $v_x = 0$ at $z = 0$ (screw root) - $v_x = v_{bx}$ at $z = H$ (barrel) **Step 4 — Velocity profile:** $$v_x(z) = v_{bx} \cdot \frac{z}{H} - \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} z(H - z)$$ **Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate by integration:** $$Q = W \int_0^H v_x(z) \, dz$$ Compute the integral: $$Q = W \int_0^H \left[ \frac{v_{bx} \cdot z}{H} - \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\Delta P}{L} z(H - z) \right] dz$$ Step-by-step integration: $$Q = W \left[ \frac{v_{bx}}{H} \cdot \frac{z^2}{2} \bigg|_0^H - \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \left( \frac{H z^2}{2} - \frac{z^3}{3} \right) \bigg|_0^H \right]$$ $$Q = W \left[ \frac{v_{bx} H}{2} - \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \left( \frac{H^3}{2} - \frac{H^3}{3} \right) \right]$$ $$Q = W \left[ \frac{v_{bx} H}{2} - \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \cdot \frac{H^3}{6} \right]$$ $$Q = \frac{1}{2} v_{bx} W H - \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$ **Step 6 — Final Net Output Expression:** $$\boxed{Q_{net} = \frac{1}{2} \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot W H - \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}}$$ The first term is **drag flow** and the second is **pressure flow (back flow)**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common error students make is **forgetting the negative sign convention** (pressure flow opposes drag flow). The final expression must clearly show $Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$. Some students also drop the **helix angle $\cos\phi$** term — losing 1 mark. For full credit, **state assumptions explicitly** (steady, laminar, Newtonian, fully developed, no leakage over flights) at the start of the derivation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Processing Techniques** ### Core Concepts - ☐ Polymer processing converts raw polymer into shaped products using **heat + pressure + time + flow**. - ☐ Classification: Molding (compression, transfer, injection), Continuous forming (extrusion, calendering), Secondary forming (thermoforming, blow molding), Rotational, Casting, Reinforced plastic processing. ### Injection Molding - ☐ Cycle: Clamp → Fill → Pack → Cool → Plasticize → Eject - ☐ Clamping tonnage equation: $F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}$ - ☐ Cooling time scales as **$s^2$** (square of wall thickness) - ☐ Typical defects: flash, short shot, sink marks, weld lines, warping, voids, jetting ### Extrusion - ☐ Screw zones: **Feed → Compression → Metering** - ☐ L/D ratio: **20:1 to 30:1**; Helix angle: **17.5°** - ☐ Net output: $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$ - ☐ Drag flow: $Q_d = \frac{1}{2} v_{bx} W H$ ; Pressure flow: $Q_p = \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$ ### Compression Molding - ☐ Used for **thermosets, FRP, large bulky parts** - ☐ Cure + heat + pressure simultaneously - ☐ High flash, lower dimensional accuracy ### Transfer Molding - ☐ Material preheated in **transfer pot**, then forced into closed mold - ☐ Better accuracy than compression molding; suited for **inserts** ### Blow Molding - ☐ Parison inflated by compressed air - ☐ Three types: **Extrusion, Injection, Stretch blow molding** ### Thermoforming - ☐ Heated sheet formed by vacuum / pressure / mechanical force - ☐ Low mold cost, suited for **large surface area, thin-walled parts** ### Rotational Molding - ☐ Biaxial rotation + heat; powder melts and coats mold - ☐ Stress-free, large hollow parts ### Calendering - ☐ Series of counter-rotating heated rollers - ☐ Used for **PVC films and sheets** ### FRP Processing - ☐ Hand lay-up, Spray-up, Filament Winding, Pultrusion - ☐ Used for high-strength composite parts ### Quick Numerical Formulas to Memorize - ☐ Hagen-Poiseuille for power-law fluids in circular channels - ☐ Drag + pressure flow for extruder output - ☐ Cooling time equation with thermal diffusivity - ☐ Apparent shear rate in rectangular die: $\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \frac{6Q}{W H^2}$ ### Key Differences to Recall in Exam - ☐ Compression vs. Transfer molding (preheating + plunger) - ☐ Injection vs. Extrusion (cyclic vs. continuous) - ☐ Blow molding vs. Thermoforming (parison vs. sheet) - ☐ Rotational vs. Injection molding (centrifugal vs. pressure) > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Day Strategy (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Draw a clean block diagram for **each** processing technique asked. Always state **process parameters** (temperature, pressure, cycle time). For derivations, list **assumptions** first, then proceed with the standard momentum balance. Practice the extruder derivation at least 3 times — it is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in KTU Module 3 exams. ---

topics.title: Compression Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compression Moulding**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compression Moulding (CM)** is a closed-mould, high-pressure thermoset plastic manufacturing process in which a measured charge of **moulding compound** (powder, preform, granulate, sheet, or dough) is placed directly into an open, heated mould cavity. The mould is then closed by a hydraulic press, applying pressure (**typically 7–35 MPa**) and heat (**typically 120–200 °C**) simultaneously, which forces the material to conform to the cavity shape while a cross-linking (curing) reaction is initiated. Once the material has hardened through irreversible chemical cross-linking, the mould opens and the finished part is ejected.

Compression moulding is classified as a **"pressure–heat combined"** moulding technique and is considered the **oldest** industrial polymer forming process (patented in the 19th century for rubber and later adapted for phenolic resins in 1907 by Leo Baekeland).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Waffle Iron"**
> Imagine a heavy cast-iron waffle maker on a hot stove. You pour batter onto the lower plate (the *charge* of material), close the heavy lid (the *press*), and lock it shut. Heat cooks the batter, while the weight of the lid spreads it evenly into the lattice pattern. When the waffle is golden and rigid, you open the iron and pop the waffle out.
>
> - The **stove** is the *heated platen*.
> - The **batter** is the *uncured thermoset charge*.
> - The **closing action** is the *press ram*.
> - The **cooking reaction** is the *exothermic cure*.
> - The **popped waffle** is the *ejected moulded part*.

In a polymer compression mould, the "cooking" is actually a permanent chemical reaction: linear or branched polymer chains are irreversibly joined into a 3-D network — the part cannot be remelted.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Constants

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Moulding Pressure** | Hydraulic force per unit projected area, typically **7–35 MPa** |
| **Moulding Temperature** | Platen set-point, typically **120–200 °C** |
| **Cure Time** | Time at temperature to achieve 95–99% conversion, typically **30 s – 10 min** |
| **Bulk Factor (BF)** | Ratio of bulk volume of charge to its moulded volume (typically **2.0 – 10.0**) |
| **Shrinkage** | Linear contraction on cooling, typically **0.1 – 0.5 %** for filled systems |
| **Flash** | Excess material that escapes through mould-parting line; thickness typically **0.05 – 0.2 mm** |
| **Preform** | Pre-shaped tablet of charge (reduces bulk factor, air entrapment) |
| **L/D ratio of charge** | Should typically be < **1:1** to avoid trapped air |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU PCPOT502 Module 3 expects students to *define*, *classify*, *draw the process schematic*, and *list the operating parameters and defects* of compression moulding. The KTU Examiner's Report (2022–2023 batch) explicitly notes that students lose marks when they fail to differentiate *flash-type*, *positive-type*, and *semi-positive-type* moulds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and the "How"

Compression moulding is governed by **two simultaneous physical–chemical phenomena**:

1. **Rheological flow** (the resin must flow to fill the cavity).
2. **Cure kinetics** (the resin must cross-link before flow ceases).

The success of the process depends on ensuring that the **flow time** is **longer** than the **mould-filling time**, but the **cure time** is **shorter** than the **time spent in the mould**. This is a critical competing-rate design constraint.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

**Step 1 — Material Preparation (Preforming / Preheating)**
- *Why:* Raw thermoset powder is bulky (BF ≈ 6) and contains trapped air. Preforming into a *tablet* or *preform* reduces BF toward 1.5–2.0 and preheating (RF or IR) drops viscosity.
- *How:* Powder is compacted in a small auxiliary press at **~ 5 MPa** to make a tablet, or heated by dielectric/IR to **80–110 °C** for 10–30 s.

**Step 2 — Charge Loading**
- *Why:* The exact *mass* of charge determines the *part thickness* and the *flash allowance*.
- *How:* Charge is placed manually or by a *chute/hopper* into the open, heated lower cavity. Charge mass is calculated as:

$$
m_{charge} \;=\; \rho_{moulded}} \,\times\, V_{cavity} \,\times\, (1 + f_{flash})
$$

where $\rho_{moulded}}$ is the cured density, $V_{cavity}}$ the cavity volume, and $f_{flash}$ the flash-fraction (typically **0.03–0.07** or 3–7 %).

**Step 3 — Mould Closing**
- *Why:* Air must be pushed out as the cavity closes; otherwise porosity forms.
- *How:* The upper force (male) descends at **fast approach → slow close → high-pressure hold**. The "breathing" technique momentarily opens the mould to vent residual air.

**Step 4 — Pressure Application & Cure**
- *Why:* Pressure forces material into ribs, bosses, and thin sections; heat drives the cross-linking.
- *How:* Hydraulic pressure ramps to **7–35 MPa** and is held for the entire *cure time* $t_c$. Curing is **exothermic**; peak exotherm temperature may reach **180–220 °C** even with platens at 150 °C.

**Step 5 — Mould Opening & Part Ejection**
- *Why:* The mould must be opened *before* the part fully cools to avoid thermal-stress cracking; ejector pins (typically **6–10 mm diameter**) push the part free.
- *How:* Compressed air or mechanical ejectors at **0.3–0.5 MPa** lift the part.

## 2.2 Classification of Compression Moulds

> [!NOTE]
> This classification is **highly tested** in KTU exams. Memorize the table.

| Mould Type | Flash? | Moulded Part Height Control | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash Mould** | Yes (intentional, 0.05–0.2 mm) | Determined by **cavity depth** (only) | Thick-walled, low-tolerance parts (e.g., electrical switch housings) |
| **Positive (Landless) Mould** | No | Determined by **charge mass only** | High-precision parts requiring tight thickness control |
| **Semi-Positive Mould** | Minimal (acts as safety vent) | Determined by **either** charge mass or land depth | Most industrial moulds (best compromise) |

**Why the difference matters:** In a *flash mould*, the part height equals the depth of the female cavity. In a *positive mould*, all the material is forced to fill the cavity; if you put in too much charge, the press stalls. In a *semi-positive mould*, a small clearance between the force and the cavity wall (the *land*) allows excess material to escape as a thin flash.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU exam staples**. Each must be derivable from first principles.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Moulding pressure | **MPa** |
| 2 | $F = P \cdot A_{proj}$ | Press tonnage (force) = pressure × projected area | **kN, tonnes-force** |
| 3 | $BF = \dfrac{\rho_{moulded}}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | Bulk factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_{charge} = BF \cdot V_{cavity}$ | Required charge volume | **cm³** |
| 5 | $m_{charge} = \rho_{moulded} \cdot V_{cavity} \cdot (1+f_{flash})$ | Mass of charge per shot | **g** |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{D_{mould} - D_{part}}{D_{mould}} \times 100$ | Linear mould-shrinkage | **%** |
| 7 | $C_{th} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat required to bring charge to moulding temperature | **J** |
| 8 | $t_c \approx C \cdot e^{E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius cure-time model | **s** |
| 9 | $\eta(T) = \eta_0 \cdot e^{E_\eta/RT}$ | Viscosity vs. temperature (Doolittle / Arrhenius) | **Pa·s** |
| 10 | $q_{gen} = \rho \cdot H_R \cdot \dfrac{d\alpha}{dt}$ | Volumetric heat generation from cure | **W/m³** |

Where $E_a$ is the activation energy (**kJ/mol**), $R = 8.314$ **J/mol·K**, $H_R$ is the total heat of reaction, and $\alpha$ is the degree of cure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electrical Industry:** Phenolic and melamine mouldings for switchgear, bakelite handles, distributor caps (high heat resistance, dielectric strength).
- **Automotive:** SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound) body panels — Corvette, truck fenders — using **> 2000-tonne presses**.
- **Cookware & Tableware:** Melamine dinnerware (heat-resistant, food-safe).
- **Aerospace Composites:** Compression moulding of prepregs (BMI, epoxy/carbon) for structural ribs.
- **Rubber Goods:** Tyre retreading, O-rings, gaskets, vibration mounts.

> [!NOTE]
> The same fundamental principle (heat + pressure + cure in a closed cavity) underlies **SMC compression moulding**, which dominates automotive Class-A body panels worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Press Tonnage Required

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem.** A phenolic moulded part has a projected area of $320 \,\text{cm}^2$. If the recommended moulding pressure is $15 \,\text{MPa}$, find the press tonnage required.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**

$$
P = \frac{F}{A_{proj}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad F = P \cdot A_{proj}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert area to SI units.**

$$
A_{proj} = 320 \;\text{cm}^2 \times \left(\frac{1\,\text{m}}{100\,\text{cm}}\right)^{2} = 320 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2 = 0.0320 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute force in newtons.**

$$
F = 15 \;\text{MPa} \times 0.0320 \;\text{m}^2 = 15 \times 10^{6} \;\text{Pa} \times 0.0320 \;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
F = 480\,000 \;\text{N} = 480 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to tonnes-force (1 tonne-f ≈ 9.81 kN).**

$$
F = \frac{480 \;\text{kN}}{9.81 \;\text{kN/tonne}} \approx 48.93 \;\text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor (industrial practice: ×1.25).**

$$
F_{design} = 48.93 \times 1.25 \approx 61.2 \;\text{tonnes} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{round up to a 75-tonne press.}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state the **safety factor** explicitly — most students lose 1–2 marks by quoting a single raw tonnage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Charge Mass & Bulk Factor

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem.** A urea-formaldehyde moulding powder has a bulk density of $0.40 \,\text{g/cm}^3$ and a moulded (cured) density of $1.50 \,\text{g/cm}^3$. The cavity volume is $85 \,\text{cm}^3$. Compute (a) the bulk factor, (b) the volume of powder required, and (c) the mass of powder per shot (assuming 5 % flash allowance).

**Step 1 — Bulk factor.**

$$
BF = \frac{\rho_{moulded}}{\rho_{bulk}} = \frac{1.50}{0.40} = 3.75
$$

**Step 2 — Bulk volume of powder required (ignoring flash).**

$$
V_{bulk} = BF \cdot V_{cavity} = 3.75 \times 85 = 318.75 \;\text{cm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Mass per shot with flash allowance $f_{flash} = 0.05$.**

$$
m_{charge} = \rho_{moulded} \cdot V_{cavity} \cdot (1 + f_{flash}) = 1.50 \times 85 \times 1.05
$$

$$
m_{charge} = 133.875 \;\text{g} \;\approx\; 134 \;\text{g per shot}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mould Shrinkage from Cavity & Part Dimensions

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem.** A steel mould cavity is $80.0 \,\text{mm}$ wide. After moulding and cooling, the part measures $79.6 \,\text{mm}$. Calculate the linear mould shrinkage and design the corrected cavity dimension for a target part size of $80.0 \,\text{mm}$.

**Step 1 — Compute measured shrinkage.**

$$
S = \frac{D_{mould} - D_{part}}{D_{mould}} \times 100 = \frac{80.0 - 79.6}{80.0} \times 100
$$

$$
S = \frac{0.4}{80.0} \times 100 = 0.50\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute required cavity dimension $D_{cav}^*}$ for an 80.0 mm target part.**

$$
D_{cav}^* = D_{target}} \cdot (1 + S/100) = 80.0 \times (1 + 0.005)
$$

$$
D_{cav}^* = 80.0 \times 1.005 = 80.40 \;\text{mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mould-makers over-size** the cavity by the shrinkage percentage. This is why the same part can be moulded in different resins with different cavity sizes.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cure Time from Arrhenius Equation

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem.** A phenolic resin cures in 90 seconds at 160 °C. The activation energy is $E_a = 80 \,\text{kJ/mol}$. Estimate the cure time if the mould temperature is raised to 180 °C.

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius model.**

$$
t_c = A \cdot e^{E_a / RT}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio of cure times at two temperatures.**

$$
\frac{t_1}{t_2} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$
T_1 = 160 + 273 = 433 \;\text{K}, \qquad T_2 = 180 + 273 = 453 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numerical values.**

$$
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{433} - \frac{1}{453} = \frac{453 - 433}{433 \times 453} = \frac{20}{196\,149}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \approx 1.0197 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the exponential argument.**

$$
\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{80\,000}{8.314} \times 1.0197 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
= 9622.6 \times 1.0197 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.9814
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\frac{t_1}{t_2} = e^{0.9814} \approx 2.668
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the new cure time.**

$$
t_2 = \frac{t_1}{2.668} = \frac{90}{2.668} \approx 33.7 \;\text{seconds}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Raising the mould temperature by **20 °C** reduces cure time by **~ 62 %** — this is the strong non-linear temperature sensitivity of thermoset cure.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Material Suitability for Compression Moulding

| Material Family | Resin | Cure T (°C) | Pressure (MPa) | Shrinkage (%) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phenolics (PF)** | Phenol-formaldehyde | 150–180 | 14–35 | 0.4–0.8 | Switchgear, handles |
| **Urea-Formaldehyde (UF)** | Urea-methanal | 130–160 | 14–28 | 0.5–1.0 | White appliance parts |
| **Melamine-Formaldehyde (MF)** | Melamine | 150–180 | 14–35 | 0.4–0.8 | Tableware, laminates |
| **Unsaturated Polyester (UP)** | Polyester + styrene | 130–170 | 3.5–14 | 0.2–0.6 | SMC, BMC composites |
| **Epoxy (EP)** | DGEBA + anhydride | 150–200 | 7–35 | 0.1–0.5 | Aerospace prepregs |
| **Bismaleimide (BMI)** | BMI resin | 180–250 | 7–14 | 0.1–0.3 | High-temp composites |
| **Silicone (SiR)** | PDMS | 160–200 | 5–14 | 0.3–0.6 | Gaskets, medical parts |
| **Natural / SBR Rubber** | Cross-linked rubber | 140–180 | 7–21 | 1.5–3.0 | Tyre treads, mounts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU exam frequently asks: *"Why is a thermoplastic *not* compression moulded in industry?"* Answer: thermoplastics melt and re-solidify without cure, so injection moulding is far more cycle-time-efficient; compression moulding is reserved for **thermosets** and **rubbers**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram maps the **six operational stages** of compression moulding together with the **ancillary subsystems** (preheater, preformer, press, controller, ejector). It is rendered using Mermaid syntax that is fully safe for KTU 2024 Scheme LMS platforms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Thermoset Powder or Prepreg] --> B[Preforming Station 5 MPa tablet press]
    B --> C[Preheater RF or IR 80-110 C]
    C --> D[Charge Loader manual or robotic chute]
    D --> E[Lower Heated Platen 120-200 C]
    E --> F[Upper Force Descends closing]
    F --> G{Has mould closed fully?}
    G -- No --> H[Breathing vent cycle]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[High Pressure Hold 7-35 MPa for cure time t_c]
    I --> J[Cure Complete exothermic crosslink]
    J --> K[Press Opens]
    K --> L[Ejector Pins 0.3-0.5 MPa]
    L --> M[Moulded Part to Trimming]
    M --> N[Flash Removal deflashing]
    N --> O[Final Inspection]
    O --> P[Packaging]
    I -.monitors.-> Q[PID Temperature Pressure Controller]
    Q -.feedback.-> E
    Q -.feedback.-> I
```

## 4.2 Mould-Type Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compression Moulds] --> B[Flash Mould]
    A --> C[Positive Mould]
    A --> D[Semi-Positive Mould]
    B --> B1[Intentional flash escapes at parting line]
    B --> B2[Part height = cavity depth only]
    C --> C1[No flash fully confined]
    C --> C2[Part height controlled by charge mass]
    D --> D1[Small clearance acts as vent]
    D --> D2[Height controlled by either mass or land depth]
```

## 4.3 Mould Anatomy (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Upper_Half [Upper Force Assembly]
        U1[Upper Platen]
        U2[Force Plug Punch]
        U3[Guide Pins]
        U4[Heating Channels]
    end
    subgraph Lower_Half [Lower Cavity Assembly]
        L1[Lower Platen]
        L2[Cavity Block]
        L3[Heating Channels]
        L4[Ejector Pins]
        L5[Support Plate]
    end
    subgraph Periphery [Peripheral Systems]
        P1[Hydraulic Ram]
        P2[Steam or Oil Heater]
        P3[Controller Cabinet]
        P4[Chute or Robotic Loader]
    end
    P1 --> U1
    P2 --> U4
    P2 --> L3
    P3 --> P1
    P4 --> L2
    U1 --> L1
```

> [!TIP]
