P = \dfrac{600{,}000 \text{ N}}{0.01 \text{ m}^2}
$$

$$
P = 60{,}000{,}000 \text{ N/m}^2 = 60 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Conversion logic:** 1 Pa = 1 N/m², and 1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** 60 MPa is on the higher end of the typical 7–35 MPa range. This indicates either an over-powered press, or that the cavity depth is small forcing a high contact pressure for flow. For phenolic moulding, **15–25 MPa** is the optimum range.

**Step 3 — Cavity Volume**

$$
V_c = A \times h
$$

$$
V_c = 0.01 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.012 \text{ m} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Conversion logic:** mm to m conversion → 12 mm = 0.012 m.

In cm³ (more convenient for grams):

$$
V_c = 10{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 \times 12 \text{ mm} = 120{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Since 1 cm³ = 1000 mm³:

$$
V_c = 120 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Required Charge Mass**

Use the bulk density of the preform to determine how much raw material must be loaded:

$$
m = \rho_{\text{bulk}} \times V_c
$$

$$
m = 0.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 120 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
m = 102 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification via Final Part Mass**

The cured part mass can be cross-checked:

$$
m_{\text{part}} = \rho_{\text{cured}} \times V_c
$$

$$
m_{\text{part}} = 1.30 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 120 \text{ cm}^3 = 156 \text{ g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $m_{\text{part}} \neq m_{\text{charge}}$?** Because the charge contains fillers, resin, and water/condensation byproducts that evolve during cure. The preform loses volatiles, so you must load by the *preform bulk density* (lower), not the cured density.

### 3.2 Derivation: Semi-Positive Mould Land Width Calculation

**Problem Context:** A semi-positive mould is to be designed for a 200 mm diameter disc-shaped BMC part. The moulding pressure is 20 MPa, and the maximum tolerable flash thickness is 0.1 mm. Calculate the **horizontal land width** required.

**Step 1 — Clamping Force on the Land**

The land must resist the polymer pressure from below over the projected annular land area:

$$
F_{\text{land}} = P \cdot A_{\text{land}} = P \cdot \pi \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) / 4
$$

where $D_i$ = part diameter (200 mm), and $D_o = D_i + 2 W_L$, with $W_L$ = land width.

**Step 2 — Equating to Vertical Force on Plunger**

For static equilibrium, the upward polymer thrust on the plunger must equal the downward force on the land:

$$
P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_i^2}{4} = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi (D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for Land Width**

$$
D_i^2 = D_o^2 - D_i^2
$$

$$
D_o^2 = 2 D_i^2
$$

$$
D_o = D_i \sqrt{2}
$$

$$
D_o = 200 \times 1.4142 = 282.84 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
W_L = \dfrac{D_o - D_i}{2} = \dfrac{282.84 - 200}{2} = 41.42 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that $W_L$ must satisfy *both* the force-balance and the practical machining limit. A typical engineering compromise is **$W_L \approx D_i / 8$ to $D_i / 6$**, which here gives 25–33 mm. The 41 mm result is the *theoretical minimum*; in practice, the land is wider for machinability and to accommodate trimming blades.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Positive Mould | Semi-Positive Mould | Flash Mould |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|-------------|
| Cavity depth control | **Fixed (positive stop)** | **Positive + flash relief** | Variable |
| Flash generation | **None** | Slight (only if overcharged) | Continuous flash ring |
| Charge weight tolerance | ±1% (very strict) | ±3% (moderate) | ±10% (loose) |
| Dimensional accuracy | Highest | High | Moderate |
| Preform preparation | Mandatory (weighed/preformed) | Standard | Loose bulk allowed |
| Press tonnage requirement | High (full force on part) | Moderate | Low |
| Cycle time | Longest | Medium | Shortest |
| Mould cost | Highest | High | Lowest |
| Typical applications | Electrical, aerospace | Automotive BMC, cookware | Caps, buttons |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Positive Mould — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Preweighed Charge"] --> B["Load into Female Cavity"]
    B --> C["Male Platen Descends"]
    C --> D["Positive Stop Engagement"]
    D --> E["Cavity Sealed at Fixed Depth h"]
    E --> F["Pressure Generation 7 to 35 MPa"]
    F --> G["Cure and Polymerization"]
    G --> H["Demould Flashless Part"]
    H --> I["Inspection No Trimming Required"]
```

### 4.2 Semi-Positive Mould — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Charge Loaded"] --> B{"Charge Mass Exact"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Plunger Bottoms on Part"]
    C --> D["Zero Flash Quality Path"]
    B -- No Slight Excess --> E["Plunger Rides Up on Land"]
    E --> F["Flash Escapes Through Land Clearance"]
    F --> G["Plunger Bottoms on Land Stop"]
    G --> H["Trimmable Flash on Land Edge"]
    D --> I["Demould Cured Part"]
    H --> I
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Cross-Section Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Upper_Platen
        U1["Male Die Block"]
        U2["Heating Channel"]
    end
    subgraph Cavity_Region
        C1["Cavity Volume Vc"]
        C2["Positive Stop Flange"]
        C3["Plunger Piston"]
        C4["Hardened Land Stop Ring"]
    end
    subgraph Lower_Platen
        L1["Female Die Block"]
        L2["Ejector Pins"]
        L3["Heating Channel"]
    end
    U1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C3 --> C4
    C1 --> L1
    L2 --> L1
    U2 -.-> U1
    L3 -.-> L1
```

### 4.4 Pressure–Cavity Closure Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["t1 Platen Contact"] --> S2["t2 Cavity Sealing"]
    S2 --> S3["t3 Full Pressure Ramp"]
    S3 --> S4["t4 Cure Hold"]
    S4 --> S5["t5 Decompression Demould"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Reading:** The four-stage profile above represents the **platen travel vs. time** during a compression cycle. The ramp from t1 to t3 is critical: a too-fast closure traps air (causing voids); a too-slow closure pre-cures the resin (causing under-fill). Typical closure speed = **5 to 25 mm/s** in the *breathing phase* and **1 to 5 mm/s** in the *final closure phase*.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define a positive mould. Why is it also called a flashless mould?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A positive mould is a compression mould in which the cavity depth is positively defined by a mechanical stop between the male and female die halves. The mould cannot be closed fully unless the exact charge is loaded, hence no material escapes as flash. Therefore, it is called a **flashless mould**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for stop mechanism, 1 for the no-flash reason.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam]**
*Distinguish between a positive mould and a semi-positive mould in two points.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Positive | Semi-Positive |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| Flash | No flash produced | Slight flash on land only if overcharged |
| Charge tolerance | Very strict ±1% | Moderate ±3% |
| Plunger design | Direct positive stop | Plunger + land stop ring |

*(3 marks: 1 per row or 1.5+1.5 split for two clear points.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a **positive mould** used in compression moulding of thermosets. List **two advantages** and **two limitations**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A phenolic resin part of dimensions 150 mm × 100 mm × 8 mm is to be moulded in a positive mould. The press tonnage is 350 kN. The bulk density of the preform is 0.9 g/cm³. Calculate:
   (i) The moulding pressure. *(2 marks)*
   (ii) The required charge mass. *(2 marks)*
   (iii) Comment on whether the part will have flash. *(1 mark)*
   (iv) If the part shrinkage is 1.2%, calculate the mould cavity dimensions. *(2 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Upper platen carries the **male die (punch)** with heating channels.
- Lower platen carries the **female die (cavity block)** with hardened guide pins.
- A **positive stop shoulder** machined on the mating flange defines the closed depth.
- **Ejector pins** are housed in the lower platen.
- Guide pillars ensure alignment.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. A pre-weighed charge is loaded into the female cavity.
2. The press closes; the male punch descends.
3. On contacting the cavity, the positive stop is engaged; the cavity is sealed at a fixed depth *h*.
4. Hydraulic pressure consolidates and cures the part.
5. The mould opens; ejector pins push the part out. No trimming needed.

**Advantages (0.5 each, total 1 mark):**
- Flashless, hence no secondary trimming operation.
- Tight dimensional tolerance.

**Limitations (0.5 each, total 1 mark):**
- Strict charge-weight control required.
- High press tonnage needed; high mould cost.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Moulding Pressure (2 marks):**

$$
A = 150 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm} = 15{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.015 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{350{,}000 \text{ N}}{0.015 \text{ m}^2} = 23.33 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Charge Mass (2 marks):**

$$
V_c = 0.015 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.008 \text{ m} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 120 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
m = \rho_{\text{bulk}} \cdot V_c = 0.9 \times 120 = 108 \text{ g}
$$

**(iii) Flash Comment (1 mark):**
Since this is a positive mould with a fixed positive stop, the part will have **no flash** provided the charge is exactly 108 g.

**(iv) Mould Cavity Dimensions with Shrinkage Allowance (2 marks):**

$$
D_m = D_p (1 + S)
$$

Length:
$$
L_m = 150 \times (1 + 0.012) = 151.8 \text{ mm}
$$

Width:
$$
W_m = 100 \times (1 + 0.012) = 101.2 \text{ mm}
$$

Thickness:
$$
h_m = 8 \times (1 + 0.012) = 8.096 \text{ mm}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **semi-positive mould** with a clear diagram. State **three situations** where you would prefer a semi-positive mould over a positive mould. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A BMC disc of diameter 200 mm and thickness 6 mm is moulded in a semi-positive mould. The land width is 30 mm. The press tonnage is 800 kN. Take preform density = 1.6 g/cm³.
   (i) Calculate the projected area of the land. *(2 marks)*
   (ii) Calculate the moulding pressure on the part. *(2 marks)*
   (iii) Calculate the force exerted on the land and comment on its adequacy. *(2 marks)*
   (iv) Calculate the required charge mass. *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A **plunger (inner positive stop)** is fitted inside the main cavity.
- The plunger rides against a **hardened land (outer stop ring)** machined into the upper face of the female cavity.
- The land provides a horizontal sealing surface of width $W_L$.
- Guide pins, ejector pins, and heating channels are as in standard compression moulds.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Charge is loaded into the cavity.
2. The press closes; the plunger descends.
3. **Case (i)** — If the charge is exact, the plunger bottoms on the part surface → no flash.
4. **Case (ii)** — If the charge is in slight excess, polymer escapes up the gap between plunger and land, forming a thin flash ring. The plunger now bottoms on the land (not the part), maintaining part thickness.
5. After cure, mould opens; flash is trimmed along the land edge.

**Three situations where semi-positive is preferred (1 mark: 0.33 each, or 0.5 + 0.5):**
- When charge-weight control is imprecise (e.g., BMC with fibre variation).
- When the part has a relatively large projected area where flash would be unsightly.
- When a compromise between dimensional accuracy and process tolerance is needed.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Projected Area of Land (2 marks):**

Outer diameter: $D_o = D_i + 2 W_L = 200 + 2(30) = 260$ mm.

$$
A_{\text{land}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)
$$

$$
A_{\text{land}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(260^2 - 200^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(67{,}600 - 40{,}000) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(27{,}600)
$$

$$
A_{\text{land}} = 21{,}677 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 0.02168 \text{ m}^2
$$

**(ii) Moulding Pressure on the Part (2 marks):**

Part projected area:
$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_i^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 200^2 = 31{,}416 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{F}{A_p} = \dfrac{800{,}000 \text{ N}}{0.03142 \text{ m}^2} = 25.46 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Force on the Land and Adequacy (2 marks):**

$$
F_{\text{land}} = P \cdot A_{\text{land}} = 25.46 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \times 0.02168 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F_{\text{land}} = 552{,}000 \text{ N} = 552 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Adequacy comment:** The land is being loaded with 552 kN against a total press tonnage of 800 kN. This is **69% of the press capacity** — high but acceptable, as the remaining 31% is balanced by the male-die reaction force. A land width slightly larger (35–40 mm) would be advisable for safety.

**(iv) Required Charge Mass (1 mark):**

$$
V_c = A_p \cdot h = 31{,}416 \text{ mm}^2 \times 6 \text{ mm} = 188{,}496 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 188.5 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
m = \rho \cdot V_c = 1.6 \times 188.5 = 301.6 \text{ g}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–1 mark):** Confusing **positive mould** with **flash mould**. Many students write "positive mould produces flash" — this is the *flash mould's* job, not the positive mould's. Positive = flashless.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting unit conversion in pressure problems. Mixing mm² and m² gives pressures off by a factor of 10⁶. Always check: $F$ in N, $A$ in m², gives $P$ in Pa; divide by 10⁶ to get MPa.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Using the **cured** density instead of the **preform bulk density** when computing the charge mass. The preform is *less dense* than the cured part; you must use the bulk (loose) density.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–2 marks):** Drawing a positive mould diagram without clearly showing the **positive stop** (mechanical shoulder). The examiner awards 1–2 marks for the stop mechanism being clearly visible.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to apply the **shrinkage allowance** when computing mould dimensions. The cavity is *always* oversized relative to the part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
> - ✅ **Positive Mould:** Fixed-depth cavity, **NO flash**, requires **exact pre-weighed charge**, highest dimensional accuracy, highest mould cost, highest press tonnage.
> - ✅ **Semi-Positive Mould:** Plunger + land (stop ring), **slight flash only when overcharged**, tolerates ±3% charge variation, high (but not flashless) accuracy.
> - ✅ **Flash Mould:** No positive stop, free escape of excess, variable thickness, lowest cost, lowest accuracy.
> - ✅ **Master Equation 1:** $P = F/A$ (Force in N, Area in m², Pressure in Pa → /10⁶ for MPa).
> - ✅ **Master Equation 2:** $m = \rho_{\text{bulk}} \cdot V_c$ (use preform density, NOT cured density).
> - ✅ **Shrinkage Formula:** $D_{\text{mould}} = D_{\text{part}} (1 + S)$ where $S$ = fractional shrinkage.
> - ✅ **Land Width Range:** $W_L = 3$ to $6$ mm typical; theoretical minimum from force balance: $D_o = D_i \sqrt{2}$.
> - ✅ **Land Clearance for Air Escape:** $0.025$ to $0.05$ mm.
> - ✅ **Typical Moulding Pressure for Phenolic/BMC:** $7$ to $35$ MPa; phenolic optimum $\approx 15$–$25$ MPa.
> - ✅ **Platen Travel Profile:** Breathing phase (5–25 mm/s) → Final closure (1–5 mm/s) → Cure hold → Decompress → Demould.
> - ✅ **Examiner Hot-Buttons:** "Why no flash?" "Why pre-weigh?" "Why land?" — these three 'why' questions are asked every KTU cycle in some form.
> - ✅ **Always quote units** in the final answer; this is a 0.5-mark "soft mark" that is routinely lost by inattentive students.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression Moulding Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Compression Moulding Cycle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Compression moulding** is a closed-mould, pressure-intensive thermoset processing technique in which a pre-measured charge of polymer (powder, preform, sheet moulding compound, or bulk moulding compound) is placed directly into the open cavity of a heated mould, the mould is closed, and pressure combined with heat forces the material to flow, consolidate, and crosslink (cure) into a net-shape or near-net-shape rigid part. The **Compression Moulding Cycle** refers to the chronologically ordered sequence of operational events — from the instant the mould begins to close on the charge to the instant the cured part is ejected — encompassing *mould closing, breathing/degassing, full pressurisation, cure, breathing release, mould opening, and ejection*. The cycle defines the productivity (parts/hour), energy consumption, and dimensional consistency of the process. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic belongs to **Module 3 — Moulding of Thermosets** and forms the basis of 14-mark analytical problems involving cycle time estimation, mould pressure calculations, and cure kinetics for phenolic / epoxy / polyester / SMC / BMC systems. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Idli Maker Think of compression moulding exactly like making an **idli on a traditional two-plate cast-iron griddle**: | Stage in Compression Moulding | Analogy in Idli Making | |-------------------------------|------------------------| | Charge preparation (preform/SMC) | Measured batter poured into the greased cavity of the lower plate | | Mould closing | Placing the upper hot plate on top with weight | | Heat + pressure transfer | Steam inside the batter cooks it while the top weight squeezes it flat | | Breathing (degassing) | Tiny vents letting steam out so the idli is not porous | | Cure cycle | 6–8 minutes of consistent heat until the batter solidifies | | Mould opening | Lifting the upper plate after the timer beeps | | Ejection | Scraping the perfectly shaped, hard idli off the plate | The batter is liquid-plastic at the start (like a thermoset resin in its B-stage); heat and pressure turn it irreversibly rigid — exactly like the chemistry of phenol-formaldehyde, melamine, or unsaturated polyester resins. You cannot "un-cook" the idli, just as you cannot remelt a thermoset. ### 1.3 Standard Process Metrics (Engineering Constants) - **Specific Moulding Pressure:** **0.7 – 70 MPa** (typical **7 – 35 MPa** for SMC/BMC; **15 – 70 MPa** for phenolic powders) - **Mould Temperature:** **130 °C – 200 °C** for phenolics; **140 °C – 180 °C** for unsaturated polyester - **Cure Time:** **30 s – 300 s** depending on part thickness and resin reactivity - **Breathing Pressure Release:** **0.2 – 0.5 MPa** momentary vent - **Platen Closing Velocity:** **10 – 50 mm/s** (fast close → 1–5 mm/s (slow squeeze)) - **Clamp Tonnage Rule:** **2.5 – 4 tonnes per square inch** of projected area for thermosets > [!NOTE] > **Why thermosets require compression moulding:** Thermosetting resins crosslink via irreversible covalent bonding upon heating. Unlike thermoplastics, they cannot be remelted and re-injected, so the charge must be *shaped and cured in a single stroke* — exactly what compression moulding delivers. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Eight Logical Stages of a Compression Moulding Cycle The cycle is not merely "close-press-open." It is a precisely orchestrated sequence whose individual stages dictate the ultimate part quality. Each stage is an engineering decision variable. 1. **Charge Preparation (Pre-stage, Outside Cycle Clock)** - Bulk Moulding Compound (BMC), Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC), phenolic preform, or granular powder is pre-weighed to **50–80 % of cavity volume** (the "fill factor" or "load factor"). - **Fill factor (f)** = $\dfrac{V_{charge}}{V_{cavity}}$ - Typical $f = 0.5$ to $0.8$ — leaves headspace for breathing and gas escape. 2. **Mould Charging (Loading)** - Charge placed manually or robotically into the lower half (cavity). - For SMC, multiple plies (charge pattern follows a **charge pattern diagram** or "ply layout") are stacked to minimise knit-lines. 3. **Mould Closing — Two-Velocity Stroke** - **Fast close** (10–50 mm/s): positions the mould near the charge. - **Slow close** (1–5 mm/s): allows the polymer to **plasticise** and begin flowing without entrapping air. 4. **Breathing (Degassing Stroke)** - At ~0.2–0.5 MPa, mould is held briefly (~1–3 s) with vents open. - Allows **trapped air, moisture, and reaction volatiles** to escape — prevents blisters and porosity. 5. **Full Pressurisation and Cure** - Final closing pressure applied. Material flows to fill cavity, then **crosslinking begins**. - Exothermic cure raises internal temperature beyond mould surface temperature ("exotherm peak"). 6. **Pressure Release / Mould Breathing on Cure Completion** - Vents any residual gas from final cure stage. 7. **Mould Opening** - After cure, mould halves separate. Part remains on the half containing the ejector pins. 8. **Ejection** - Part is lifted out by ejector pins / stripper plate / air blast. ### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Stage - **Why preheat the charge?** To reduce viscosity, shorten flow time, reduce mould wear, and lower required cure time. - **Why two closing speeds?** Fast stroke saves cycle time; slow stroke prevents air entrapment and charge displacement. - **Why breathe?** Trapped gas becomes porosity → weak, blister-prone parts. - **Why hold under pressure after cure?** Compensates for cure shrinkage (typically **1 – 4 %** for thermosets) and thermal contraction. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Engineering Use | Units | |---|--------------------|---------------------------|-------| | 1 | $f = \dfrac{V_{charge}}{V_{cavity}}$ | Fill factor (load factor) — governs preform mass | dimensionless | | 2 | $m = \rho_{compound} \cdot V_{cavity} \cdot f$ | Mass of charge required | kg | | 3 | $F_{clamp} = P_{mould} \cdot A_{proj}$ | Clamp tonnage selection | N | | 4 | $\dfrac{Q}{t} = \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{x}$ | Fourier heat conduction through part | W | | 5 | $t_{cure} = \dfrac{x^{2} \rho c_{p}}{k \pi^{2}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_{0} - T_{w}}{T_{c} - T_{w}}\right)$ | Cure time for thick thermoset part | s | | 6 | $t_{cycle} = t_{close} + t_{breathe} + t_{cure} + t_{open} + t_{eject}$ | Total cycle time — productivity metric | s | | 7 | $\dot{Q}_{exo} = H_{rxn} \cdot \dfrac{d\alpha}{dt}$ | Heat generated by cure reaction | W | | 8 | $T_{peak} = T_{w} + \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{exo}\, x^{2}}{2k}$ | Exotherm peak temperature (1-D slab) | K or °C | | 9 | $P_{specific} = \dfrac{F}{A_{cavity}}$ | Specific moulding pressure | Pa | | 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{t_{cure}}{t_{cycle}}$ | Cure efficiency ratio (higher = more productive) | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Units discipline:** Always keep $k$ in $\mathrm{W/(m\cdot K)}$, $\rho$ in $\mathrm{kg/m^{3}}$, $c_{p}$ in $\mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}$, $x$ in metres. Mismatch in units is the **#1 valuation trap** for KTU 14-mark numericals. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Phenolic/BMI composites for interior panels, ablatives (rocket nozzle liners) - **Automotive:** SMC body panels (BMW, Tata), Class-A surface components - **Electrical:** Bakelite switchgear, MCB housings, busbar supports - **Cookware:** Melamine-formaldehyde tableware - **Industrial:** Rubber vulcanisation (compression moulding of tyre retreads, gaskets) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cure Time in a Thick Thermoset Slab (1-D Transient Heat Conduction) For a thick thermoset charge of half-thickness $L$, initially at $T_{0}$, sandwiched between two mould walls at $T_{w}$, the time required for the centreline to reach the cure-onset temperature $T_{c}$ is obtained from the transient conduction solution. **Step 1:** Start with Fourier's law in 1-D slab geometry, with constant surface temperature $T_{w}$: $$ \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} $$ where $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_{p}}$ is the **thermal diffusivity** of the thermoset compound. **Step 2:** Apply the boundary conditions: - At $x = L$: $T(L, t) = T_{w}$ - Initial condition: $T(x, 0) = T_{0}$ - Symmetry at $x = 0$: $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$ **Step 3:** Use separation of variables. The solution for the centreline ($x = 0$) temperature is: $$ \dfrac{T_{0} - T_{w}}{T(0,t) - T_{w}} = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{n}}{2n+1}\exp\!\left[-\alpha(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}t/(4L^{2})\right] $$ **Step 4:** For long cure times ($t \to \infty$), only the first term ($n = 0$) dominates: $$ \dfrac{T(0,t) - T_{w}}{T_{0} - T_{w}} \approx \dfrac{4}{\pi}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{\alpha \pi^{2} t}{4L^{2}}\right] $$ **Step 5:** Solve for $t$ when centreline temperature reaches the cure temperature $T_{c}$: $$ \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{\alpha \pi^{2} t_{cure}}{4L^{2}}\right] = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{T_{c} - T_{w}}{T_{0} - T_{w}} $$ **Step 6:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides and rearrange: $$ -\dfrac{\alpha \pi^{2} t_{cure}}{4L^{2}} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_{c} - T_{w}}{T_{0} - T_{w}}\right) $$ **Step 7:** Recognise that $\ln(\pi/4) \approx -0.242$ and combine: $$ t_{cure} = \dfrac{4 L^{2}}{\alpha \pi^{2}} \left[\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4}{\pi}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_{0} - T_{w}}{T_{c} - T_{w}}\right)\right] $$ **Step 8:** Combine logarithms: $$ t_{cure} = \dfrac{4 L^{2}}{\alpha \pi^{2}} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{4(T_{0}-T_{w})}{\pi(T_{c}-T_{w})}\right] $$ Substituting back $\alpha = k/(\rho c_{p})$ and noting $L = x/2$ (full thickness $x$): $$ t_{cure} = \dfrac{x^{2}\rho c_{p}}{k\pi^{2}} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{4(T_{0}-T_{w})}{\pi(T_{c}-T_{w})}\right] $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering insight:** Cure time scales with **thickness squared** ($x^{2}$). Doubling the wall thickness of a phenolic component *quadruples* the cure time. This is why KTU problems frequently ask you to compare cycle times of two thicknesses. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Clamp Tonnage Requirement Given a part with projected area $A_{proj}$ on the parting line and a required specific moulding pressure $P_{spec}$: **Step 1:** The total compressive force the press must deliver is: $$ F_{clamp} = P_{spec} \cdot A_{proj} $$ **Step 2:** Convert to tonnage (1 tonne-force $\approx 9.81 \times 10^{3}$ N): $$ T_{press} = \dfrac{P_{spec} \cdot A_{proj}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} $$ **Step 3:** Apply a safety factor of 1.25 for press selection: $$ T_{design} = 1.25 \, T_{press} $$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Problem) **Problem:** A phenolic powder part has cavity thickness $x = 12$ mm, mould wall temperature $T_{w} = 170$ °C, charge temperature $T_{0} = 30$ °C, cure-onset temperature $T_{c} = 140$ °C. The compound has $\rho = 1450$ kg/m³, $c_{p} = 1670$ J/(kg·K), $k = 0.28$ W/(m·K). Estimate the cure time. **Step 1:** Convert thickness to metres: $x = 0.012$ m. **Step 2:** Compute thermal diffusivity: $$ \alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_{p}} = \dfrac{0.28}{1450 \times 1670} = \dfrac{0.28}{2{,}421{,}500} = 1.156 \times 10^{-7} \ \mathrm{m^{2}/s} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the cure-time equation: $$ t_{cure} = \dfrac{(0.012)^{2}(1450)(1670)}{(0.28)(\pi^{2})} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{4(30 - 170)}{\pi(140 - 170)}\right] $$ **Step 4:** Compute the prefactor: $$ \dfrac{(1.44 \times 10^{-4})(1450)(1670)}{0.28 \cdot 9.8696} = \dfrac{348.84}{2.7635} = 126.23 \ \mathrm{s} $$ **Step 5:** Compute the logarithmic term: $$ \ln\!\left[\dfrac{4(-140)}{\pi(-30)}\right] = \ln\!\left[\dfrac{-560}{-94.248}\right] = \ln(5.943) = 1.782 $$ **Step 6:** Multiply: $$ t_{cure} = 126.23 \times 1.782 \approx 224.9 \ \mathrm{s} \approx 3.75 \ \mathrm{minutes} $$ This is consistent with industrial SMC/phenolic cure times for a 12 mm thick section. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Process Variables | Parameter | Symbol | Increase Effect on Cycle | Increase Effect on Quality | |-----------|--------|--------------------------|----------------------------| | Mould temperature | $T_{w} \uparrow$ | Cycle time $\downarrow$ | Risk of pre-cure, scorch | | Specific pressure | $P_{spec} \uparrow$ | Slight cycle $\downarrow$ | Lower porosity, better surface | | Charge preheat | $T_{0} \uparrow$ | Cycle time $\downarrow$ | Lower viscosity, better flow | | Part thickness | $x \uparrow$ | Cycle time $\uparrow$ ($x^{2}$) | Greater exotherm risk | | Fill factor | $f \uparrow$ | Slight cycle $\downarrow$ | Risk of flash, mould damage | | Resin reactivity | $\uparrow$ | Cycle time $\downarrow$ | Risk of pre-cure in transfer | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Flow of a Compression Moulding Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Charge Preparation
Preweigh BMC or SMC] B[Mould Charging
Load into cavity] C[Fast Mould Close
10 to 50 mm per s] D[Slow Mould Close
1 to 5 mm per s] E[Breathing Stroke
Vent air and volatiles] F[Full Pressurisation
Apply specific pressure] G[Cure Hold
Crosslink under heat] H[Pressure Release
Final vent] I[Mould Opening
Separate halves] J[Part Ejection
Ejector pins or stripper] K[Mould Cleaning
Remove flash and residue] L[Cycle Reset
Reload charge] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J --> K K --> L L --> A ``` ### 4.2 Pressure-Temperature-Time Profile (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PT[Pressure Temperature Time Profile] direction LR P1[Pressure Ramps Up
0 to 7 to 35 MPa] P2[Pressure Hold During Cure
Constant at Pmould] P3[Pressure Release
Before Opening] T1[Mould Wall Tw Rises
to 130 to 200 C] T2[Cure Exotherm Peak
Centreline Hotter] T3[Cooling and Ejection
at Tw Open] TIM[Cycle Clock t1 plus t2 plus t3] end P1 --> P2 --> P3 T1 --> T2 --> T3 P1 -.coincides.-> T1 P2 -.coincides.-> T2 P3 -.coincides.-> T3 P2 -.gated by.-> TIM ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Mould Half Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph UP[Upper Mould Half Force Platen] UP1[Heating Channels Tw Top] UP2[Upper Cavity Register] UP3[Guide Pins] end subgraph CHG[Charge Zone] CH1[Preform or SMC Charge] CH2[Fill Factor 0.5 to 0.8] CH3[Breathing Vents] end subgraph LO[Lower Mould Half Cavity Platen] LO1[Heating Channels Tw Bottom] LO2[Cavity Geometry] LO3[Ejector Pins] LO4[Stripper Plate] end subgraph CTL[Press Control] CTL1[Clamp Force F] CTL2[Platen Velocity V] CTL3[Timer and Cure Logic] end UP -.closes on.-> CHG CHG -.flows into.-> LO CTL --> UP CTL --> LO ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define the term "breathing" in a compression moulding cycle. Why is it essential for SMC moulding?* **Model Answer:** Breathing is the momentary release of mould closing pressure (typically at 0.2–0.5 MPa) during the early phase of mould closure, during which trapped air, moisture, and reaction volatiles are allowed to escape through vent grooves in the parting line. For SMC moulding, breathing is essential because: - SMC charges contain up to **20 % glass fibre and 15 % filler**, leaving substantial interstitial air. - Without breathing, trapped air forms **porosity, blisters, and knit lines**, drastically reducing mechanical strength. - Breath time is typically **1–3 s**, allowing flow stabilisation before full pressure application. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *What is the "fill factor" in compression moulding? Why is it always kept below 1.0?* **Model Answer:** The fill factor (or load factor) $f$ is the ratio of charge volume to cavity volume: $$ f = \dfrac{V_{charge}}{V_{cavity}} $$ It is kept between **0.5 and 0.8** (always below 1.0) because: - It provides **headspace for material flow** during pressurisation. - It allows **gas escape** through vents during the breathing stroke. - It prevents **excessive flash** that would damage mould edges. - It compensates for **bulk factor** of the loose charge (powders and fibres have low bulk density). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > - Always show **unit conversions** explicitly (mm → m, °C → K only if Kelvin equation demands it). > - Cure-time numericals must include the **logarithmic term** with $4/\pi$ — forgetting the constant inside $\ln$ costs 2–3 marks. > - In cycle-time questions, examiners often split marks between *cure time calculation* and *productivity rate*. Failing to compute parts/hour loses easy 4 marks. > - Always state the **assumptions** (1-D conduction, constant $k$, negligible exotherm at first-pass estimate) — examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for valid assumptions. --- **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, modified per 2024 scheme]** (a) **Explain with a neat block-flow diagram the complete compression moulding cycle for an SMC component. Discuss the role of each stage in determining the final part quality.** **(7 marks)** (b) **A phenolic moulding powder charge of thickness 15 mm is placed between mould walls at 175 °C. The charge is initially at 25 °C. The cure-onset temperature is 145 °C. Properties: $\rho = 1400$ kg/m³, $c_{p} = 1700$ J/(kg·K), $k = 0.30$ W/(m·K). Estimate the cure time and the total cycle time if auxiliary operations take 60 s.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** Refer to the functional flow diagram in Section 4.1. Each stage contributes as follows: | Stage | Quality Contribution | |-------|---------------------| | Charge preparation | Ensures correct mass, fibre orientation | | Mould charging | Avoids knit-lines; balanced charge pattern | | Fast close | Productivity gain | | Slow close | Prevents charge displacement, air entrapment | | Breathing | Removes porosity-causing gases | | Pressurisation | Drives flow and consolidation | | Cure hold | Ensures complete crosslinking (residual cure < 1 %) | | Pressure release | Prevents part cracking on opening | | Mould opening | Determines part sticking side | | Ejection | Avoids surface damage | **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1:** Convert units: $x = 0.015$ m. **Step 2:** Thermal diffusivity: $$ \alpha = \dfrac{0.30}{1400 \times 1700} = \dfrac{0.30}{2{,}380{,}000} = 1.2605 \times 10^{-7} \ \mathrm{m^{2}/s} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute into cure-time equation: $$ t_{cure} = \dfrac{(0.015)^{2}(1400)(1700)}{(0.30)(\pi^{2})} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{4(25-175)}{\pi(145-175)}\right] $$ **Step 4:** Prefactor: $$ \dfrac{(2.25 \times 10^{-4})(1400)(1700)}{0.30 \cdot 9.8696} = \dfrac{535.5}{2.9609} = 180.85 \ \mathrm{s} $$ **Step 5:** Logarithmic term: $$ \ln\!\left[\dfrac{4(-150)}{\pi(-30)}\right] = \ln\!\left[\dfrac{-600}{-94.248}\right] = \ln(6.366) = 1.851 $$ **Step 6:** Multiply: $$ t_{cure} = 180.85 \times 1.851 \approx 334.75 \ \mathrm{s} $$ **Step 7:** Total cycle time: $$ t_{cycle} = t_{cure} + t_{aux} = 334.75 + 60 = 394.75 \ \mathrm{s} \approx 6.58 \ \mathrm{minutes} $$ **Step 8:** Productivity: $$ \dot{N} = \dfrac{3600}{394.75} \approx 9.12 \ \mathrm{parts/hour} $$ --- **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, re-aligned to 2024 scheme]** (a) **Discuss the influence of (i) mould temperature, (ii) specific pressure, and (iii) fill factor on the cycle time and part quality of a compression-moulded thermoset component.** **(7 marks)** (b) **Design the clamp tonnage for an SMC moulding press to produce a car bumper fascia of projected area 0.85 m². Required specific pressure is 12 MPa. Apply a safety factor of 1.25.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** Three influences analysed: **(i) Mould temperature $T_{w}$:** Higher $T_{w}$ reduces $t_{cure}$ exponentially (since $\Delta T$ in the $\ln$ term increases). However, excessive $T_{w}$ causes **pre-cure** (resin crosslinks before full cavity fill) and **thermal degradation**. Optimum is just below resin decomposition onset. **(ii) Specific pressure $P_{spec}$:** Adequate pressure ensures cavity fill, fibre wetting, and density. Excessive pressure causes **flash**, mould damage, fibre fracture, and higher tool wear. Typical optimum for SMC: 7–15 MPa. **(iii) Fill factor $f$:** Lower $f$ (e.g., 0.5) → more flow required → potential knit-lines and fibre orientation variation. Higher $f$ (e.g., 0.85) → less flow → uniform properties but higher flash risk. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1:** Convert projected area: $A = 0.85$ m². **Step 2:** Required clamp force: $$ F_{clamp} = P_{spec} \cdot A = 12 \times 10^{6} \times 0.85 = 10.2 \times 10^{6} \ \mathrm{N} $$ **Step 3:** Convert to tonnes (1 tonne ≈ $9.81 \times 10^{3}$ N): $$ T_{press} = \dfrac{10.2 \times 10^{6}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} = 1039.76 \ \mathrm{tonnes} $$ **Step 4:** Apply safety factor: $$ T_{design} = 1.25 \times 1039.76 = 1299.7 \ \mathrm{tonnes} $$ **Step 5:** Round up to the nearest standard press size: **1500 tonnes**. **Conclusion:** A **1500-tonne hydraulic compression press** should be selected to produce the SMC bumper fascia. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - ✅ Compression moulding cycle = **8 sequential stages**: charge prep → loading → fast close → slow close → breathing → pressurisation → cure → opening → ejection. - ✅ **Fill factor** $f$ is always $0.5 - 0.8$ — never equal to 1. - ✅ **Breathing** eliminates trapped gas → critical for SMC, BMC. - ✅ Cure time $t_{cure} \propto x^{2}$ — thickness squared rule. - ✅ Specific pressure range: **7–70 MPa** depending on resin system. - ✅ Two-velocity closing: **fast** for productivity, **slow** for quality. - ✅ Clamp tonnage $T_{press} = \dfrac{P_{spec} \cdot A}{g} \times \text{safety factor}$. - ✅ Cure time formula must include $\dfrac{4}{\pi}$ inside the logarithm. - ✅ Cycle time = cure time + auxiliary time; productivity = 3600 / cycle time. - ✅ Always convert **mm → m** before substituting into heat-conduction equations. - ✅ Thermosets undergo **irreversible crosslinking** — no remelting possible. - ✅ Exotherm peak can exceed mould wall temperature for thick sections — risk of thermal degradation.

topics.title: Troubleshooting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Troubleshooting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Troubleshooting in Polymer Processing** is the systematic methodology of **identifying, diagnosing, and rectifying defects** that arise during primary conversion operations (injection molding, extrusion, blow molding, thermoforming, rotational molding, etc.) and secondary processing operations. It involves correlating **process anomalies** (temperature, pressure, flow, cooling) with **product defects** (visual, dimensional, mechanical) by employing causal analysis tools such as the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) diagram** and **Pareto analysis** within the framework of Design of Experiments (DoE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** Troubleshooting of polymer processing operations and product defects — cause-effect relationships and corrective actions. The examiner expects students to link *one specific defect → multiple root causes → specific machine/material/design remedies*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of troubleshooting in polymer processing exactly like a **doctor diagnosing a patient**:

| Medical Diagnosis (Analogy) | Polymer Troubleshooting (Real) |
|---|---|
| Patient reports **symptom** (headache) | Moulded part shows **defect** (sink mark) |
| Doctor checks **vitals** (BP, temperature) | Engineer checks **process parameters** (melt temp, injection pressure) |
| Doctor orders **tests** (blood test) | Engineer runs **DoE trials** or process logs |
| Doctor prescribes **medicine** | Engineer applies **corrective action** (raise pack pressure) |
| **Cure** = symptom disappears | **Cure** = defect-free part |

> [!NOTE]
> A defect is rarely caused by a single factor. A **sink mark** can be due to insufficient pack pressure, low mould temperature, thick section, or material shrinkage — the troubleshooter's job is to find the **dominant root cause** using a structured, scientific approach.

## 1.3 Categorical Classification of Defects

Polymer defects are conventionally classified into **four super-categories** based on the stage of the value chain where they originate:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4M+1E Framework of Defect Causation (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Material (M₁):** Resin grade, MFI, moisture content, contamination
> 2. **Machine (M₂):** Barrel/screw wear, heater band failure, hydraulic pressure fluctuation
> 3. **Mould / Die (M₃):** Gate location, cooling channel layout, surface finish
> 4. **Method / Process (M₄):** Injection speed, melt temperature, cooling time, holding pressure profile
> 5. **Environment (E):** Ambient humidity, ambient temperature (often overlooked!)

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Structured Troubleshooting

A KTU examiner expects you to remember the **closed-loop control philosophy** of troubleshooting:

$$
\boxed{\text{Detect} \longrightarrow \text{Diagnose (5-Why / Ishikawa)} \longrightarrow \text{Eliminate Root Cause} \longrightarrow \text{Verify (SPC / Cp-Cpk)}}
$$

The minimum standard metric used in industry to declare a process "cured" is a **Process Capability Index** of:

$$
\boxed{C_{pk} \geq 1.33 \quad \text{(Industry Standard)}}
$$

A value of $C_{pk} < 1.00$ indicates the process is statistically **incapable** and the defect will recur.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Systematic Defect Matrix — Injection Moulding

> [!NOTE]
> Injection moulding is the **most defect-prone** process because it involves the largest number of simultaneously controlled parameters (typically 15+). It is therefore the **most frequently tested topic** in KTU Module 3.

### 2.1.1 Short Shot (Incomplete Filling)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Part is incomplete; molten polymer freezes before reaching cavity extremities |
| **Material causes** | Melt Flow Index (MFI) too low; high viscosity; wet resin (hydrolysis of nylon/PET → bubbles block flow) |
| **Machine causes** | Insufficient shot size; barrel heater failure; back-flow check valve (non-return) leakage |
| **Mould causes** | Inadequate venting; thin sections; long flow length; cold mould |
| **Process (Method) causes** | Low melt temperature; low injection pressure; low injection speed; insufficient injection time |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) Dry resin (≥4 hrs @ 80°C for nylon), (2) Raise melt temp by 10–20°C, (3) Raise injection pressure to machine limit, (4) Improve venting at end-of-cavity, (5) Add or relocate gate to thicker section |

### 2.1.2 Flash (Burr / Flash Lines)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Excess thin plastic leaking at the parting line, ejector pin gaps, or vent lands |
| **Primary cause** | **Clamping force insufficient** OR **mould closed dimension worn** |
| **Quantitative criterion** | $$\boxed{F_{clamp} \geq P_{inj} \times A_{proj} \times 10^3}$$ where $F_{clamp}$ in **kN**, $P_{inj}$ in **MPa**, $A_{proj}$ in **mm²** |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) Increase clamp tonnage, (2) Reduce injection pressure/speed, (3) Clean parting line, (4) Repair worn mould surfaces, (5) Reduce melt temperature to lower cavity pressure spike |

### 2.1.3 Sink Marks (Surface Depressions)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Localized depressions on the surface, usually over thick sections, ribs, or bosses |
| **Physics** | **Volumetric shrinkage during cooling** is not compensated by additional melt — internal void forms |
| **Volumetric shrinkage** | $$\boxed{\Delta V = \beta \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{mould})}$$ where $\beta$ is the coefficient of volumetric shrinkage (semi-crystalline: 0.015–0.025; amorphous: 0.005–0.010) |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) Increase **pack/hold pressure** (most effective), (2) Increase **pack time**, (3) Reduce melt temperature, (4) Redesign part with **uniform wall thickness** (no ribs > 60% of nominal wall), (5) Increase cooling time to fully densify |

### 2.1.4 Voids (Internal Bubbles)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Internal cavities inside thick sections, often invisible externally |
| **Difference from sink** | Voids are **internal**; sinks are **external** — both arise from the same root cause (insufficient packing) but the geometry determines which manifests |
| **Critical rule** | If $\text{wall thickness} > 6 \text{ mm}$, internal voids become statistically certain without redesign |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) Increase pack pressure & time, (2) Lower melt temperature to slow skin formation, (3) Core out the thick section, (4) Use **gas-assist** or **water-assist** internal cooling |

### 2.1.5 Warpage (Distortion)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Part curls, twists, or bows out of specification after ejection |
| **Mechanism** | **Differential residual stresses** between skin and core, or between fibres in fibre-filled compounds |
| **Differential shrinkage formula** | $$\boxed{\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T}$$ where $\alpha$ is the linear coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE). For fibre-filled polymers, $\alpha$ is **anisotropic** |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) Balance cooling circuit — both mould halves at equal temperature, (2) Increase cooling time to reduce thermal gradient, (3) Redesign with uniform wall, (4) Use **fibre orientation control** through gate placement, (5) Anneal the part |

### 2.1.6 Weld Lines (Knit Lines)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | A visible line on the part surface where two melt fronts meet after flowing around a hole, boss, or insert |
| **Mechanical impact** | Tensile strength can drop by **20–70%** at the weld line — a critical KTU exam point |
| **Weld line strength** | $$\boxed{\sigma_{weld} = \sigma_{bulk} \cdot f(T_{melt}, P_{cavity}, V_{gate})}$$ Higher $T_{melt}$, higher $P_{cavity}$, and higher $V_{gate}$ improve weld strength |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) Raise mould & melt temperature, (2) Increase packing pressure, (3) Add overflow tab at weld location, (4) Use **flow leader** to push weld line into non-critical area, (5) Relocate gate |

### 2.1.7 Burn Marks (Diesel Effect / Air Burn)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Brown/black streaks, especially at end-of-fill or weld lines |
| **Mechanism** | **Adiabatic compression of trapped air** in the cavity — air temperature can exceed **600°C**, charring the polymer (Diesel Effect) |
| **Air ignition temperature equation** | $$\boxed{T_{adiabatic} = T_0 \left(\frac{P_{cavity}}{P_{atm}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}}$$ For air, $\gamma = 1.4$ |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) **Improve venting** at end-of-fill (vent depth 0.02–0.05 mm), (2) Reduce injection speed to allow air escape, (3) Use **vacuum-assisted moulding** (mould evacuation to <50 mbar) |

### 2.1.8 Jetting

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Serpentine, snake-like surface folds emanating from the gate |
| **Mechanism** | Melt enters cavity as a **free jet** that folds back on itself — surface is not properly welded |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) **Relocate gate to thicker section** so melt hits a wall, (2) Use **tab gate** or **fan gate** to redirect flow, (3) Reduce injection speed, (4) Increase mould temperature |

### 2.1.9 Splay / Silver Streaks

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Silver, silvery-white, or brown streaks on the part surface |
| **Root cause #1** | **Moisture in resin** — water vaporizes at melt temperature, creating micro-bubbles. Critical for hygroscopic resins (nylon, PC, PET, PMMA) |
| **Root cause #2** | **Overheating / degradation** in barrel — melt decomposes and releases volatiles |
| **Drying rule** | Nylon 6,6: dry at **80°C for 4–6 hours**; PC: 120°C for 4 hours; PET: 140°C for 4 hours |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) **Dry the resin** properly, (2) Reduce melt temperature, (3) Reduce back pressure, (4) Purge barrel, (5) Check for screw/barrel wear (dead spots causing stagnation) |

### 2.1.10 Flow Marks (Halo / Air Trapping at Surface)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Wavy, ripple-like patterns on the surface |
| **Cause** | Melt front sticks to cold mould wall, then slips intermittently |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) Increase mould temperature, (2) Reduce injection speed, (3) Use **skin-flow control** (variable mould temperature) |

## 2.2 Systematic Defect Matrix — Extrusion

> [!NOTE]
> Extrusion defects are dominated by **flow instabilities at the polymer–metal interface** of the die. This is a key distinguishing feature vs. injection moulding.

### 2.2.1 Melt Fracture (Sharkskin)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Matte, rough, or cracked surface — small amplitude periodic (sharkskin) or large amplitude chaotic (gross melt fracture) |
| **Physics** | When wall shear stress $\tau_w$ exceeds the **critical stress** $\tau_c$, the polymer slips unstably at the die wall |
| **Critical condition** | $$\boxed{\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L} \geq \tau_c \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 0.4 \text{ MPa}}$$ |
| **Apparent shear rate** | $$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}}$$ for a circular die of diameter $D$ and volumetric flow rate $Q$ |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) **Lower output rate** (most effective), (2) **Raise melt temperature**, (3) Use **processing aids** (e.g., fluoropolymer elastomers, 50–500 ppm), (4) Redesign die with **larger land length** or coat die with low-surface-energy coating |

### 2.2.2 Die Drool (Die Lip Build-up)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Accumulated degraded polymer at the die exit |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) Use **flow promoter** in formulation, (2) Reduce die land temperature, (3) Use **die face cutter** (in-line), (4) Re-design die geometry to reduce stagnation |

### 2.2.3 Surging

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Pulsating output — extrudate thickness/diameter varies cyclically |
| **Root cause** | **Insufficient feed (starvation)** from hopper — screw is not flooded |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) Increase feed zone temperature, (2) Use **grooved feed throat**, (3) Use crammer feeder, (4) Check screw design — proper compression ratio (2:1 to 3:1) |

### 2.2.4 Bambooing

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Visual symptom** | Periodic thick-thin regions resembling bamboo stem |
| **Cause** | Stick-slip melt fracture compounded with surging |
| **Corrective actions** | (1) Lower shear rate, (2) Add processing aid, (3) Optimize screw speed |

## 2.3 Systematic Defect Matrix — Blow Moulding

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Poor wall thickness distribution** | Improper parison programming; sag | Use **parison profile programmer**; pre-blow timing; use Mooney viscosity-matched resin |
| **Parison sag (extrusion blow)** | Resin too hot / too low MI / parison too long | Reduce melt temp; use higher MI grade; support parison with **calibration** |
| **Pinholes** | Contamination, degraded resin | Filter melt; purge barrel; check resin for gels |
| **Pitting / Orange peel** | Insufficient blow pressure; cold mould | Increase air pressure; raise mould temp |
| **Drift / off-center** | Improper mould alignment | Re-align mould; check platen parallelism |

## 2.4 Systematic Defect Matrix — Thermoforming

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Webbing (excess material)** | Sheet too hot OR vacuum too fast | Lower heater temperature; **delay vacuum** (pre-blow stage) |
| **Chill marks** | Cold spots on sheet | Balance heater zones; check for burnt-out elements |
| **Mark-off** | Sheet stuck to mould | Increase mould temperature; use **plug-assist** |
| **Blisters** | Trapped air or moisture | Pre-heat adequately; use **vacuum snap-back** |
| **Incomplete forming** | Insufficient vacuum / pressure | Check vacuum pump; increase pressure; adjust heater profile |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 7 equations — they cover 90% of KTU numerical questions in Module 3.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{clamp} \geq P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj} \cdot 10^{-3}$ | Clamp tonnage requirement to prevent flash | kN, MPa, mm² |
| 2 | $\Delta V = \beta \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{mould})$ | Volumetric shrinkage (sink/void) | m³ |
| 3 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | Linear thermal contraction (warpage) | m |
| 4 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$ | Wall shear stress in die (melt fracture) | Pa |
| 5 | $\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \dfrac{32Q}{\pi D^3}$ | Apparent shear rate in pipe die | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_{th}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}{T_{ej} - T_{mould}}\right)$ | Cooling time (Schenk-Springer, simplified) | s |
| 7 | $C_{pk} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | Process capability index | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In equation 6, $s$ is part wall thickness, $\alpha_{th}$ is thermal diffusivity, $T_{ej}$ is ejection temperature. This is the **most-derived** cooling-time formula in KTU exams.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Industry

These relationships are used in:
- **Automotive**: Class-A surface defect control (weld lines invisible, no sink marks on bumpers)
- **Medical**: Defect-free transparent PC/PET housings (no splay, no voids)
- **Packaging**: Continuous blown-film extrusion with sub-1% gauge variation
- **Electronics**: Zero-flash connectors (high-clamp tonnage precision)
- **Quality 4.0**: Real-time **machine-learning** defect classification (Cognex, KLA-Tencor vision systems)

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** The following derivations are **expected to appear** in either Part B (14-mark) sub-part (a) or as a Part A (3-mark) "derive the expression" question. Memorize the structure, not just the result.

## 3.1 Derivation: Cooling Time in Injection Moulding (Simplified 1-D Heat Conduction)

### Problem Setup

A semi-infinite slab of polymer at initial temperature $T_{melt}$ is placed in contact with a mould wall at $T_{mould}$ (constant). Determine the time required for the **centreline temperature** to reach the ejection temperature $T_{ej}$.

### Assumptions
- 1-D heat conduction (slab of thickness $s$, mould wall at $x = 0$)
- Constant thermal diffusivity $\alpha_{th}$
- Mould acts as infinite sink (constant $T_{mould}$)
- Negligible internal heat generation

### Governing Equation (Fourier's Law)

The transient 1-D heat equation in Cartesian coordinates:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha_{th} \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}
$$

### Boundary & Initial Conditions

$$
T(x, 0) = T_{melt} \quad \text{(at } t = 0\text{)}
$$

$$
T(0, t) = T_{mould} \quad \text{(at mould surface)}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x = s} = 0 \quad \text{(symmetry at centreline)}
$$

### Solution by Separation of Variables

We seek a series solution of the form:

$$
\theta(x, t) = \frac{T(x, t) - T_{mould}}{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}
$$

The dimensionless temperature solution is:

$$
\theta(x, t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{4}{(2n+1)\pi} \sin\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2s}\right] \exp\!\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 \alpha_{th} t}{4 s^2}\right]
$$

### Centreline Temperature (x = s)

At the centreline, $\sin\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi}{2}\right] = (-1)^n$. The series becomes:

$$
\theta(s, t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha_{th} t}{4 s^2}\right) - \frac{1}{3}\exp\!\left(-\frac{9\pi^2 \alpha_{th} t}{4 s^2}\right) + \frac{1}{5}\exp\!\left(-\frac{25\pi^2 \alpha_{th} t}{4 s^2}\right) - \cdots\right]
$$

### Simplification (Dominant First Term)

For the times of practical interest ($t > 0.5 s$ for typical thermoplastics), the first term dominates:

$$
\theta(s, t) \approx \frac{4}{\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha_{th} t}{4 s^2}\right)
$$

### Solving for Cooling Time $t_{cool}$

Setting $T(s, t_{cool}) = T_{ej}$:

$$
\frac{T_{ej} - T_{mould}}{T_{melt} - T_{mould}} = \frac{4}{\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha_{th} t_{cool}}{4 s^2}\right)
$$

Take natural log of both sides:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{T_{ej} - T_{mould}}{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}\right) = -\frac{\pi^2 \alpha_{th} t_{cool}}{4 s^2}
$$

Multiply both sides by $-1$:

$$
-\ln\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{T_{ej} - T_{mould}}{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}\right) = \frac{\pi^2 \alpha_{th} t_{cool}}{4 s^2}
$$

Solve for $t_{cool}$:

$$
\boxed{t_{cool} = \frac{4 s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_{th}} \ln\!\left(\frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}{T_{ej} - T_{mould}}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 version is the **Schenk–Springer simplified form** (the one in the formula table above, Section 2.5, equation 6). The difference is a factor of $\frac{8}{\pi^2}$ inside the log, accounting for the **average temperature** of the part, not just the centreline. Both forms are accepted.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU 3-Mark Style)

> **Given:** PP part, $s = 3$ mm, $T_{melt} = 230$°C, $T_{mould} = 40$°C, $T_{ej} = 90$°C, $\alpha_{th} = 0.09$ mm²/s. Find cooling time.

**Step 1:** Plug into the simplified formula:

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_{th}} \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}{T_{ej} - T_{mould}}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{(3)^2}{\pi^2 \cdot 0.09} \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{230 - 40}{90 - 40}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate bracket:

$$
\frac{8}{\pi^2} \approx \frac{8}{9.8696} \approx 0.8106
$$

$$
\frac{230 - 40}{90 - 40} = \frac{190}{50} = 3.8
$$

$$
0.8106 \times 3.8 = 3.0803
$$

**Step 4:** Take natural log:

$$
\ln(3.0803) \approx 1.1250
$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation:

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{9}{0.8883} \times 1.1250 = 10.13 \times 1.1250 \approx 11.4 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer: $t_{cool} \approx 11.4$ seconds.** This is the *minimum* cooling time; industry practice adds a **10–20% safety factor**, giving 12.5–13.7 s.

## 3.2 Derivation: Wall Shear Stress in Extrusion Die (Hagen-Poiseuille Law)

### Problem Setup

A polymer melt flows through a **circular die** of radius $R$ and length $L$ under a pressure drop $\Delta P$. The melt is assumed to be a **Newtonian fluid** for this introductory derivation (in Section 3.3 we extend to a Power-Law model).

### Governing Equations

For steady, fully-developed, incompressible, laminar flow in a circular pipe, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to:

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \eta \left[\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right)\right]
$$

The pressure gradient is constant:

$$
-\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \frac{\Delta P}{L}
$$

So:

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\Delta P}{\eta L}
$$

### Integration (First)

Multiply both sides by $r$:

$$
\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{\eta L}
$$

Integrate with respect to $r$:

$$
r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r^2}{2 \eta L} + C_1
$$

Divide by $r$:

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 \eta L} + \frac{C_1}{r}
$$

### Boundary Condition #1 (No-Slip at Wall)

At $r = R$, the velocity $v_z = 0$. This is a **Stokes-type** condition.

First integrate again:

$$
v_z(r) = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r^2}{4 \eta L} + C_1 \ln(r) + C_2
$$

At $r = R$:

$$
0 = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4 \eta L} + C_1 \ln(R) + C_2
$$

### Boundary Condition #2 (Finite Velocity at Centre)

At $r = 0$, the velocity must be **finite**. Since $\ln(0) = -\infty$, the only way to avoid singularity is:

$$
\boxed{C_1 = 0}
$$

Then:

$$
C_2 = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4 \eta L}
$$

### Velocity Profile (Parabolic)

$$
\boxed{v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4 \eta L} \left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right]}
$$

### Wall Shear Stress

Differentiate $v_z$ and evaluate at $r = R$:

$$
\tau_w = \eta \frac{dv_z}{dr}\bigg|_{r=R} = \eta \cdot \left(-\frac{\Delta P \cdot 2r}{4 \eta L}\right)\bigg|_{r=R} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}
$$

Taking the magnitude (since shear stress is conventionally reported positive on the wall):

$$
\boxed{\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **exactly** equation 4 in the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.5). The negative sign indicates the fluid drags the wall in the direction of flow (Newton's third law). This formula is the **trigger condition for melt fracture**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Power-Law (Ostwald-de Waele) Extension for Shear Rate in Extrusion Die

### Problem Setup

For **non-Newtonian shear-thinning polymer melts**, viscosity is a function of shear rate:

$$
\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}
$$

where $K$ is the **consistency index** and $n$ is the **power-law index** ($n < 1$ for shear-thinning).

### Apparent Shear Rate at Wall

Substituting into the shear rate at the wall:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \left(\frac{3n+1}{4n}\right) \cdot \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}
$$

The correction factor $\left(\frac{3n+1}{4n}\right)$ is the **Rabinowitsch correction** (for $n = 1$, it equals 1 and we recover the Newtonian result).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** If the question says "Newtonian" → use the simple form (equation 5 in Section 2.5). If it says "Power-Law / pseudoplastic" → multiply by the Rabinowitsch factor.

## 3.4 Derivation: Clamping Force Criterion (Anti-Flash)

### Problem Setup

A mould has projected area $A_{proj}$ on the parting line. The cavity pressure during injection peaks at $P_{inj}$. Determine the **minimum clamping force** $F_{clamp}$ required to prevent the mould from opening.

### Force Balance

The **opening force** on the mould (mould tries to open under cavity pressure):

$$
F_{open} = P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj}
$$

The **clamping force** must balance or exceed this:

$$
F_{clamp} \geq P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj}
$$

### Unit Conversion (Kerala University Convention)

In KTU problems, $P_{inj}$ is given in **MPa** (= N/mm²), $A_{proj}$ in **mm²**, and $F_{clamp}$ is conventionally reported in **kN**. Therefore:

$$
F_{clamp} \text{ (N)} = P_{inj} \text{ (N/mm}^2\text{)} \times A_{proj} \text{ (mm}^2\text{)}
$$

$$
F_{clamp} \text{ (kN)} = \frac{P_{inj} \text{ (MPa)} \times A_{proj} \text{ (mm}^2\text{)}}{1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} \geq \frac{P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj}}{1000}}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style Sub-Part)

> **Given:** $A_{proj} = 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 25{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$. Peak cavity pressure $P_{inj} = 60$ MPa. The available machine is 1200 kN. Will there be flash?

**Step 1:** Compute minimum clamp tonnage:

$$
F_{clamp, \min} = \frac{60 \times 25{,}000}{1000} = 1500 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare to available:

$$
F_{avail} = 1200 \text{ kN} < F_{clamp, \min} = 1500 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion:

$$
\boxed{\text{Flash is INEVITABLE. Increase clamp tonnage to at least 1500 kN, or reduce } P_{inj} \text{ to } \leq 48 \text{ MPa.}}
$$

## 3.5 Process Capability Index Derivation

### Definition

The $C_{pk}$ index measures how well a process stays within specification limits, accounting for both **centering** and **spread**:

$$
\boxed{C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)}
$$

where USL = Upper Specification Limit, LSL = Lower Specification Limit, $\mu$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = standard deviation.

### KTU Interpretation Table

| $C_{pk}$ Value | Interpretation | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $\geq 1.67$ | World-class / Six-Sigma capable | None — celebrate! |
| $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ | Industry-capable | Maintain process |
| $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ | Marginally capable | Tighten process control |
| $< 1.00$ | **Incapable — defects will occur** | Root-cause investigation |

## 4.1 Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram — Universal Defect Diagnostic Tool

> [!NOTE]
> The Ishikawa diagram is the **#1 most-tested diagram** in KTU Module 3 examiner's answer keys. Always draw it with the **defect as the "head"** and the **4M+1E branches as the "bones"**.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DEFECT: e.g., Sink Mark] --> B[Material]
    A --> C[Machine]
    A --> D[Mould]
    A --> E[Method]
    A --> F[Environment]
    B --> B1[High MFI resin]
    B --> B2[Wet resin]
    B --> B3[Wrong grade selection]
    C --> C1[Insufficient pack pressure]

    C --> C3[Check valve leakage]
    D --> D1[Thick section geometry]
    D --> D2[Inadequate cooling channels]
    D --> D3[Improper gate location]
    E --> E1[Low pack pressure]
    E --> E2[Insufficient pack time]
    E --> E3[High melt temperature]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Read Before Attempting!**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for writing "use proper drying" without specifying **temperature AND time AND dew point**.
> - Students **lose 3 marks** for listing all 4M causes without identifying the **dominant** cause.
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for stating "increase pressure" without indicating **which pressure** (injection, pack, hold, back, or clamp).
> - Students **lose 1 mark** for not including a **diagram** in 14-mark questions where one is requested.
> - **Negative marking**: Writing "increase pressure" for a short-shot problem when flash is also occurring will be marked **contradictory** — always check for *cascading* defects.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: List any three common defects in injection moulding and state ONE corrective action for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Defect | One Corrective Action (1 mark each) |
|---|---|
| **(i) Sink marks** | Increase **pack/hold pressure** during the cooling phase to compensate for volumetric shrinkage. |
| **(ii) Flash** | Increase **clamp tonnage** so that the clamping force exceeds the cavity pressure × projected area. |
| **(iii) Short shot** | **Dry the resin** thoroughly (e.g., PA66 at 80°C for 4–6 h) to remove moisture that otherwise vaporizes and blocks flow; alternatively, raise melt temperature by 10–20°C. |

> **Marking key:** 1 mark per defect + correct action. Action must be specific (not "change parameters"). No half-mark for vague answers.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: What is "Diesel Effect" in injection moulding? How is it prevented?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Diesel Effect** (or *air burn*) refers to the **adiabatic compression of trapped air** inside the mould cavity during the high-velocity injection phase. Because the air cannot escape, it is compressed rapidly, and its temperature can exceed **600 °C** (per the adiabatic compression equation $T = T_0 \left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$), which **chars/degrades the polymer** at end-of-fill locations, producing brown/black streaks.

**Prevention (2 marks):**
1. **Improve mould venting** at the end-of-fill region (vent depth: 0.02–0.05 mm for most thermoplastics).
2. **Reduce injection speed** to allow trapped air to escape progressively.
3. Use **vacuum-assisted moulding** (mould cavity evacuated to < 50 mbar before injection).

> **Marking key:** Definition (1) + Mechanism with equation (1) + Two prevention methods (1). Equation is optional but earns extra credit.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) diagram** methodology for troubleshooting injection moulding defects. Apply it to diagnose the **"weld line" defect**, listing at least 2 root causes from each of the 4M+1E categories.

**(b) [7 marks]** A mould with a projected area of **320 cm²** is used to mould a polypropylene part. The peak cavity pressure during injection is **55 MPa**. The available machine has a clamping force of **1500 kN**. Determine whether **flash** will occur. If yes, suggest **two machine-level remedies** and **two process-level remedies**.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a) — Ishikawa Diagram (7 marks)**

**Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram Definition (2 marks):**

The Ishikawa diagram, also called the **Cause-and-Effect diagram** or **Fishbone diagram**, is a structured brainstorming tool developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa (1968)** that organizes potential root causes of a defect into **five branches** (4M+1E):

1. **Material (M₁):** Resin grade, MFI, additives, moisture, contamination
2. **Machine (M₂):** Barrel/screw wear, heater bands, hydraulic pressure, check valve
3. **Mould (M₃):** Cooling channels, gate location, venting, surface finish
4. **Method (M₄):** Process parameters — temperature, pressure, speed, time
5. **Environment (E):** Ambient humidity, temperature, dust

**Diagram (to be drawn on answer sheet — 2 marks):**

```
DEFECT: Weld Line
        │
   ┌────┼────┬─────┬─────┐
   │    │    │     │     │
MATERIAL  MACHINE  MOULD  METHOD  ENVIRONMENT
(M₁)      (M₂)     (M₃)   (M₄)    (E)
```

**Application to Weld Line Defect (3 marks — at least 2 causes per category):**

| Category | Root Cause |
|---|---|
| **Material** | (i) Low MFI / high viscosity resin → poor fusion at weld; (ii) Contamination by foreign material preventing polymer chain interdiffusion |
| **Machine** | (i) Heater band failure at barrel front zone; (ii) Non-return valve partially open causing pressure drop |
| **Mould** | (i) Inadequate venting at weld location; (ii) Mould temperature too low → premature freeze; (iii) Insufficient draft in cavity (unnecessary obstacles) |
| **Method** | (i) Low melt temperature; (ii) Low injection speed; (iii) Insufficient pack pressure; (iv) Injection time too short |
| **Environment** | (i) High ambient humidity causing surface moisture on mould; (ii) Draft of cold air over mould surface causing uneven cooling |

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b) — Flash Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $A_{proj} = 320 \text{ cm}^2 = 320 \times 100 = 32{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$
- $P_{inj} = 55 \text{ MPa} = 55 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $F_{clamp,avail} = 1500 \text{ kN} = 1{,}500{,}000 \text{ N}$

**Step 1: Required Clamping Force (3 marks)**

$$
F_{clamp,req} = \frac{P_{inj} \times A_{proj}}{1000} = \frac{55 \times 32{,}000}{1000} = \frac{1{,}760{,}000}{1000} = 1760 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with Available (2 marks)**

$$
F_{clamp,avail} = 1500 \text{ kN} < F_{clamp,req} = 1760 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Conclusion (1 mark)**

Since the available clamping force is **insufficient**, **flash will occur** on the parting line.

**Step 4: Remedies (1 mark for any 4 = 0.25 × 4, but framed as 2 machine-level + 2 process-level):**

**Machine-level remedies:**
1. Use a **larger machine** with clamp tonnage ≥ 1760 kN (or ≥ 1800 kN with 5% safety margin).
2. Service the **tie bars and platen** for parallelism; check **mould height** setting.

**Process-level remedies:**
1. **Reduce injection pressure** to: $P_{max} = \frac{1500 \times 1000}{32{,}000} = 46.9$ MPa.
2. **Reduce injection speed** to delay pressure peak; or **reduce melt temperature** to lower viscosity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** What is **melt fracture** in polymer extrusion? Explain the mechanism with a **shear stress vs. shear rate plot**. Derive the expression for the **wall shear stress in a circular die** and state the critical stress condition for melt fracture onset.

**(b) [7 marks]** A polypropylene part of wall thickness **4 mm** is moulded at a melt temperature of **240 °C** with a mould temperature of **35 °C**. The ejection temperature is **95 °C**, and the thermal diffusivity of PP is **0.10 mm²/s**. Calculate the **minimum cooling time** using the simplified Schenk-Springer equation. State the **maximum permissible wall thickness** if the cycle time must be **≤ 25 s**.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a) — Melt Fracture (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**

**Melt fracture** is a flow instability that occurs in polymer extrusion when the polymer melt, on exiting the die, exhibits a **rough, cracked, or matte surface** (small-amplitude "sharkskin") or **chaotic, helical distortions** (large-amplitude "gross melt fracture"). It is caused by a **stick-slip instability** at the polymer-die wall interface when the wall shear stress exceeds a critical value.

**Mechanism with Plot (2 marks):**

```
  Shear Stress (τ)
       │
   τ_c ├───────/─────────────────────
       │      /  │ ←─ Stick-slip region
       │     /   │     (melt fracture)
       │    /    │
       │   /  Stable (Newtonian)
       │  /      flow
       │ /
       │/___________________________
       0                            → Shear Rate (γ̇)
```

- At low $\dot{\gamma}$: smooth, stable flow (Newtonian or shear-thinning).
- At $\dot{\gamma} > \dot{\gamma}_c$: surface oscillates between stick (no slip) and slip states → sharkskin.
- At even higher $\dot{\gamma}$: chaotic rupture → gross melt fracture.

**Derivation of Wall Shear Stress (3 marks):**

For a Newtonian fluid in a circular die, from the Hagen-Poiseuille equation (refer Section 3.2):

$$
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}
$$

**Critical condition for melt fracture:**

$$
\boxed{\tau_w \geq \tau_c \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 0.4 \text{ MPa (typical for LLDPE)}}
$$

When this condition is met, the wall-slip instability initiates.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b) — Cooling Time Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $s = 4$ mm
- $T_{melt} = 240$ °C
- $T_{mould} = 35$ °C
- $T_{ej} = 95$ °C
- $\alpha_{th} = 0.10$ mm²/s

**Step 1: Substitute into Simplified Schenk-Springer Equation (2 marks)**

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_{th}} \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}{T_{ej} - T_{mould}}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate Temperature Ratios (1 mark)**

$$
\frac{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}{T_{ej} - T_{mould}} = \frac{240 - 35}{95 - 35} = \frac{205}{60} = 3.4167
$$

$$
\frac{8}{\pi^2} = \frac{8}{9.8696} = 0.8106
$$

$$
0.8106 \times 3.4167 = 2.7696
$$

**Step 3: Natural Logarithm (1 mark)**

$$
\ln(2.7696) = 1.0188
$$

**Step 4: Final Calculation (1 mark)**

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{(4)^2}{\pi^2 \times 0.10} \times 1.0188 = \frac{16}{0.9870} \times 1.0188 = 16.21 \times 1.0188 = 16.51 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion (0.5 mark)**

$$
\boxed{t_{cool} \approx 16.5 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 6: Maximum Permissible Wall Thickness for $t_{cycle} \leq 25$ s (1.5 marks)**

Assuming cooling dominates the cycle (i.e., $t_{cool} \approx 0.66 \times t_{cycle}$ conservatively, or directly $t_{cool} \leq 25$ s):

$$
25 = \frac{s_{max}^2}{\pi^2 \times 0.10} \times 1.0188
$$

Solve for $s_{max}^2$:

$$
s_{max}^2 = \frac{25 \times \pi^2 \times 0.10}{1.0188} = \frac{25 \times 0.9870}{1.0188} = \frac{24.676}{1.0188} = 24.22
$$

$$
\boxed{s_{max} = \sqrt{24.22} \approx 4.92 \text{ mm}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** For a cycle time ≤ 25 s, the wall thickness must be ≤ **~4.9 mm**. This explains the industry rule-of-thumb: **wall thickness ≤ 4 mm** for most commodity thermoplastics to maintain sub-30-s cycle times.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU 2024 revision — 30-second scan checklist:**

### ✅ **Top 10 Defects → Top 3 Remedies Each**

| # | Defect | Quickest Single Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Short shot** | Dry resin + raise melt temp 10–20 °C |
| 2 | **Flash** | Increase clamp tonnage to $P \times A / 1000$ |
| 3 | **Sink mark** | Increase pack pressure |
| 4 | **Weld line** | Raise melt temp + raise mould temp |
| 5 | **Burn mark** | Improve venting at end-of-fill |
| 6 | **Warpage** | Balance mould cooling; uniform wall |
| 7 | **Melt fracture** | Lower output rate + add processing aid |
| 8 | **Splay** | Dry resin (esp. PA, PC, PET, PMMA) |
| 9 | **Jetting** | Relocate gate to thicker wall |
| 10 | **Die drool** | Reduce die land temperature; use flow promoter |

### ✅ **The 7 Formulas (Know Each by Heart)**

1. $F_{clamp} = P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj} / 1000$
2. $\Delta V = \beta \cdot \Delta T$
3. $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$
4. $\tau_w = \Delta P \cdot R / (2L)$
5. $\dot{\gamma}_{app} = 32Q / (\pi D^3)$
6. $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_{th}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}{T_{ej} - T_{mould}}\right)$
7. $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$

### ✅ **The 4M+1E Mnemonic for Ishikawa**

> **"My Mould Makes Every Error"** → **M**aterial, **M**achine, **M**ould, **M**ethod, **E**nvironment

### ✅ **Drying Conditions of Common Hygroscopic Resins (Memorize!)**

| Resin | Drying Temperature | Drying Time | Max Moisture |
|---|---|---|---|
| PA 6,6 (Nylon) | 80 °C | 4–6 h | < 0.02% |
| PC (Polycarbonate) | 120 °C | 4 h | < 0.02% |
| PET | 140 °C | 4 h | < 0.005% |
| PMMA (Acrylic) | 80 °C | 3 h | < 0.05% |
| ABS | 80 °C | 2–4 h | < 0.05% |

### ✅ **Cascade Effects to Watch For (Common Exam Trap)**

- **Raising melt temp** → fixes short shot BUT may cause flash, sink, or burn.
- **Raising injection pressure** → fixes short shot BUT may cause flash, warpage.
- **Reducing cooling time** → faster cycle BUT causes sink/voids/warpage.
- **Adding filler (fibre)** → fixes warpage/sink BUT causes weld-line weakness and surface roughness.

### ✅ **Key Numerical Constants**

- Critical wall shear stress for melt fracture: $\tau_c \approx 0.1$–$0.4$ MPa
- Vent depth for venting: $0.02$–$0.05$ mm
- Wall thickness for uniform moulding: $\leq 4$ mm (typical), thick section > 6 mm needs redesign
- Maximum rib thickness: $60\%$ of nominal wall
- Target process capability: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Compression Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automatic Compression Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automatic Compression Moulding** is a highly mechanized, production-grade variant of the classical compression moulding process in which the entire manufacturing cycle — comprising *preform charging, mould closing, material compression, heating/curing, mould opening, and part ejection* — is executed by pre-programmed electromechanical, hydraulic, and pneumatic subsystems under the supervision of a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** or a microprocessor-based Human-Machine Interface (HMI), with minimal or zero direct human intervention during the active cycle.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it falls under **Moulding of Thermosets and Elastomers**, and is specifically classified under the **Class C Moulding Operations** (per ASTM D883) — a process where **pressure**, **heat**, and **time** are the three cardinal process variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Automatic compression moulding is treated as an *advancement over semi-automatic moulding*, emphasizing the use of **automatic charge loading**, **automatic mould breathing**, **automatic ejection**, and **closed-loop control** of cure, pressure, and platen temperature.

> [!NOTE]
