<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six advantages of blow molding over injection molding for hollow containers.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lower tooling cost** — aluminum molds cost 30–50% less than steel injection molds.
2. **Seamless hollow parts in single shot** — eliminates welding/joining of two halves.
3. **No weld or knit lines** — parison is one continuous melt; no flow-front junction defects.
4. **Faster cycle times for large parts** — 5–15 s per part versus longer IM cycles.
5. **Better material utilization** — flash and rejects can be reground and reused (in-plant recyclability).
6. **Easier incorporation of handles, threads, and inserts** in a single molding step.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Mention four advantages of rotational molding for large hollow plastic products.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lowest tooling cost** among all polymer processes (cast aluminum or fabricated steel).
2. **Stress-free products** — no molding pressure, hence zero residual internal stresses.
3. **Uniform wall thickness** (±5%) controlled by powder charge mass.
4. **Massive part capability** — up to 50,000 L tanks, unconstrained by machine shot size.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **advantages of blow molding** as a polymer processing technique for hollow plastic products. Categorize your answer under **economic, design, operational, and product quality** heads. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **extrusion blow molding process**. Derive the relation between parison wall thickness and final part wall thickness using the blow-up ratio. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### (a) Advantages of Blow Molding (7 marks)

**Economic Advantages:**
- Lower tooling cost (~30–50% cheaper than injection molds); aluminum molds widely used.
- High material utilization; flash and rejects can be **reground and reused** with minimal property loss.
- Low scrap rate, typically **<5%**.

**Design Advantages:**
- Complex hollow geometries including handles, threads, and inserts molded in one step.
- Wide part size range: from a few milliliters (cosmetics) to 1000 L (drums).
- Compatible with **PE, PP, PET, PVC, PC, PA**.

**Operational Advantages:**
- Short cycle times (**5–15 s** per part) suitable for mass production.
- Continuous extrusion-blow variants enable very high throughput.
- Simple machine operation and control.

**Product Quality Advantages:**
- No weld or knit lines; the parison is one continuous melt.
- Uniform wall thickness when BUR is properly controlled.
- Lightweight parts due to controlled wall thickness.

#### (b) Extrusion Blow Molding Process + Derivation (7 marks)

**Process Steps (Sketch description):**
1. Polymer is melted in an extruder and pushed through an annular die to form a hollow **parison**.
2. The parison is captured between two halves of a cooled mold.
3. A **blow pin** introduces compressed air into the parison, inflating it against the mold cavity.
4. The plastic cools and solidifies in mold shape.
5. The mold opens and the part is ejected; flash is trimmed.

**Derivation:**

Given: Parison diameter $D_p$, parison wall thickness $t_p$, mold diameter $D_m$, molded wall thickness $t_w$.

By **conservation of material volume**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_p &= V_m \\
\pi \, D_p \, t_p \, L &= \pi \, D_m \, t_w \, L
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving:
$$
t_w = \frac{D_p}{D_m} \cdot t_p = \frac{t_p}{BUR}
$$

where $BUR = \dfrac{D_m}{D_p}$ is the **blow-up ratio**.

**Conclusion:** Higher BUR gives thinner final walls; proper parison programming is critical.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Enumerate the **advantages of thermoforming** for large-area plastic parts. Compare it briefly with injection molding in terms of cost, part size, and tooling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **rotational molding process** in detail. Discuss its advantages and derive an expression for wall thickness from the powder charge mass. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### (a) Advantages of Thermoforming + Comparison (7 marks)

**Advantages:**
1. **Lowest tooling cost** for prototypes and short runs (aluminum or even wooden molds).
2. **Fast turnaround** — prototypes in hours.
3. **Large part capability** — up to 3 m × 6 m panels.
4. **Excellent surface detail** — captures mold texture, gloss, and grain.
5. **Material versatility** — PS, PVC, PET, PP, ABS, PMMA, PC, HDPE.

**Comparison with Injection Molding:**

| Parameter | Thermoforming | Injection Molding |
|---|---|---|
| Tooling cost | **Low** | High |
| Part size | **Very large** | Limited by machine platen |
| Cycle time | Short for thin parts | Longer |
| Material waste | Moderate (skeleton) | Low |

#### (b) Rotational Molding Process + Derivation (7 marks)

**Process Steps:**
1. A measured quantity of **polymer powder** (or liquid) is charged into a hollow mold.
2. The mold is closed, heated, and **rotated biaxially** (e.g., 4:1 ratio on two perpendicular axes).
3. Centrifugal force coats the inner mold surface uniformly with molten polymer.
4. The mold is cooled while still rotating to maintain uniform wall thickness.
5. The mold opens and the **hollow, stress-free part** is demolded.

**Derivation of Wall Thickness:**

Let $m$ = powder charge mass, $\rho$ = melt density, $A_m$ = mold inner surface area.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \rho \, A_m \, t_w \\
t_w &= \frac{m}{\rho \, A_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Wall thickness in RM is controlled **directly by powder charge mass**, making it uniquely tunable without changing process parameters.

**Advantages Recap:**
- Lowest tooling cost; **stress-free parts**; uniform wall (±5%); seamless hollow parts; **multi-layer molding possible**; very large parts (up to 50,000 L); no weld lines.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Listing only 4–5 advantages when the question demands 8–10.** A 14-mark answer needs a *categorized, exhaustive* list — not just bullet points.
> 2. **Missing the categorization.** Examiners award extra marks when you classify advantages under **economic / design / operational / quality** heads.
> 3. **Conflating blow molding with thermoforming.** These are different processes — students often mix up parison (BM) with sheet (TF). Always clarify the starting form.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation.** When asked to "derive," never give just the final formula — show every algebraic step with proper units.
> 5. **Forgetting real-world examples.** Adding one-line applications (e.g., "PET bottles, kayaks, refrigerator liners") demonstrates applied understanding and scores partial-credit marks even if the core content is incomplete.
> 6. **Not mentioning stress-free nature of RM.** This is RM's signature advantage and frequently asked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Blow Molding (BM):** Hollow, seamless, lightweight parts; aluminum molds; 5–15 s cycles; **BUR controls wall thickness** ($t_w = t_p / BUR$).
- **Thermoforming (TF):** Heated sheet formed over/into a mold; **large-area panels**; fast prototyping; corner thinning is the main limitation.
- **Rotational Molding (RM):** Powder charged into rotating heated mold; **stress-free, multi-layered, massive** hollow parts; **wall thickness directly from powder mass** ($t_w = m / (\rho A_m)$).
- **Common Advantages Across Module 4 Processes:** Low tooling cost (vs. IM), hollow seamless parts, good material utilization, design flexibility.
- **Signature Advantages (KTU Hot Questions):**
  - BM → *no weld lines*, *low tooling cost*, *seamless hollow containers*.
  - TF → *large-area parts*, *fast prototyping*, *low capital*.
  - RM → *stress-free*, *multi-layer capability*, *50,000 L part sizes*.
- **Tooling Cost Ratio:** $C_{\text{RM}} : C_{\text{BM}} : C_{\text{IM}} = 1 : 5 : 10$.
- **Formula Highlights:** $BUR = D_m / D_p$; $t_w = t_p / BUR$ (BM); $t_w = m / (\rho A_m)$ (RM).
- **Real-World Map:** Bottles → BM; Trays → TF; Tanks → RM.
- **Always categorize** advantages under **Economic / Design / Operational / Quality** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **PCPOT502 – Polymer Processing (Module 4: Processing of Thermosets and Polymer Composites)**, the term **"Limitations"** refers to the inherent operational, technical, economic, and design-related constraints or drawbacks associated with each major polymer processing technique. These are the factors that restrict the **widespread applicability, dimensional accuracy, production rate, material compatibility, and cost-effectiveness** of processes such as **Compression Molding, Transfer Molding, Injection Molding of Thermosets, Hand Lay-up, Spray-up, Filament Winding, Pultrusion, and Resin Transfer Molding (RTM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *"Limitations of a polymer processing technique are the boundary conditions, technical constraints, and operational drawbacks imposed by the equipment, material rheology, mold design, and curing chemistry that restrict the part geometry, production economics, and final product performance."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swiss Army Knife" View

Think of each polymer processing method as a **specialized tool** in a workshop:

- A **compression mold** is like a heavy-duty **panini press** — excellent for thick, flat items but completely useless for intricate 3D shapes.
- A **filament winding machine** is like a **potter's wheel with strict rules** — superb for rotationally symmetric parts (pipes, tanks) but cannot easily make a rectangular box.
- A **hand lay-up** process is like **painting a wall with a brush** — versatile and cheap, but slow, labor-intensive, and quality depends on the worker's skill.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway for Students:** Every polymer process is a *trade-off* between **cost, speed, part complexity, dimensional accuracy, and mechanical performance**. Limitations define *where* a process is *not* suitable.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Valuation

The following metrics are evaluated when comparing process limitations:

| Metric | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Cycle Time** | **30 s – 24 h** | Production economics |
| **Wastage (Flash/Trimmings)** | **2 % – 30 %** | Material efficiency |
| **Tolerance Achievable** | **±0.05 mm – ±0.5 mm** | Dimensional precision |
| **Tooling Cost** | **₹10,000 – ₹50,00,000+** | Capital investment |
| **Part Size Limit** | **few cm – 10 m+** | Application scope |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** KTU board questions often ask *"Why is hand lay-up not used for mass production?"* or *"Why can't filament winding be used for rectangular components?"* — these are *direct limitation* questions worth **3 to 7 marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categorical Breakdown of Process Limitations

Limitations in polymer processing can be systematically classified into **five fundamental categories**:

### 2.1.1 Operational / Process Limitations
These are constraints imposed by the **physics and chemistry** of the process itself:
- Long cure times (thermosets)
- High pressure requirements
- Temperature sensitivity
- Inability to handle complex geometries

### 2.1.2 Material Limitations
- Viscosity constraints of the resin
- Fiber wet-out issues
- Thermal degradation windows
- Shelf-life of prepregs

### 2.1.3 Design / Geometric Limitations
- Draft angle requirements
- Undercuts and ribs
- Wall thickness uniformity
- Radius of curvature

### 2.1.4 Economic Limitations
- High capital cost
- Skilled labor dependence
- Scrap rate
- Energy consumption

### 2.1.5 Quality Limitations
- Void content in composites
- Fiber misalignment
- Surface finish
- Reproducibility

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Questions on limitations are often asked in the form *"Discuss the limitations of [Process X] in the context of [specific application]."* Always answer by structuring into the **five categories above**.

## 2.2 Process-wise Limitations — Comprehensive Analysis

### 2.2.1 Compression Molding Limitations

| # | Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Long cure time** | Low production rate |
| 2 | **High pressure required** (10–200 MPa) | Expensive hydraulic press |
| 3 | **Limited part complexity** | Undercuts, thin ribs not possible |
| 4 | **High flash wastage** | Material loss (5–10 %) |
| 5 | **Manual loading/unloading** | Labor-intensive, inconsistent |
| 6 | **Exothermic heat buildup** | Thermal degradation in thick parts |
| 7 | **Limited to simpler shapes** | Not for intricate 3D parts |

### 2.2.2 Transfer Molding Limitations

| # | Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Higher equipment cost** than compression | More capital investment |
| 2 | **Material wastage in cull and sprue** | 10–20 % scrap |
| 3 | **Limited to medium-sized parts** | Transfer pot size restricts shot weight |
| 4 | **Possible pre-cure in transfer pot** | Material loss, blocked gates |
| 5 | **Complex mold design** | Higher tooling cost |

### 2.2.3 Injection Molding of Thermosets — Limitations

| # | Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Narrow processing window** | Tight temperature/viscosity control |
| 2 | **Screw/barrel heating risk** | Premature cure → machine jam |
| 3 | **Faster fiber attrition** in screw | Reduced fiber length in composites |
| 4 | **Higher tool wear** due to abrasive fillers | Reduced mold life |
| 5 | **Limited to BMC/SMC-type compounds** | Not all thermosets injectable |

### 2.2.4 Hand Lay-up Limitations

| # | Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Low production rate** (1–2 parts/day) | Not for mass production |
| 2 | **High void content** (3–5 %) | Reduced mechanical strength |
| 3 | **Quality depends on operator skill** | Poor reproducibility |
| 4 | **High VOC emission** from styrene | Environmental/health hazard |
| 5 | **Limited fiber volume fraction** (max 35–40 %) | Sub-optimal composite properties |
| 6 | **Labor-intensive** | High recurring cost |
| 7 | **Long curing time** at room temperature | Slow throughput |
| 8 | **Unidirectional fiber orientation** only | Anisotropic properties |

### 2.2.5 Spray-up Limitations

| # | Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High resin content** → poor strength | Lower mechanical properties |
| 2 | **Non-uniform fiber length** | Property variability |
| 3 | **High styrene emission** | Workplace safety issue |
| 4 | **Limited to open molds** | Single-side surface finish only |
| 5 | **Operator-dependent quality** | Inconsistent parts |

### 2.2.6 Filament Winding Limitations

| # | Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Limited to axisymmetric/closed shapes** | Cannot make rectangular parts |
| 2 | **External surface only** | Inner surface has poor finish |
| 3 | **High capital cost** of winding machine | ₹25 lakh – ₹2 crore+ |
| 4 | **Difficult to incorporate inserts/branching** | Design limitations |
| 5 | **Tension control is critical** | Operator/equipment dependency |
| 6 | **Mandrel cost for complex parts** is high | Special mandrels needed |
| 7 | **Limited fiber orientations** (hoop, helical) | Not for 0°/90° layups |

### 2.2.7 Pultrusion Limitations

| # | Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Limited to constant cross-section parts** | No tapers or curves |
| 2 | **Slow process speed** (0.5–2 m/min) | Limited throughput |
| 3 | **High die cost** and wear | Capital and maintenance |
| 4 | **Resin-rich surface** issues | Surface quality problems |
| 5 | **Difficult to make thick sections** | Heat transfer limits |
| 6 | **Pulling force restrictions** | Limits part size and fiber content |

### 2.2.8 Resin Transfer Molding (RTM) Limitations

| # | Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High mold cost** (closed two-sided mold) | Capital investment |
| 2 | **Race-tracking** in preform | Dry spots, voids |
| 3 | **Limited fiber volume fraction** (50–55 % max) | Sub-optimal for aerospace |
| 4 | **Long injection time** for large parts | Cycle time increases |
| 5 | **Resin viscosity sensitivity** | Pressure and flow issues |
| 6 | **Preform handling is critical** | Skilled labor needed |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although "Limitations" is a **qualitative topic**, the following quantitative relations are frequently co-examined:

| Equation / Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cure Time (Thermoset)** | $t_{cure} \approx k \cdot T^n$ | seconds | Process design |
| **Fiber Volume Fraction** | $V_f = \dfrac{A_f}{A_c}$ | dimensionless | Composite strength |
| **Void Content** | $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{exp}}{\rho_{th}} \times 100$ | % | Quality check |
| **Maximum Pulling Force (Pultrusion)** | $F_{pull} = \sigma_f \cdot A_f \cdot f$ | N | Process limit |
| **Cycle Time (Compression)** | $t_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{cure} + t_{open}$ | s | Productivity |
| **Winding Angle (Filament)** | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{\pi D}{L_{wind}}$ | radians | Design constraint |
| **Resin Flow (RTM)** | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{K \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | m³/s | Mold filling |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value in tables to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding process limitations is **not academic** — it directly drives:

1. **Material Selection:** Choosing epoxy over polyester when low VOC is required.
2. **Process Selection:** Selecting RTM over hand lay-up for aerospace-grade components.
3. **Cost Estimation:** Predicting tooling cost vs. production volume break-even.
4. **Design for Manufacturing (DFM):** Draft angles, radii, and parting lines are dictated by process limits.
5. **Failure Analysis:** Voids in hand lay-up explain why a component failed below its theoretical strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connect:** The **Boeing 787 Dreamliner** uses Automated Fiber Placement (AFP) — a direct evolution to overcome the **limitation of filament winding** (which cannot produce non-axisymmetric, complex-curvature parts).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Limitation-Solving Framework

## 3.1 The KTU "LIMIT" Analytical Framework

For any limitation question, KTU examiners expect students to follow a structured 5-step analytical matrix. Below is the exhaustive breakdown:

### Step 1: **L** — List the Process

Identify the specific process being asked.

$$
\text{Process} \in \{\text{Compression}, \text{Transfer}, \text{Injection}, \text{Hand Lay-up}, \text{Spray-up}, \text{Filament Winding}, \text{Pultrusion}, \text{RTM}\}
$$

### Step 2: **I** — Identify Limitation Categories

Categorize into the **5 universal limitation types**:

$$
L_{total} = L_{op} + L_{mat} + L_{des} + L_{econ} + L_{qual}
$$

where:
- $L_{op}$ = Operational limitation
- $L_{mat}$ = Material limitation
- $L_{des}$ = Design/Geometric limitation
- $L_{econ}$ = Economic limitation
- $L_{qual}$ = Quality limitation

### Step 3: **M** — Map Each Limitation to a Consequence

For every limitation, state its **engineering consequence**:

$$
\text{Limitation}_i \xrightarrow{\text{causes}} \text{Consequence}_i
$$

**Example Mapping:**

$$
\text{Hand Lay-up} \Rightarrow \text{Low } V_f \Rightarrow \text{Low strength-to-weight ratio} \Rightarrow \text{Not for aerospace}
$$

$$
\text{Filament Winding} \Rightarrow \text{Axisymmetric only} \Rightarrow \text{No rectangular tanks} \Rightarrow \text{Limits design freedom}
$$

$$
\text{Pultrusion} \Rightarrow \text{Constant CS} \Rightarrow \text{No tapered beams} \Rightarrow \text{Catalog-only profiles}
$$

### Step 4: **I** — Identify Overcoming Strategies (Bonus Marks)

KTU examiners award **bonus marks** when students mention how limitations are mitigated:

| Limitation | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|
| Hand lay-up voids | Vacuum Bagging / VARTM |
| Filament winding — non-axisymmetric | Automated Fiber Placement (AFP) |
| Compression molding — slow | Pre-heating (RF/induction) |
| Pultrusion — constant CS | Pulforming (modified pultrusion) |
| RTM — race-tracking | Proper preform design, flow media |
| Injection thermoset — pre-cure | Cooled barrel, screw design |

### Step 5: **T** — Tabulate / Conclude

Always present the final answer as a **structured table** with **at least 4–6 limitations** for a **7-mark question**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style Limitation Analysis

**Problem:** *Explain the limitations of hand lay-up process for polymer composite manufacturing. How do these limitations affect part quality and production economics?*

### Model Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given:** Hand lay-up is the oldest and simplest composite manufacturing process.

**Step 1 — Identify the Process:**

$$
\text{Process} = \text{Hand Lay-up}, \quad T_{cure} = 25\text{–}80\,°\text{C (RT cure)}
$$

**Step 2 — State the Operational Limitations:**

$$
L_{op} = \{\text{Manual layup}, \text{No compaction pressure}, \text{Room T cure}\}
$$

**Step 3 — State the Material Limitations:**

$$
V_f^{max} = 0.35 - 0.40, \quad V_v = 3\text{–}5\%
$$

The fiber volume fraction in hand lay-up is restricted by the **mechanical hand-rolling compaction**, which cannot consolidate the laminate to the theoretical density. Using the rule of mixtures:

$$
E_c = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot (1 - V_f)
$$

For $E_f = 70\,\text{GPa}$ (carbon), $E_m = 3\,\text{GPa}$ (epoxy), $V_f = 0.40$:

$$
E_c = (70 \times 0.40) + (3 \times 0.60) = 28 + 1.8 = 29.8\,\text{GPa}
$$

Compare to autoclave-cured prepreg with $V_f = 0.60$:

$$
E_c^{autoclave} = (70 \times 0.60) + (3 \times 0.40) = 42 + 1.2 = 43.2\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Quality Consequence:**

$$
\Delta E = 43.2 - 29.8 = 13.4\,\text{GPa} \approx 31\% \text{ reduction}
$$

**Step 5 — Economic Consequence:**

Production rate:

$$
R_{hand} \approx 1\text{–}5\,\text{kg/hr}, \quad R_{RTM} \approx 10\text{–}30\,\text{kg/hr}
$$

$$
\text{Cost Ratio:} \quad \frac{C_{hand}}{C_{RTM}} \approx 0.3 - 0.5
$$

So hand lay-up is **cheaper for prototypes** but **expensive for large volumes** because of labor cost.

**Step 6 — Tabulated Conclusion:**

| # | Limitation | Effect on Quality | Effect on Economics |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Low compaction pressure | High void content (3–5 %) | Reduces in-service life |
| 2 | Manual operation | Skill-dependent quality | Inconsistent parts |
| 3 | Max $V_f$ ~ 0.40 | Lower strength | Higher material usage |
| 4 | Long room-T cure | Slow throughput | High labor cost |
| 5 | Styrene emission (polyester) | Health hazard | Ventilation cost |
| 6 | Unidirectional layup | Anisotropic | Wasted reinforcement |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Secret:** A 7-mark answer with **5+ limitations, 1 numerical comparison, and 1 mitigation strategy** scores **full marks**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C[Material]

    A --> E[Economic]
    A --> F[Quality]

    B --> B2[High pressure]

    E --> E3[Slow throughput]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State any three major limitations of hand lay-up process in polymer composite manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Low fiber volume fraction** — Limited to a maximum of **35–40 %** because hand rolling cannot fully consolidate the laminate, leading to **lower mechanical strength** compared to autoclave-cured composites. (1 mark)

2. **High void content (3–5 %)** — Absence of external compaction pressure during cure results in trapped air bubbles, which act as **stress concentrators** and reduce fatigue life. (1 mark)

3. **Operator-dependent quality** — The process relies heavily on the **skill and experience of the laminator**, leading to poor reproducibility and inconsistency between parts. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Additional valid points (for higher scoring):** High styrene emission (health hazard), long room-temperature cure time, anisotropic behavior due to unidirectional lay-up.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is filament winding restricted to axisymmetric components only? Explain in brief.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Filament winding is inherently restricted to **axisymmetric shapes** because of the following reasons:

1. The fiber is wound around a **rotating mandrel**, and the **winding angle** is determined by the ratio of mandrel rotational speed to carriage traverse speed. (1 mark)

$$
\tan \alpha = \frac{\pi D \cdot N_{rot}}{v_{carriage}}
$$

This equation holds true only for bodies of revolution (pipes, pressure vessels, cones). For non-axisymmetric parts, the relative motion between mandrel and carriage cannot maintain a **constant winding angle**, leading to fiber slippage, gaps, and resin-rich zones. (1 mark)

2. The **mandrel extraction** is also a challenge for non-axisymmetric parts as the mandrel must be collapsible or soluble. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice (OR option). Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the various **limitations of compression molding** process for thermoset polymers. Discuss how these limitations affect the **production rate, part geometry, and material wastage**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **limitations of hand lay-up and filament winding** processes. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** Compression molding is a closed-mold process where a pre-weighed charge of thermoset molding compound (BMC, SMC, or powder) is placed in a heated mold cavity and cured under pressure.

**Limitations (5 marks, 1 mark each for any five):**

1. **Long cure time:** Thermoset cure requires **5–15 minutes per part** depending on wall thickness. The exothermic reaction in thick parts can cause **internal overheating and thermal degradation**.

2. **High pressure requirement (10–200 MPa):** Demands expensive **hydraulic presses** with high-tonnage capacity, raising capital investment.

3. **Limited part complexity:** Cannot produce **intricate 3D geometries, undercuts, or thin ribs** because of unidirectional mold closing.

4. **Flash wastage (5–10 %):** Excess material escapes as flash, requiring **manual trimming** and creating material loss.

5. **Manual loading/unloading:** Labor-intensive and ergonomically challenging, leading to **inconsistent part quality**.

6. **Exothermic heat buildup:** In thick sections, the **center of the part retains heat** even after mold opening, causing warpage and residual stresses.

**Effect on Production, Geometry, Wastage (1 mark):** Compression molding is **slow**, **restricted to simple shapes**, and **material-inefficient** — making it suitable for **medium-volume, large, thick-walled parts** like electrical switchgear and automotive panels.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Limitation Aspect | Hand Lay-up | Filament Winding |
|---|---|---|
| **Production rate** | 1–2 parts/day | 5–20 parts/day |
| **Part geometry** | Any shape (open mold) | Axisymmetric only |
| **Fiber volume fraction** | 35–40 % | 60–75 % |
| **Void content** | 3–5 % | <1 % |
| **Capital cost** | Very low (₹50k–₹2 lakh) | Very high (₹25 lakh–₹2 crore) |
| **Operator skill** | Moderate (manual layup) | High (CNC programming) |
| **Surface finish** | Mould-side good, other side rough | Inner mandrel side rough, outer smooth |
| **Typical product** | Boat hulls, bathroom fixtures | Pressure vessels, rocket motor casings |

**Preference Criteria (1 mark):**
- **Choose Hand Lay-up** for: prototypes, large one-off structures, low budget, complex non-axisymmetric shapes.
- **Choose Filament Winding** for: high-strength pressure vessels, pipes, rocket casings, where **high $V_f$ and continuous fiber orientation** are critical.

---

### **QUESTION B (Alternative for Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **limitations of pultrusion process**. Why is pultrusion restricted to **constant cross-section profiles** only? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **key limitations of Resin Transfer Molding (RTM)** and **how these limitations are addressed in VARTM (Vacuum-Assisted RTM)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** Pultrusion is a continuous process where dry fibers are pulled through a resin bath and a heated die to produce a cured composite profile of constant cross-section.

**Limitations (5 marks):**

1. **Constant cross-section only** — The process geometry inherently requires a **fixed die shape**, making it impossible to produce **tapered, curved, or variable-thickness** profiles in a single pass.

2. **Slow process speed (0.5–2 m/min)** — Long residence time in the die is required to **cure thick sections** because of poor through-thickness thermal conductivity of the composite.

3. **High die cost and wear** — Heated steel dies cost **₹5 lakh–₹50 lakh**, and abrasive glass/carbon fibers cause **die wear** over time.

4. **Resin-rich surface layers** — As the resin-rich outer layer contacts the hot die first, it cures faster than the inner fibers, causing **internal stresses and surface defects**.

5. **Limited pulling force** — The maximum pull force is restricted by the **tensile strength of the dry fiber bundle** before impregnation, limiting part cross-sectional area.

6. **Difficult to produce thick sections (>25 mm)** — Heat transfer through thick laminates is slow, leading to **incomplete cure in the core**.

**Why Constant CS Only? (1 mark):** The pulling mechanism and die geometry are designed for a **fixed cross-section** because any change in shape would require **multiple dies or an interrupted process**, defeating the continuous nature of pultrusion.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**RTM Definition (1 mark):** RTM is a closed-mold process where dry fiber preform is placed in a matched metal mold and resin is injected under pressure to impregnate the fibers before curing.

**Limitations (4 marks):**

1. **High mold cost** — Matched metal molds cost **₹10 lakh–₹1 crore**, restricting RTM to medium-to-high volume production.

2. **Race-tracking** — Uncontrolled resin flow along the **edges of the preform** creates dry spots and voids in the center.

3. **Limited fiber volume fraction (50–55 %)** — Restricted by the permeability of the preform and the **maximum allowable injection pressure** (~1 MPa).

4. **Long injection time for large parts** — Darcy's law governs the flow:

$$
t_{inj} = \frac{\mu \cdot L^2}{2 K \cdot \Delta P}
$$

For large parts, $L$ is large, so $t_{inj}$ increases quadratically.

5. **Preform handling complexity** — Preforms can distort during mold closure, causing **fiber misalignment**.

**VARTM as Solution (2 marks):** VARTM uses **vacuum (negative pressure ~0.1 MPa)** on the outlet side instead of positive injection pressure. This:
- Allows **larger parts** (boat hulls up to 25 m)
- Reduces **mold cost** (only one rigid mold + vacuum bag)
- Achieves **higher $V_f$ (up to 60 %)** due to lower spring-back
- Eliminates race-tracking through proper flow-media placement

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Limitations Questions:**
>
> 1. **Generic answer pitfall:** Writing *"long cycle time"* without explaining **why** it occurs (cure kinetics, mold thermal mass). Examiner expects **cause-effect linkage**. Deducts **1–2 marks** for shallow answers.
>
> 2. **No numerical support:** Always include **at least 2 quantitative values** (e.g., $V_f = 40\%$, void content = 3–5 %, $t_{cure}$ = 10 min). Answers without numbers are considered **incomplete** and lose **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "How to overcome" part:** Even if the question only asks for limitations, mentioning **mitigation strategies** (vacuum bagging, AFP, pulforming) earns **bonus 1–2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing process names:** Students often interchange "pultrusion" with "pulforming" or "filament winding" with "automated fiber placement". KTU examiner deducts **half mark per confusion**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the comparison table:** For 7-mark comparison questions, **always present a table**. A well-structured table is worth **2–3 marks by itself**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Limitations**

### **A. Universal Limitation Categories (Memorize First)**
- ✅ Operational (cure time, pressure, temperature)
- ✅ Material (viscosity, fiber attrition, shelf life)
- ✅ Design/Geometric (axisymmetric-only, constant CS, draft angles)
- ✅ Economic (tooling cost, labor, throughput)
- ✅ Quality (voids, surface finish, reproducibility)

### **B. Process-Specific One-Line Limitations (Must-Memorize)**

| Process | #1 Killer Limitation |
|---|---|
| Compression molding | Long cure time + flash wastage |
| Transfer molding | Pre-cure in pot + sprue waste |
| Injection of thermoset | Pre-cure in barrel + fiber attrition |
| **Hand lay-up** | **High voids + low $V_f$ + skill-dependent** |
| Spray-up | Non-uniform fiber length + high resin |
| **Filament winding** | **Axisymmetric parts only** |
| **Pultrusion** | **Constant cross-section only** |
| **RTM** | **Race-tracking + high mold cost** |

### **C. Key Numerical Values to Remember**
- $V_f$ in hand lay-up: **35–40 %**
- $V_f$ in filament winding: **60–75 %**
- $V_f$ in RTM: **50–55 %**
- Void content in hand lay-up: **3–5 %**
- Void content in autoclave: **<1 %**
- Pultrusion speed: **0.5–2 m/min**
- Compression molding pressure: **10–200 MPa**
- Hand lay-up production rate: **1–5 kg/hr**

### **D. KTU High-Yield Mnemonics**

- **For Hand Lay-up Limitations — "SLOPE"**: **S**kill-dependent, **L**ow $V_f$, **O**pen-mold (VOC), **P**oor finish, **E**xcessive voids
- **For Pultrusion Limitations — "CCS"**: **C**onstant **C**ross-**S**ection only
- **For Filament Winding Limitations — "AIM"**: **A**xisymmetric, **I**nsert difficult, **M**andrel cost

### **E. Mitigation Strategies (Bonus Marks)**
- Hand lay-up voids → **Vacuum bagging / VARTM**
- Filament winding — non-axisymmetric → **Automated Fiber Placement (AFP)**
- Pultrusion — constant CS → **Pulforming**
- Compression — slow cure → **RF/induction preheating**
- RTM — race-tracking → **Flow media + preform design**
- Injection thermoset — pre-cure → **Cooled barrel + screw design**

### **F. Typical KTU Question Patterns**
1. *"Discuss the limitations of [Process]."* (7 marks — 5 limitations + table)
2. *"Compare the limitations of [Process A] and [Process B]."* (7 marks — table + preference criteria)
3. *"Why is [Process] not used for [Application]?"* (3–7 marks — limitation-based reasoning)
4. *"What are the limitations of [Process]? How are they overcome?"* (7 marks — limitations + mitigation)

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always end your answer with a **concluding sentence** linking the limitations to a **specific application domain** (e.g., *"Hence, hand lay-up is preferred for prototypes but RTM is chosen for aerospace-grade components."*). This signals to the KTU examiner that you understand the **engineering context**.

---

**End of Notes — PCPOT502 Module 4: Limitations**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) Board Examination Standards*
