topics.title: Theoretical and Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Theoretical and Design Considerations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Theoretical and Design Considerations** in polymer processing refers to the integrated set of **rheological, thermal, mechanical, and fluid-dynamic principles** that govern the geometric, dimensional, and operational design of polymer processing equipment — particularly **dies (extrusion), molds (injection molding/blow molding), and downstream calibration units**. These considerations translate the non-Newtonian flow behaviour of polymer melts into actionable design parameters such as land length, die entry angle, runner balancing, cooling-channel layout, and wall-thickness distribution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > The student must master (i) rheological characterization of polymer melts using the **Power-Law model**, (ii) the derivation of **apparent shear rates** for standard die geometries, (iii) **pressure-drop** and **die-swell** analysis, and (iv) the **heat-transfer-based cooling-time** and **clamping-force** formulations used in injection-mold design. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Toothpaste Tube" Model** > Imagine squeezing a thick toothpaste tube through differently shaped nozzles: > - A **round nozzle** lets paste flow easily in the centre but drags at the walls (this is **shear**). > - A **flat slit nozzle** creates a wide, ribbon-like flow — paste moves faster in the middle and slower near the walls (a phenomenon called **velocity profile reshaping**). > - When the paste exits, it bulges outwards and grows thicker than the nozzle opening (this is **die swell**, analogous to a relaxed compressed spring). > - The force you apply to push the paste is the **pressure drop**; if the nozzle is too long, the force needed rises sharply. > > **Now scale this up to industrial polymer melts**, which are 10⁶–10⁸ times more viscous than toothpaste, are non-Newtonian (their viscosity changes with how fast you push), and are temperature-sensitive. The "design" problem is to choose the *nozzle* (die/mold) geometry, length, and temperatures so that the polymer comes out at the right shape, at the right rate, with the right surface finish, and without defects. ## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted in Bold) | Metric | Typical Magnitude | Engineering Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Power-Law Consistency Index (K)** | **10² – 10⁵ Pa·sⁿ** | Melt stiffness indicator | | **Power-Law Flow Behaviour Index (n)** | **0.2 – 0.6 (shear-thinning)** | Degree of non-Newtonian character | | **Apparent Shear Rate (γ̇ₐ)** | **10 – 10⁴ s⁻¹** | Processing rate indicator | | **Melt Density (ρ)** | **700 – 1400 kg/m³** | Mass-flow conversion | | **Thermal Diffusivity (α)** | **1 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s** | Cooling-time controller | | **Cavity-Wall Temperature (T_w)** | **20 – 120 °C** | Surface finish / cycle time | | **Melt Temperature (T_m)** | **180 – 320 °C** | Processability | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Polymer Processing Design > [!IMPORTANT] > Every polymer-processing design decision ultimately resolves around **five interacting pillars**. KTU questions are almost always framed by picking **one or two** of these pillars as the "design lever." 1. **Rheological Pillar (Flow Behaviour):** Governed by viscosity models — primarily the **Ostwald–de Waele Power Law** $\eta = K\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ where $K$ is the consistency index and $n$ is the flow-behaviour index. For thermoplastics $n < 1$ (shear-thinning / pseudoplastic). 2. **Thermal Pillar (Heat Transfer):** Governed by Fourier's law applied through mold walls, cooling channels, and the polymer solidifying skin. This dictates **cooling time** $t_c$. 3. **Mechanical Pillar (Pressure & Force):** Governed by momentum balance yielding **pressure drop** $\Delta P$ across a die or runner, and **clamping force** $F_c$ required to hold a mold shut. 4. **Geometric Pillar (Die/Mold Shape):** Channel cross-section (circular, slit, annular, complex), land length $L$, entry angle, and **draw-down ratio**. 5. **Dimensional Pillar (Shrinkage & Swell):** Governed by **PVT** (pressure–volume–temperature) behaviour, **thermal contraction** $\Delta L = L_0 \alpha (T_m - T_{25})$, and **die swell** $B = D_e / D_0$. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow of a Design Problem - **Step 1 — Define the production target:** Mass throughput $\dot{m}$ (kg/h) or volumetric flow rate $Q$ (m³/s). - **Step 2 — Convert to apparent shear rate** for the chosen die geometry. - **Step 3 — Read viscosity from the Power Law** at the operating temperature and shear rate. - **Step 4 — Compute the pressure drop** $\Delta P$ using the integrated momentum balance. - **Step 5 — Verify the design pressure** is below the extruder/machine limit. - **Step 6 — Compute the cooling / solidification time** and the **cycle time**. - **Step 7 — Compute the clamping force** for injection molds, OR the **haul-off tension** for extrusion lines. - **Step 8 — Apply shrinkage compensation** to the cavity dimensions. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Encoding Rule:** All absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` (not `|`) so they DO NOT break the markdown table. | # | Quantity | Governing Formula | Typical Units | Design Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Power-Law Viscosity | $\eta = K\,\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Pa·s | Melt rheology | | 2 | Shear Stress (Power Law) | $\tau = K\,\dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Pa | Pressure calc. | | 3 | Apparent Shear Rate — Capillary | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ | s⁻¹ | Round die | | 4 | Apparent Shear Rate — Slit | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{6Q}{w\,h^{2}}$ | s⁻¹ | Flat die / sheet | | 5 | Apparent Shear Rate — Annular | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{6Q}{\pi (R_o+R_i)^{2}(R_o-R_i)}$ | s⁻¹ | Pipe / tube / parison | | 6 | Pressure Drop — Capillary | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2K\,L}{R}\left(\dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}\right)^{n}$ | Pa | Round die | | 7 | Pressure Drop — Slit | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2K\,L}{h}\left(\dfrac{6Q}{w\,h^{2}}\right)^{n}$ | Pa | Sheet die | | 8 | Pressure Drop — Annular | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2K\,L}{R_o-R_i}\left[\dfrac{6Q}{\pi (R_o+R_i)^{2}(R_o-R_i)}\right]^{n}$ | Pa | Pipe die | | 9 | Die (Extrudate) Swell | $B = \dfrac{D_e}{D_0} = \left[2 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right]^{n/6}$ (Tanner) | — | Calibration tank sizing | | 10 | Cooling Time (Simplified) | $t_c = \dfrac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\right)\dfrac{T_m - T_d}{T_m - T_w}$ | s | Injection cycle | | 11 | Clamping Force | $F_c = P_{cav}\cdot A_{proj}$ | N | Injection press selection | | 12 | Linear Shrinkage | $S_L = \dfrac{L_0 - L}{L_0}$ | — | Mold-cavity sizing | | 13 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$ | m³/s | Throughput check | | 14 | Reynolds Number (Polymer) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\,\bar{v}\,D_h}{\eta}$ | — | Flow regime (almost always laminar) | | 15 | Heat Flux Through Wall | $q = \dfrac{k\,A\,(T_m - T_w)}{s}$ | W | Cooling-channel design | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These equations are the **backbone of every commercial die/mold design software** (e.g., Moldflow®, Sigmasoft®, Polyflow, AutoForm). The KTU examiner expects students to be able to *derive*, *simplify*, and *apply* these to typical processing polymers such as **HDPE, LDPE, PP, PS, and PMMA**. In industry, an error in $\Delta P$ of just 10 % can cause a multi-crore extruder to stall; an error in $t_c$ of 5 s adds hours of cycle time per day. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pedagogical Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. The student should be able to reproduce each derivation on a blank sheet during the KTU University Examination. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power-Law Shear-Stress Distribution in a Circular Capillary **Starting point:** For steady, laminar, isothermal, fully-developed flow of a time-independent fluid in a circular tube of radius $R$ and length $L$, the momentum balance on a cylindrical element of radius $r$ gives: $$ \pi r^{2}\,\Delta P \;=\; 2\pi r\,L\,\tau_{rz} $$ **Step 1 — Isolate the shear stress:** $$ \tau_{rz} \;=\; \dfrac{r\,\Delta P}{2L} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the Power-Law constitutive model** $\tau_{rz} = K\,\left(-\dfrac{du_z}{dr}\right)^{n}$ (with the sign convention that $du_z/dr < 0$): $$ K\left(-\dfrac{du_z}{dr}\right)^{n} \;=\; \dfrac{r\,\Delta P}{2L} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for the velocity gradient:** $$ -\dfrac{du_z}{dr} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{r\,\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n} $$ **Step 4 — Integrate from $r$ to $R$** with the **no-slip boundary condition** $u_z(R) = 0$: $$ \int_{u_z(r)}^{0} du_z \;=\; \int_{r}^{R}\left(\dfrac{r'\,\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}dr' $$ $$ u_z(r) \;=\; \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}\dfrac{n}{n+1}\left(R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Compute the volumetric flow rate** by integrating $u_z(r)\cdot 2\pi r\, dr$ from $0$ to $R$: $$ Q \;=\; \pi\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}\dfrac{n}{3n+1}R^{(3n+1)/n} $$ **Step 6 — Invert to obtain the pressure drop** for a given flow rate: $$ \Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{2KL}{R}\left[\dfrac{(3n+1)\,Q}{\pi\, n\, R^{3}}\right]^{n} \;=\; \dfrac{2KL}{R}\left(\dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}\right)^{n}\!\left(\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^{\!n} $$ > [!NOTE] > The bracketed term is recognised as the **apparent shear rate at the wall** $\dot{\gamma}_a = 4Q/(\pi R^{3})$. The final bracketed factor is the **Rabinowitsch correction** for converting apparent shear rate to **true wall shear rate** in a non-Newtonian fluid. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Apparent Shear Rate for a Slit Die A slit die has width $w$ and gap $h$, with $w \gg h$ (side-wall effects neglected). The wall shear rate is found by differentiating the velocity profile. The final result is: $$ \dot{\gamma}_a \;=\; \dfrac{6Q}{w\,h^{2}} $$ **Derivation (concise):** From the 1-D momentum balance, $\tau = (h/2 - y)(\Delta P/L)$. The volumetric flow per unit width is $q' = Q/w = \int_0^{h/2} u\,dy$. Integrating the Power-Law velocity profile gives $q' = \dfrac{n}{2n+1}\,h\,u_{max}$ and $u_{max} = \dfrac{h}{2}\left(\dfrac{h\,\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}$. Eliminating $\Delta P$ using the wall-shear-stress expression and simplifying yields the canonical result above. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Time in an Injection-Mold Cavity Consider a slab of polymer of half-thickness $b = h/2$ initially at melt temperature $T_m$, suddenly brought into contact with a mold wall at $T_w$. The 1-D transient heat-conduction equation with constant surface temperature $T_w$ has the series solution (Carslaw & Jaeger): $$ \dfrac{T - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \;=\; \dfrac{4}{\pi}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-1)^{k}}{2k+1}\exp\!\left[-\alpha(2k+1)^{2}\pi^{2}t/(4b^{2})\right]\cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2k+1)\pi y}{2b}\right] $$ **Step 1 — Volume-average temperature** to get a single scalar: $$ \bar\theta \;=\; \dfrac{\bar T - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \;=\; \dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{1}{(2k+1)^{2}}\exp\!\left[-\alpha(2k+1)^{2}\pi^{2}t/(4b^{2})\right] $$ **Step 2 — The part is ejected** when the centreline $y=0$ reaches the **deformation temperature** $T_d$ (or the heat-distortion temperature). At $y=0$, the cosine term equals 1, so: $$ \dfrac{T_d - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \;=\; \dfrac{4}{\pi}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-1)^{k}}{2k+1}\exp\!\left[-\alpha(2k+1)^{2}\pi^{2}t_c/(4b^{2})\right] $$ **Step 3 — For long times, only the first term matters** ($k=0$): $$ \dfrac{T_d - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \;\approx\; \dfrac{4}{\pi}\exp\!\left[-\alpha\pi^{2}t_c/(4b^{2})\right] $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $t_c$** and rewrite with $b = h/2$: $$ t_c \;=\; \dfrac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{4}{\pi}\cdot\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_d - T_w}\right] $$ **Step 5 — The KTU-canonical form** uses $\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}$ (average-temperature form) instead of $\dfrac{4}{\pi}$ (centreline form), giving the formula listed in the cheat sheet: $$ \boxed{\,t_c \;=\; \dfrac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\right)\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_d - T_w}\,} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Clamping-Force Requirement The mold cavity pressure $P_{cav}$ is the **average pressure** acting on the projected area of the cavity $A_{proj}$ (the largest cross-section perpendicular to the clamping direction). The clamping force $F_c$ must exceed the separating force: $$ F_c \;\geq\; P_{cav}\cdot A_{proj} $$ For a typical **edge-gated single cavity**, $P_{cav}$ is estimated as $\approx 0.5$ to $0.7$ times the injection pressure $P_{inj}$, depending on flow length, melt temperature, and wall thickness. The factor is called the **cavity-pressure efficiency** $\eta_p$: $$ P_{cav} \;=\; \eta_p\,P_{inj}, \qquad \eta_p \;\approx\; 0.5 - 0.7 $$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU-Style) ### Example A — Capillary Die Pressure Drop **Given:** $Q = 2\times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$, $R = 2\,\text{mm} = 2\times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, $L = 40\,\text{mm} = 0.04\,\text{m}$, $K = 15000\,\text{Pa·s}^{n}$, $n = 0.4$. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Apparent shear rate:** $$ \dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}} = \dfrac{4(2\times 10^{-6})}{\pi(2\times 10^{-3})^{3}} = \dfrac{8\times 10^{-6}}{\pi\cdot 8\times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{10^{3}}{\pi} \approx 318.3\,\text{s}^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — Pressure drop (capillary formula):** $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{2KL}{R}\,\dot{\gamma}_a^{\,n} = \dfrac{2(15000)(0.04)}{2\times 10^{-3}}\,(318.3)^{0.4} $$ $$ = 6\times 10^{5}\times(318.3)^{0.4} $$ $$ (318.3)^{0.4} = e^{0.4\ln 318.3} = e^{0.4\times 5.763} = e^{2.305} \approx 10.03 $$ $$ \Delta P \approx 6\times 10^{5}\times 10.03 \approx 6.02\times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} \;=\; 6.02\,\text{MPa} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** $\Delta P \approx 6.0$ MPa. This is comfortably below a typical extruder's 25–35 MPa limit, so the design is **safe**. ### Example B — Cooling Time of an Injection-Molded PS Disc **Given:** $h = 3\,\text{mm} = 0.003\,\text{m}$, $\alpha = 9.2\times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$ (PS), $T_m = 220\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_w = 40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_d = 90\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (heat-distortion temperature of PS). **Solution:** $$ t_c = \dfrac{(0.003)^{2}}{\pi^{2}(9.2\times 10^{-8})}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\right)\dfrac{220-40}{90-40} $$ **Compute term by term:** $$ \dfrac{(0.003)^{2}}{\pi^{2}(9.2\times 10^{-8})} = \dfrac{9\times 10^{-6}}{9.07\times 10^{-7}} \approx 9.92\,\text{s} $$ $$ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\right) = \ln(0.8106) = -0.210 $$ $$ \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_d - T_w} = \dfrac{180}{50} = 3.6 $$ $$ t_c = 9.92\times(-0.210)\times 3.6 \;\;\text{[re-check sign and formulation]} $$ **Correction — The KTU formula uses absolute value of the logarithmic term OR a positive formulation:** The equivalent positive form is $$ t_c = \dfrac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_d - T_w}\right] $$ $$ = \dfrac{9\times 10^{-6}}{9.07\times 10^{-7}}\,\ln\!\left[0.8106\times 3.6\right] = 9.92\,\ln(2.918) = 9.92\times 1.071 \approx 10.6\,\text{s} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** $t_c \approx 10.6$ s. The cycle time will be slightly longer due to plasticating + mold-open/close; a typical 3 mm PS part cycles in ~15 s. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Polymer Processing Design ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Production Target
kg per h and Shape] --> B[Melt Rheology
Power Law K and n] B --> C[Apparent Shear Rate
gamma dot a] C --> D[Viscosity at Wall
eta wall] D --> E[Pressure Drop
delta P across die or runner] A --> F[Heat Transfer
Fourier and Cooling] F --> G[Cooling Time
t c] A --> H[Projected Area
A proj] E --> I[Clamping Force
F c] G --> J[Cycle Time
T cycle] I --> K[Machine Selection
Press tonnage] J --> K B --> L[Die Swell
B from Tanner] L --> M[Calibration Tank
and Sizing] F --> M E --> N[Defect Diagnosis
Shark skin Melt fracture Blow hole] G --> N L --> N N --> O[Design Iteration
Modify L h T w Gate] O --> B O --> F O --> H ``` ## 4.2 Shear-Rate vs Geometry Selection Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Polymer Melt] --> Q{Die Geometry} Q --> R1[Capillary
R] Q --> R2[Slit
w h] Q --> R3[Annular
R o minus R i] R1 --> S1[gamma dot a = 4Q / pi R cubed] R2 --> S2[gamma dot a = 6Q / w h squared] R3 --> S3[gamma dot a = 6Q / pi Ro plus Ri squared Ro minus Ri] S1 --> T[Pressure Drop delta P] S2 --> T S3 --> T T --> U[Compare to Extruder Limit] U --> V{Within Limit} V -- Yes --> W[Design Acceptable] V -- No --> X[Resize L or h or T] ``` ## 4.3 Cooling-Time Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Part Geometry
h, geometry] --> B0[Material Selection
alpha, T m, T d] B0 --> C0[Mold Surface Temp
T w choice] C0 --> D0[Apply Cooling Time
Equation] D0 --> E0[t c Result] E0 --> F0{Meets Cycle Target} F0 -- Yes --> G0[Approve Mold Layout] F0 -- No --> H0[Lower T w OR
Increase alpha via
Better Coolant Flow] H0 --> D0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** > - The first block diagram shows the **closed-loop interaction** of the five design pillars (Rheology, Heat Transfer, Geometry, Mechanical Force, Dimensional Stability). The arrows returning to *Melt Rheology* indicate the **iterative nature** of design — a parameter is changed and the whole analysis is re-run. > - The second diagram is a **decision tree** for selecting the correct apparent-shear-rate formula based on the chosen die geometry. This is the single most-tested concept in KTU Module 4. > - The third diagram is a **cooling-time optimisation loop** showing how $t_c$ is iteratively reduced by lowering $T_w$ or improving the heat-transfer coefficient. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State the Ostwald–de Waele Power-Law model for a polymer melt and explain the physical significance of the flow-behaviour index $n$.** **Model Answer:** The Power Law (Ostwald–de Waele) model is $$ \eta \;=\; K\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} $$ where $K$ is the **consistency index** (Pa·sⁿ) and $n$ is the **flow-behaviour index** (dimensionless). - If $n = 1$ → Newtonian fluid with $\eta = K$. - If $n < 1$ → **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)**, which is the case for virtually all thermoplastics in the processing range. - If $n > 1$ → **shear-thickening (dilatant)**, rare in polymer melts. The lower the value of $n$, the **more pronounced** the viscosity drop with increasing shear rate, which is exploited in processing to reduce $\Delta P$ at high throughputs. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define "apparent shear rate" and write its expression for (i) a circular capillary die and (ii) a slit die.** **Model Answer:** The **apparent shear rate** is the shear rate computed from the volumetric flow rate assuming a Newtonian velocity profile; it differs from the **true wall shear rate** for non-Newtonian fluids by the Rabinowitsch factor. (i) **Capillary (circular) die** of radius $R$: $$ \dot{\gamma}_a \;=\; \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}} $$ (ii) **Slit die** of width $w$ and gap $h$: $$ \dot{\gamma}_a \;=\; \dfrac{6Q}{w\,h^{2}} $$ ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **volumetric flow rate** of a Power-Law fluid through a circular capillary die of radius $R$ and length $L$ under a pressure drop $\Delta P$. **(b) [7 Marks]** A polypropylene melt is extruded through a circular die of $R = 3$ mm and $L = 50$ mm. The Power-Law constants at the operating temperature are $K = 18000$ Pa·sⁿ and $n = 0.35$. If the mass flow rate is 5 kg/h and the melt density is 750 kg/m³, compute (i) the apparent shear rate, (ii) the pressure drop across the die, and (iii) the wall shear stress. State whether the design is acceptable for an extruder with a maximum pressure limit of 25 MPa. #### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):** **Starting point:** The shear-stress distribution is obtained from a force balance on a cylindrical element of radius $r$: $$ \Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} \;=\; \tau_{rz}\cdot 2\pi r L \;\Longrightarrow\; \tau_{rz} = \dfrac{r\,\Delta P}{2L} $$ **Power-Law constitutive equation:** $$ \tau_{rz} \;=\; K\left(-\dfrac{du_z}{dr}\right)^{n} \;\Longrightarrow\; -\dfrac{du_z}{dr} = \left(\dfrac{r\,\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n} $$ **Integrate from $r$ to $R$ with $u_z(R) = 0$:** $$ u_z(r) = \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}\dfrac{n}{n+1}\left(R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n}\right) $$ **Integrate to get volumetric flow rate:** $$ Q = \int_0^R u_z(r)\, 2\pi r\, dr = \pi\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}\dfrac{n}{3n+1}R^{(3n+1)/n} $$ $$ \boxed{\;Q = \dfrac{\pi n}{3n+1}\,R^{3}\!\left(\dfrac{R\,\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}\;} $$ #### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):** **(i) Apparent shear rate:** $$ Q = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{5}{750\times 3600} = 1.852\times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{3}\text{/s} $$ $$ \dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}} = \dfrac{4(1.852\times 10^{-6})}{\pi(3\times 10^{-3})^{3}} = \dfrac{7.408\times 10^{-6}}{\pi\cdot 2.7\times 10^{-8}} \approx 87.3\,\text{s}^{-1} $$ **(ii) Pressure drop:** $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{2KL}{R}\dot{\gamma}_a^{\,n} = \dfrac{2(18000)(0.05)}{3\times 10^{-3}}\,(87.3)^{0.35} $$ $$ = 6\times 10^{5}\times(87.3)^{0.35} $$ $$ (87.3)^{0.35} = e^{0.35\ln 87.3} = e^{0.35\times 4.470} = e^{1.5645} \approx 4.78 $$ $$ \Delta P \approx 6\times 10^{5}\times 4.78 \approx 2.87\times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} = 2.87\,\text{MPa} $$ **(iii) Wall shear stress:** $$ \tau_w = K\,\dot{\gamma}_a^{\,n} = 18000\times 4.78 \approx 8.6\times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa} = 86\,\text{kPa} $$ **Design check:** $\Delta P = 2.87$ MPa $\ll$ 25 MPa limit → **Design is ACCEPTABLE**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a clear sketch, describe the various **zones of an injection-mold cavity** (feed system, gate, cavity, cooling channel) and explain the **design considerations** for each zone. **(b) [7 Marks]** An injection-molded HDPE container has a maximum wall thickness of $h = 4$ mm. The melt temperature is $T_m = 230\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, the mold-wall temperature is $T_w = 30\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, and the heat-distortion temperature is $T_d = 80\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. The thermal diffusivity of HDPE is $\alpha = 1.3\times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$. Estimate the **cooling time** using the simplified KTU formula. If the projected area of the cavity is $A_{proj} = 250\,\text{cm}^{2}$ and the cavity pressure is $P_{cav} = 30$ MPa, compute the **clamping force** required. #### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):** A typical injection mold has **four functional zones**, each with distinct design considerations: | Zone | Function | Design Considerations | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Feed System (Sprue + Runner)** | Channel melt from nozzle to gate | Diameter, length, layout (balanced vs unbalanced), cold-slug-well design, surface finish, draft angle. | | **Gate** | Final restriction before cavity; controls flow rate, packing, and shear | Gate type (edge, fan, tab, tunnel, hot-runner), gate size (controls $P_{cav}$ and weld-line position), gate location (flow length symmetry). | | **Cavity** | Defines the part geometry | Wall-thickness uniformity (avoids sink marks and warpage), draft angles (1–2°), surface finish, ejection layout, shrinkage compensation (1.5–3 % for HDPE). | | **Cooling System** | Removes heat to solidify the part | Channel diameter (8–12 mm), channel spacing (3–5 × wall thickness), coolant temperature and flow rate (Reynolds > 10 000 for turbulent flow), baffles/conduits for uniform surface cooling. | **Additional considerations:** venting (depth 0.02–0.05 mm), ejector-pin layout, parting-line location, and mould-material selection (P-20, H-13, S-7). #### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):** **Cooling time:** $$ t_c = \dfrac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_d - T_w}\right] $$ **Numerical substitution:** $$ \dfrac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha} = \dfrac{(4\times 10^{-3})^{2}}{\pi^{2}(1.3\times 10^{-7})} = \dfrac{1.6\times 10^{-5}}{1.283\times 10^{-6}} \approx 12.47\,\text{s} $$ $$ \dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_d - T_w} = 0.8106\times\dfrac{230-30}{80-30} = 0.8106\times 4 = 3.242 $$ $$ \ln(3.242) = 1.176 $$ $$ \boxed{\;t_c \;\approx\; 12.47\times 1.176 \;\approx\; 14.7\,\text{s}\;} $$ **Clamping force:** $$ F_c = P_{cav}\cdot A_{proj} = 30\times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}\times 250\times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2} $$ $$ F_c = 30\times 10^{6}\times 2.5\times 10^{-2} = 7.5\times 10^{5}\,\text{N} \;=\; 750\,\text{kN} \;\approx\; 77\,\text{tonnes} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the density conversion** when the problem gives *mass* flow rate (kg/h) instead of volumetric flow rate. Always convert $\dot{m} \to Q = \dot{m}/\rho$ first. > 2. **Unit mismatches in the cooling-time formula.** $h$ MUST be in metres, $\alpha$ in m²/s. A common error is using $h$ in mm and forgetting the $10^{-3}$ factor — this shifts $t_c$ by a factor of $10^{6}$. > 3. **Mixing the $4/\pi$ and $8/\pi^{2}$ constants.** The $4/\pi$ form gives the **centreline** cooling time; the $8/\pi^{2}$ form gives the **volume-averaged** cooling time. The KTU Module 4 question bank almost always uses the $8/\pi^{2}$ form. > 4. **Ignoring the Rabinowitsch correction.** If a question specifically asks for the *true* wall shear rate, multiply $\dot{\gamma}_a$ by $(3n+1)/(4n)$. > 5. **In shrinkage calculations,** students often confuse *linear* and *volumetric* shrinkage. Linear shrinkage $S_L$ is converted to mould-cavity oversize by $L_{cavity} = L_{part}/(1 - S_L)$. > 6. **Clamping-force problems** — remember to use the *projected* area (largest cross-section perpendicular to clamping direction), not the *surface* area of the part. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Theoretical and Design Considerations"** - ✅ The **Power-Law model** $\eta = K\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ is the workhorse of all KTU design problems. Memorise it. - ✅ For a **circular capillary** of radius $R$: $\dot{\gamma}_a = 4Q/(\pi R^{3})$; $\Delta P = (2KL/R)\,\dot{\gamma}_a^{n}$. - ✅ For a **slit die** of gap $h$, width $w$: $\dot{\gamma}_a = 6Q/(w h^{2})$; $\Delta P = (2KL/h)\,\dot{\gamma}_a^{n}$. - ✅ For an **annular die** of outer and inner radii $R_o, R_i$: $\dot{\gamma}_a = 6Q/[\pi (R_o+R_i)^{2}(R_o-R_i)]$. - ✅ **Die swell (Tanner):** $B = D_e/D_0 = [2 - 1/n]^{n/6}$. Calibrate the haul-off / take-up accordingly. - ✅ **Cooling time (KTU canonical):** $t_c = \dfrac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_d - T_w}\right]$. Always use SI units. - ✅ **Clamping force:** $F_c = P_{cav}\cdot A_{proj} = \eta_p\,P_{inj}\cdot A_{proj}$, with $\eta_p \approx 0.5 - 0.7$. - ✅ **Flow regime:** Polymer-melt flow in dies and runners is **almost always laminar** ($Re \ll 2100$); do NOT use the Moody chart or friction-factor correlations. - ✅ **Design is iterative.** A change in $h$, $L$, $T_w$, or material affects $\Delta P$, $t_c$, $F_c$, and $B$ simultaneously. Always re-validate the whole set. - ✅ **High-yield numerical values to memorise:** $\ln(8/\pi^{2}) \approx -0.210$; $\eta_p$(average) $\approx 0.6$; typical mould-wall temperature rise permitted = 1–2 °C; typical HDPE linear shrinkage = 1.5–3 %. - ✅ **KTU Hot Words** to use in answers: *isothermal, steady, fully-developed, laminar, non-Newtonian, shear-thinning, Rabinowitsch correction, apparent shear rate, true shear rate, cavity-pressure efficiency, thermal diffusivity, heat-distortion temperature, projected area, volumetric flow rate, draw-down ratio, draw ratio balance.*

topics.title: General Mould Design Consideration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** General Mould Design Consideration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Mould Design Consideration** refers to the systematic engineering methodology that integrates part geometry analysis, material behaviour prediction, thermal management, mechanical actuation, and manufacturability constraints to design a precision tooling system (the **mould**) capable of producing polymer components consistently at production volumes with acceptable quality, cost, and cycle time.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with NEP 2020 outcomes, mould design is treated as a **multi-objective optimization problem** where the designer must simultaneously satisfy:

1. **Dimensional accuracy** (tolerances, shrinkage compensation)
2. **Aesthetic quality** (surface finish, weld line control)
3. **Production economy** (cycle time, mould life)
4. **Manufacturability** (machinability, assembly)
5. **Functional performance** of the moulded part

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Waffle Iron Principle:** Think of a polymer mould exactly like a sophisticated **industrial waffle iron**. The molten polymer (batter) is injected (poured) into a precisely shaped cavity (the waffle grid). It cools and solidifies under controlled conditions (heat transfer). The mould then opens (the iron hinges open) and the part is ejected (waffle lifted out). The key difference: a waffle iron makes one shape repeatedly, while a polymer mould must be designed to release a complex 3D geometry without damage, do this millions of times, and maintain micron-level precision throughout its life.

### Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Mould Design

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cavity Pressure | $P_c$ | **20 – 100** | MPa |
| Mould Temperature | $T_{mould}$ | **20 – 200** | °C |
| Clamping Tonnage | $F_{clamp}$ | **50 – 4000** | tonnes (kN) |
| Part Shrinkage | $S$ | **0.2 – 3.0** | % |
| Draft Angle | $\alpha$ | **0.5° – 2°** | degrees |
| Mould Life | $N$ | **10⁵ – 10⁷** | shots/cycles |

### The Five Pillars of Mould Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Pillars of Mould Design (KTU Syllabus Core):**
> 1. **Cavity & Core Geometry** — defines the part shape
> 2. **Gating & Runner System** — controls melt flow path
> 3. **Cooling / Heating System** — governs cycle time
> 4. **Ejection System** — ensures part release without damage
> 5. **Parting Line & Venting** — enables mould separation and air evacuation
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework for Mould Design

**Pillar 1 — Cavity and Core Design**

The cavity (female half) and core (male half) together form the part envelope. Design considerations include:
- **Shrinkage allowance:** Mould dimension = Part dimension × (1 + S)
- **Draft angle ($\alpha$):** Taper on vertical walls to allow part release. Typically **1°** for depth up to 25 mm, **0.5°** beyond.
- **Wall thickness uniformity:** Variations cause **sink marks**, **voids**, and **warpage**.
- **Corner radii:** Sharp corners cause stress concentration → use fillet radius $\geq$ **0.5 × wall thickness**.
- **Undercuts:** Must be avoided unless side-action (slider/lifter) mechanisms are incorporated.

**Pillar 2 — Gating System Design**

The gate is the narrowest restriction between the runner and the cavity. It controls:
- Fill rate
- Pack pressure transmission
- Gate vestige (mark left on part)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gate Location Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):** Place gate at the **thickest section** of the part to ensure proper packing, minimize sink marks, and allow melt flow from thick → thin regions (avoids jetting and premature freezing).

**Pillar 3 — Cooling System Design**

Cooling channels remove heat from the moulded part. Heat must flow:
1. From molten polymer → mould cavity surface (conduction)
2. From cavity surface → cooling channel wall (conduction through mould steel)
3. From channel wall → coolant (convection)

Cooling time typically governs the overall cycle time. Standard channels are **Ø6 mm to Ø12 mm**, spaced **3D to 5D** apart (D = channel diameter), located at a distance of **1D to 3D** from cavity surface.

**Pillar 4 — Ejection System**

Ejector pins, sleeves, or stripper plates push the solidified part out of the mould. Ejection force:
$$F_{ej} = \mu \cdot N_{normal}$$
where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction (polymer on steel ≈ **0.2 – 0.4**) and $N_{normal}$ is the normal force from part shrinkage gripping the core.

**Pillar 5 — Parting Line & Venting**

The **parting line** is the boundary where the two mould halves meet. It must be positioned:
- At the maximum cross-section (easier mould closing)
- Along edges (avoids visible flash on aesthetic surfaces)
- Perpendicular to the draw direction

**Vents** are shallow channels (**0.02 – 0.05 mm deep**) that allow trapped air to escape. Without proper venting, air compression causes **diesel effect** (ignition), **short shots**, and **burn marks**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_m = L_p (1 + S)$ | Mould dimension from part dimension & shrinkage | mm |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{L_m - L_p}{L_m} \times 100\%$ | Volumetric / linear shrinkage definition | % |
| 3 | $F_{clamp} \geq P_{avg} \cdot A_{proj}$ | Required clamping tonnage | kN |
| 4 | $P_{avg} \approx 0.5 \cdot P_{inj}$ | Average cavity pressure ≈ ½ injection pressure | MPa |
| 5 | $V_{inj} = n \cdot V_{part} + V_{runner}$ | Total injection volume (n = cavities) | cm³ |
| 6 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_p} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_e - T_{wall}}\right)$ | Simplified cooling time (Chung & Stevenson) | s |
| 7 | $F_{ej} = \mu \cdot p \cdot A_{contact}$ | Ejection force (sticking friction) | N |
| 8 | $d_{runner} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{4 \cdot \dot{m}}{\pi \cdot \rho \cdot v}}$ | Runner diameter (mass flow continuity) | mm |
| 9 | $t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{eject} + t_{open/close}$ | Total cycle time | s |
| 10 | $Q = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{h \cdot A_{channel}}$ | Coolant flow rate (heat balance) | L/min |
| 11 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot n \cdot V_{part} / t_{cycle}$ | Required shot mass rate | kg/s |
| 12 | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho_c v D}{\mu_c}$ | Reynolds number for coolant flow | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** For shrinkage problems, **always use the part dimension as reference**, then compute the mould dimension. For injection pressure, remember the rule: $P_{inj} \approx 2 \times P_{cavity}$ for filled parts.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mould design considerations are the bedrock of:
- **Automotive industry:** Bumpers, dashboards, instrument panels
- **Medical devices:** Syringes, IV components (require ultra-clean moulds)
- **Consumer electronics:** Phone housings, laptop shells (tight tolerances, aesthetic finish)
- **Packaging:** Bottle caps, closures (high cavitation, fast cycles)
- **Aerospace:** Composite preforms, structural polymer parts
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shrinkage Compensation

**Given:** A polypropylene (PP) part must have a length of $L_p = 100.0$ mm at room temperature. The volumetric shrinkage of PP is known to be $S = 1.8\%$.

**Find:** The required mould cavity dimension $L_m$.

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental shrinkage relation.**
The shrinkage is defined as the relative change in dimension from mould to part:

$$S = \frac{L_m - L_p}{L_m} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $L_m$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \cdot L_m &= 100 \cdot (L_m - L_p) \\[4pt]
0.018 \cdot L_m &= L_m - L_p \\[4pt]
L_m - 0.018 \cdot L_m &= L_p \\[4pt]
L_m (1 - 0.018) &= L_p \\[4pt]
L_m \cdot 0.982 &= L_p \\[4pt]
L_m &= \frac{L_p}{0.982} = \frac{L_p}{1 - S/100}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_m &= \frac{100.0 \text{ mm}}{0.982} \\[4pt]
L_m &= 101.83 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The mould cavity must be machined to **$L_m = 101.83$ mm** so that the part shrinks to the target **100.0 mm** after cooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The shrinkage value used in design should be the **mould shrinkage** (measured on an ISO test bar under standard processing conditions), not the textbook thermodynamic free-shrinkage, because processing conditions significantly affect final part dimensions.

### 3.2 Derivation: Clamping Force Requirement

**Given:** A 2-cavity mould produces rectangular plates of $120 \text{ mm} \times 80 \text{ mm} \times 3 \text{ mm}$. The runner system has a projected area of $2500 \text{ mm}^2$. Average cavity pressure is $P_{avg} = 40$ MPa.

**Find:** Required clamping tonnage $F_{clamp}$.

**Step 1 — Compute projected area of the part per cavity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{part} &= L \times W = 120 \text{ mm} \times 80 \text{ mm} = 9600 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total projected area (2 cavities + runner).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{proj} &= 2 \cdot A_{part} + A_{runner} \\[4pt]
A_{proj} &= 2(9600) + 2500 = 21{,}700 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to consistent SI units.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{proj} &= 21{,}700 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 0.0217 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply clamping force equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= P_{avg} \cdot A_{proj} \\[4pt]
F_{clamp} &= 40 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \times 0.0217 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
F_{clamp} &= 868{,}000 \text{ N} = 868 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to tonnes (1 tonne-force ≈ 9.81 kN).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= \frac{868}{9.81} \approx 88.5 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A machine of at least **100 tonnes** nominal capacity must be selected (always include 10–15% safety margin).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Pattern:** KTU examiners commonly give projected area, cavity count, and average pressure — then ask for tonnage. Remember the unit conversion chain: **MPa × mm² = N**, then **N ÷ 1000 = kN**, then **kN ÷ 9.81 = tonnes**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Cooling Time Estimate (Chung–Stevenson Approximation)

**Given:** A polystyrene moulding has wall thickness $h = 3$ mm. Melt temperature $T_m = 220°\text{C}$, ejection temperature $T_e = 90°\text{C}$, mould wall temperature $T_{wall} = 40°\text{C}$. Thermal diffusivity of PS $\alpha_p = 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

**Find:** Approximate cooling time $t_{cool}$.

**Step 1 — Write the Chung–Stevenson cooling time correlation.**

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_p} \ln\!\left( \frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_e - T_{wall}} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the thickness-squared prefactor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_p} &= \frac{(3 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-7}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 9.12 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature ratio inside the logarithm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_e - T_{wall}} &= \frac{8}{9.8696} \cdot \frac{220 - 40}{90 - 40} \\[4pt]
&= 0.8106 \cdot \frac{180}{50} \\[4pt]
&= 0.8106 \times 3.6 = 2.918
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural log and multiply.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cool} &= 9.12 \times \ln(2.918) \\[4pt]
&= 9.12 \times 1.0716 \\[4pt]
&\approx 9.77 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Approximate cooling time is **≈ 9.8 seconds**, which dominates the overall cycle time.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Mould Design Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Effect on Cycle Time | Effect on Part Quality | Effect on Mould Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| More cooling channels | ↓ Faster cooling | ↑ Better dimensional control | ↑ Higher machining cost |
| Hot runner system | ↓ Eliminates runner cooling | ↑ Less waste, better gate quality | ↑↑ Much higher mould cost |
| Draft angle increase | ↑ Easier ejection | ↓ May affect tolerance | ↔ Negligible |
| Smaller gate | ↓ Faster freeze | ↑ Better gate vestige | ↔ Negligible |
| More ejector pins | ↔ Slight increase | ↓ Reduces part stress | ↑ More machining |
| Beryllium copper inserts | ↑ Faster local cooling | ↑ Reduced warpage | ↑ Material cost |
| Conformal cooling (3D printed) | ↓↓ Major reduction | ↑ Uniform cooling | ↑↑↑ Very high initial cost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Two-Plate Injection Mould

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Injection Unit\nNozzle & Sprue] --> B[Sprue Bushing\nChannel to Runner]
    B --> C[Runner System\nCold Runner Network]
    C --> D[Gate\nNarrow Restriction]
    D --> E[Cavity & Core\nPart Geometry]
    E --> F[Cooling Channels\nWater/Oil Circulation]
    E --> G[Vents\nAir Evacuation]
    E --> H[Parting Line\nMould Halves Interface]
    H --> I[Ejector Pins\nPart Release]
    I --> J[Moulded Part\nFinished Component]

    subgraph SUPPORT[Support Systems]
        K[Guide Pillars & Bushes] --> H
        L[Clamping Platens] --> H
        M[Coolant Manifold] --> F
    end
```

### 4.2 Logical Flow of Mould Design Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Part Drawing &\nSpecifications] --> P1[Material Selection\n& Shrinkage Data]
    P1 --> P2[Shrinkage Compensation\nCavity Sizing]
    P2 --> P3[Parting Line\nSelection]
    P3 --> P4[Gate Location &\nType Selection]
    P4 --> P5[Runner System\nLayout]
    P5 --> P6[Cooling System\nDesign]
    P6 --> P7[Ejection System\nDesign]
    P7 --> P8[Venting & Draft\nAngle Application]
    P8 --> P9[Clamping Force\nCalculation]
    P9 --> P10[Mould Material\nSelection]
    P10 --> P11[Final Mould\nDrawings & BOM]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow of Polymer inside the Mould

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Melting in\nInjection Unit] --> A2[Injection through\nSprue]
    A2 --> A3[Splitting into\nRunner Branches]
    A3 --> A4[Gate\nRestriction]
    A4 --> A5[Cavity\nFilling]
    A5 --> A6[Pressure\nPacking Phase]
    A6 --> A7[Cooling &\nSolidification]
    A7 --> A8[Part Ejection\n& Mould Opens]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Design Considerations Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[Mould Design\nConsiderations]

    TOP --> T1[Part Geometry]
    TOP --> T2[Material Behaviour]
    TOP --> T3[Process Parameters]
    TOP --> T4[Production Volume]

    T1 --> B1[Wall Thickness\nUniformity]
    T1 --> B2[Draft Angles\n& Undercuts]

    T2 --> C1[Shrinkage\nCompensation]
    T2 --> C2[Viscosity &\nFlow Length]

    T3 --> D1[Injection Pressure\n& Clamping Force]
    T3 --> D2[Cooling Time\n& Cycle Optimisation]

    T4 --> E1[Number of\nCavities]
    T4 --> E2[Mould Material\n& Life Expectancy]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is **draft angle** in mould design? Why is it necessary, and what happens if it is omitted?

> **Model Answer:**
> A draft angle is a small taper (typically **0.5° to 2°**) applied to all vertical walls of a mould cavity/core in the direction of mould opening.
> **Necessity:** It allows the moulded part to release cleanly from the core without scraping, scratching, or jamming. As the polymer cools, it grips the steel tightly due to shrinkage; a draft angle converts the release from sliding friction to near-zero-perpendicular separation.
> **If omitted:** The part will resist ejection, leading to surface damage (drag marks), part distortion, increased ejection force, mould wear, and in severe cases, the part will be permanently stuck inside the cavity, causing production stoppage.

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between a **two-plate mould** and a **three-plate mould** with respect to gating and runner system.

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Feature | Two-Plate Mould | Three-Plate Mould |
> |---|---|---|
> | Number of parting lines | 1 | 2 |
> | Runner location | Within one plate | Stripper plate separates runner from part |
> | Gate type | Edge / side gate | Pin-point / submarine gate |
> | Number of cavities | Limited (usually 1–4) | Can be 4, 8, 16, 32+ |
> | Runner separation | Manual (re-grind) | Automatic ejection |
> | Cycle time | Shorter | Slightly longer |
> | Cost | Lower | Higher |
> | Automation suitability | Lower | Higher (cold runner drops out) |

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]** 

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **main components of a two-plate injection mould** and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mould produces a $150 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm} \times 4 \text{ mm}$ ABS rectangular plate. The mould has **4 cavities** with a runner projected area of $3000 \text{ mm}^2$. The material has a mould shrinkage of **0.6%** and the average cavity pressure is **50 MPa**. Calculate:
   1. The required mould cavity dimension along the 150 mm direction.
   2. The required clamping tonnage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two-plate mould components (7 marks):**

1. **Cavity & Core (A and B plates):** Form the part geometry. Cavity = female half, Core = male half.
2. **Sprue bushing:** Guides melt from the machine nozzle into the mould.
3. **Runner system:** Distributes melt from sprue to each cavity.
4. **Gate:** Restricts flow and controls packing pressure.
5. **Cooling channels:** Circulate water/oil to remove heat.
6. **Ejector pins/plates:** Push the solidified part out.
7. **Guide pillars & bushes:** Align the two mould halves during closing.
8. **Parting line surface:** Where the two plates meet; flash may form here.
9. **Support plate & backing plate:** Provide structural rigidity.
10. **Vents:** Allow air to escape during cavity filling.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mould dimension along 150 mm direction (shrinkage compensation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_m &= \frac{L_p}{1 - S/100} = \frac{150}{1 - 0.006} = \frac{150}{0.994} \\[4pt]
L_m &= 150.906 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Projected area of one part:**

$$
A_{part} = 150 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm} = 15{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Total projected area (4 cavities + runner):**

$$
A_{proj} = 4(15{,}000) + 3{,}000 = 63{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.063 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Clamping force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= P_{avg} \times A_{proj} = 50 \times 10^6 \times 0.063 \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}150{,}000 \text{ N} = 3150 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to tonnes:**

$$
F_{clamp} = \frac{3150}{9.81} \approx 321.1 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Final Answer:** A machine of at least **350 tonnes** nominal capacity (with safety margin) must be selected.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **gating system in injection moulds**. Discuss different types of gates with neat sketches and their applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular disc of diameter 80 mm and thickness 2.5 mm is moulded from polypropylene. Calculate:
   1. The required cooling time using the Chung–Stevenson equation.
   2. The ejection force, given that the part sticks to the core with a normal stress of 1.5 MPa and coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$.
   
   **Data:** Melt temp = $230°\text{C}$, ejection temp = $100°\text{C}$, mould wall temp = $50°\text{C}$, thermal diffusivity $\alpha_p = 9.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Gating system (7 marks):**

The gating system delivers melt from the runner to the cavity and controls fill rate, packing, and gate quality. **Types of gates:**

1. **Edge / Side gate:** Rectangular, located at the part edge. Most common. Easy to machine and trim.
2. **Pin-point gate:** Small circular opening (Ø0.5–1.5 mm). Used in three-plate moulds. Leaves a small vestige.
3. **Submarine (tunnel) gate:** Curved channel that automatically shears off the gate vestige.
4. **Diaphragm gate:** Circular ring around a central core; used for tubular parts to avoid weld lines.
5. **Fan gate:** Wide, thin rectangular opening; used for wide, flat parts requiring uniform fill.
6. **Tab gate:** Small tab attached to the part; gate vestige is removed with the tab.

**Application selection rule:** Pin-point for cosmetic parts; edge gate for general purpose; fan gate for flat panels; diaphragm for tubes; submarine for automatic de-gating.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Cooling time via Chung–Stevenson:**

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_p} \ln\!\left( \frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_e - T_{wall}} \right)
$$

Thickness $h = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

Prefactor:

$$
\frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_p} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi^2 \times 9.0 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{8.882 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 7.04 \text{ s}
$$

Logarithm argument:

$$
\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{230 - 50}{100 - 50} = 0.8106 \times \frac{180}{50} = 0.8106 \times 3.6 = 2.918
$$

Final:

$$
t_{cool} = 7.04 \times \ln(2.918) = 7.04 \times 1.0716 \approx 7.54 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Ejection force:**

The part grips the core over its full disc area:

$$
A_{contact} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Ejection force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ej} &= \mu \cdot p \cdot A_{contact} \\[4pt]
&= 0.3 \times 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
&= 2262 \text{ N} \approx 2.26 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Cooling time ≈ **7.5 s**, Ejection force ≈ **2.26 kN**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors are the #1 reason students lose marks** in numerical problems. Always write: MPa × mm² = N → ÷ 1000 = kN → ÷ 9.81 = tonnes. Show every step.
> 2. **Shrinkage formula direction confusion:** KTU boards consistently use $L_m = L_p / (1 - S/100)$. Writing $L_m = L_p(1+S)$ is incorrect because $S$ is a percentage expressed as a decimal.
> 3. **Forgetting runner projected area** in clamping force calculation: The runner system also resists mould opening. Omitting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **No sketch in Part B (a):** A labelled component diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in design questions. Verbal description alone gets at most 50% marks.
> 5. **Ejection force sign error:** Forgetting $\mu$ or using the wrong contact area (using volume instead of surface area).
> 6. **Cooling channel placement rule forgotten:** Examiners love asking "why spacing should be 3D–5D?" — answer: too close = uneven cooling, too far = hot spots and warpage.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for General Mould Design Consideration:**
>
> ✅ Mould dimension always **includes shrinkage compensation** — $L_m = L_p/(1 - S)$
> ✅ Average cavity pressure ≈ **½ injection pressure** for short-shots avoidance analysis
> ✅ Clamping force = **Average pressure × Total projected area** (parts + runners)
> ✅ Draft angle: **1° for shallow parts, 2° for deep, textured, or glass-filled resins**
> ✅ Vents: **0.02–0.05 mm deep**, located at the **last-to-fill** points
> ✅ Cooling channel diameter **Ø6–12 mm**, placed **1D–3D from cavity surface**, spaced **3D–5D apart**
> ✅ Gate location should be at the **thickest section** to minimize sink marks
> ✅ Ejection force = $\mu \cdot p \cdot A$ — large area + high friction = risk of part damage
> ✅ Two-plate mould = 1 parting line; Three-plate = 2 parting lines + auto runner separation
> ✅ Mould materials: P20, H13, S7 for cavities; beryllium copper for high-heat-removal inserts
> ✅ Mould life: aluminium ≈ 10⁵, P20 steel ≈ 5×10⁵, H13 hardened ≈ 10⁶–10⁷ shots
> ✅ Parting line placed on **draft direction** and at **maximum cross-section** to minimize flash
> ✅ **Weld lines** occur when two flow fronts meet — relocate gate to avoid them on cosmetic surfaces
> ✅ **Sink marks** = non-uniform wall thickness — redesign part, not mould
> ✅ Hot runner = **no cold runner waste**, lower cycle time, much higher mould cost
> ✅ Cooling time (Chung–Stevenson) ≈ $h^2/(\pi^2 \alpha_p) \cdot \ln(0.81 \cdot (T_m-T_w)/(T_e-T_w))$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Troubleshooting
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Troubleshooting

# 🛠️ Troubleshooting in Polymer Processing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Troubleshooting in Polymer Processing** is the systematic, scientific methodology of **identifying, diagnosing, and rectifying process-related defects and abnormalities** that occur during primary and secondary polymer conversion operations (injection molding, extrusion, blow molding, thermoforming, compression molding, rotational molding, and calendering) by correlating the **observable defect signature** with the **root cause** lying in one or more of the **4M+E pillars** — *Material, Machine, Method, Manpower, and Environment*.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, troubleshooting is classified under the cognitive domain of **CO-4 (Apply)** and **CO-5 (Analyze & Evaluate)** of PCPOT502, requiring students to interpret defect manifestations and prescribe corrective actions based on the **Cause–Effect–Remedy (CER) framework**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Doctor-Patient" Analogy for Polymer Defects**
>
> Imagine a polymer product as a **patient visiting a doctor**. The **defect** (sink mark, flash, warpage) is the **symptom** (fever, cough). The doctor does NOT immediately prescribe medicine — instead, the doctor:
> 1. **Observes the symptom** (looks at the defect)
> 2. **Orders diagnostic tests** (checks melt temperature, pressure, cooling time)
> 3. **Identifies the root cause** (low injection pressure, improper cooling)
> 4. **Prescribes a remedy** (increase pressure, extend cooling time)
> 5. **Monitors recovery** (re-runs the part and inspects)
>
> Polymer troubleshooting is **exactly the same workflow** — never treat the symptom, always diagnose the **root cause** within the **Material–Machine–Method** triangle.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Polymer Defects

> [!NOTE]
> **Every polymer defect originates from a deviation in one of three categories:**
>
> - **Material (M)** → Wrong grade, contamination, moisture, degraded polymer, improper additive dispersion, incorrect MFI/MFR.
> - **Machine (M)** → Worn screw/barrel, defective heater band, faulty thermocouple, clogged nozzle, improper mold temperature control, hydraulic leaks.
> - **Method (M)** → Incorrect temperature profile, wrong injection speed/pressure, insufficient cooling time, improper mold design, poor venting.
>
> The **3M Rule** is the foundational heuristic every KTU examiner expects students to invoke when answering troubleshooting questions.

### 1.4 Key Terminology with Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard Unit / Metric |
|------|------------|------------------------|
| **Defect** | Any deviation from the specified part quality standard | Categorical (Accept/Reject) |
| **Sink Mark** | Localized surface depression on a thick section | Depth in **mm** (typically > **0.05 mm**) |
| **Flash (Burr)** | Excess material escaping from parting line | Thickness in **mm** |
| **Warpage** | Out-of-plane distortion after ejection | Deflection in **mm** (per length in mm) |
| **Short Shot** | Incomplete filling of mold cavity | % cavity filled |
| **Reject Rate (Scrap Rate)** | Proportion of defective parts in total production | **%** (Industry benchmark: < **2%**) |
| **Cycle Time Deviation** | Difference between set and actual cycle | **seconds (s)** |
| **Melt Temperature Window** | Optimal processing temperature range | **°C** (material-specific) |
| **Cooling Time** | Time for part to solidify in mold | **seconds (s)** |
| **Drying Temperature** | Required hopper drying for hygroscopic polymers | **°C** (e.g., PA66 at **80°C** for **4–6 hrs**) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Polymer Defects (KTU High-Yield Categorization)

Polymer processing defects are systematically classified into **three major families** based on their origin and visual manifestation:

### A. Surface Defects (Cosmetic / Aesthetic Family)

> [!NOTE]
> Surface defects affect the **visual and tactile quality** of the part without necessarily compromising mechanical integrity. They are the **most frequently asked category** in KTU university examinations.

| Defect | Visual Signature | Root Cause Domain |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Sink Marks** | Localized depression over ribs/bosses | Insufficient packing pressure, thick section |
| **Voids** | Internal bubble (visible in translucent parts) | Volumetric shrinkage without compensation |
| **Silver Streaks** | Long silver-white lines on surface | Moisture in resin, degradation, trapped air |
| **Flow Marks / Flow Lines** | Wavy patterns following flow path | Low melt temperature, slow injection speed |
| **Weld Lines (Knit Lines)** | Line where two melt fronts meet | Insufficient mold temperature, poor venting |
| **Burn Marks (Diesel Effect)** | Black/brown streaks at end of fill | Trapped air, excessive injection speed |
| **Splay / Splash** | Splash-pattern marks near gate | Moisture, contamination, excessive shear |
| **Gloss Variation** | Uneven surface sheen | Uneven mold temperature, inconsistent melt temp |
| **Frosting / Hazing** | Whitish, dull surface layer | Mold surface contamination, low mold temp |

### B. Dimensional Defects (Geometric / Form Family)

| Defect | Signature | Root Cause Domain |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Warpage** | Twisting, bending, dimensional out-of-tolerance | Uneven cooling, internal stress, fiber orientation |
| **Flash** | Thin excess at parting line, ejector pins | Excessive clamp tonnage imbalance, worn mold |
| **Short Shot** | Incomplete part, missing features | Insufficient injection pressure, frozen gate, venting |
| **Dimensional Shrinkage** | Part smaller than mold cavity | High mold temp, high melt temp, inadequate packing |
| **Oversize Part** | Part larger than specification | Over-packing, low mold temp, insufficient cooling |

### C. Structural / Mechanical Defects (Integrity Family)

| Defect | Signature | Root Cause Domain |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Weld Line Weakness** | Low strength at weld line | Cold melt meeting, poor fusion, moisture |
| **Voids & Bubbles** | Internal cavities (CT-detectable) | Inadequate packing, gas entrapment |
| **Delamination** | Layered peeling on surface | Contamination, incompatible polymer blend |
| **Brittleness** | Cracking under low stress | Degradation, high MFI shift, moisture |
| **Residual Stress / Cracking** | Stress cracking, Crazing | Excessive shear, internal stress, chemical attack |
| **Jetting** | Snake-like, coiled surface pattern | Incorrect gate design, low melt temp |

## 2.2 The Cause-Effect-Remedy (CER) Analytical Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CER Framework** is the gold-standard analytical tool KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers. Every defect question must be answered using this 3-column structure.

$$
\text{Defect}_{\text{observed}} \xrightarrow{\text{Diagnose}} \text{Cause}_{\text{root}} \xrightarrow{\text{Prescribe}} \text{Remedy}_{\text{corrective}}
$$

**Universal Troubleshooting Decision Tree Logic:**

1. **Step 1 — Visual Identification**: Classify the defect (Surface / Dimensional / Structural).
2. **Step 2 — Frequency & Location Analysis**: Is the defect **scattered randomly** (→ Material issue) or **consistent in one location** (→ Machine/Mold issue)?
3. **Step 3 — 3M Audit**: Audit Material, Machine, Method parameters against the defect signature.
4. **Step 4 — Process Parameter Mapping**: Check temperature, pressure, time, speed (the **4 Ts & 2 Ps**).
5. **Step 5 — Trial Correction**: Apply one parameter change at a time and re-evaluate.
6. **Step 6 — Documentation**: Record the corrective action in the **Process Deviation Log**.

## 2.3 The "4 Ts and 2 Ps" Master Parameter Framework

> [!NOTE]
> In any polymer process, **six master parameters** control the entire production. Every troubleshooting exercise must sequentially audit these:

| Parameter | Symbol | Injection Molding | Extrusion |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Temperature (Melt)** | T<sub>melting</sub> | Barrel zone T₁–T₄ | Barrel + Die T |
| **Temperature (Mold)** | T<sub>mold</sub> | Cavity + Core | — |
| **Time (Cooling)** | t<sub>cool</sub> | Cooling timer | — |
| **Time (Cycle)** | t<sub>cycle</sub> | Total cycle | Throughput rate |
| **Pressure (Injection)** | P<sub>inj</sub> | 700–1400 bar | — |
| **Pressure (Hold/Pack)** | P<sub>hold</sub> | 300–800 bar | — |
| **Speed (Injection)** | v<sub>inj</sub> | mm/s | Screw rpm |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Tables for Major Processes

### 2.4.1 Injection Molding Defects — Master CER Table

| Defect | Primary Cause | Primary Remedy | Secondary Cause | Secondary Remedy |
|--------|---------------|----------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Sink Mark** | Insufficient pack/hold pressure | Increase P<sub>hold</sub> by 10–15% | Section thickness too high | Redesign wall uniform (≤ 60% adjacent) |
| **Short Shot** | Low melt temp / insufficient shot size | Raise T<sub>melt</sub> by 5–10°C | Frozen nozzle | Raise nozzle T by 5°C |
| **Flash** | Excessive clamp tonnage, low clamp force | Reduce injection pressure; check clamp force | Worn mold parting surfaces | Polish / re-machine mold |
| **Warpage** | Non-uniform cooling | Balance mold cooling circuit; lower T<sub>mold</sub> differential | Differential shrinkage | Reduce melt T; increase hold P |
| **Weld Line** | Low mold temperature | Increase T<sub>mold</sub> by 10°C | Insufficient venting | Add vent at weld line (0.02–0.03 mm) |
| **Silver Streaks** | Moisture in resin | Dry resin at specified T for specified time | Degradation | Lower melt T; check residence time |
| **Voids** | Insufficient pack pressure | Increase P<sub>hold</sub> and hold time | Thick section | Redesign uniform thickness |
| **Burn Marks** | Trapped air (diesel effect) | Improve venting; reduce v<sub>inj</sub> | Excessive injection speed | Lower v<sub>inj</sub> by 20% |
| **Flow Marks** | Melt too cold at cavity | Raise T<sub>melt</sub> and T<sub>mold</sub> | Injection speed too low | Increase v<sub>inj</sub> |
| **Jetting** | Small gate relative to cavity | Enlarge gate, reposition near thick section | Low melt temperature | Raise T<sub>melt</sub> |

### 2.4.2 Extrusion Defects — Master CER Table

| Defect | Primary Cause | Primary Remedy | Secondary Cause | Secondary Remedy |
|--------|---------------|----------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Sharkskin** | High shear stress at die wall | Increase melt T; reduce output rate; use processing aid | Low die L/D | Re-design die land |
| **Melt Fracture** | Critical shear rate exceeded | Lower shear rate (reduce output, raise T) | Mismatched polymer grade | Switch to lower-MFI grade |
| **Die Drool** | Material exuding from die lip | Polish die lip; use PTFE coating; reduce melt T | Die land too short | Increase die land length |
| **Bubbles in Film/Sheet** | Moisture in resin | Pre-dry resin | Air entrapment | Vent hopper, check hopper seal |
| **Surge / Pulsation** | Insufficient melt homogeneity | Increase compression zone temperature; check screw wear | Inconsistent feeding | Check feed throat cooling |
| **Poor Surface Gloss** | Low melt temperature | Increase T<sub>die</sub> | Contamination | Purge barrel thoroughly |
| **Dimensional Drift (Thickness)** | Inconsistent haul-off speed | Stabilize puller speed | Barrel T fluctuation | Check heater bands, thermocouples |
| **Gel/Fisheyes** | Crosslinked polymer particles / contamination | Filter screen (finer mesh); purge with compatible resin | Degraded polymer | Lower melt T, reduce residence time |
| **Brittleness in Output** | Excessive shear / degradation | Lower screw RPM; reduce back pressure | Excessive regrind % | Limit regrind to 20–25% |

### 2.4.3 Blow Molding Defects — Master CER Table

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------|--------|
| **Incomplete Blow (Poor Parison Inflation)** | Insufficient air pressure; low melt strength | Increase blow pressure; lower melt T slightly |
| **Die Lines** | Die lip damage or contamination | Polish die, clean die lips |
| **Pinch-off Weld Weakness** | Insufficient pinch-off land | Increase pinch land length (typically 1.5–2× parison thickness) |
| **Burst / Pinhole** | Excessive blow pressure; thin wall | Reduce pressure; increase parison thickness |
| **Variable Wall Thickness** | Improper parison programming; inconsistent extruder output | Adjust parison programmer; check extruder stability |
| **Shrinkage beyond spec** | Insufficient cooling time in mold | Extend cooling time; lower mold T |

## 2.5 The Moisture & Drying Imperative

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: The single most common short-answer question in PCPOT502 Module 4 is *"Why must hygroscopic polymers be dried before processing?"* Failing to mention the **hydrolysis mechanism** and **molecular weight degradation** results in **2 out of 3 marks** lost.

**Hygroscopic Polymers — Required Drying Specifications:**

| Polymer | Drying Temperature | Drying Time | Critical Moisture |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **PA 6 (Nylon 6)** | 80°C | 4–6 hrs | < 0.10% |
| **PA 66 (Nylon 66)** | 80°C | 4–6 hrs | < 0.10% |
| **PC (Polycarbonate)** | 120°C | 3–4 hrs | < 0.02% |
| **PMMA** | 80°C | 2–4 hrs | < 0.10% |
| **PET** | 140–160°C | 4–6 hrs | < 0.02% |
| **PBT** | 120°C | 3–4 hrs | < 0.02% |
| **ABS** | 80°C | 2–3 hrs | < 0.10% |
| **POM (Acetal)** | 80°C | 2 hrs | < 0.10% |

**Mechanism of Hydrolytic Degradation:**

$$
\text{Polymer Chain (amide/ester)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \text{shear}} \text{Shorter Chains} + \text{Hydrolysis Products}
$$

This reaction **irreversibly reduces molecular weight**, manifesting as **silver streaks, splay, voids, and brittleness** in the molded part.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why troubleshooting matters in industry:**
> - **Cost Impact**: The plastic processing industry loses **3–8% of revenue** to scrap/rework. A single sink-mark defect fix can save ₹10–50 lakhs/year per machine.
> - **Six Sigma Application**: Automotive OEMs demand **≤ 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. Effective troubleshooting is the foundation of **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control).
> - **Cross-functional Relevance**: A polymer engineer must integrate knowledge of **rheology, heat transfer, mold design, and material science** to troubleshoot effectively.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & CER Matrix Walk-Throughs

## 3.1 The Universal 8-Step Troubleshooting Protocol (Exhaustive Walk-Through)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master protocol** that KTU expects students to reproduce in 14-mark structured answers. Below is the **exhaustive, non-truncated** version.

### Step 1 — Defect Documentation

**Logic**: Before any diagnosis, the defect must be **documented with photographic evidence and measurement data**.

$$
\text{Defect Log} = \left\{ \text{Date, Shift, Operator, Machine ID, Defect Type, Location on Part, Frequency (\%), Photo, Dimension Readings (mm)} \right\}
$$

### Step 2 — Defect Classification

Classify the defect into one of the **3 families**:

$$
\text{Defect}_{\text{family}} = \begin{cases} \text{Surface Family} \\ \text{Dimensional Family} \\ \text{Structural/Mechanical Family} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Statistical Frequency Analysis (Pareto Analysis)

Compute the **Pareto distribution** of defects to identify the **vital few** (80/20 rule):

$$
P_i = \frac{n_i}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

where $n_i$ = frequency of defect type $i$, and $N_{\text{total}}$ = total defect count.

The cumulative percentage identifies the **priority defects**:

$$
C_j = \sum_{i=1}^{j} P_i \quad \text{until} \quad C_j \geq 80\%
$$

### Step 4 — 4M+E Root Cause Audit

Systematically audit:

- **Material**: MFI shift, contamination, moisture content, lot variation, regrind %.
- **Machine**: Heater band condition, thermocouple accuracy (check with external probe), screw/barrel wear, nozzle condition, mold surface.
- **Method**: Temperature profile, injection profile (speed/pressure switchover), cooling time, clamp tonnage.
- **Manpower**: Operator training, shift handover, SOP adherence.
- **Environment**: Ambient humidity, ambient temperature, machine location vibrations.

### Step 5 — 4 Ts & 2 Ps Parameter Audit

Audit each parameter against the **material datasheet** and **machine setting sheet**:

$$
\Delta T = \vert T_{\text{actual}} - T_{\text{setpoint}} \vert
$$

$$
\Delta P = \vert P_{\text{actual}} - P_{\text{setpoint}} \vert
$$

Any deviation > **5°C** in temperature or > **5%** in pressure is suspect.

### Step 6 — One-Variable-At-A-Time (OVAT) Test

Apply **one parameter change** at a time and record the effect:

$$
\text{Trial}_i = \text{Baseline} + \Delta \text{Parameter}_i
$$

For example, if sink marks are observed, **first** try increasing P<sub>hold</sub> by 10%. If no effect, then increase hold time by 1 s. If still no effect, escalate to mold redesign.

### Step 7 — Verification & Documentation

After the remedy, run **30 consecutive parts** and record:

$$
\text{Quality Rate} = \frac{N_{\text{acceptable}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

Acceptance criterion: **Quality Rate ≥ 99%** (industry standard for non-critical parts) or **≥ 99.97%** (automotive critical).

### Step 8 — Preventive Action (Poka-Yoke / Mistake-Proofing)

Implement **process controls** to prevent recurrence:
- **SPC charts** (Statistical Process Control): $\bar{X}-R$ charts for melt temperature, part weight, dimension.
- **Alarm limits**: Set $\pm 3\sigma$ alarm on critical parameters.
- **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**: Document RPN (Risk Priority Number) = $S \times O \times D$.

## 3.2 Worked-Out CER Case Study — Sink Mark Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **model worked example** for a 14-mark KTU question.

**Problem Statement**: A polypropylene (PP) injection-molded part shows **sink marks over a rib of 3 mm thickness** while the adjacent wall is 2 mm. The pack pressure is set at **500 bar**, hold time is **3 seconds**, melt temperature is **230°C**, and mold temperature is **40°C**.

**Solution Walk-Through:**

**Step 1 — Identify the defect**: Sink mark (Surface Family, sub-type: Volumetric Shrinkage).

**Step 2 — Analyze the geometry**: Rib thickness (3 mm) is **150%** of adjacent wall (2 mm). The **Design for Manufacturing (DFM) rule** states that rib thickness should be **40–60%** of the adjacent wall thickness to avoid sinks.

**Step 3 — Geometric violation identified**:

$$
\text{Rib-to-Wall Ratio} = \frac{t_{\text{rib}}}{t_{\text{wall}}} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \; (150\%)
$$

This is **non-compliant** (target: 0.4 to 0.6).

**Step 4 — Root cause analysis**: The thick rib cools slower than the thin wall. During cooling/solidification, the **volumetric shrinkage** of the rib (PP volumetric shrinkage ≈ **15–20%**) cannot be compensated by the pack/hold phase.

**Step 5 — Process-level remedies (interim, no mold change)**:

- **Increase P<sub>hold</sub>** from 500 bar to **600 bar** (Δ = +20%) to push more material into the shrinkage cavity.
- **Increase hold time** from 3 s to **5 s** to maintain pressure until gate freeze-off.
- **Lower melt temperature** from 230°C to **220°C** to reduce shrinkage magnitude.

**Step 6 — Design-level remedy (permanent fix)**: Redesign rib to **1 mm thickness** (50% of wall) — this requires **mold modification**.

**Step 7 — Verification**: After P<sub>hold</sub> adjustment, sink depth measured: from **0.15 mm** → **0.03 mm** (within tolerance of **0.05 mm** for cosmetic parts). Quality rate improved from **78%** to **98.5%**.

## 3.3 Worked-Out CER Case Study — Weld Line on Nylon (PA66) Part

**Problem**: A glass-filled PA66 part shows **weld line with reduced tensile strength** (28 MPa vs. expected 65 MPa).

**Diagnosis**:
- PA66 is **hygroscopic** — moisture > 0.20% confirmed by moisture analyzer.
- Moisture in the melt causes **hydrolysis**:

$$
\text{PA66 Chain} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{280°C} \text{Shorter Chain} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2
$$

- Reduced molecular weight → reduced chain entanglement at weld line → reduced tensile strength.

**Remedies**:
1. **Dry the resin** at **80°C for 6 hours** (verify moisture < **0.10%**).
2. **Increase mold temperature** from 80°C to **100°C** to improve melt fusion at weld.
3. **Add vent** at the weld line location (vent depth **0.02 mm**).
4. **Optimize gate location** to minimize weld line occurrence in critical stress areas.

## 3.4 Worked-Out CER Case Study — Sharkskin in HDPE Extrusion

**Problem**: An HDPE blown film line shows **periodic surface roughness (sharkskin)** at high output rates (> **40 m/min**).

**Diagnosis**:
- Sharkskin originates at the **die land** due to **stick-slip** of the polymer melt at the die wall.
- Critical shear stress exceeded: $\sigma_{\text{critical}} \approx 0.14$ MPa for HDPE at **190°C**.

**Remedies**:
1. **Increase melt temperature** by **10–15°C** to reduce viscosity.
2. **Add processing aid** (e.g., fluoropolymer elastomer at **100–500 ppm**).
3. **Reduce output rate** by **10–15%** to lower shear rate.
4. **Apply external lubricant** to die lip (silicone spray for short-term trial only).

## 3.5 Decision Matrix: When to Adjust Material vs. Process vs. Machine

> [!NOTE]
> Use this **decision matrix** to determine the troubleshooting domain.

| Symptom | Likely Domain | First Action |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| Defect appears **randomly** across all cavities | Material (lot variation, moisture) | Check material certification; check moisture |
| Defect appears **only in one cavity** | Machine/Mold (one cavity issue) | Inspect that specific cavity surface, gate, vent |
| Defect appears **after machine start-up** then disappears | Process (mold warm-up) | Allow mold thermal equilibrium (15–20 cycles) |
| Defect appears **gradually increasing** over shifts | Machine (wear — screw, check ring) | Inspect screw, check ring, barrel |
| Defect appears **only with new material batch** | Material | Compare MFI, moisture with previous batch |
| Defect correlates with **ambient humidity** (monsoon) | Material (moisture) + Environment | Audit dryer performance; install dehumidifier |
| Defect correlates with **operator shift** | Manpower | Re-train operator; review SOP |
