# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q: List any six common defects encountered in injection molding and state the primary cause of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

1. **Sink Mark** — Primary cause: Insufficient pack/hold pressure, thick section geometry.
2. **Short Shot** — Primary cause: Inadequate injection pressure, frozen gate, low melt temperature.
3. **Flash** — Primary cause: Excessive injection pressure and/or insufficient clamp tonnage.
4. **Weld Line** — Primary cause: Two melt fronts meeting at low temperature; insufficient mold temperature.
5. **Warpage** — Primary cause: Non-uniform cooling; differential shrinkage due to fiber orientation.
6. **Silver Streaks** — Primary cause: Moisture in resin causing steam formation during injection.

*(1 mark per defect for any 3 = 3 marks. For 6 defects, allocate 0.5 each = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Why is it essential to dry hygroscopic polymers like Nylon 66 and Polycarbonate before processing? What happens if drying is inadequate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

Nylon 66 (PA66) and Polycarbonate (PC) are **hygroscopic polymers** that absorb moisture from the atmosphere. If processed without adequate drying:

- **At processing temperatures (260–300°C)**, the absorbed moisture reacts with the polymer chain, causing **hydrolytic degradation**.
- This reaction **breaks the long polymer chains into shorter fragments**, reducing molecular weight ($M_w$).
- Reduced $M_w$ manifests as **silver streaks, splay marks, voids, and brittleness** in the molded part.
- Mechanical properties like **tensile strength and impact resistance drop by 30–50%**.

**Remedy**: PA66 should be dried at **80°C for 4–6 hours** to achieve moisture content < **0.10%**; PC at **120°C for 3–4 hours** to achieve moisture < **0.02%**.

*(Mechanism = 1 mark; Defect manifestation = 1 mark; Drying specifications = 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

> **Q (a)** Explain in detail the **classification of defects in injection molding** with specific examples under each category. Discuss the role of the **3M framework (Material, Machine, Method)** in troubleshooting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

#### (a) Classification of Injection Molding Defects

Injection molding defects are classified into **three major families**:

**1. Surface Defects (Aesthetic Family)** — These affect the visual and tactile quality of the part.

- **Sink Marks**: Localized depressions over thick sections, ribs, or bosses caused by volumetric shrinkage when the pack/hold phase cannot compensate.
- **Flow Marks / Flow Lines**: Wavy patterns formed when the melt front cools faster than the incoming melt, due to low melt or mold temperature.
- **Weld Lines (Knit Lines)**: Visible lines where two melt fronts meet but fail to fuse completely due to low temperature or poor venting.
- **Silver Streaks / Splay**: Long silver-white lines caused by moisture vaporization, air entrapment, or polymer degradation.
- **Burn Marks**: Black streaks caused by trapped air (diesel effect) undergoing adiabatic compression and burning.
- **Jetting**: Coiled or snake-like surface patterns formed when melt enters a large cavity at high velocity from a small gate.
- **Gloss Variation**: Uneven surface sheen due to differential mold temperature or inconsistent melt viscosity.

**2. Dimensional Defects (Geometric Family)** — These affect the geometric tolerances.

- **Flash (burr)**: Excess material escaping from the parting line due to excessive injection pressure or insufficient clamp tonnage.
- **Short shot**: Incomplete cavity filling due to insufficient material, low pressure, or premature gate freeze.
- **Warpage**: Out-of-plane distortion caused by non-uniform cooling, internal residual stress, or anisotropic fiber orientation.
- **Dimensional shrinkage**: Part smaller than mold cavity due to inadequate packing.
- **Oversized part**: Caused by over-packing or low mold temperature preventing proper contraction.

**3. Structural / Mechanical Defects (Integrity Family)** — These compromise mechanical performance.

- **Voids**: Internal cavities formed by entrapped air or vacuum during shrinkage.
- **Weld line weakness**: Reduced tensile strength at weld line due to incomplete chain entanglement.
- **Delamination**: Layered peeling indicating contamination or incompatible polymer blending.
- **Brittleness**: Cracking at low stress, caused by polymer degradation or excessive shear heating.

#### Role of the 3M Framework

The **3M (Material–Machine–Method)** framework is the **diagnostic compass** for systematic troubleshooting:

- **Material (M)**: The first audit domain. Check **moisture content** (using moisture analyzer), **MFI/MFR** (using melt flow indexer), **lot consistency** (compare certificates of analysis), and **regrind percentage** (typically limited to 20–25%). Material issues typically produce **randomly distributed defects** across all cavities and machines.

- **Machine (M)**: The second audit domain. Verify **heater band integrity** (compare setpoint vs. actual temperature with external probe), **thermocouple accuracy**, **screw and barrel wear** (check shot size and plasticizing time), **nozzle condition** (check for drool or blockage), and **mold surface** (check for scratches, contamination, or corrosion). Machine issues typically produce **location-specific** defects (e.g., one cavity, one machine).

- **Method (M)**: The third audit domain. Audit the **4 Ts and 2 Ps**: Melt Temperature, Mold Temperature, Cooling Time, Cycle Time, Injection Pressure, and Hold/Pack Pressure. Compare each against the **material datasheet** recommendations and **machine setting sheet**. Any deviation > **5°C** in temperature or > **5%** in pressure requires correction. Method issues produce **systematic, reproducible defects** that resolve with parameter adjustment.

The **3M framework ensures exhaustive diagnosis** by preventing the technician from jumping to conclusions and ignoring root causes in domains other than the obvious one.

---

> **Q (b)** Discuss in detail the **troubleshooting of weld lines in injection-molded plastic parts**. Cover the causes, effects, and remedies with process parameter recommendations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

#### (b) Weld Line Troubleshooting in Injection Molding

**Definition**: A **weld line** (also called a **knit line**) is a visible (and mechanically weaker) line formed on the surface of a molded part where **two or more melt fronts meet** but fail to fuse completely into a homogeneous mass.

**Formation Mechanism**:

When polymer melt flows from the gate into the cavity, it may split into **multiple flow streams** due to:
- Presence of **obstacles** (holes, cores, inserts, ribs) in the cavity.
- **Multiple gates** feeding the part.
- **Long flow length** causing the melt to cool and meet at a junction.

When the two cooled melt fronts meet, the **chain molecules at the interface have insufficient mobility and time to interdiffuse**, resulting in a **weaker bond with lower molecular entanglement**. This is called the **"Helfand–Saito" interface** in polymer rheology.

**Causes of Weld Lines**:

1. **Low mold temperature** — causes the melt to solidify prematurely before fusion.
2. **Low melt temperature** — reduces molecular mobility at the interface.
3. **Excessive injection speed** — causes jetting and turbulence at the meeting point.
4. **Insufficient venting** at the weld line — trapped air prevents complete fusion.
5. **Contamination** at the weld line from mold release agents or degraded polymer.
6. **Improper gate location** — forces melt to travel around obstacles.
7. **Low hold/pack pressure** — inadequate consolidation at the interface.
8. **Moisture in resin** — causes steam at the weld line, further weakening it.

**Effects of Weld Lines**:

- **Reduced tensile strength** at the weld line (30–70% lower than base material).
- **Reduced impact strength** — weld lines are crack initiation sites.
- **Visible cosmetic defect** — appears as a line, often darker or glossier than the surroundings.
- **Potential leak path** in pressure vessels or fluid containers.
- **Stress concentration** under cyclic loading → fatigue failure.

**Remedies — Process Parameter Recommendations**:

| Parameter | Adjustment | Effect |
|-----------|------------|--------|
| **Mold Temperature (T<sub>mold</sub>)** | Increase by **10–15°C** | Promotes chain mobility at interface, better fusion |
| **Melt Temperature (T<sub>melt</sub>)** | Increase by **5–10°C** | Lower viscosity, better interfacial diffusion |
| **Injection Speed** | Adjust to **moderate-to-high** | Ensures melt reaches junction before cooling |
| **Hold Pressure (P<sub>hold</sub>)** | Increase by **10–15%** | Compensates for shrinkage at weld |
| **Hold Time** | Extend by **0.5–1 s** | Maintains pressure during gate freeze |
| **Venting** | Add vent at weld line (**0.02–0.03 mm depth**) | Removes trapped air, allows complete fusion |
| **Gate Location** | Relocate gate to **minimize weld in critical area** | Moves weld to non-critical region |
| **Material Drying** | Dry resin to specification | Eliminates steam-induced weakness |
| **Mold Release Agent** | Eliminate or reduce | Prevents contamination at interface |
| **Mold Surface Finish** | Polish to **mirror finish** | Reduces cooling rate at surface, improves fusion |

**Verification**: Conduct **tensile testing** on specimens cut across the weld line. Target: **≥ 85% of base material strength** for non-critical applications; **≥ 95%** for structural applications.

**Conclusion**: Weld line troubleshooting is a **multi-parameter optimization** problem requiring simultaneous adjustment of mold design, material preparation, and process parameters. The **DFM principle** of placing gates to minimize weld lines in critical stress zones is the most permanent solution.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

> **Q (a)** Describe the **classification of polymer processing defects** based on **Material, Machine, and Method (3M)** domains. Explain with at least **three examples per domain**, including the cause and remedy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

#### (a) 3M Classification of Polymer Processing Defects

The **3M framework** is a diagnostic taxonomy that classifies every defect according to its **root cause domain**, enabling systematic troubleshooting.

#### **M-1: Material-Related Defects**

Material defects arise from **incoming resin quality issues**, contamination, or improper preparation.

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------|--------|
| **Silver Streaks / Splay** | Moisture in hygroscopic resin (e.g., PA, PC, PET) | Dry resin per specification (e.g., PA66 at 80°C/6 hrs; PC at 120°C/4 hrs); verify moisture < 0.10% |
| **Voids and Bubbles** | Volatile content (residual monomer, solvent) | Pre-dry; use vented barrel extruder |
| **Brittleness / Low Impact** | Degraded polymer from previous thermal history; high MFI shift | Reduce regrind % to < 25%; check resin shelf life; lower melt temperature |
| **Fisheyes / Gels** | Crosslinked polymer particles or contamination from different polymer | Purge barrel; use finer screen pack; ensure clean hopper |
| **Gloss Variation between lots** | Different MFI between batches | Specify tighter MFI tolerance with supplier; blend within ± 0.5 g/10 min |
| **Delamination** | Incompatible polymer contamination; excessive mold release | Audit material handling; eliminate mold release or use compatible grade |

#### **M-2: Machine-Related Defects**

Machine defects arise from **equipment condition issues** — wear, malfunction, calibration drift, or mold damage.

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------|--------|
| **Flash at Parting Line** | Worn mold parting surfaces; damaged clamp interlocks | Polish/re-machine mold; check clamp tonnage calibration |
| **Short Shot (intermittent)** | Partial nozzle blockage; defective thermocouple | Clean/nozzle replace; verify actual vs. setpoint T with external probe |
| **Surface Streaks (burning)** | Heater band failure causing localized hot spot; barrel carbon buildup | Replace heater band; purge with cleaning compound |
| **Dimensional Inconsistency** | Screw and barrel wear; check ring malfunction | Inspect screw OD; replace check ring; check shot size consistency |
| **Sink Marks (localized)** | Blocked cooling channel; uneven mold surface T | Clean cooling circuit; verify mold T at multiple points |
| **Weld Line Variation** | Vents clogged; core pin shift | Clean vents; verify core pin alignment with CMM |

#### **M-3: Method-Related Defects**

Method defects arise from **incorrect process parameters** or **operating procedure deviations**.

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------|--------|
| **Warpage** | Excessive mold T differential; non-uniform cooling time; over-packing | Balance cooling circuit; lower mold T differential to < 3°C; reduce P<sub>hold</sub> |
| **Sink Marks** | Insufficient P<sub>hold</sub> and/or hold time; high melt T | Increase P<sub>hold</sub> by 10–15%; extend hold time by 1 s; lower melt T by 5–10°C |
| **Flow Marks** | Low melt T at cavity; low injection speed | Raise melt T by 10°C; increase injection speed to fill before freeze-off |
| **Voids (Internal)** | Inadequate packing; high melt T; thick section | Increase P<sub>hold</sub> and hold time; reduce melt T; redesign to uniform wall |
| **Jetting** | Small gate relative to cavity; low melt T | Enlarge gate, reposition; raise melt T by 10°C |
| **Over-packed / Sticking Part** | Excessive P<sub>hold</sub>; insufficient cooling time; over-filled cavity | Reduce P<sub>hold</sub> by 10%; extend cooling time by 1–2 s |
| **Cycle Time Drift** | Inconsistent machine response; sensor drift | Calibrate sensors; implement SPC alarm limits |

**Conclusion**: The 3M framework ensures that the troubleshooter **exhausts all possible root causes** before declaring a defect "unresolvable". The **frequency and location pattern** of the defect is the key diagnostic indicator: randomly distributed defects → Material; location-specific → Machine; systematic across all parts → Method.

---

> **Q (b)** Explain the **role of moisture and drying in polymer processing**. Discuss the **mechanism of hydrolytic degradation** in nylon and polycarbonate. Provide the recommended **drying specifications** for at least four hygroscopic polymers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

#### (b) Role of Moisture and Drying in Polymer Processing

**Why Moisture is a Critical Concern**:

Many engineering polymers are **hygroscopic** — they absorb moisture from the atmosphere due to **polar functional groups** (amide, ester, carbonate) in their backbone that form **hydrogen bonds with water molecules**. When such moisture-laden polymers are processed at temperatures above **200°C**, the water **vaporizes violently** and reacts with the polymer chain, causing irreversible chemical degradation.

**Mechanism of Hydrolytic Degradation**:

**In Polyamide (Nylon 6, Nylon 66)**:

The amide linkage undergoes hydrolysis at processing temperatures:

$$
\text{—[NH—(CH}_2)_5\text{—CO]}—_n + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{260–290°C} \text{—[NH—(CH}_2)_5\text{—COOH]} + \text{H}_2\text{N—(CH}_2)_5\text{—CO—}
$$

The polymer chain is **cleaved at the amide bond**, reducing the **molecular weight ($M_w$)** and the **degree of polymerization (DP)**. A small reduction in $M_w$ (e.g., 20,000 → 12,000) can reduce impact strength by **50%**.

**In Polycarbonate (PC)**:

The carbonate ester linkage is hydrolyzed:

$$
\text{—[O—(C}_6\text{H}_4\text{)—C(CH}_3)_2\text{—(C}_6\text{H}_4\text{)—O—CO—O]}—_n + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{280–320°C} \text{Shorter Chains} + \text{Bisphenol A} + \text{CO}_2
$$

The chain scission reduces molecular weight, leading to **embrittlement and reduced mechanical performance**.

**Visual and Mechanical Manifestations of Moisture-Induced Defects**:

- **Silver Streaks** — steam bubbles trapped in the melt create silver-white lines.
- **Splay / Splash** — explosive vaporization at the gate causes splash-pattern marks.
- **Voids and Bubbles** — internal steam pockets.
- **Brittleness** — reduced $M_w$ causes low impact strength.
- **Reduced Tensile Strength** — molecular chain scission.
- **Surface Cracks / Crazing** — residual stress + degraded polymer.

**Recommended Drying Specifications for Hygroscopic Polymers**:

| Polymer | Drying Temperature | Drying Time | Maximum Allowable Moisture |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|----------------------------|
| **PA 6 (Nylon 6)** | **80°C** | **4–6 hrs** | **< 0.10%** |
| **PA 66 (Nylon 66)** | **80°C** | **4–6 hrs** | **< 0.10%** |
| **PC (Polycarbonate)** | **120°C** | **3–4 hrs** | **< 0.02%** |
| **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** | **140–160°C** | **4–6 hrs** | **< 0.02%** |
| **PBT (Polybutylene Terephthalate)** | **120°C** | **3–4 hrs** | **< 0.02%** |
| **PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)** | **80°C** | **2–4 hrs** | **< 0.10%** |
| **POM (Polyoxymethylene / Acetal)** | **80°C** | **2 hrs** | **< 0.10%** |
| **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** | **80°C** | **2–3 hrs** | **< 0.10%** |

**Drying Equipment**:

- **Dehumidifying Dryer (DHD)** — uses desiccant (molecular sieve) to supply air with **dew point < –40°C**.
- **Hot Air Oven** — used for small batches; less efficient for continuous production.
- **Vacuum Dryer** — used for ultra-hygroscopic polymers and high-temperature drying.

**Verification of Drying**:

Use a **moisture analyzer** (infrared or capacitive type) to verify that the dried resin meets the specified moisture content before processing.

**Conclusion**: Moisture control is a **non-negotiable prerequisite** for processing hygroscopic polymers. Proper drying prevents hydrolytic degradation, ensures consistent part quality, and avoids costly rejection. The drying time, temperature, and equipment must be selected based on the polymer's **hygroscopicity and processing temperature**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **The "Symptom-Fix" Mistake**: Students often suggest remedies that **treat the visible symptom** without diagnosing the root cause. Example: For sink marks, simply increasing pressure is correct only **after** confirming that the root cause is insufficient packing — not for design-related thick sections. **Always state "Diagnose first, then prescribe."**
> 2. **Ignoring Material Domain**: Many students focus only on **process parameters** and forget the **Material (M)** domain. For silver streaks, **drying** is the primary remedy, not temperature adjustment.
> 3. **Missing Quantitative Data**: Vague answers like "increase temperature" lose marks. Always specify **numerical values** (e.g., "increase T<sub>hold</sub> by 5–10°C" or "P<sub>hold</sub> from 500 to 600 bar").
> 4. **No Flowchart in 14-Mark Answers**: Examiners award **1–2 marks** for a clear, labeled troubleshooting flowchart. Always include one in long answers.
> 5. **Drying Specifications Without Mechanism**: Stating "dry nylon at 80°C" without explaining **why** (hydrolysis mechanism, molecular weight reduction) loses **1 mark** in 3-mark questions.
> 6. **Mixing Up Drying Temperature with Processing Temperature**: Drying temperatures (e.g., 80°C for PA66) are **much lower** than processing temperatures (e.g., 270–290°C). Confusing these is a common error.
> 7. **Not Mentioning Regrind Limit**: For material-related defects, mentioning that **regrind should be limited to 20–25%** earns a bonus mark in advanced questions.
> 8. **Skipping the Verification Step**: The 8-step protocol's **Step 7 (run 30 parts verification)** is often forgotten. Always close with a verification step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Density Revision Bulletins for KTU PCPOT502 — Module 4: Troubleshooting**

### 🔑 Foundational Concepts
- ✅ **Definition**: Troubleshooting = systematic identification, diagnosis, and rectification of process defects using the **3M+E (Material, Machine, Method, Manpower, Environment)** framework.
- ✅ **3M Framework**: Material, Machine, Method — every defect is classified into one of these domains.
- ✅ **4 Ts and 2 Ps**: Melt Temperature, Mold Temperature, Cooling Time, Cycle Time, Injection Pressure, Hold Pressure.
- ✅ **Cause-Effect-Remedy (CER)**: The 3-column framework for every troubleshooting question.

### 🏭 Defect Classification (Must Memorize)
- ✅ **3 Families**: Surface, Dimensional, Structural.
- ✅ **10+ Critical Defects**: Sink mark, short shot, flash, warpage, weld line, silver streak, flow mark, burn mark, jetting, void, brittleness, delamination.
- ✅ **DFM Rule**: Rib thickness should be **40–60%** of adjacent wall thickness to prevent sink marks.
- ✅ **Weld line formation**: Two melt fronts meeting at low T → incomplete chain interdiffusion → weak bond.

### 🌡️ Processing Knowledge
- ✅ **Hygroscopic Polymers**: PA6, PA66, PC, PET, PBT, PMMA, POM, ABS.
- ✅ **Drying Specs** (memorize the table): Nylon 80°C/4–6 hrs; PC 120°C/3–4 hrs; PET 140–160°C/4–6 hrs.
- ✅ **Critical Moisture**: < 0.10% for Nylon; < 0.02% for PC and PET.
- ✅ **Hydrolytic Degradation**: Water + Polymer Chain at high T → Chain Scission → Reduced $M_w$ → Brittle part.

### 📊 Quality Metrics
- ✅ **Acceptable Quality Rate**: ≥ 99% for non-critical; ≥ 99.97% for automotive critical (Six Sigma).
- ✅ **Pareto (80/20 Rule)**: 80% of defects come from 20% of causes.
- ✅ **OVAT Principle**: Change One Variable At A Time during troubleshooting trials.
- ✅ **Verification**: Always run **30 parts** after a corrective action.

### 🧠 Process-Specific Quick Fixes
- ✅ **Sink Mark** → Increase P<sub>hold</sub> by 10–15% + extend hold time + reduce rib thickness.
- ✅ **Short Shot** → Raise melt T by 5–10°C + increase injection pressure + check venting.
- ✅ **Flash** → Reduce injection pressure + check clamp tonnage + polish mold parting line.
- ✅ **Warpage** → Lower mold T differential + increase hold P + check fiber orientation.
- ✅ **Weld Line** → Increase mold T by 10°C + add vent at weld line (0.02 mm) + relocate gate.
- ✅ **Silver Streaks** → Dry resin properly + lower melt T + check residence time.
- ✅ **Sharkskin (Extrusion)** → Increase melt T + reduce output rate + use processing aid.
- ✅ **Melt Fracture (Extrusion)** → Lower shear rate + raise melt T + switch to lower-MFI grade.

### 🛠️ Troubleshooting Protocol (8 Steps)
- ✅ **Step 1**: Document defect (location, frequency, photos, dimensions).
- ✅ **Step 2**: Classify into 3 families (Surface / Dimensional / Structural).
- ✅ **Step 3**: Pareto analysis to identify vital few defects.
- ✅ **Step 4**: 4M+E root cause audit.
- ✅ **Step 5**: 4 Ts and 2 Ps parameter audit.
- ✅ **Step 6**: OVAT (One-Variable-At-A-Time) trial correction.
- ✅ **Step 7**: Run 30-parts verification → Quality Rate ≥ 99%.
- ✅ **Step 8**: Preventive action — SPC charts, alarm limits, FMEA, CAPA.

### 💡 High-Weight KTU Numerical Concepts
- ✅ **Rib-to-Wall Ratio**: $t_{rib}/t_{wall}$ target = 0.4 to 0.6 (max 0.6).
- ✅ **Mold Venting Depth**: 0.02–0.03 mm at weld lines and flow ends.
- ✅ **Pinch-off Land Length (Blow Molding)**: 1.5–2× parison thickness.
- ✅ **Shrinkage Allowance (PP)**: 1.5–2.0%; (PA66): 1.5–2.0%; (PC): 0.5–0.7%; (ABS): 0.4–0.7%.
- ✅ **Hold Pressure Range**: 300–800 bar (30–80 MPa) for most thermoplastics.
- ✅ **Injection Pressure Range**: 700–1400 bar (70–140 MPa) for most thermoplastics.

### 📈 KTU Exam Strategy
- ✅ For **3-mark questions**: List 3 defects with **cause + remedy** in tabular form.
- ✅ For **7-mark sub-questions**: Use **CER framework** + include a **flowchart** (1–2 marks reserved).
- ✅ For **14-mark questions**: Structure answer as **(a) Classification/CER + (b) Process-specific deep dive**.
- ✅ Always include **quantitative values** (temperature in °C, pressure in bar, depth in mm, time in seconds).
- ✅ End every long answer with a **verification step** and a **preventive action** statement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calenders and Accessories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calenders and Accessories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Calendering** is a continuous, pressure-driven polymer processing operation in which a highly viscous, plasticized (or melted) polymeric mass is passed through a series of counter-rotating, heated rolls to produce **films, sheets, or coated substrates** of uniform gauge and surface finish. The operation relies on the squeezing of a polymer melt/compound between two or more rolls rotating in opposite directions, generating a sheet whose final thickness is determined by the inter-roll gap (the *nip*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Tag:** Calendering is classified as a **melt-processing technique** within the *primary shaping* family of polymer operations, alongside extrusion and injection molding. It is distinct from extrusion because the driving pressure is generated by the *rotation of the rolls*, not by an external screw pump.

**Calender** (noun): The complete machine assembly comprising rolls, bearings, roll-adjustment (nip) mechanism, heating/cooling channels, drive system, and auxiliary accessories.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dough Roller" Intuition

Imagine a baker rolling out chapati dough. The baker feeds a lump of dough between two rolling pins and turns the handles. The dough emerges thinner, wider, and with a smoother surface. That is **exactly** what a calender does to plasticized PVC (or rubber, or EVA compound), only:

1. The "dough" is molten polymer at **150 °C – 200 °C**.
2. The rolling pins are massive **cast-iron or chilled-steel rolls** that are internally heated with steam/hot oil or cooled with water.
3. The rolls rotate continuously (not manually) and the dough (feed) is supplied by an **extruder or roll-mill pre-mixer** as a continuous bank of material.
4. There may be **2, 3, 4, or even 5 rolls in series** (an "L", "F", "Z", or inverted-L configuration) so that the sheet is progressively thinned in *stages* rather than in one go.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a single pair of rolls?** Because a single nip cannot take the melt from, say, 5 mm thickness down to 0.2 mm in one pass without creating flow instabilities, excessive roll-separating force, and poor surface quality. Multi-roll arrangements allow *progressive* thickness reduction, each nip doing a small portion of the work.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Nip** | The line of closest approach between two adjacent rolls (the smallest gap). |
| **Bank** | The reservoir of polymer melt that sits above the nip and feeds the rolls. |
| **Bite / Engagement** | The angular region where the melt first contacts the roll surface, characterized by angle $\theta_b$. |
| **Roll Crown** | A slight outward bulge at the roll center (deliberate) to compensate for roll deflection under load. |
| **Cross-Axis (Crossing)** | A small angle introduced between two adjacent roll axes to locally widen the nip and compensate for wear/deflection. |
| **Roll Skewing (Skewed Rolls)** | Offsetting one roll axially relative to its neighbor to control gauge uniformity across the width. |
| **Gauge** | Thickness of the produced sheet/film. |
| **Stripping Roll** | A small auxiliary roll that peels the finished sheet off the main calender roll. |
| **Take-Up System** | Windup station for the finished sheet (tension control). |
| **Embossing Unit** | A patterned roll that impresses texture (e.g., leather grain) on the sheet. |
| **Cooling Train** | A series of chilled rolls that cool the sheet to ambient temperature post-calendering. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The syllabus explicitly asks for **"Calenders and Accessories"** — meaning students must know not only the calender itself but also the *auxiliary equipment* around it: feed extruder, pre-heater (e.g., Banbury mixer output, gear pump), embossing unit, cooling/calibrating rolls, thickness gauge (beta or nuclear), edge-trimmer, corona treater, and windup. Always include accessories in long-answer answers.

### 1.4 Industrial Applications Snapshot

- **Rigid PVC (RPVC) sheets** — for thermoforming (trays, blister packs).
- **Plasticized PVC (PPVC) films/sheets** — for upholstery, stationery, blood bags, shower curtains.
- **PVC floor coverings** — with embossed surface.
- **Rubber sheets** — calendering is the *primary* method for rubber sheet production (tire inner liners, conveyor belts, gaskets).
- **Laminates and coated fabrics** — calender is used as a coating device.
- **EVA, TPU films** — for packaging and lamination.

The **global calender count** is dominated by **PVC and rubber sheet** production, with sheet widths up to **3.5 m** and line speeds up to **120 m/min** in modern installations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics in a Nutshell

The calendering process can be decomposed into **three sequential zones** as polymer moves through a single nip:

1. **Convergence Zone (Bite Region):** The polymer melt from the bank first contacts the rolls. The melt is dragged into the nip by **friction (viscous traction)** at the roll surface. The pressure rises from atmospheric to a maximum value.
2. **Nip / Constriction Zone:** Between the rolls, the melt experiences **extensional and shear flow** as the gap closes from $h_0$ (entry thickness, twice the bank half-height) to $h_1$ (exit thickness = roll gap). The pressure is *highest* just before the nip centerline and then *drops sharply* at the exit. The pressure is *sub-atmospheric* (sometimes a vacuum) just past the nip — this is called the **"negative pressure region"** and is critical for certain surface defects.
3. **Separation / Take-Away Zone:** The thin film/sheet leaves the nip and adheres to one of the roll surfaces (the faster roll, usually). The stripping roll peels it off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world insight:** A *pressure peak* forms at the nip center; if the melt is not degassed properly, *voids* (air entrapment) can collapse catastrophically, creating **blow holes** or **pits** — a common KTU practical exam question.

### 2.2 Roll Configurations (The Heart of Calender Design)

Modern calenders are classified by their **roll arrangement letter** (the letter traces the path of the polymer sheet through the rolls):

| Configuration | Number of Rolls | Sheet Path | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2-Roll Vertical** | 2 | Straight through | Simple sheeting, rubber |
| **3-Roll "I" (Vertical stack)** | 3 | Top→mid→bottom | Basic PVC sheet |
| **4-Roll "I"** | 4 | Vertical stack | Higher gauge control |
| **4-Roll "L"** | 4 | Horizontal then down | Most common — general PVC sheeting |
| **4-Roll "F"** | 4 | Two horizontal + two vertical below | Modern wide-line |
| **4-Roll "Z"** | 4 | Each roll offset horizontally | Premium gauge accuracy |
| **4-Roll "Inverted L"** | 4 | Down then horizontal | Floor covering, embossing in-line |
| **5-Roll "L" or "Z"** | 5 | Extra roll for ultra-fine gauge | High-precision film |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** *"Compare L-type and Z-type calenders"* or *"Why are calender rolls arranged in L/Z patterns instead of I?"* — The answer is: **I-type** rolls suffer from gravity-induced material flow asymmetry and the *bank* cannot be controlled independently at each nip. **L, F, and Z** types allow each nip to have its own well-defined bank, giving far better thickness control and access for the operator to adjust the bank profile.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (CRITICAL — Memorize These)

> [!WARNING]
> The symbol `|` in the table below is rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

**Table 2.1 — Calender Engineering Formulas (Newtonian Melt Assumption)**

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = 2 v \, h_1 \, w$ | Volumetric throughput ($m^3/s$); $v$ = roll surface velocity, $h_1$ = exit half-thickness, $w$ = sheet width. |
| 2 | $v = \pi D N = 2 \pi R N$ | Tangential velocity of roll surface. |
| 3 | $Q = \pi D N \, h_1 \, w$ | Combined throughput form. |
| 4 | $h(\theta) = h_1 (1 + \cos\theta)$ | Local half-gap as a function of angular position $\theta$ (symmetric two-roll calender). |
| 5 | $p(\theta) = \dfrac{\mu v}{h_1} \cdot \dfrac{\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta - \sin\theta \cos\theta}{1 + \sin^{2}\theta}$ | **Gaskell pressure distribution** in the nip. |
| 6 | $p_{\max} \approx \dfrac{\pi \mu v}{2 h_1}$ | Maximum pressure at the nip centerline ($\theta = 0$). |
| 7 | $\dfrac{F}{w} = 2 \mu v R \int_{0}^{\theta_b} \dfrac{\cos\theta \, d\theta}{h(\theta)}$ | Roll separating force per unit width (general form). |
| 8 | $\dfrac{F}{w} \approx \dfrac{2 \mu v R}{h_1} \left[ 1 - \dfrac{h_1}{h_0} \right]$ | Approximate roll separating force (simplified, $h_0 \gg h_1$). |
| 9 | $M = 2 F R$ | Total roll-separating torque (both rolls). |
| 10 | $P = M \omega = 2 \pi N M$ | Power required to drive the calender rolls. |
| 11 | $N_{Bb} = \dfrac{v^{2} \rho R}{g h_0}$ | Bank stability number (Bamberg–Beyreuther dimensionless number); must be low to avoid bank roll-over. |
| 12 | $\sigma_{t} = \dfrac{q w}{A}$ | Sheet tension in take-up, $q$ = pulling force per unit width. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Convention (SI):** $\mu$ in $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$, $v$ in $\text{m/s}$, $R$ in $\text{m}$, $h$ in $\text{m}$, $p$ in $\text{Pa}$, $F$ in $\text{N}$, $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $N$ in rev/s, $\omega$ in rad/s, $P$ in $\text{W}$.
> 
> **Conversion:** $1 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ N/m}^{2}$; $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$. Calender pressures often fall in the range **5 – 15 MPa**.

### 2.4 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Key Concept

#### Why does a calender require pre-heated polymer?
Because viscosity $\mu$ is **strongly temperature-dependent** (typically a 5–10% reduction per °C near the processing window). If the polymer is too cold, the roll-separating force becomes enormous and the roll frame may deflect plastically. Calendering temperatures for PVC are typically **165 °C – 195 °C** depending on plasticizer content.

#### Why are calender rolls crowned?
A perfectly cylindrical roll **deflects inward (bows)** under the immense separating force at the nip. This causes the *center* of the nip to be wider than the *edges*, producing a sheet that is *thicker in the middle*. To compensate, the roll is ground with a **convex profile** (a few hundredths of a millimetre) so that under load, the roll becomes effectively cylindrical. This is called **crowning**. Modern calenders also use **cross-axis (roll crossing)** and **roll bending** to provide *dynamic* compensation.

#### Why is the bank important?
The bank is the **reservoir** of melt that feeds the nip. If the bank is too small, the nip is starved and the sheet has thickness variations (low spots). If the bank is too large or too hot, it can **roll over** (rotate like a ball) due to centrifugal effects — governed by the **Bamberg number** $N_{Bb} < 1$ (rule of thumb).

#### How does calendering differ from extrusion in terms of throughput control?
In **extrusion**, throughput is set by the **screw speed** and is largely independent of die pressure (with a gear pump, fully independent). In **calendering**, throughput is *imposed* by the **roll speed**, and the roll gap is *adjusted* to achieve the desired thickness. The melt must flow into the nip at exactly the rate the rolls are withdrawing it; mismatch causes bank instability.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | How Calender Theory is Used |
|---|---|
| **Process Design** | Sizing rolls (D, R) and drive motors (P) for a target throughput. |
| **Quality Control** | Using pressure-distribution formula to predict nip-related defects (blow holes, fisheyes, gauge bands). |
| **Predictive Maintenance** | Monitoring roll-separating force trend to detect roll-bearing wear or contamination. |
| **Material Selection** | Choosing plasticizer or lubricant to tune $\mu$ for acceptable F at given line speed. |
| **Energy Audit** | Using the power formula to estimate kWh per kg of sheet — relevant to ESG metrics. |
| **R&D (New Compounds)** | Estimating whether a new PVC formulation can be calendered at existing line speed. |

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throughput Equation from Continuity

We start with the **law of conservation of mass** for an incompressible polymer melt (a valid assumption since $\rho_{\text{PVC}} \approx 1350\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$ changes < 1% in the calender temperature range).

**Step 1 — Define control volume:** Consider the region between the two symmetric rolls from the entry angle $\theta_b$ (where gap = $h_0$) to the exit ($\theta = 0$ where gap = $2h_1$).

**Step 2 — Mass balance (steady state):** Mass entering = Mass leaving.

$$
\rho \, v \, h_0 \, w = \rho \, v \, 2h_1 \, w
$$

This reduces to $h_0 = 2h_1$, i.e., the entry half-gap is twice the exit half-gap. But this is only a *trivially satisfied* relationship — the bank supplies any deficit.

**Step 3 — Express the actual throughput as a function of exit conditions:** Since the roll surface drags material out at velocity $v$, the volumetric flow per unit width exiting the nip is $v \cdot h_1$. With *two* rolls (top and bottom), the *total* throughput (top sheet + bottom sheet, in a double-sheet calender) is:

$$
Q = 2 \, v \, h_1 \, w
$$

**Step 4 — Replace $v$ with the rotational speed:** Since $v = \pi D N$,

$$
Q = 2 \pi D N \, h_1 \, w
$$

This is the **calender throughput equation** that appears in **every KTU numerical problem**. 

> [!NOTE]
> **Key nuance for KTU:** In a *single-sheet* calender where the polymer sticks to only one roll, $Q = v \, h_1 \, w$ (no factor of 2). The factor of 2 only applies when both rolls are producing sheet (e.g., two-stream embossing line). Always read the question carefully.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gaskell Pressure Distribution in the Nip

This is the most analytically demanding derivation in this module. We assume:

- **Newtonian fluid** (constant $\mu$).
- **Steady-state**, **isothermal**, **laminar flow**.
- **Symmetric rolls** of equal radius $R$ and equal surface speed $v$ in opposite directions.
- **No slip** at roll surface.
- **Negligible inertia and gravity** (small nip, high viscosity).
- **Thin gap approximation:** $h \ll R$ so the flow can be treated locally as flow between two parallel plates moving at $\pm v$.

**Step 1 — Geometry and gap function:**

$$
h(\theta) = h_1 + R(1 - \cos\theta) \approx h_1 \left( 1 + \frac{R}{h_1}(1 - \cos\theta) \right)
$$

For small angles, $1 - \cos\theta \approx \theta^{2}/2$, so $h(\theta) \approx h_1 + R\theta^{2}/2$. However, we use the exact geometric form:

$$
h(\theta) = h_0 - 2R(1 - \cos\theta) \quad \text{(two-roll, total gap)}
$$

So the **half-gap** is:

$$
H(\theta) = \frac{h(\theta)}{2} = h_1 (1 + \cos\theta)
$$

where $h_1$ is the half-gap at $\theta = 0$ and $h_1 (1 + \cos\theta_b) = h_0/2$ at the bite.

**Step 2 — Pressure-driven and drag-driven flow (lubrication approximation):**

In the thin-gap approximation, the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:

$$
\frac{dp}{dx} = \mu \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}
$$

where $x$ is the flow direction along the roll surface and $y$ is the cross-gap direction.

**Step 3 — Integrate twice with boundary conditions $u(\pm H) = \pm v$ (no slip at moving walls):**

$$
u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dp}{dx} (y^{2} - H^{2}) + v \frac{y}{H}
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric flow rate per unit width at angle $\theta$:**

$$
q = \int_{-H}^{+H} u(y) \, dy = -\frac{2}{3\mu} \frac{dp}{dx} H^{3} + 2 v H
$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state continuity** (since the rolls are not moving toward or away from each other, the flow rate is *constant* along the nip):

$$
q = 2 v h_1 \quad \text{(constant)}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert $x \to \theta$ using $dx = R \, d\theta$ and $H(\theta) = h_1(1 + \cos\theta)$:**

$$
\frac{dp}{d\theta} = \frac{3 \mu v}{R} \cdot \frac{(1 + \cos\theta)^{2} - (1 + \cos\theta_b)^{2}}{(1 + \cos\theta)^{3}}
$$

where $\cos\theta_b = (h_0/(2h_1)) - 1$.

**Step 7 — Integrate with boundary condition $p(\theta_b) = 0$ (atmospheric at the bank):**

$$
p(\theta) = \frac{3 \mu v}{2 R} \cdot \int_{\theta}^{\theta_b} \frac{(1 + \cos\theta')^{2} - (1 + \cos\theta_b)^{2}}{(1 + \cos\theta')^{3}} d\theta'
$$

After significant trigonometric simplification (presented by Gaskell, 1950), this evaluates to:

$$
p(\theta) = \frac{\mu v}{h_1} \cdot \frac{\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta - \sin\theta \cos\theta}{1 + \sin^{2}\theta}
$$

This is the **Gaskell pressure profile** in the calender nip.

**Step 8 — Maximum pressure** at $\theta = 0$:

$$
p_{\max} = p(0) = \frac{\mu v}{h_1} \cdot \frac{\pi/2}{1 + 0} = \frac{\pi \mu v}{2 h_1}
$$

Substituting typical values: $\mu = 10^{4}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $v = 0.1\,\text{m/s}$, $h_1 = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$ (0.5 mm exit half-thickness = 1 mm sheet):

$$
p_{\max} = \frac{\pi \times 10^{4} \times 0.1}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3141.6}{10^{-3}} \approx 3.14 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} \approx 3.14\,\text{MPa}
$$

This is the **order of magnitude** of calender pressures. With higher speeds or thicker melts, pressures of **10–15 MPa** are easily reached — high enough to deform the roll frame if not designed properly.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Roll Separating Force

The roll separating force is the **vertical force each roll experiences**, which the roll frame and bearings must withstand. It is obtained by integrating the vertical component of the pressure over the roll surface in contact with the melt.

**Step 1 — Vertical component of pressure force:** Only the *cosine* component of the pressure contributes to the vertical force on the roll. So:

$$
F = 2 w R \int_{0}^{\theta_b} p(\theta) \cos\theta \, d\theta
$$

(the factor of 2 accounts for both sides of the nip being symmetric, and $w$ is the sheet width; $R \, d\theta$ is the arc length element).

**Step 2 — Substitute Gaskell's $p(\theta)$:**

$$
F = \frac{2 \mu v R w}{h_1} \int_{0}^{\theta_b} \frac{\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta - \sin\theta \cos\theta\right) \cos\theta}{1 + \sin^{2}\theta} d\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral numerically.** For typical calender bite angles $\theta_b \approx 0.2$ rad (≈ 11°), the integral evaluates to approximately:

$$
\int_{0}^{\theta_b} (\cdots) \, d\theta \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.55
$$

**Step 4 — Use the engineering approximation** (valid for $h_0 \gg h_1$, i.e., the bite angle is small):

$$
\frac{F}{w} \approx \frac{2 \mu v R}{h_1}
$$

This is the **most-cited** form of the roll separating force equation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Shortcut:** For exam purposes, the simplified form $F/w = 2\mu v R / h_1$ is acceptable when the problem states "assume $h_0 \gg h_1$" or "neglect bite region effects." Otherwise, present the integral and use the Gaskell function value (look up in standard tables).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Consumption

**Step 1 — Torque on each roll from viscous traction:** The shear stress at the roll surface is $\tau_w = \mu (du/dy)_{y=H}$. From the velocity profile:

$$
\tau_w = \mu \left[ \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dp}{dx} H - \frac{v}{H} \right] = H \frac{dp}{dx} - \frac{\mu v}{H}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque per roll:**

$$
M_{\text{per roll}} = w R^{2} \int_{0}^{\theta_b} \tau_w(\theta) \, d\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering approximation** (when $h_0 \gg h_1$): The pressure-driven contribution is small, and torque is dominated by the viscous-drag term:

$$
M_{\text{per roll}} \approx 2 \mu v R w \int_{0}^{\theta_b} \frac{R \, d\theta}{h_1 (1 + \cos\theta)} \approx 2 \mu v R^{2} w \left( \frac{1}{h_1} - \frac{1}{h_0} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Total torque (both rolls):**

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 2 M_{\text{per roll}} = 4 \mu v R^{2} w \left( \frac{1}{h_1} - \frac{1}{h_0} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Power:**

$$
P = M_{\text{total}} \cdot \omega = M_{\text{total}} \cdot 2 \pi N
$$

For a typical 4-roll calender with $R = 0.25\,\text{m}$, $w = 2\,\text{m}$, $h_1 = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, $h_0 = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, $v = 0.5\,\text{m/s}$, $\mu = 5 \times 10^{3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$:

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 5000 \times 0.5 \times 0.0625 \times 2 \times (2000 - 200) = 4.5 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

$$
\omega = v/R = 0.5/0.25 = 2\,\text{rad/s} \quad (\text{equivalently, } N = 2/(2\pi) \approx 0.318\,\text{rev/s})
$$

$$
P = 4.5 \times 10^{5} \times 2 = 9 \times 10^{5}\,\text{W} = 900\,\text{kW}
$$

This is realistic for an industrial calender line.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A PVC calender produces a sheet of thickness $1.0\,\text{mm}$ and width $1.5\,\text{m}$ at a roll surface speed of $30\,\text{m/min}$. The polymer melt has a Newtonian viscosity of $8 \times 10^{3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ at the processing temperature. The rolls have radius $0.20\,\text{m}$. The entry half-thickness is $h_0/2 = 3.0\,\text{mm}$. Calculate:
(a) Volumetric throughput.
(b) Maximum pressure at the nip.
(c) Approximate roll separating force per unit width.
(d) Power required (both rolls combined).

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Convert $v = 30\,\text{m/min} = 0.5\,\text{m/s}$. With $h_1 = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$ and $w = 1.5\,\text{m}$:

$$
Q = 2 \, v \, h_1 \, w = 2 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 2.7\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{hr}
$$

**Part (b):**

$$
p_{\max} = \frac{\pi \mu v}{2 h_1} = \frac{\pi \times 8 \times 10^{3} \times 0.5}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{3}}{10^{-3}} = 4\pi \times 10^{6} \approx 12.57\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Part (c):** With $R = 0.20\,\text{m}$, $h_1 = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, $h_0/2 = 3 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$ → $h_0 = 6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$. So $h_0/h_1 = 12$, satisfying $h_0 \gg h_1$:

$$
\frac{F}{w} = \frac{2 \mu v R}{h_1} = \frac{2 \times 8 \times 10^{3} \times 0.5 \times 0.20}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.2 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N/m}
$$

Total force $F = 3.2 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5 = 4.8 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N}$ (about **480 tonnes** of force per nip — this is why calender frames are massive cast-steel structures!).

**Part (d):** $\omega = v/R = 0.5/0.20 = 2.5\,\text{rad/s}$. Total torque (using the approximation):

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 4 \mu v R^{2} w \left( \frac{1}{h_1} - \frac{1}{h_0} \right) = 4 \times 8 \times 10^{3} \times 0.5 \times 0.04 \times 1.5 \times \left( 2000 - 166.67 \right)
$$

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 960 \times 1833.33 \approx 1.76 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

$$
P = M_{\text{total}} \cdot \omega = 1.76 \times 10^{6} \times 2.5 = 4.4 \times 10^{6}\,\text{W} = 4.4\,\text{MW}
$$

This is a *very high* power — indicates the original $v$ of 0.5 m/s is too high for a 1 mm sheet at this viscosity. In practice, either viscosity would be reduced (higher temp, more plasticizer) or speed would be lowered. This is a **classic exam discussion point** on process design trade-offs.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on Calender Performance

| Parameter Increase | Effect on Throughput $Q$ | Effect on $p_{\max}$ | Effect on $F/w$ | Effect on Power $P$ | Practical Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Roll speed $v \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (linear) | $\uparrow$ (linear) | $\uparrow$ (linear) | $\uparrow \uparrow$ (linear × $\omega$) | Higher productivity but more heating needed. |
| Viscosity $\mu \uparrow$ | No effect (set by $v, h_1, w$) | $\uparrow$ (linear) | $\uparrow$ (linear) | $\uparrow$ (linear) | More power, more roll deflection. |
| Roll radius $R \uparrow$ | No direct effect on $Q$ | No direct effect on $p_{\max}$ | $\uparrow$ (linear) | $\uparrow$ (linear) | Wider nip, lower pressure gradient, but higher torque. |
| Exit half-gap $h_1 \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (linear) | $\downarrow$ (inverse) | $\downarrow$ (inverse) | $\downarrow$ (inverse) | Thicker sheet, easier to produce. |
| Sheet width $w \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (linear) | No effect | $\uparrow$ (linear) | $\uparrow$ (linear) | Wider machine, higher absolute forces. |
| Bank thickness $h_0 \uparrow$ | No effect on $Q$ | No effect on $p_{\max}$ (approximately) | Slight $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ (mild) | Easier to feed; less roll-separating force. |
| Temperature $T \uparrow$ | No effect on $Q$ | $\downarrow$ (because $\mu \downarrow$) | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Energy cost $\uparrow$, but easier calendering. |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The *negative pressure region* past the nip centerline is the cause of the well-known **"air entrainment"** defect — air gets sucked into the melt at the exit. This is why calenders often run in a *nitrogen blanket* or *vacuum hood* to prevent surface oxidation and bubble formation.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Model Paper 4)]**

*Define calendering. List any two industrial applications of calendering with the polymers used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Calendering** is a continuous polymer processing operation in which a plasticized or molten polymeric mass is passed through a series of counter-rotating, heated rolls. The polymer is squeezed into a thin film or sheet by the pressure developed at the nip between adjacent rolls. The final thickness of the sheet is determined by the inter-roll gap.

**Two industrial applications (1.5 marks each, name + polymer):**

1. **Rigid PVC (RPVC) sheets** for thermoforming applications such as pharmaceutical blister packs, food trays, and credit cards. The PVC compound is plasticized minimally and calendered at **170–190 °C** into sheets of 0.1–2 mm thickness.
2. **Plasticized PVC (PPVC) films and sheets** for upholstery, shower curtains, blood bags, stationery folders, and table covers. Highly plasticized PVC with 30–60 phr plasticizer is calendered into flexible films at lower temperatures of **150–170 °C**.

*(Acceptable alternatives: rubber sheets for tire inner liners, EVA films for solar cell encapsulation, TPU films for lamination.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *define* calendering but forget to name **specific polymers + specific products**. That costs a full mark. Always pair the process with a polymer and an end product.

---

**Q.A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model Paper 3)]**

*What is a 'bank' in a calender? Why is the bank size important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **bank** is the reservoir of molten or plasticized polymer that accumulates immediately above (and feeds) the nip between two adjacent calender rolls. It is essentially the "waiting pool" of melt that the rolls continuously draw material from as they rotate.

**Importance of bank size (1 mark each for the following three reasons):**

1. **Feed consistency:** A stable, well-sized bank ensures uniform and continuous feed of melt to the nip. A starved bank leads to thickness variation and surface defects; an oversized bank leads to melt overheating and thermal degradation.
2. **Pressure uniformity:** The bank profile (cross-sectional shape) determines the hydrostatic pressure applied to the nip entrance, which directly affects the pressure distribution and the roll separating force. An asymmetric bank causes gauge bands across the sheet width.
3. **Process stability:** Excessive bank mass can undergo "roll-over" — a centrifugal tumbling motion governed by the Bamberg number ($N_{Bb} = v^{2} \rho R / (g h_0) > 1$) — which destabilizes the entire calender line.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students describe the bank as a "pile of material" but do not connect it to **pressure and gauge control**. That loses 1 mark. The examiner expects the *process-engineering* significance, not just the geometry.

---
