### 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

---

**Solution to Q.A(a) — L-Type Calender Construction & Working (7 marks)**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**

An L-type calender consists of **four rolls** arranged in an "L" shape. From the schematic below, the rolls are designated Roll 1, Roll 2, Roll 3, and Roll 4. Rolls 1 and 2 are mounted horizontally one above the other, while Rolls 3 and 4 are stacked vertically below Roll 2. The polymer sheet enters as a *bank* between Rolls 1 and 2 (the first nip), is squeezed into a thinner sheet, then passes to the second nip (Rolls 2 and 3), third nip (Rolls 3 and 4), and finally exits horizontally as a thin sheet.

Each roll is a **hollow chilled-cast-iron or carbon-steel cylinder** with internal bores for circulating hot oil (for heating) or chilled water (for cooling). The roll surface is precision-ground and chrome-plated. The rolls are mounted in massive cast-steel bearing housings (side frames) and the nip gap is adjusted by **hydraulic rams** acting on the bearing blocks.

| Roll | Diameter Range | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Roll 1 (Top Horizontal) | 0.40–0.60 m | First reduction, may be temperature-controlled. |
| Roll 2 (Middle Horizontal) | 0.40–0.60 m | Second reduction; closest to feed. |
| Roll 3 (Middle Vertical) | 0.40–0.60 m | Third reduction; gauge-finishing roll. |
| Roll 4 (Bottom Vertical) | 0.50–0.70 m | Final nip; produces the final gauge. |

**Working (3.5 marks):**

1. The plasticized PVC compound (a "blank" or "gob" from a Banbury mixer or roll mill) is fed continuously as a strip into the **first nip (Rolls 1 & 2)**. The pressure at this nip compresses the polymer into a continuous sheet.
2. The sheet wraps around Roll 2 and is presented to the **second nip (Rolls 2 & 3)**. A **bank** forms here, controlled by the relative speed of the rolls and the feed rate.
3. The sheet is further thinned at the second and **third nips (Rolls 3 & 4)**, with each nip providing incremental thickness reduction.
4. The final sheet is **stripped from Roll 4 by a stripping roll** and conveyed to the cooling train.
5. Throughout, the rolls are heated to **160–195 °C** (depending on formulation) to maintain the polymer in a plastic state. The **drive system** (typically DC motor + gear reducer, or modern AC servo) provides synchronized rotation, with each pair of rolls having independently controllable speed for fine thickness adjustment.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Sheet thickness $h = 0.8\,\text{mm} \Rightarrow h_1 = h/2 = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- Width $w = 1.8\,\text{m}$
- Roll radius $R = 0.18\,\text{m}$
- Roll speed $N = 8\,\text{rpm} = 8/60 = 0.1333\,\text{rev/s}$
- Viscosity $\mu = 6 \times 10^{3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$

**Part (i) — Volumetric throughput (2 marks):**

Roll surface velocity:

$$
v = \pi D N = \pi \times 0.36 \times 0.1333 = 0.1507\,\text{m/s}
$$

Throughput:

$$
Q = 2 \, v \, h_1 \, w = 2 \times 0.1507 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.8
$$

$$
Q = 2.17 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 0.781\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{hr}
$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum nip pressure (2 marks):**

$$
p_{\max} = \frac{\pi \mu v}{2 h_1} = \frac{\pi \times 6 \times 10^{3} \times 0.1507}{2 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
p_{\max} = \frac{2841.6}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.55 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} \approx 3.55\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Part (iii) — Roll separating force per unit width (3 marks):**

Using the engineering approximation $F/w = 2\mu v R / h_1$:

$$
\frac{F}{w} = \frac{2 \times 6 \times 10^{3} \times 0.1507 \times 0.18}{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\frac{F}{w} = \frac{325.5}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 8.14 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/m}
$$

Total force on both rolls: $F = 8.14 \times 10^{5} \times 1.8 = 1.46 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N}$ (≈ **150 tonnes** per nip). This is in the typical industrial range.

---

**Solution to Q.B(a) — Gauge Control Methods (7 marks)**

**Roll Crowning (1.5 marks):**

A *static* crowning is imparted to the roll during manufacture by grinding the roll with a slightly convex (barrel-shaped) profile. The center of the roll is larger in diameter by **0.05–0.20 mm** compared to the ends. This compensates for the natural inward *bowing deflection* of the roll under the high separating force at the nip. It is a *fixed* compensation that works for one specific operating point (roll speed, polymer viscosity). If conditions change, the roll may be over- or under-compensated.

**Roll Bending (1.5 marks):**

A *dynamic* method. Hydraulic cylinders apply a bending moment at the roll bearing ends, effectively pushing the roll ends inward to create an "artificial crown" that can be adjusted in real time. Operators can compensate for changing viscosity, throughput, or sheet width without stopping the line. The bending force is controlled via a closed-loop feedback from the online thickness gauge.

**Roll Crossing (Cross-Axis) (2 marks):**

The axes of two adjacent rolls are set at a small angle (typically 0.5° to 1.5°) to each other instead of being perfectly parallel. This causes the nip to be *wider at the center* and *narrower at the ends*, exactly mimicking a crown effect — but in a controllable, adjustable way. The angle is set by a precise screw mechanism at the bearing housings.

**Roll Skewing (2 marks):**

One roll is shifted **axially** (along its own length) relative to its neighbor, so that the two rolls are no longer perfectly aligned along the width. This is used to *fine-tune* the gauge profile across the width — if the sheet is too thick on the right side, the right-side roll is shifted axially to compensate. The adjustment range is small (a few mm).

**Why used together? (Bonus 1 mark)**

Modern high-precision calenders use all four methods *in combination* because each method has a different *spatial frequency* of compensation:
- **Crown** → gross, fixed compensation across full width.
- **Bending** → adjustable compensation across full width.
- **Crossing** → parabolic compensation (most effect at center).
- **Skewing** → localized, asymmetric compensation (corrects one side).

A combination delivers uniform gauge across the width under all operating conditions, with no single method being sufficient by itself for the **±2% gauge tolerance** demanded by modern film and sheet specifications.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b) — Calender Accessories (7 marks)**

**1. Feed System (1 mark):**
The calender is fed by a **continuous strip of pre-mixed, plasticized polymer** (PVC compound or rubber). The feed is supplied by either an **extruder with a slot die**, a **gear pump** (for high-pressure precise metering), or a **two-roll mill** (for rubber and stiff PVC). The feed strip dimensions must match the bite length of the first nip. A **metal detector** is installed in the feed line to prevent metal contamination from damaging the polished roll surfaces.

**2. Online Thickness Gauge (1 mark):**
Modern calenders use **beta-ray** (for thin films) or **nuclear/X-ray** gauges mounted on a traversing carriage that scans across the sheet width. The gauge is non-contact and provides a real-time thickness profile, fed back to the **PLC/DCS** for closed-loop control of roll crossing, roll bending, and roll speed. The gauge resolution is typically **±1 μm**.

**3. Cooling Train (1.5 marks):**
After the final calender roll, the sheet passes over a series of **6 to 12 chrome-plated, internally water-cooled rolls** arranged alternately above and below the sheet. The sheet is cooled from **~180 °C to ~40 °C** gradually, in 5–10 °C steps, to prevent internal stresses, warping, and surface blush. The cooling rate is critical: too fast → warping; too slow → sheet distortion on windup.

**4. Embossing Unit (1.5 marks):**
For decorative products (floor coverings, leather-look upholstery), the sheet passes through an **embossing nip** consisting of a **patterned steel roll** and a **rubber counter-roll**. The pattern (wood grain, leather, geometric) is engraved on the steel roll and transferred to the still-warm PVC sheet under pressure. The embossing roll is heated to **~150 °C** to ensure a clean pattern transfer.

**5. Corona Treater (1 mark):**
PVC is naturally non-polar and has poor printability and laminatability. A **corona discharge unit** (high-voltage, high-frequency AC discharge at **~10–30 kV**) bombards the sheet surface, oxidizing it and creating polar functional groups (-OH, -COOH) that allow inks, adhesives, and laminating polymers to bond strongly. Power density is **~2–5 W·m/min** of sheet width.

**6. Windup Station (1 mark):**
The finished sheet is wound onto cardboard or plastic cores using a **center-drive winder** with two or four stations (so that one roll can be unwound while the next is being wound without line stoppage). The winder maintains **constant tension** via a **dancer roll** and **tapered torque control**, so the roll is wound hard at the core and progressively softer at the outer diameter. Maximum roll diameter can be **1.0–1.5 m**, and automatic roll-change systems allow **continuous 24/7 operation**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Skipping the sketch in Q.A(a):** A "neat sketch" is worth 1–2 marks by itself. Without it, you lose marks even if the description is perfect.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Q.A(b):** Numerical answers without units get **zero** credit in KTU evaluation. Always show $v$ in m/s, $Q$ in $\text{m}^{3}/\text{hr}$, $F$ in N.
> 3. **Mixing up Crown vs. Bending in Q.B(a):** Many students define them as the same. The examiner wants: Crown = **static, mechanical**; Bending = **dynamic, hydraulic**.
> 4. **Naming accessories without explaining their *function* in Q.B(b):** Saying "there is a corona treater" is not enough. The examiner expects *what it does and why* — e.g., "corona treater oxidizes the surface to improve print adhesion by creating polar functional groups."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your last-minute (night-before-exam) revision sheet. Tick off each item once you've revised it.**

- [ ] **Definition:** Calendering = polymer melt squeezed through counter-rotating heated rolls → thin film/sheet. Classified as *primary shaping* (melt processing).
- [ ] **Most common polymers:** Plasticized PVC (PPVC), rigid PVC (RPVC), rubber, EVA, TPU.
- [ ] **Common products:** PVC sheets/films, floor coverings, rubber sheets, coated fabrics, tire inner liners.
- [ ] **Roll configurations** (must recognize and sketch): 2-roll, 3-roll I, 4-roll I/L/F/Z, 5-roll. **L-type is the most common in industry.**
- [ ] **L-type > I-type** because L-type allows independent bank control at each nip → better gauge uniformity.
- [ ] **Key formulas (memorize, with units):**
  - $Q = 2 v h_1 w$
  - $v = \pi D N$
  - $p_{\max} = \pi \mu v / (2 h_1)$
  - $F/w = 2 \mu v R / h_1$ (for $h_0 \gg h_1$)
  - $P = 2 \pi N M$ where $M = 2 F R$
- [ ] **Gaskell pressure distribution** formula — recognize its form and the maximum pressure condition at $\theta = 0$.
- [ ] **Bank:** the melt reservoir above the nip. Bank size affects feed consistency, pressure, and stability. **Bamberg number $N_{Bb} < 1$** for stable bank.
- [ ] **Gauge control methods:** Roll crowning (static), roll bending (dynamic), cross-axis/crossing, roll skewing. **Used in combination for ±2% tolerance.**
- [ ] **Calender pressures** are typically in the range **3–15 MPa**. **Roll separating force** per nip can be **100–500 tonnes** in industrial machines.
- [ ] **Nip zones:** Convergence → peak pressure → negative pressure (risk of air entrainment) → exit. **Always degas the melt before calendering.**
- [ ] **Accessories (must know all 6 for KTU):** Feed system (extruder/gear pump/mill), online gauge (beta/nuclear), cooling train (6–12 chilled rolls), embossing unit, corona treater, windup (center drive, dancer roll).
- [ ] **Differences vs. Extrusion:** Throughput set by *roll speed*, not screw; *Pressure* generated by rolls, not screw; *Thickness* controlled by gap, not by die swell.
- [ ] **Calender vs. Roll Mill:** A roll mill (2-roll) is a *mixing/compounding* device; a calender is a *shaping* device. Some machinery (e.g., rubber calenders) blurs this distinction.
- [ ] **Material property impact:** Higher plasticizer → lower $\mu$ → lower $p_{\max}$ and lower $F$ → higher line speed possible.
- [ ] **Temperature control:** Rolls are heated (160–195 °C for PVC); cooling-train rolls are chilled. **Thermal uniformity is critical for gauge.**
- [ ] **Industrial throughput benchmark:** Modern 4-roll calenders produce up to **120 m/min** of sheet at widths up to **3.5 m**, with throughputs of **1–5 tons/hr**.
- [ ] **Common defects to recognize:** Air entrainment (negative pressure), gauge bands (asymmetric bank), fisheyes (contamination), warping (uneven cooling), emboss pattern washout (temperature mismatch), gauge variation (crown mis-set).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mantra for KTU 2024 Calender Module:**
> *"L-type calender, 4 rolls, Gaskell pressure, roll crown, gauge ±2%, banks stable, throughput set by roll speed, accessories include gauge + corona + embosser + winder."* — If you can recite this and reproduce the five formulas, you are exam-ready.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machineries for Specific Products - Tyre and Tubes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machineries for Specific Products - Tyre and Tubes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tyre: Formal Definition
A **pneumatic tyre** is a toroidal-shaped, composite, engineered rubber product mounted on the rim of a vehicle wheel. It serves to provide traction, support the vehicle load, absorb road shocks, and transmit driving and braking forces. From a polymer processing standpoint, a tyre is a multi-component, multi-material, multi-layer composite structure composed of various rubber compounds, fabric/steel reinforcements, and bead wires, all bonded together through vulcanization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Tyres are categorized as the most mechanically demanding polymer product because they combine **elastomers** (natural rubber, SBR, BR, butyl rubber), **reinforcements** (polyester, nylon, steel cords), **fillers** (carbon black, silica), **plasticizers**, and **curing agents** into a single integrated engineered component.

## 1.2 Tube: Formal Definition
A **tyre tube** is an inflatable, closed toroidal bladder made of butyl rubber (or a butyl/natural rubber blend) that retains compressed air inside a tubed tyre assembly. It is manufactured primarily through **extrusion**, **splicing**, and **vulcanization** processes.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of a tyre as a multi-storey apartment building.
> - The **rubber compounds** are the bricks and mortar.
> - The **steel belts and fabric plies** are the reinforced concrete skeleton.
> - The **bead wires** are the foundation holding the building to the ground.
> - The **inner liner** (for tubeless) or **tube** is the water tank that holds air.
> - The **vulcanization press** is the construction crew that welds everything together permanently.

Just as an apartment building requires a step-by-step construction process (foundation → walls → floors → finishing), a tyre requires a step-by-step assembly process (bead making → ply cutting → calendering → extrusion → building → curing).

## 1.4 Tyre Classification (KTU Standard)
| Tyre Type | Description | Application |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Diagonal (Bias) Tyre** | Ply cords run diagonally across the bead | Light commercial vehicles, vintage vehicles |
| **Radial Tyre** | Ply cords run radially (90° to centreline); steel belts at 0° | Modern cars, trucks, aircraft |
| **Tubeless Tyre** | Has an inner liner of halobutyl rubber; no separate tube | All modern passenger vehicles |
| **Tube Tyre** | Uses an inner tube made of butyl rubber | Two-wheelers, commercial vehicles, off-road |
| **Solid Tyre** | No air cavity; solid rubber | Forklifts, industrial carts |

## 1.5 Basic Tyre Components
A modern pneumatic tyre consists of the following key components:

1. **Tread** – Outer rubber layer that contacts the road. Provides wear resistance, traction, and groove pattern.
2. **Carcass (Body Ply)** – Layers of rubber-coated fabric cords (rayon, nylon, polyester) that form the structural backbone.
3. **Belt (Steel Belt in Radial Tyre)** – Steel cord layers positioned at low angles (12–20°) beneath the tread, restricting deformation.
4. **Bead** – A loop of high-tensile steel wire bundles coated with rubber that anchors the tyre firmly to the rim.
5. **Sidewall** – Protective rubber layer between the tread and the bead. Carries information (size, manufacturer).
6. **Inner Liner** – Airtight halobutyl rubber layer (only in tubeless tyres) that replaces the function of the tube.
7. **Apex / Bead Filler** – Rubber compound (often dual-compound) used to stiffen the lower sidewall and bead area.
8. **Chafer** – Rubber/plastic strip protecting the bead area from rim chafing.
9. **Cap Ply / Nylon Overlay** – Nylon cord layer fitted circumferentially to enhance high-speed uniformity.
10. **Splicing Tape** – Thin rubber strip used to join components during building.

## 1.6 Tyre Size Designation (KTU 2024 Standard)
Example: **195/65 R 15 91V**

| Parameter | Value | Meaning |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| 195 | Tyre width | 195 mm |
| 65 | Aspect ratio | Sidewall height = 65% of width |
| R | Radial construction | Ply orientation |
| 15 | Rim diameter | 15 inches |
| 91 | Load index | 615 kg per tyre |
| V | Speed symbol | Up to 240 km/h |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tyre Manufacturing Process Flow
The tyre manufacturing process is divided into **two major stages** based on the type of raw material being processed:

### Stage A: Component Preparation (Pre-Building)
1. **Masterbatch and Compound Mixing** (Internal mixer / Banbury)
2. **Bead Wire Processing** (Bead winding and assembly)
3. **Ply / Cord Fabric Preparation** (Calendering and cutting)
4. **Tread and Sidewall Extrusion** (Hot feed / cold feed extruders)
5. **Inner Liner and Apex Preparation**

### Stage B: Tyre Assembly and Curing
6. **First-stage Tyre Building** (Drum builder for radial/bias)
7. **Second-stage Tyre Building** (Passenger and truck building machines)
8. **Curing / Vulcanization** (Curing press / autoclave)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Focus:** The examiner often asks students to draw a **flowchart of tyre manufacturing machinery** along with the process step. A clear, labelled process flow chart is mandatory for full marks.

## 2.2 Machinery for Compound Preparation

### 2.2.1 Internal Mixer (Banbury Mixer)
- **Type:** Batch mixer with two counter-rotating rotors inside a closed chamber.
- **Capacity:** 50–650 litres.
- **Function:** Intensive mixing of rubber with carbon black, oils, curatives, and additives.
- **Operational parameters:**
  - Rotor speed: 30–80 rpm
  - Mixing temperature: 120–160 °C
  - Pressure on batch: 0.3–0.6 MPa
- **Discharge:** Drop-door or sliding-door for batch release onto a two-roll mill.

### 2.2.2 Two-Roll Mill (Open Mill)
- Used for **warming up**, **plasticizing**, and **sheeting** mixed compounds.
- Roll diameter: 400–900 mm; friction ratio: 1:1.1 to 1:1.3.

## 2.3 Machinery for Bead Manufacturing

### 2.3.1 Bead Winder / Bead Ring Assembly Machine
- **Input:** Spools of high-tensile steel wire (0.96–1.83 mm diameter).
- **Process:** Multiple strands of bead wire (typically 8–30 wires) are pulled through a **die (former)** and wound in a hexagonal or hexagonal-with-crown cross-section.
- **Output:** A continuous bead ring of exact diameter.
- **Equipment:** **Bead winding machine** with multiple spools, tension control, and a rotating/folding die head.

### 2.3.2 Bead Wrapping / Bead Covering Extruder
- A cross-head extruder applies a layer of **rubber cover compound** around the bead ring to:
  - Encapsulate the wire bundle.
  - Provide cushion between bead and rim.
  - Bond with adjacent components during curing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** Bead wires are **NOT** extruded; they are **mechanically wound** with high tension (50–200 N) and then covered with rubber via cross-head extrusion.

## 2.4 Machinery for Calendering (Ply / Cord Fabric Production)

### 2.4.1 Calender
- A calender is a precision machine with **3, 4, or 5 heated rolls** used to produce thin, uniform rubber sheets or to coat fabric/steel cord with rubber.
- **Types:**
  - **Sheeting calender** – Produces unsupported rubber sheets (used for inner liner, sidewall).
  - **Frictioning calender** – Coats fabric with high-friction rubber to penetrate cord interstices.
  - **Skimming calender** – Coats fabric with a thin rubber skin (light coating).

### 2.4.2 Key Calender Parameters
| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| Roll temperature | 70–110 °C | Affects rubber viscosity and adhesion |
| Linear load | 200–500 N/mm | Determines gauge and penetration |
| Roll speed | 5–80 m/min | Affects throughput |
| Roll configuration | L, F, Z, I | Determines fabric path |

## 2.5 Machinery for Ply Cutting
- **Ply cutter** uses a **drum or table-type** design with rotating cutting blades.
- **Bias cutter:** Cuts fabric at an angle (typically 30°–60° to the cord direction) for diagonal tyres.
- **Radial cutter:** Cuts fabric at 90° (square cut) for radial tyres.
- The cut pieces are called **plies**.

## 2.6 Machinery for Tread and Sidewall Extrusion

### 2.6.1 Cold Feed Extruder
- A modern L/D 16:1 to 20:1 extruder with a **vacuum vent** and **temperature-controlled barrel**.
- **Output:** Continuous strips of tread, sidewall, apex, and chafer compounds.
- **Die:** Pre-form die with multiple components to produce intricate cross-sections.

### 2.6.2 Hot Feed Extruder (Legacy)
- Uses pre-warmed compound from a separate strip feed unit.
- Less common in modern plants but still used in some tube and retread applications.

## 2.7 Machinery for Tyre Building (Most Critical Step)

### 2.7.1 First Stage Builder (Drum Builder)
- A horizontal **rotating collapsible drum** of variable diameter (typically 200–1100 mm).
- **For Radial Tyre Construction (RFT method):**
  - Step 1: First ply (radial) is wrapped on the drum.
  - Step 2: Beads are placed.
  - Step 3: Turn-up of first ply around the bead.
  - Step 4: Second ply applied.
  - Step 5: Jointless belt (JLB) construction – belts applied using a belt drum expander.
- **For Bias Tyre:** Multiple plies cut on the bias are wrapped in sequence on the drum.

### 2.7.2 Second Stage Builder (Green Tyre Builder)
- **Function:** Shapes the first-stage cylindrical "green tyre" into a toroidal shape.
- **Mechanism:** Shaping bladder inflates and pushes the carcass outward, forcing the green tyre to take its final shape.
- **Output:** A "green" (un-cured) tyre ready for vulcanization.

### 2.7.3 Modern Automated Tyre Building Machines
Examples: **MMR-1, MMR-2, MIRS (Michelin Integrated Robotized System), VMI-Maxx**, and **Hosea Build System**.
- These use **robotic gantries** and **servo-controlled drum and belt stations**.
- Output: 1 tyre every 30–60 seconds for passenger tyres.

## 2.8 Machinery for Curing / Vulcanization

### 2.8.1 Tyre Curing Press (Most Important for KTU)
- **Configuration:** Hydraulic or mechanical (toggle) press with a heated mould.
- **Mould segments:** Top mould, bottom mould, and side moulds (multiple).
- **Heating medium:** Steam, hot water, electric heating, or induction.
- **Curing parameters:**
  - Temperature: 150–200 °C
  - Pressure: 100–250 psi (moulding pressure)
  - Curing time: 8–30 minutes (depending on size)
  - Bag/bladder pressure: 15–35 bar (for shaping)

### 2.8.2 Curing Process Steps
1. Green tyre loaded into the press.
2. Mould closes and bladder is inflated.
3. Heat transfer (from mould + bladder) vulcanizes rubber.
4. After cure, bladder deflates, mould opens, and cured tyre is ejected.
5. **Post-cure inflation** in a **post-cure inflator** for radial tyres to cool and set dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curing Equation (KTU High-Yield):**
> 
> $$\boxed{C = T \times \log t}$$
> 
> Where:
> - $C$ = **Cure constant** (rubber-specific, depends on compound)
> - $T$ = **Curing temperature** (absolute, in Kelvin or Rankine)
> - $t$ = **Cure time** (minutes)
> 
> This shows that cure time decreases exponentially as temperature increases. Typical: for every 10 °C rise in cure temperature, the cure time is roughly halved.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Formula / Concept | Variables | KTU Application |
|------|-------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Cure relationship: $C = T \cdot \log t$ | $C$ = cure constant, $T$ = temperature, $t$ = time | Predicting cure time at different temperatures |
| 2 | Pressure inside curing bladder: $P_{bladder} = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cdot t_{ply}}{R_{cavity}}$ | $\sigma$ = stress, $t_{ply}$ = ply thickness, $R$ = radius | Calculating bladder pressure for green tyre shaping |
| 3 | Friction ratio: $FR = \dfrac{V_{fast}}{V_{slow}}$ | Velocities of fast and slow rolls | Two-roll mill design |
| 4 | Aspect ratio: $AR = \dfrac{H_{sidewall}}{W_{tread}}$ | $H$ = height, $W$ = width | Tyre classification |
| 5 | Throughput (extrusion): $Q = \alpha \cdot N - \beta \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu}$ | $N$ = screw speed, $\Delta P$ = pressure, $\mu$ = viscosity | Tread/sidewall output |
| 6 | Bead wire tension: $T_{total} = n \cdot T_{wire}$ | $n$ = number of wires, $T_{wire}$ = per-wire tension | Bead design |
| 7 | Ply angle: $\theta = 90° - \alpha$ (for radial) | $\theta$ = cut angle, $\alpha$ = cord angle | Ply cutter settings |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** The two most commonly tested formulas in this module are **(i) the cure equation** and **(ii) the friction ratio of the two-roll mill.** Memorize these for certain marks.

## 2.10 Machinery for Inner Tube Manufacturing

### 2.10.1 Tube Extrusion Line
- **Type:** Cold feed extruder with L/D 20:1, vacuum vented, equipped with a **tube die**.
- **Die design:** A pin-and-bus die producing a continuous extruded tube profile.
- **Line speed:** 5–25 m/min.

### 2.10.2 Splicing Unit
- The continuous extruded tube is cut and **spliced (joined) into a torus** using:
  - **Butt splice** with reinforcement patch, or
  - **Vulcanized overlap splice** with a vulcanizing solution.
- Splicing may be done on a **splicing lathe** or by a **butt-jointing machine**.

### 2.10.3 Tube Curing
- Tubes are vulcanized in **steam-heated autoclaves** or **mandrel-type curing presses**.
- **Curing conditions:** 150–170 °C, 60–100 psi steam pressure, 8–15 minutes cure time.
- Tubes are often cured on a **mandrel** (a metal mould shaped like the inner contour of the tube) to maintain dimension and uniformity.

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive industry:** Tyre manufacturing represents ~70% of global rubber consumption.
- **Aviation:** Aircraft tyres require specialized curing presses with multiple heating zones.
- **Off-the-road (OTR) tyres:** Require massive building drums (3–5 m diameter) and curing presses capable of 500+ tons clamping force.
- **Sustainability:** Modern "Green Tyre Technology" uses silica compounds, lower rolling resistance, and extended cure life.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cure Time as a Function of Temperature

The **cure equation** for rubber is a classical application of the **Arrhenius reaction kinetics** to crosslinking reactions in elastomers.

**Starting equation (assumed):**

$$C = T \cdot \log t$$

Where:
- $C$ = cure constant (a rubber-specific number, e.g., 36 for NR)
- $T$ = temperature in °C (or in absolute units depending on convention)
- $t$ = cure time in minutes

**Step 1: We are given two cure conditions:**
- Condition 1: $T_1 = 140\,°C$, $t_1 = 30\,\text{min}$
- Condition 2: $T_2 = 150\,°C$, $t_2 = ?$

**Step 2: Apply the cure equation at both conditions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= T_1 \cdot \log t_1 \\
C_2 &= T_2 \cdot \log t_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Since it is the same rubber, $C_1 = C_2$. Equate the two:**

$$T_1 \cdot \log t_1 = T_2 \cdot \log t_2$$

**Step 4: Solve for $t_2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log t_2 &= \frac{T_1}{T_2} \cdot \log t_1 \\
t_2 &= 10^{\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2} \cdot \log t_1\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log t_2 &= \frac{140}{150} \cdot \log(30) \\
\log t_2 &= 0.9333 \cdot 1.4771 \\
\log t_2 &= 1.3786 \\
t_2 &= 10^{1.3786} \\
t_2 &\approx 23.92 \, \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A 10 °C increase in cure temperature reduces the cure time from 30 minutes to approximately **24 minutes**, a reduction of ~20%. This is consistent with rubber industry observations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Two-Roll Mill Friction Ratio

**Given:**
- Speed of fast roll: $V_f = 30\,\text{m/min}$
- Speed of slow roll: $V_s = 25\,\text{m/min}$

**Step 1: Definition of Friction Ratio (FR):**

$$FR = \frac{V_f}{V_s}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
FR &= \frac{30}{25} \\
FR &= 1.2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Significance:**
- $FR > 1$ creates **friction** between the two rolls, which generates **shear stress** in the rubber compound.
- A higher FR gives better mixing and dispersion but increases energy consumption and roll wear.
- Standard friction ratio for rubber compounding: **1:1.1 to 1:1.3**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Tyre Aspect Ratio

**Given:**
- Tyre width: $W = 205\,\text{mm}$
- Sidewall height: $H = 55\,\text{mm}$

**Step 1: Aspect ratio formula:**

$$AR = \frac{H}{W} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2: Substitute values:**

$$AR = \frac{55}{205} \times 100\% = 26.83\%$$

**Step 3: Interpretation:**
- This tyre would be classified as a "low-profile" tyre (AR < 50).
- Low-profile tyres offer better handling and steering response.

## 3.4 Derivation: Bead Wire Total Tension

**Given:**
- Number of bead wires: $n = 14$
- Tension per wire: $T_{wire} = 80\,\text{N}$

**Step 1: Total bead tension formula:**

$$T_{total} = n \cdot T_{wire}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values:**

$$T_{total} = 14 \times 80 = 1120\,\text{N}$$

**Step 3: Significance:** This total tension determines the **bead fit tightness** on the rim and the **resistance to bead unseating** during cornering. Higher tension means better bead-to-rim grip, but excessive tension can cause wire breakage.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Diagonal vs Radial Tyre Manufacturing

| Parameter | Diagonal (Bias) Tyre | Radial Tyre |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------|
| Number of plies | 4–8 | 1–2 (main carcass) |
| Cord angle (with centreline) | 30–40° | 90° (radial) |
| Belt structure | None (sometimes 2) | 2–4 steel belts at 12–20° |
| Building machine | Drum builder | First-stage + second-stage builder |
| Sidewall flex | High | Independent (steel belts stabilize tread) |
| Heat build-up | Higher | Lower |
| Tyre life | Lower | Higher (~30–40% more) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Curing pressure required | Lower | Higher |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Tyre vs Tube Manufacturing Machinery

| Process Step | Tyre Machinery | Tube Machinery |
|--------------|----------------|----------------|
| Raw material prep | Banbury mixer + open mill | Banbury mixer + open mill |
| Forming | Calender + ply cutter + tread extruder | Cold feed tube extruder |
| Assembly | Drum builder + second-stage builder | Splicing lathe / butt-jointing machine |
| Curing | Curing press (hydraulic/toggle) | Autoclave or mandrel press |
| Inspection | X-ray + uniformity + visual | Air retention + dimension + visual |
| Final product | Torus with tread, plies, belts, bead | Torus of butyl rubber (no internal components) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

Use **rectangular boxes** for unit operations and **arrows** for material flow as demonstrated above.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Ply Angle" in a Tyre. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **ply angle** (or cord angle) is the acute angle formed between the centreline of the tyre and the direction of the reinforcement cords in a ply. In a **diagonal (bias) tyre**, the ply angle is typically **30°–40°**, while in a **radial tyre**, the ply angle is **90°** (cord runs radially from bead to bead). Lower ply angles give higher sidewall stiffness, while higher angles provide better cushioning but lower steering response. (Word limit: 80–100)

### Question 2: What is a Curing Bladder? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **curing bladder** is a flexible, heat-resistant, pressurized rubber bladder (made of butyl or EPDM rubber) mounted inside a tyre curing press. Its function is to:
1. **Inflate** inside the green tyre to press the unvulcanized rubber compound against the heated mould.
2. **Shape** the green tyre into the final toroidal configuration.
3. **Transfer heat** from the inside to vulcanize the inner liner and inner plies.

It operates at pressures of **15–35 bar** and temperatures of **150–200 °C**, and can perform **300–500 cycles** before replacement. (Word limit: 80–100)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Tyre Manufacturing Process [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Q: With a neat flow chart, explain the various unit operations and machineries used in the manufacture of a pneumatic radial passenger car tyre.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compound Mixing (1 mark)**
- Raw materials (NR, SBR, BR, carbon black, oils, sulfur, accelerators) are mixed in a **Banbury internal mixer** (capacity 200–300 L) at 120–160 °C, followed by **two-roll mill** sheeting.

**Step 2: Bead Manufacturing (1 mark)**
- High-tensile steel wires (0.96–1.83 mm, 14–30 wires per bead) are wound into a **hexagonal ring** on a **bead winding machine**, then covered with rubber in a **bead covering extruder** (cross-head die).

**Step 3: Calendering and Ply Cutting (1 mark)**
- Fabric (rayon, nylon, polyester) is coated with rubber on a **3-roll or 4-roll calender** (frictioning + skim coating).
- Coated fabric is cut into plies at 90° (radial) on a **bias cutter / radial cutter**.

**Step 4: Tread and Sidewall Extrusion (1 mark)**
- Compound is fed into a **cold feed extruder** (L/D 20:1, vented) with a **pre-form die** to produce tread, sidewall, and apex strips of exact cross-section.

**Step 5: First-Stage Building (1.5 marks)**
- On a **collapsible drum builder**, the first radial ply is wrapped, beads are placed, the ply is turned up around the beads, and a second ply is added. Steel belts and nylon overlay (cap ply) are applied using a **belt drum expander**.

**Step 6: Second-Stage Shaping (1 mark)**
- The cylindrical green tyre is shaped into a torus on a **second-stage builder** using a **shaping bladder** that inflates to push the carcass outward.

**Step 7: Curing (0.5 marks)**
- The green tyre is vulcanized in a **hydraulic curing press** at 150–200 °C, 100–250 psi, for 8–30 minutes.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Q: Explain the construction of a tyre curing press. List the different heating methods used.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Frame and Clamping System (2 marks)**
- The press consists of a **heavy cast iron or fabricated steel frame** with top and bottom platens.
- The clamping mechanism is either:
  - **Mechanical (toggle) press** – using a mechanical linkage for fast closing.
  - **Hydraulic press** – using hydraulic cylinders for high and uniform clamping force (50–500 tons).
- For OTR (off-the-road) tyres, **hydraulic presses** with clamping force of 2000+ tons are used.

**Step 2: Mould Assembly (2 marks)**
- The mould is a **segmented construction** with:
  - **Top mould** (with tread pattern and side details).
  - **Bottom mould**.
  - **Side mould segments** (8–24 segments) that move horizontally to open the mould.
- The mould is made of **high-quality forged or cast steel** (often aluminium for OTR) and is chrome-plated for wear resistance.
- **Tread pattern, sidewall markings, and size designation** are engraved on the mould.

**Step 3: Curing Bladder (1.5 marks)**
- An **EPDM or butyl rubber bladder** is mounted in the lower mould.
- It is filled with **hot water, steam, or nitrogen** at 15–35 bar and 180–210 °C.
- Functions: shaping, internal pressure, and internal heating.

**Step 4: Heating System (1 mark)**
Heating methods:
- **Steam heating** (most common): hollow mould passages with high-pressure steam.
- **Hot water heating** (for better heat transfer in some applications).
- **Electric heating** (cartridge heaters in mould).
- **Induction heating** (advanced, used in some high-precision presses).
- **Platen heating** (heating the entire press platen with hot oil circulation).

**Step 5: Control System (0.5 marks)**
- Modern presses use a **PLC-based control system** with:
  - **Temperature sensors** (RTD / thermocouples).
  - **Pressure sensors** (for bladder and clamping).
  - **Timer module** for cure time.
  - **HMI screen** for operator interface.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Tube Manufacturing and Curing Concepts [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Q: Describe the manufacturing process of an inner tube for a two-wheeler tyre, with a detailed explanation of the extrusion, splicing, and curing steps.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compound Preparation (1 mark)**
- Butyl rubber (IIR) is mixed with carbon black, plasticizer, curing agents in a **Banbury mixer** (or intermix), then sheeted on a **two-roll mill**.
- Butyl is preferred over NR for its **low air permeability** (~10× lower than NR).

**Step 2: Extrusion (2 marks)**
- The compound is fed into a **cold feed extruder** (L/D 20:1, vacuum vented, barrel temperature 50–90 °C).
- A **tube die (pin-and-bus type)** produces a continuous, seamless, hollow tube profile.
- **Line speed:** 8–20 m/min.
- **Wall thickness:** 1.5–3.0 mm.
- **Bore diameter:** 30–150 mm (depending on tyre size).
- The extruded tube is cooled in a **water bath / spray cooling system**.

**Step 3: Splicing (2 marks)**
- The continuous tube is cut into precise lengths.
- Each length is formed into a **torus** by joining the two ends:
  - **Method 1: Butt splice with patch** – ends are cut square, joined using a vulcanizing rubber solution, and reinforced with an internal patch.
  - **Method 2: Overlap splice** – ends overlapped by 25–40 mm and bonded with a thin layer of unvulcanized rubber.
- Splicing is performed on a **splicing lathe** or **butt-jointing machine** with high precision to ensure joint strength and air-tightness.

**Step 4: Curing (1.5 marks)**
- Tubes are loaded on **aluminium mandrels** (shaped to the exact inner contour of the finished tube) and placed in an **autoclave** (steam-heated pressure vessel).
- **Curing conditions:** 150–170 °C, steam pressure 60–100 psi, cure time 8–15 minutes.
- Alternative: **Mandrel-type curing press** for high-volume production.

**Step 5: Inspection and Finishing (0.5 marks)**
- Tubes are deflashed, dimensionally checked, and tested for **air retention** (24-hour pressure test).
- Packaged with valve cores pre-fitted.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Q: Compare diagonal (bias) and radial ply tyre construction with respect to: (i) cord orientation, (ii) number of plies, (iii) sidewall flexibility, (iv) heat build-up, and (v) typical applications.**

**Model Solution:**

| S.No | Parameter | Diagonal (Bias) Tyre | Radial Tyre |
|------|-----------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Cord orientation** | Plies cross diagonally at 30–40° to centreline | Plies run radially (90° to centreline) |
| 2 | **Number of plies** | 4–8 fabric plies | 1–2 fabric plies + 2–4 steel belts |
| 3 | **Sidewall flexibility** | Sidewall and tread flex together | Independent (steel belts stabilize tread; sidewall flexes) |
| 4 | **Heat build-up** | Higher (inter-ply friction) | Lower (less inter-ply shear) |
| 5 | **Tyre life** | 30,000–50,000 km | 50,000–1,00,000 km |
| 6 | **Fuel efficiency** | Lower | Higher (10–15% improvement) |
| 7 | **Steering response** | Slower | Sharper and quicker |
| 8 | **Comfort** | Better (softer sidewall) | Slightly harsher |
| 9 | **Load capacity** | Lower | Higher (steel belts distribute load) |
| 10 | **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| 11 | **Typical applications** | Commercial vehicles, two-wheelers, off-road | Modern cars, trucks, aircraft, high-performance vehicles |
| 12 | **Speed capability** | Lower | Higher |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Ply Angle" with "Belt Angle."** A radial tyre has ply at 90° BUT belts at 12–20°. Marks are deducted when students incorrectly state the belt angle.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse "Tube" with "Inner Liner."** A tube is a separate component, but an inner liner is part of a tubeless tyre and is made of halobutyl rubber.
> 3. **Curing time calculation:** Always show substitution of values. Skipping the calculation step loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Diagrams without arrows and labels** = -2 to -3 marks. Always annotate the flow direction in flow charts.
> 5. **For tube manufacturing**, the die is a **pin-and-bus type**, not a "sheet die" or "profile die." Examiner deducts marks for incorrect die nomenclature.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 - Tyre and Tubes:**

- **Tyre = Toroidal composite product** made of rubber + fabric + steel + chemicals.
- **Tube = Inflatable butyl rubber bladder** used only in tubed tyres.
- **Tyre components:** Tread, Carcass, Belt, Bead, Sidewall, Inner Liner, Apex, Chafer, Cap Ply.
- **Diagonal (Bias) tyre:** Ply angle 30–40°; no steel belt; cheaper; lower performance.
- **Radial tyre:** Ply angle 90°; steel belt at 12–20°; higher performance; longer life.
- **Tyre size code:** 195/65 R 15 → 195 mm width, 65% aspect ratio, Radial, 15-inch rim.
- **Compound mixing:** Banbury internal mixer + two-roll open mill.
- **Bead making:** Bead winding machine (NOT extrusion) + bead covering extruder.
- **Calendering:** 3, 4, or 5 roll; produces rubber-coated fabric or rubber sheets.
- **Ply cutting:** 90° (radial) or 30–40° (diagonal).
- **Tread/Sidewall extrusion:** Cold feed extruder, L/D 20:1, vented.
- **Tyre building:** Two-stage: First-stage drum builder + Second-stage shaper.
- **Curing press:** Hydraulic or toggle; mould segments; curing bladder; 150–200 °C; 100–250 psi; 8–30 min.
- **Cure equation:** $C = T \cdot \log t$ (most tested formula).
- **Friction ratio:** $FR = V_{fast}/V_{slow}$ (typically 1:1.1 to 1:1.3).
- **Inner tube process:** Banbury → Mill → Cold feed extruder with pin-bus die → Splicing (butt/overlap) → Mandrel loading → Autoclave curing (150–170 °C) → Inspection.
- **Butyl rubber (IIR):** Low air permeability, hence preferred for inner tubes and inner liners.
- **Curing bladder:** EPDM/butyl; 15–35 bar; 180–210 °C; shapes + heats tyre from inside.
- **Post-cure inflator:** Cools and sets dimensions of radial tyres after curing.
- **Modern trends:** Silica-based "green tyres," MIRS/MMR automated building, RFID-embedded tyres.
- **Key distinctions:** Curing ≠ Vulcanization (often used interchangeably); Curing press ≠ Compression moulding press; Bead wire ≠ Tyre reinforcement (bead is at the rim interface).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Liner Memory Aid for KTU 2024 Exam:**
> *"MIX → CALENDER → CUT → EXTRUDE → BUILD → CURE → INSPECT = TYRE."*
> *"MIX → EXTRUDE → SPLICE → MANDREL → AUTOCLAVE = TUBE."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Footwear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Footwear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Footwear** in the polymer processing domain refers to the engineering discipline dedicated to the design, material selection, and manufacture of shoes, boots, sandals, and other protective wear in which **synthetic polymers and elastomers** form the structural, aesthetic, and functional backbone. According to KTU 2024 syllabus, polymer footwear processing encompasses the conversion of raw polymers—primarily **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride), PU (Polyurethane), TPR (Thermoplastic Rubber), TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane), EVA (Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate), SBS (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene), and Natural/Synthetic Rubber compounds**—into finished footwear components such as soles, uppers, insoles, and heel units through a sequence of polymer-specific shaping and assembly operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Note:** Footwear manufacturing is the largest single consumer of **plasticized PVC** in the world and the second largest consumer of **polyurethane** systems after the automotive industry.

### 1.2 Component Architecture of a Polymer Shoe

A finished shoe is an **assembly of engineered components**, each manufactured using a specific polymer process. The major components are:

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **Upper** | Covers the foot; provides aesthetics and breathability | Leather, fabric, synthetic upper coated with PU/PVC |
| **Sole (Outsole)** | Ground contact; provides traction and wear resistance | PVC, TPR, TPU, Vulcanized Rubber, PU |
| **Midsole** | Cushioning layer between insole and outsole | EVA, PU Foam, Phylon (compressed EVA) |
| **Insole (Sock)** | Foot-bed comfort | EVA, Foam, Leather board |
| **Heel** | Elevation and stability | ABS, TPU, Wood-PVC composite |
| **Adhesives** | Bond upper to sole | PU adhesive, Neoprene, Hot-melt |
| **Toe-cap / Counter** | Shape retention at front/back | Thermoplastic sheets, Celastic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — The Three-Tier Material Strategy:** Modern footwear uses a layered material strategy:
> - **Soft inner layer** (EVA/foam) for comfort
> - **Mid structural layer** (PU) for energy return
> - **Hard outer layer** (TPR/Rubber) for abrasion resistance

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Sandwich Analogy":** Think of a polymer shoe like a **layered sandwich**:
> - The **bread** is the hard rubber/TPR outsole (crunchy, protective).
> - The **cheese** is the soft PU/EVA midsole (cushioning, bouncy).
> - The **filling** is the upper that wraps the foot (flexible, breathable).
> - The **toothpick holding it together** is the adhesive bond between upper and sole.
>
> Just as a sandwich requires different ingredients assembled in a specific order, a shoe requires different polymer parts manufactured by **different processes** and then **assembled on a "last"** (a foot-shaped mold) in a fixed sequence.

### 1.4 Why Polymers Dominate Footwear

The **global footwear market** exceeds **$400 billion**, with polymers (including rubber) accounting for over **65%** of material mass in a typical sports shoe. Key drivers:

1. **Design freedom** — Polymers can be molded into complex ergonomic shapes impossible with wood/leather.
2. **Mass production** — Injection molding enables **thousands of pairs per shift**.
3. **Tunable properties** — Hardness (Shore A 30 to Shore D 80), density, color, and flexibility can be engineered.
4. **Cost efficiency** — PVC and EVA soles cost a fraction of leather equivalents.
5. **Water resistance & durability** — PVC and PU uppers outperform natural materials in wet environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Footwear by Manufacturing Process

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically stresses the **process-based classification** of footwear:

#### A. Direct Injection Molding Process (DIP)

This is the **most common modern process** for casual and sports shoes. The PVC/PU/TPR melt is injected directly onto the upper that has been clamped to a metal **last (aluminum foot-mold)**.

**Operational Sequence:**
1. **Last Preparation** — Aluminum lasts are cleaned and release-agent coated.
2. **Upper Lasting** — The upper is stretched over the last and clamped.
3. **Mold Closing** — The last + upper is placed into a two-plate sole-mold.
4. **Injection** — Molten polymer (PVC paste or TPR melt) is injected at **80–200 bar** into the sole cavity.
5. **Cooling & Curing** — Held under pressure for 30–90 seconds.
6. **Demolding** — The shoe is stripped from the last, trimmed, and finished.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** In **Direct PVC Injection (Slush Molding variant)**, PVC plastisol (paste) is used. The mold is rotated and heated to **180–200 °C**, causing the paste to gel and fuse against the mold wall forming the sole. This is called the **"DIP process"** (Direct Injection Process) and is the industry standard for school shoes and rain boots.

#### B. PVC Slush Molding (for Boots)

Used for **gumboots, Wellington boots, and fashion boots**.

**Process Steps:**
1. PVC plastisol (liquid dispersion of PVC resin in plasticizer) is poured into a heated, rotating mold.
2. Mold is rotated biaxially (in two planes) for 8–15 minutes at **180–200 °C**.
3. The plastisol gels on the mold wall to the desired thickness.
4. Excess liquid plastisol is poured out.
5. Mold is reheated to fuse the gelled layer.
6. Boot is stripped, trimmed, and assembled with the upper.

#### C. Calendering Process (for PVC Sheet Soles)

PVC compound is passed through a series of **heated rollers (calender)** to produce continuous sheets of controlled thickness (**1 mm to 12 mm**). The sheets are die-cut into sole blanks and cemented to uppers.

#### D. Compression Molding

Used for **vulcanized rubber soles** and **PU shoe soles**.
- Pre-weighed rubber compound is placed in a heated mold.
- Mold closes at **100–200 bar**, temperature **150–170 °C**.
- Vulcanization time: **3–8 minutes** depending on sole thickness.

#### E. Extrusion

Used to produce **continuous TPR/PVC profiles** for shoe welts, edge strips, and decorative trims.

#### F. Assembly / Lasting Operation

After sole manufacture, the upper is attached by:
- **Cementing (cold/hot)** — Adhesive bonding
- **Strobel stitching** — Insole stitched to upper
- **Moccasin construction** — Bottom seam
- **Vulcanized construction** — Rubber directly vulcanized to upper (as in classic sneakers)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Equation | Formula | Units | Application |
|----------------------|---------|-------|-------------|
| **Shore Hardness to Young's Modulus (empirical)** | $E \approx \dfrac{0.0981 \times (56 + 7.66 \cdot S)}{0.137505 \times (145 - 5.66 \cdot S)}$ | MPa | Predicting flex from Shore A |
| **Compression Set (CS)** | $CS = \dfrac{h_0 - h_1}{h_0 - h_s} \times 100$ | % | Sponge/midsole recovery |
| **Abrasion Resistance (DIN)** | $V_{loss} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho}$ | mm³ | Sole wear testing |
| **Injection Molding Clamp Force** | $F_{clamp} = P_{inj} \times A_{proj} \div 10$ | kN | DIP machine sizing |
| **Plastisol Viscosity (Brookfield)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Pa·s | Slush molding control |
| **Gelation Temperature (PVC Plastisol)** | $T_{gel} \approx 80\text{–}100$ | °C | Slush molding onset |
| **Fusion Temperature (PVC Plastisol)** | $T_{fusion} \approx 160\text{–}200$ | °C | Slush molding completion |
| **Mold Cooling Time (empirical)** | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\!\left(\dfrac{T_{melt} - T_{mold}}{T_{eject} - T_{mold}}\right)$ | s | DIP cycle estimation |
| **Cushion Energy (EVA midsole)** | $E_c = \int_0^{\delta} F \, dx$ | J | Impact absorption |
| **Density of EVA foam (foaming)** | $\rho_f = \rho_{poly} \times \dfrac{1}{1 + R}$ | g/cm³ | Where R = expansion ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Shore A Scale** is the standard for soft elastomers (30–95 Shore A for shoe soles). **Shore D** is used for rigid plastics like heel counters and toe caps. A typical sports shoe sole uses **55–70 Shore A** for an optimal hardness-comfort balance.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sport shoe industry** (Nike, Adidas, Puma) uses **PU/EVA** midsoles produced by direct injection and compression molding.
- **Safety footwear** (steel-toe boots) uses **TPU/TPR** outsoles with Shore A 75–85 for slip resistance and oil resistance (tested per **IS 15298 / EN ISO 20345**).
- **Fashion boots** (gumboots, rain boots) use **PVC slush molding** as the sole forming method.
- **Cost-effective school shoes** are mass-produced via **PVC DIP process** in factories producing **>10,000 pairs/day**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem: Cycle Time Estimation for Direct PVC Injection of a Sole

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A PVC sole of thickness $h = 12 \text{ mm}$ is to be produced by Direct Injection Molding. The melt temperature is $T_{melt} = 190\,^\circ\text{C}$, the mold surface temperature is $T_{mold} = 40\,^\circ\text{C}$, and the ejection temperature is $T_{eject} = 80\,^\circ\text{C}$. The thermal diffusivity of solidified PVC is $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Estimate the cooling time per cycle. (Assume **1-D transient heat conduction** through a slab of thickness $h$.)

**Step 1 — Define the Biot-number-based slab model**
The characteristic cooling time of a slab of half-thickness $L = h/2$ cooled from both faces is given by the lumped/transient formula:

$$
t_{cool} = \dfrac{L^2}{\pi^2 \, \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_{melt} - T_{mold}}{T_{eject} - T_{mold}}\right)
$$

> **Logic:** This equation arises from the 1-D transient conduction solution for a plane slab (Carslaw & Jaeger). The temperature distribution inside the slab is treated as a series; the **first-term approximation** gives the above closed form, valid when the **centerline temperature** must drop to $T_{eject}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values**

$$
L = \dfrac{h}{2} = \dfrac{12 \text{ mm}}{2} = 6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
L^2 = (6 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 36 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature ratio**

$$
T_{melt} - T_{mold} = 190 - 40 = 150\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
T_{eject} - T_{mold} = 80 - 40 = 40\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{150}{40} = 3.75
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the natural logarithm**

$$
\ln(3.75) \approx 1.3218
$$

**Step 5 — Plug into the cooling-time formula**

$$
t_{cool} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{(\pi^2)(1.2 \times 10^{-7})} \times 1.3218
$$

$$
\pi^2 \approx 9.8696
$$

$$
\pi^2 \cdot \alpha = (9.8696)(1.2 \times 10^{-7}) = 1.1844 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
t_{cool} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{1.1844 \times 10^{-6}} \times 1.3218
$$

$$
\dfrac{3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{1.1844 \times 10^{-6}} = 30.4 \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_{cool} = 30.4 \times 1.3218 \approx 40.2 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Result and Engineering Interpretation**

$$
\boxed{t_{cool} \approx 40.2 \text{ seconds}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** With injection time (~5 s), curing/gelling (~15 s), demolding (~3 s), and finishing, the **total DIP cycle ≈ 60–70 seconds**, giving a productivity of ~50 pairs/hour per mold station. A 24-station rotary machine can thus produce ~1200 pairs/hour, illustrating why DIP is the **industry standard for mass-market footwear**.

### 3.2 Analytical Problem: Compression Set of an EVA Midsole

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
An EVA midsole is compressed in a flex test. Initial thickness $h_0 = 18 \text{ mm}$. It is held between spacers of total height $h_s = 12 \text{ mm}$ for 6 hours at 50 °C. After 30 minutes recovery, the thickness is $h_1 = 16.8 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the **Compression Set (CS)**.

**Step 1 — Apply the standard CS formula**

$$
CS = \dfrac{h_0 - h_1}{h_0 - h_s} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$
h_0 - h_1 = 18 - 16.8 = 1.2 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
h_0 - h_s = 18 - 12 = 6.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio**

$$
CS = \dfrac{1.2}{6.0} \times 100\% = 0.2 \times 100\% = 20\%
$$

$$
\boxed{CS = 20\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A **CS of 20%** is considered **good** for EVA midsoles (typical spec: < 30% per ASTM D395 Method B). Lower CS = better long-term cushioning. If CS > 40%, the midsole is "dead" and the shoe loses energy-return.

### 3.3 Analytical Problem: Clamp Tonnage for DIP Machine Sizing

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A DIP machine injects PVC at pressure $P_{inj} = 800 \text{ bar} = 80 \text{ MPa}$ onto a sole of projected area $A_{proj} = 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.025 \text{ m}^2$. Calculate the required **clamping force**.

**Step 1 — Apply the clamp force formula**

$$
F_{clamp} = P_{inj} \times A_{proj} \times \text{safety factor}
$$

> **Logic:** The clamp must overcome the injection pressure acting on the projected area; a safety factor of **1.1–1.3** is standard for footwear machines (lower than general injection molding because pressures are typically < 1000 bar).

**Step 2 — Calculate (with SF = 1.2)**

$$
F_{clamp} = 80 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \times 0.025 \text{ m}^2 \times 1.2
$$

$$
F_{clamp} = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ N} \times 1.2 = 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ N} = 2400 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} \approx 240 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 240-tonne clamp DIP machine is the typical **mid-range machine** used in Indian/Kerala footwear clusters (e.g., the units in Ambur, Ranipet, and Agra footwear parks).

### 3.4 Comparative Process Selection Matrix

| Selection Criterion | DIP | Slush Molding | Calendering | Compression Molding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Best for** | Casual/sport shoes | Boots (gumboots) | Sheet soles | Vulcanized rubber soles |
| **Tooling cost** | Medium-High | High (rotary molds) | Low (rollers) | Medium |
| **Cycle time** | 60–90 s | 8–15 min | Continuous | 3–8 min |
| **Material waste** | Low | Medium (excess plastisol) | Medium (trim) | Low |
| **Surface finish** | Excellent (mold-defined) | Excellent | Smooth sheet | Excellent |
| **Wall thickness control** | ±0.3 mm | ±0.5 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.4 mm |
| **Capital cost** | High | Very High | Medium | Medium |
| **KTU exam relevance** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Polymer Shoe

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Polymer Feed] --> B{Process Selection}
    B -->|DIP| C[PVC / TPR Melt Injection]
    B -->|Slush| D[PVC Plastisol Rotary Mold]
    B -->|Calender| E[PVC Sheet Extrusion]
    B -->|Compression| F[Rubber / PU Compression Mold]
    C --> G[Sole Forming]
    D --> G
    E --> H[Die-cut Sole Blanks]
    F --> G
    H --> I[Adhesive Application]
    G --> I
    J[Upper Preparation] --> K[Lasting Operation]
    K --> I
    I --> L[Final Assembly]
    L --> M[Quality Inspection]
    M --> N[Finished Footwear]
```

### 4.2 Direct Injection Process (DIP) Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aluminum Last] --> B[Release Agent Spray]
    B --> C[Upper Lasting and Clamping]
    C --> D[Mold Closure with Last]
    D --> E[PVC Plastisol Injection at 80-200 bar]
    E --> F[Heating to 180-200 C]
    F --> G[Cooling and Gelation]
    G --> H[Mold Opening]
    H --> I[Shoe Stripping from Last]
    I --> J[Edge Trimming and Buffing]
    J --> K[Finished Sole-Bonded Shoe]
```

### 4.3 PVC Slush Molding for Boot Manufacture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid PVC Plastisol] --> B[Pour into Heated Mold]
    B --> C[Biaxial Rotation at 180-200 C]
    C --> D[8-15 min Gelation]
    D --> E[Excess Plastisol Pour-out]
    E --> F[Re-fusion Heating]
    F --> G[Cooling]
    G --> H[Mold Opening]
    H --> I[Boot Stripping]
    I --> J[Trimming and Inspection]
    J --> K[Final Boot with Upper Assembly]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Architecture of a Multi-Layer Shoe Sole

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Outsole: TPR or Vulcanized Rubber] --> B[Midsole: EVA or PU Foam]
    B --> C[Insole: Soft EVA or Leather Board]
    C --> D[Strobel Stitch or Adhesive Bond]
    D --> E[Upper: PVC Coated Fabric or Leather]
    E --> F[Toe Cap: TPU Reinforcement]
    E --> G[Heel Counter: ABS or TPU]
```

### 4.5 Footwear Process Selection Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Footwear Type] --> B{Type?}
    B -->|Casual/Sport| C[DIP Process]
    B -->|Boot Gumboot| D[Slush Molding]
    B -->|Sheet Sole Sandal| E[Calendering]
    B -->|Premium Vulcanized| F[Compression Molding]
    B -->|Sole Strip| G[Extrusion]
    C --> H[PVC or TPR]
    D --> I[PVC Plastisol]
    E --> J[PVC Compound]
    F --> K[Natural Rubber or PU]
    G --> L[TPR or PVC]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Direct Injection Process (DIP) in footwear manufacturing. Name two polymers commonly processed by DIP.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Direct Injection Process (DIP)** is a footwear manufacturing technique in which molten polymer (typically **PVC plastisol** or **TPR**) is injected directly into a closed mold cavity containing a lasted upper, forming and bonding the sole in a single operation. (2 marks)
>
> Two polymers commonly processed by DIP are:
> 1. **Plasticized PVC** (for low-cost school and casual shoes)
> 2. **TPR (Thermoplastic Rubber)** or **TPU** (for premium sports and safety shoes) (1 mark)

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the function of a "last" in footwear manufacturing? What material is it typically made of?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **last** is a foot-shaped form (mold) over which the shoe upper is stretched and shaped during manufacture; it provides the **three-dimensional geometry** of the final shoe. (2 marks)
>
> It is typically made of **aluminum alloy** (for high-volume production due to good thermal conductivity for DIP cooling), **wood** (traditional), or **plastic** (for design prototyping). (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Direct Injection Process (DIP)** for the manufacture of PVC footwear. Discuss the role of the last, mold design, plastisol rheology, and cycle time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **DIP, slush molding, and compression molding** for footwear with respect to (i) cycle time, (ii) capital cost, (iii) product range, and (iv) typical polymer used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Direct Injection Process (DIP) for PVC Footwear (7 marks):**

**1. Definition & Principle (1.5 marks):**
DIP is a single-step, single-mold process where liquid **PVC plastisol** (PVC resin dispersed in plasticizer + stabilizer + filler) is injected into a heated, closed mold containing the lasted upper. The plastisol **gels and fuses** against the hot mold surface (180–200 °C) to form the sole, which is simultaneously **chemically bonded** to the upper via the heated PVC.

**2. Last Preparation (1 mark):**
- Aluminum lasts (lightweight, high thermal conductivity) are cleaned, release-agent sprayed.
- The **upper** is then manually or robotically **lasted** (stretched and clamped) over the last.
- The lasted upper is positioned in the lower half of the sole mold.

**3. Mold Design (1.5 marks):**
- **Two-plate mold**: lower mold holds the last + upper; upper mold defines the sole cavity.
- Mold material: **aluminum** or **steel** with polished cavities.
- Integrated **runner system** and **gate** for plastisol entry.
- Cooling channels for rapid heat extraction.
- A **vacuum vent** at the far end of the cavity to remove air.

**4. Plastisol Rheology (1.5 marks):**
- PVC plastisol is a **non-Newtonian thixotropic fluid** with viscosity **2–10 Pa·s** at 25 °C.
- During heating, it undergoes **gelation** at 80–100 °C (swelling of resin particles) and **fusion** at 160–200 °C (complete plasticizer absorption).
- Viscosity must be low enough for mold filling (shear-thinning) but high enough not to drip off the last.

**5. Cycle Time (1 mark):**
A typical DIP cycle = **Injection (5 s) + Heating/Gelation (15–20 s) + Cooling (30–40 s) + Demolding (3–5 s) ≈ 60–80 s/pair**, enabling **~50 pairs/hour/mold station** on a rotary machine.

**6. Finishing (0.5 marks):**
The shoe is stripped from the last, edges are trimmed/buffed, and the upper is finished. The mold rotates to the next station in a multi-station rotary press.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | DIP | Slush Molding | Compression Molding |
|-----------|-----|---------------|---------------------|
| **(i) Cycle time** | 60–90 s | 8–15 min | 3–8 min |
| **(ii) Capital cost** | High (₹1–5 Cr) | Very High (rotary biaxial machines) | Medium (₹30L–1Cr) |
| **(iii) Product range** | Casuals, sports, school shoes | Boots (PVC gumboots, fashion boots) | Vulcanized rubber soles, PU soles |
| **(iv) Typical polymer** | PVC plastisol, TPR, TPU | PVC plastisol | Natural rubber, SBR, PU |

(1.75 marks each row)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B - 7 mark subparts):** Students often write the DIP process but **forget to mention the role of plastisol rheology and gelation** — these are 2 separate marking points in the KTU scheme. Also, the comparison table must be **drawn (not just narrated)** to earn full 7 marks. Always include **quantitative numbers** (cycle times, temperatures) — vague answers are capped at 4/7.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **manufacture of PVC gumboots by slush molding**. Discuss the role of plastisol formulation, mold rotation, and gelation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on:
   (i) **Compression molding of PU shoe soles**
   (ii) **Strobel stitching in footwear construction**
   (iii) **Shore A hardness testing for shoe soles** **(7 marks — 2.33 marks each)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) PVC Slush Molding of Gumboots (7 marks):**

**1. Principle (1 mark):**
Slush molding is a **rotational casting** process where liquid PVC plastisol is poured into a heated, rotating, hollow mold. The plastisol **gels on the hot mold wall** to form a uniform layer, excess is poured out, and the gelled layer is **fused** to produce a seamless, hollow article.

**2. Flow Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Plastisol Preparation → Mold Pouring → Biaxial Rotation (180-200 °C, 8-15 min) →
Excess Pour-out → Re-fusion (5-8 min) → Cooling → Demolding → Trimming → Boot
```

**3. Plastisol Formulation (1.5 marks):**
A typical gumboot plastisol:
- **PVC resin (K-value 65–70)**: 100 parts
- **Plasticizer (DOP, DINP)**: 70–90 phr
- **Stabilizer (Ca-Zn)**: 2–3 phr
- **Epoxidized soybean oil (ESBO)**: 3–5 phr
- **Filler (CaCO₃)**: 10–20 phr
- **Pigments**: as needed
- **Viscosity**: 3–6 Pa·s at 25 °C

**4. Mold Rotation (1.5 marks):**
- **Biaxial rotation** (rotation about two perpendicular axes) ensures **uniform wall thickness** (2–4 mm).
- Rotation speed: 5–15 rpm on each axis.
- Mold temperature: **180–200 °C** (saturated steam or oil-heated).

**5. Gelation & Fusion (1 mark):**
- At **80–100 °C**, plastisol viscosity rises sharply (gelation).
- At **160–200 °C**, complete plasticizer absorption → **fusion** → tough, elastic boot wall.

**(b) Short Notes (7 marks):**

**(i) Compression Molding of PU Shoe Soles (2.33 marks):**
- **PU system** = polyol + isocyanate (typically MDI-based) + chain extender + catalyst, mixed in a **1:1 to 1:1.2** ratio.
- The reactive mixture is poured into a **heated, closed mold** (40–55 °C for PU).
- **Polyaddition reaction** occurs in-mold for 3–6 min, producing a microcellular PU sole.
- **Density**: 0.4–0.6 g/cm³ (midsole) to 1.0 g/cm³ (outsole).
- Used in premium safety and sports shoes (e.g., Birkenstock, safety boots).

**(ii) Strobel Stitching in Footwear Construction (2.33 marks):**
- A **Strobel stitch** is a **chain stitch** sewn around the perimeter of the **insole bottom** that attaches the upper to the insole.
- The **insole** (a fabric-reinforced board) replaces the traditional heel-to-toe lasting.
- Allows **flexibility** and is used in **athletic shoes, moccasins, and casual shoes**.
- It is faster than conventional lasting and produces a more flexible shoe.

**(iii) Shore A Hardness Testing for Shoe Soles (2.33 marks):**
- **Shore A** is a durometer scale for soft elastomers (rubber, TPR, soft PU).
- Test per **ASTM D2240** / **ISO 868**: a truncated cone indenter is pressed into the specimen; depth of indentation gives the **Shore A value** (0 = very soft, 100 = very hard).
- **Typical shoe sole hardness:**
  - Soft comfort soles: **40–55 Shore A**
  - Standard running shoes: **55–65 Shore A**
  - Safety/work boots: **70–85 Shore A**
- Trade-off: **Harder** = more durable but less cushioned; **Softer** = more cushioned but wears faster.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B - 7 mark subparts - short notes):** Each short note in 7-mark subpart (b) carries approximately **2.33 marks**. To get full marks, each note MUST have:
> 1. **A clear definition** (1 mark)
> 2. **A relevant example or numerical value** (1 mark)
> 3. **An application/use** (0.33 marks)
>
> A common mistake is writing a paragraph without numerical/quantitative content — this caps your marks at 1.33/2.33 per note.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls That Cost Marks in KTU Footwear Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing DIP with Slush Molding** — DIP uses *closed-mold injection*; Slush uses *open-pour + rotation*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the "last"** — Any footwear process question demands explicit mention of the **last** function. Omitting it = -1 to -2 marks.
> 3. **No process flow diagram** — KTU valuation key gives **2 marks** for a labeled flow diagram in 7-mark subparts. Writing only text = capped at 5/7.
> 4. **Mixing units** — Plastisol viscosity in **Pa·s**, temperature in **°C**, pressure in **bar or MPa**. Mixing SI and CGS loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Generic polymer names** — Always specify **"PVC plastisol"** or **"TPR (SBS-based)"** — not just "rubber" or "plastic".

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Footwear = layered polymer assembly** on a **last**, with sole manufactured by a **polymer-specific process** and upper attached by **lasting + adhesive bonding**.
- **DIP (Direct Injection Process)** is the most common process; uses **PVC plastisol** or **TPR** injected at **80–200 bar** onto a lasted upper.
- **Slush Molding** is used for **PVC gumboots**; biaxial rotation, gelation at 80–100 °C, fusion at 160–200 °C.
- **Calendering** produces PVC sheets that are die-cut into **sole blanks** for low-cost sandals.
- **Compression Molding** is used for **vulcanized rubber** and **PU microcellular** soles.
- **Cycle time of DIP ≈ 60–80 s/pair**; cooling time governed by 1-D transient conduction: $t_{cool} = \dfrac{L^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln(\Delta T \text{ ratio})$.
- **Compression Set** test (ASTM D395) measures midsole recovery: $CS = \dfrac{h_0 - h_1}{h_0 - h_s} \times 100\%$.
- **Shore A hardness**: 30–50 soft, 55–70 standard, 70–85 hard.
- **Last** = foot-shaped mold; usually **aluminum** for DIP.
- **EVA foam** is the standard **midsole** material; **PU** for premium cushioning; **TPR/TPU** for outsoles.
- **Adhesives** for sole-upper bonding: **PU adhesive** and **neoprene-based** contact cements.
- **Strobel stitching** is the modern athletic-shoe upper-insole attachment technique.
- **DIP clamp tonnage** ≈ $P_{inj} \times A_{proj} \times 1.2$ safety factor.
- **PU shoe sole molding** uses a 1:1 polyol:isocyanate reactive mix; mold at 40–55 °C, demold in 3–6 min.
- **Safety footwear** standards: **EN ISO 20345** (EU), **IS 15298** (India/BIS).
- **Global footwear market > $400 billion**; **polymer share ≈ 65%** by mass.
- **Footwear is the largest market for plasticized PVC** and the **second-largest for PU** (after automotive).
- **Phylon** (compressed, expanded EVA) is the lightweight midsole material used in performance running shoes (e.g., Nike Phylon, Adidas LightStrike).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hoses and Belting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT502 - POLYMER PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hoses and Belting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Hoses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hose (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A flexible, hollow, tubular polymeric product designed to convey fluids (liquids, gases, or slurries) under positive or negative pressure from one point to another, typically operating under dynamic, often pulsating conditions.

A hose is essentially a **flexible pipeline** made from polymeric materials (rubber, PVC, polyurethane, silicone, or thermoplastic elastomers) combined with suitable reinforcements to withstand internal pressure, abrasion, temperature, and environmental attack.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of a garden hose you use at home. It is a soft plastic tube reinforced with mesh/fabric so that when you turn on the tap and water pushes through it, the hose does not burst. It is flexible, can be coiled, and is "bendy" because the polymer matrix stretches slightly while the textile/wire reinforcement stops it from ballooning. Industrial hoses are just a much tougher, more engineered version of this same idea.

**Composition of a typical hose (3-layer structure):**
1. **Inner Tube (Lining):** Carries the fluid; must be chemically compatible with the medium. Common materials: NBR, EPDM, NR, PTFE, PVC.
2. **Reinforcement Layer:** Provides pressure resistance. Forms: braided textile, spiral-wound wire, knitted fabric, or cord.
3. **Outer Cover:** Protects from abrasion, weathering, ozone, and chemicals. Common materials: CR, EPDM, Hypalon, CSM.

---

### 1.2 Definition of Belting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Belting (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** An endless, flexible, flat or shaped polymeric composite product used either for **power transmission** (transferring rotational motion between pulleys/shafts) or for **material handling** (conveying bulk goods and packages over distance).

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Look at a car's engine — a thick black rubber belt loops around several pulleys (crankshaft, alternator, AC compressor) and transfers rotational energy. That is a V-belt. Now imagine the belt at a supermarket checkout counter that moves groceries along — that is a conveyor belt. Both are polymer-based "belts," engineered differently for their specific job: one transmits power, the other transports goods.

**Types of Belts Covered in KTU Module 4:**
| Type | Function | Polymer Materials |
|------|----------|-------------------|
| Flat Belt | Power transmission | Leather-replaced, rubber-fabric composite, PU |
| V-Belt | High-torque power transmission | NR + fabric + cord |
| Timing/Toothed Belt | Synchronous drive | PU or NR with glass/steel cords |
| Conveyor Belt | Bulk material handling | NR/SBR rubber + canvas/steel cord |

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Units to Memorize:**
> - Working Pressure (Hoses): rated in **bar (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 14.5 psi)**
> - Burst Pressure (Hoses): typically **4× the working pressure (4:1 safety factor)**
> - Belt Linear Speed: rated in **m/s** (typical: 5–30 m/s)
> - Power Transmitted: rated in **kW (kilowatts)**
> - Center Distance: rated in **mm or m**
