## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define latex. Differentiate between natural latex and synthetic latex.**

**Model Answer:**

Latex is a colloidal dispersion of polymer micro-particles in water. The polymer phase is in the form of discrete particles (typically 0.05–5 µm) stabilized by surfactants, not dissolved.

**Natural Latex:**
- Obtained from *Hevea brasiliensis* tree (rubber plantation).
- Polymer is **cis-1,4-polyisoprene**.
- TSC of fresh latex: **30%–40%**.
- Contains natural proteins, fatty acids, and resins.

**Synthetic Latex:**
- Produced by **emulsion polymerization** of monomers (e.g., butadiene + styrene → SBR latex).
- Polymer type can be tailored (SBR, NBR, PVC, polychloroprene, acrylic).
- TSC and particle size can be controlled precisely.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three methods of latex processing and mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Latex Dipping** — Application: surgical/examination gloves, balloons.
2. **Latex Foaming (Dunlop Process)** — Application: foam mattresses and pillows.
3. **Latex Extrusion** — Application: continuous threads for elastic garments.
4. (Bonus) **Latex Casting** — Application: industrial gloves, balloons (rotational moulding).
5. (Bonus) **Latex Coating** — Application: backed carpets, upholstery fabric.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Latex and Foam*

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Module Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a) (7 Marks)**
With a neat flow diagram, explain the **Dunlop process** for the manufacture of latex foam rubber. List the major ingredients used in latex foam compounding and state the function of each.

**Model Answer (Step-by-step):**

**1. Latex Compounding (Ingredients & Functions):**

| Ingredient | Function |
|---|---|
| Latex (60% TSC) | Base polymer (cis-1,4-polyisoprene) |
| Sulfur (1.5–2.5 phr) | Vulcanizing agent |
| Zinc oxide (3 phr) | Activator |
| ZDC/ZMB (0.5–1 phr) | Accelerator |
| Antioxidant (0.5–1 phr) | Prevents oxidative degradation |
| Oleate soap (1–2 phr) | Foaming agent + stabilizer |
| Sodium fluorosilicate (Na₂SiF₆) | Gellant / stiffener |
| Mineral oil | Plasticizer (improves softness) |

**2. Process Flow (Dunlop Process):**

1. **Compounding:** All ingredients except gellant and sulfur are added to the latex in a slow-speed mixer.
2. **Foaming:** The compounded latex is fed into a **Oakes-type foamer** (high-speed mechanical whisking), where air is beaten in until the volume increases **3–4 times** (foam density drops from ~950 kg/m³ to ~250 kg/m³).
3. **Gellation:** Stiffener (Na₂SiF₆) is added just before moulding. It hydrolyses slowly to release silicic acid and NaF, which destabilize the soap and cause the foam to **gel** in the mould.
4. **Moulding:** The foam is poured into aluminium moulds.
5. **Vulcanization:** Moulds pass through a **steam-heated tunnel** at **100°C for 20–30 minutes**. Sulfur crosslinks the polymer.
6. **Washing:** Foam is washed to remove residual chemicals and soap.
7. **Drying:** Final drying in a hot-air oven (~70°C).
8. **Finishing:** Demoulded, trimmed, and inspected.

**3. Key Reaction (Gelation):**
$$
\text{Na}_2\text{SiF}_6 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Si(OH)}_4 + 2\text{NaF} + 4\text{HF}
$$
The HF and Si(OH)₄ destabilize the soap film around air bubbles, locking in the foam structure.

> [!NOTE]
> The Talalay process is an alternative that uses **vacuum + CO₂ gelation + cold vulcanization** for more uniform cell structure. (Mention as comparison for full marks.)

---

**(b) (7 Marks)**
Describe **chemical blowing agents** used in polymer foam processing. Compare **closed-cell** and **open-cell** foams with examples.

**Model Answer:**

**Chemical Blowing Agents:**
These are compounds that **decompose on heating** to release gases (mainly N₂, CO₂, CO, NH₃) which expand the polymer.

| Blowing Agent | Decomposition Temp | Gas Released |
|---|---|---|
| **Azodicarbonamide (ADC/ADCA)** | 200–220°C | N₂ + CO (yield: 220 mL/g) |
| **Sodium bicarbonate** | 100–140°C | CO₂ + H₂O (yield: 125 mL/g) |
| **Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN)** | 60–80°C | N₂ |
| **Toluene sulfonyl hydrazide (TSH)** | 105–110°C | N₂ + H₂O |
| **5-PT (5-phenyltetrazole)** | 240–250°C | N₂ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection criterion:** Decomposition temperature must match the polymer processing temperature. ADC is the most widely used globally due to high gas yield and reasonable decomposition temperature.

**Comparison of Foam Types:**

| Property | Closed-Cell Foam | Open-Cell Foam |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell structure** | Each cell sealed by polymer walls | Cells interconnected |
| **Density** | Higher (30–200 kg/m³) | Lower (20–80 kg/m³) |
| **Water absorption** | Very low | High (absorbent) |
| **Thermal insulation** | Excellent | Poor (air can move) |
| **Sound absorption** | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Mechanical strength** | High | Lower |
| **Example** | **XPS, EPS, PU rigid foam** | **PU flexible foam, sponge, latex foam** |
| **Application** | Insulation, packaging, structural | Cushioning, mattresses, acoustic panels |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Fibre Spinning Alternative*

**(a) (7 Marks)**
**Describe the melt spinning process for polymer fibres with a neat block diagram. List any three polymers commonly processed by melt spinning.**

**Model Answer:**

**Melt Spinning Process — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Polymer Feed:** Dried polymer chips/granules (e.g., PET, Nylon) are fed to the **extruder hopper**.
2. **Melting:** The extruder screw melts the polymer (PET at **260–290°C**, Nylon 6 at **240–260°C**). Inert gas (N₂) blanketing prevents oxidation.
3. **Filtration & Metering:** The melt passes through a **filter pack** (removes gels, dirt) and a **spin pump** (gear pump delivering precise flow).
4. **Extrusion through Spinneret:** Melt is forced through a **spinneret** (e.g., 24–200 holes of 0.1–0.5 mm).
5. **Quenching:** Filaments are cooled by cross-flow air in a **quench chamber**.
6. **Spin Finish Application:** Lubricant/finish is applied to prevent static and filament cohesion.
7. **Drawing:** Filaments pass through **heated godet rollers** with progressively increasing speed (draw ratio 3:1 to 6:1) — this aligns molecular chains and increases tensile strength.
8. **Crimping/Texturizing** (optional for textile yarns).
9. **Take-up Winding:** Yarn is wound on bobbins.

**Three Polymers Processed by Melt Spinning:**

1. **PET (Polyethylene terephthalate)** — polyester textile fibre, tyre cord.
2. **Nylon 6 and Nylon 6,6** — apparel, carpets, ropes.
3. **Polypropylene** — nonwovens, geotextiles, ropes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage:** Melt spinning avoids solvent recovery, making it the **most economical** and **environmentally friendly** method. **Disadvantage:** Only applicable to thermally stable polymers that melt without decomposition.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)**
**With a derivation, show that the final fibre diameter in melt spinning is given by $D_f = D_j / \sqrt{\text{DDR}}$, where DDR is the draw down ratio. A spinneret has 50 holes of diameter 0.25 mm. If the throughput per hole is 0.8 g/min and the polymer density is 1.32 g/cm³, calculate the final fibre diameter when the take-up speed is 2500 m/min.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** (As shown in Section 3.1 of these notes)

By conservation of mass: $A_j V_j = A_f V_d$

$$
D_f = \dfrac{D_j}{\sqrt{V_d/V_j}} = \dfrac{D_j}{\sqrt{\text{DDR}}}
$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Volumetric flow rate per hole.**

$$
Q = \dfrac{0.8 \text{ g/min}}{1.32 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 0.6061 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{0.6061 \times 10^{-6}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.0102 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Spinneret hole area.**

$$
A_j = \dfrac{\pi (0.25 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = 4.9087 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Jet velocity.**

$$
V_j = \dfrac{Q}{A_j} = \dfrac{1.0102 \times 10^{-8}}{4.9087 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.2058 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Take-up velocity in m/s.**

$$
V_d = \dfrac{2500}{60} = 41.667 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5: Draw Down Ratio.**

$$
\text{DDR} = \dfrac{41.667}{0.2058} = 202.46
$$

**Step 6: Final fibre diameter.**

$$
D_f = \dfrac{0.25 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{202.46}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{14.229}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D_f = 1.757 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 17.57 \text{ µm}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Most common mistake: forgetting to convert g/min → m³/s, or m/min → m/s. Always standardize units FIRST.
> 2. **In latex questions**, students often confuse **phr (parts per hundred rubber)** with percentage. The formula is: $\text{phr} = (\text{mass of ingredient}/\text{mass of rubber}) \times 100$.
> 3. **For ADC blowing agent**, students write the decomposition gases as just "nitrogen." The correct gases are **N₂ + CO** (both must be mentioned for full credit).
> 4. **In spinning derivations**, students skip the **incompressibility assumption** — this is a KTU marker for a unit mark.
> 5. **Dunlop vs. Talalay** — examiners often expect a 1–2 line comparison. Skipping this = -1 mark.
> 6. **Closed-cell vs. open-cell** — Don't just give one example; provide a *minimum of two* and contrast *three* properties for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Latex is a COLLOID, not a solution** — distinguish it from polymer solution in spin finishes.
- **Total Solid Content (TSC) of fresh natural latex ≈ 30–40%**; concentrated latex ≈ **60%** (centrifuged).
- **Dunlop process is the standard latex foam method** — key gellant is **Na₂SiF₆** (sodium fluorosilicate).
- **Talalay process** uses **vacuum + CO₂ + cold cure** for finer cell structure.
- **Melt spinning is the most economical** fibre method — used for PET, Nylon, PP — because it **eliminates solvents**.
- **Draw Down Ratio (DDR)** is the controlling parameter for fibre diameter: $D_f = D_j / \sqrt{\text{DDR}}$.
- **Denier = mass (g) per 9000 m**; **Tex = mass (g) per 1000 m**; **1 denier = 0.111 tex**.
- **Azodicarbonamide (ADC)** is the **most widely used chemical blowing agent** — decomposes at **~210°C** releasing **N₂ + CO**.
- **Closed-cell foams = insulation + structural**; **Open-cell foams = cushioning + acoustic**.
- **Dry-Jet Wet Spinning** is the secret behind ultra-high-performance fibres (Kevlar, Dyneema, Lyocell) — the **air gap** allows pre-orientation before coagulation.
- **Electrospinning** produces **sub-micron to nano-fibres** via a high-voltage electric field.
- **Foam expansion ratio** = $\rho_{\text{polymer}} / \rho_{\text{foam}}$ — typical PU foams: 20–50×.
- **Cell density (ASTM D3576)** = $(n/A)^{3/2} \times f$, where $n$ = number of cells in area $A$ of micrograph, $f$ = area correction factor.
- **Latex compounding additives mnemonic — "S-S-Z-F-A-O"**: Sulfur, Stabilizers, ZnO, Fillers, Accelerators, Oleate.
- **Latex processing methods** (7 main): Dipping, Casting, Foaming, Extrusion, Coating, Spraying, Thread production.
- **Spinning methods** (6 main): Melt, Dry, Wet, Dry-Jet Wet, Gel, Electro.
- **Always mention solvent recovery in dry/wet spinning** — this is a key KTU valuation point.
- **For KTU numericals**, always show **all unit conversions**; marks are awarded for intermediate steps.
